plus T equals zero"] B1 --> B5 B4 --> B5 B3 --> B5 ``` ## 4.3 Analysis Flowchart — Decision Tree for Classifying a Member This flowchart helps a student identify whether a given machine member qualifies as a two-force member, a torque-only member, or a general two-force + torque member. ```mermaid graph TD C1["START
Identify the rigid member"] --> C2{"How many
external forces
act on it?"} C2 -->|Two forces| C3{"Is there an
external couple
between them?"} C2 -->|More than two| C4["GENERAL CASE
Use three equilibrium
equations"] C2 -->|One force| C5["UNSTABLE
Member cannot be
in equilibrium"] C3 -->|No couple| C6["TWO-FORCE MEMBER
Forces are collinear
along the line of pins"] C3 -->|Yes couple| C7["TWO FORCES PLUS TORQUE
Use full equilibrium
set of equations"] C6 --> C8["Simplification
One equation per member"] C7 --> C9["Full Solution
Three equations required"] ``` ## 4.4 Free Body Representation of a Bell Crank (Engineering Schematic) The following block diagram models a bell-crank lever — a classic engineering embodiment of the "two forces + torque" analysis. ```mermaid graph LR D1["INPUT ARM
Length L1
Angle theta1"] -->|Pin joint P| D2["CENTRAL PIVOT
Bearing reaction
Components Rx and Ry"] D2 -->|Pin joint Q| D3["OUTPUT ARM
Length L2
Angle theta2"] D4["INPUT FORCE F1
at end of arm 1"] --> D1 D3 --> D5["OUTPUT FORCE F2
at end of arm 2"] D6["MOMENT EQUILIBRIUM"] --> D7["F1 times L1 times sin theta1
equals F2 times L2 times sin theta2"] D2 --> D6 D4 --> D6 D5 --> D6 ``` ## 4.5 Vector Diagram — Force Resolution for Two Non-Collinear Forces This block diagram shows how two non-collinear forces reduce to a single resultant via the parallelogram law, with a corrective couple to preserve moment equivalence. ```mermaid graph TD E1["FORCE F1
at point A
angle alpha1"] --> E2["PARALLELOGRAM
CONSTRUCTION"] E3["FORCE F2
at point B
angle alpha2"] --> E2 E2 --> E4["RESULTANT R
equals F1 plus F2
Magnitude and direction"] E2 --> E5["COUPLE TO BALANCE
M equals rAB cross F1
plus rAB cross F2"] E4 --> E6["EQUIVALENT SYSTEM AT A
Force R at point A
plus couple M"] E5 --> E6 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Tip:** The moment $\vec{M}$ in the final equivalent system at point $A$ is the residual couple that arises because the two forces were **not** collinear to begin with. If they were collinear, $M = 0$ and the member would qualify as a pure two-force member. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define a two-force member. State the two essential conditions that a member must satisfy to be classified as a two-force member.** **Model Answer:** A two-force member is a rigid body in equilibrium under the action of exactly two forces, applied at two distinct points on the body. **Essential conditions:** 1. **Exactly two forces** must act on the member at two distinct points. 2. **No external couple or torque** should act anywhere between these two points. Under these conditions, the two forces must be **equal in magnitude**, **opposite in direction**, and **collinear** (acting along the line joining the two points of application). > [!TIP] > **Valuation Key Insight:** A common student error is to state "equal and opposite" only. Examiners expect the **third condition — collinearity** — to be explicitly mentioned. Missing it costs 1 mark. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State and explain Varignon's Theorem with a suitable example.** **Model Answer:** **Varignon's Theorem:** *The moment of the resultant of a system of concurrent forces about any point is equal to the algebraic sum of the moments of the individual forces about the same point.* **Mathematical Statement:** $$ M_O(\vec{R}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_O(\vec{F_i}) $$ where $\vec{R} = \sum \vec{F_i}$. **Example:** Consider two forces $F_1 = 50$ N acting at angle $30°$ and $F_2 = 30$ N acting at angle $90°$, both passing through a point. The moment of their resultant about any reference point equals the sum of the individual moments computed independently. This greatly simplifies multi-force moment calculations. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 1 carries 14-mark questions with internal choice. Students answer **one out of two**. Below are TWO fully solved alternatives. ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 — Model Paper]` **A rigid horizontal bar $AB$ of length 1.2 m is pinned at end $A$. A vertical downward force of 200 N is applied at the free end $B$. A clockwise torque of 60 N·m is applied at a point $C$, located 0.4 m from $A$. Determine:** **(a)** The reactions at pin $A$. **(b)** The equivalent force-couple system at the midpoint of the bar. #### Part (a) — Solution [7 Marks] **Step 1: Free Body Diagram** Pin reaction at $A$: $A_x$ (horizontal) and $A_y$ (vertical). Applied load at $B$: 200 N downward at $L = 1.2$ m. Applied couple at $C$: $T = 60$ N·m clockwise. **Step 2: Force Equilibrium** $$ \sum F_x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_x = 0 $$ $$ \sum F_y = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_y - 200 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_y = 200 \, \text{N (upward)} $$ **Step 3: Moment Equilibrium About $A$** Taking CCW as positive: $$ \sum M_A = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad -200 \times 1.2 + (-60) = 0 $$ $$ -240 - 60 = -300 \, \text{N·m} \neq 0 $$ **Interpretation:** The system is **not in equilibrium** as stated. An additional 300 N·m CCW torque must be applied at $A$ (e.g., by a reaction couple from a fixed support) to balance it. If the support at $A$ is a fixed support (not a pin), then the support exerts a couple of **300 N·m CCW** along with the reactions $A_x = 0$, $A_y = 200$ N. #### Part (b) — Equivalent System at Midpoint $M$ (at 0.6 m from $A$) [7 Marks] **Step 1: Resultant Force** The resultant of all forces on the bar: $$ \vec{R} = \vec{A} + \vec{F_B} = 0 + (-200 \, \hat{j}) = -200 \, \hat{j} \, \text{N} $$ **Step 2: Resultant Moment at $M$** $$ M_M = (\text{Moment of } A \text{ about } M) + (\text{Moment of } F_B \text{ about } M) + T $$ - $A$ at $M$: zero moment (zero arm). - $F_B$ at distance $0.6$ m from $M$: moment $= -200 \times 0.6 = -120$ N·m (CW). - Applied torque: $-60$ N·m (CW). $$ M_M = 0 - 120 - 60 = -180 \, \text{N·m (CW)} $$ **Step 3: Equivalent System** A downward force of **200 N at $M$**, plus a clockwise couple of **180 N·m**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Model Paper]` **A connecting rod of an IC engine is treated as a two-force member. The pin at the piston end (point $A$) experiences a force of 1500 N along a line making an angle of 35° with the rod axis. The crank pin (point $B$) is located 0.3 m from $A$ along the rod. Determine:** **(a)** The force at $B$ and the orientation of the rod's line of action. **(b)** The equivalent force-couple system if an additional applied torque of 50 N·m acts at the midpoint of the rod (assume the rod is no longer a pure two-force member). #### Part (a) — Solution [7 Marks] **Step 1: Two-Force Member Property** Since the connecting rod has forces only at $A$ and $B$ with no external couple, it is a pure two-force member. **Step 2: Force at $B$** By the two-force member theorem, the force at $B$ must be **equal in magnitude and opposite in direction** to the force at $A$: $$ F_B = F_A = 1500 \, \text{N} $$ **Step 3: Direction of Force at $B$** The force at $A$ acts at $35°$ to the rod axis. Therefore, the force at $B$ must also be collinear with the line $AB$ — meaning it acts **at the same 35° angle to the rod axis** but in the opposite direction (i.e., the line of action is the same straight line through $A$ and $B$). **Conclusion:** The rod is in compression (or tension, depending on force sense), and the load is transmitted purely along its longitudinal axis. #### Part (b) — Equivalent System with Applied Torque [7 Marks] **Step 1: Acknowledgment of Non-Equilibrium** With an additional torque $T = 50$ N·m applied at the midpoint, the rod is **no longer a two-force member**. We must now apply the full equilibrium conditions. **Step 2: Force Equilibrium** Even with the torque, the force at $A$ and the force at $B$ must still sum to zero (no other external forces): $$ \vec{F_A} + \vec{F_B} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vec{F_B} = -\vec{F_A} = -1500 \, \text{N (opposite sense)} $$ **Step 3: Moment Equilibrium About $A$** Taking $A$ as reference (CCW positive): $$ \sum M_A = (\vec{r_B} - \vec{r_A}) \times \vec{F_B} + T = 0 $$ The moment of $F_B$ about $A$: - $\vec{r_{AB}} = 0.3 \, \hat{i}$ (along rod axis). - $\vec{F_B}$ at $35°$ opposite sense: components $F_{Bx} = -1500\cos(35°)$, $F_{By} = -1500\sin(35°)$ (assuming $F_A$ at +35° from rod axis). Moment about $A$ (z-component): $$ M_{A,z} = r_{AB,x} \cdot F_{By} - r_{AB,y} \cdot F_{Bx} = (0.3)(-1500\sin 35°) - 0 $$ $$ = -0.3 \times 1500 \times 0.5736 = -258.1 \, \text{N·m (CW)} $$ **Step 4: Add the Applied Torque** The applied couple $T$ is a free vector. Its moment about $A$: $$ M_T = -50 \, \text{N·m (CW)} $$ Total moment about $A$: $$ \sum M_A = -258.1 - 50 = -308.1 \, \text{N·m} \neq 0 $$ **Step 5: Required Reaction Couple** For equilibrium, a CCW reaction couple of **308.1 N·m** must be applied at the rod's support (or a real physical reaction must balance it). This shows that adding a torque to a "two-force" rod violates the two-force assumption, and the system requires an external balancing moment. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Forgetting Collinearity:** Stating "equal and opposite" without "collinear" costs **1 full mark** in 3-mark definition questions. > 2. **Sign Convention Errors:** Mixing CCW and CW in the moment equation without explicit declaration is the **#1 reason for losing 3-4 marks** in 14-mark problems. Always declare: *"Taking CCW as positive..."* > 3. **Ignoring the Couple's Free-Vector Nature:** Forgetting that a torque is location-independent leads to wrong arm distances and **incorrect moment calculations** (typical loss: 2-3 marks). > 4. **Not Drawing the FBD:** A clear free body diagram is worth **1-2 marks** in 14-mark problems. Skipping it is a guaranteed deduction. > 5. **Applying Two-Force Logic to a Non-Two-Force Member:** When a torque is added, the two-force theorem is invalid. Forgetting this leads to a **fundamentally wrong solution** (loss: 4-6 marks). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Summary — Members with Two Forces and Torque** - A **two-force member** has forces at exactly two points and **no external couple** between them. - Two-force member forces are **equal, opposite, and collinear** — the line of action joins the two force points. - A **couple** (torque) consists of two equal, opposite, parallel forces separated by a perpendicular distance. - A **couple is a free vector** — its effect is independent of its location on the body. - **Varignon's Theorem** lets us replace a multi-force system with a single resultant force and an equivalent couple. - For a 2D rigid body in equilibrium: **$\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M_O = 0$**. - For a "two forces + torque" member, use the **full three equilibrium equations** — the two-force simplification does **not** apply. - Always **draw the FBD first** — it is the single most important step in KTU board exam solutions. - **Sign convention:** Declare CCW = positive (or CW = positive) **explicitly** at the start of every solution. - For 3D problems, extend to **six scalar equations**: $\sum F_x, \sum F_y, \sum F_z, \sum M_x, \sum M_y, \sum M_z = 0$. - The **gravitational constant** $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ is standard unless a problem states otherwise. - **Pin joints** exert reaction forces (not moments); **fixed supports** exert both force and moment reactions. - The **moment arm** in 2D is the perpendicular distance from the reference point to the line of action of the force. - A **two-force member is in either pure tension or pure compression** along its axis (when forces are along the line of pins). - Recognizing two-force members early in mechanism analysis can **reduce the number of unknowns** by half. - The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **CO1 (Apply principles of statics to machinery components)** — this topic is the direct enabler.

topics.title: Free Body Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT527 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Free Body Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

A **Free Body Diagram (FBD)** is a graphical, vector-based representation of a body (or a sub-system of a mechanism) that has been **isolated from all surrounding bodies**, showing every **external force, support reaction, applied load, body weight, and inertia effect** (force and couple) acting on it. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, the FBD is the **mandatory first step** for force analysis of any static or dynamic system and serves as the bridge between the physical mechanism and the governing equilibrium equations (mapped to Course Outcomes involving force/motion analysis under NEP 2020 OBE).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Highlight):** A Free Body Diagram shows **ALL** forces acting on a body. **Every** force that crosses the imaginary cut boundary must appear; **nothing else** should appear. For dynamic problems, the **inertia force** $F_I = -m\,a_G$ and **inertia couple** $M_I = -I_G\,\alpha$ are added at the centre of gravity (CG) per **D'Alembert's Principle**, converting dynamics into a statics-style equilibrium.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a complex tower crane lifting a heavy steel beam. To understand the forces inside *only the lifting cable*, you would mentally (or with a sketch) "cut away" everything except that cable, then draw arrows for the **weight of the beam pulling down**, the **tension at the hook pulling down**, and the **tension at the top of the cable pulling up**. That isolated sketch is your **Free Body Diagram**.

Without performing that "cut", it is impossible to know which forces belong to which body, and the equilibrium equations become meaningless. The FBD is essentially a **visual inventory of every force acting on a chosen body**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Golden Rules of an FBD (Remember Forever):**
> 1. **Isolate** only the body you are analysing — draw a clear boundary around it.
> 2. **Show forces** at every point where the body previously contacted its surroundings (joints, sliders, gears, cams, ground).
> 3. For **moving bodies**, always add the **inertia force** at the CG and **inertia couple** (if rotating) before writing equilibrium equations.

## 1.3 Standard SI Units Used on KTU FBDs

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Force | $F$ | **N (Newton)** |
| Moment / Couple | $M$ | **N·m** |
| Mass | $m$ | **kg** |
| Acceleration | $a$ | **m/s²** |
| Angular acceleration | $\alpha$ | **rad/s²** |
| Length / Distance | $L, r, l$ | **m** or **mm** |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 expects students to draw a **neat, properly-labelled FBD** with clear force magnitudes, directions, and points of application before attempting any numerical solution. Marks are explicitly allotted for the quality and completeness of the FBD.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Systematic Steps for Constructing an FBD in a Mechanism

The following seven-step procedure is the **standard KTU board-exam method** for drawing a correct FBD of a moving link:

1. **Identify** the link or component to be analysed (e.g., the connecting rod of a slider-crank).
2. **Isolate** it mentally — erase all other links, but record every force that crosses the boundary.
3. **Replace each kinematic pair** by the corresponding reaction force(s):
   - Pin / revolute joint → two perpendicular components ($F_x$, $F_y$) or a single force at an angle.
   - Slider / prismatic joint → normal reaction $\perp$ to the sliding direction.
   - Cam-follower contact → normal reaction along the common normal.
   - Gear mesh → tangential force along the pressure line.
4. **Include self-weight** $W = m g$ acting vertically downwards at the CG.
5. **Add the inertia force** $\vec{F}_I = -m\,\vec{a}_G$ at the CG.
6. **Add the inertia couple** $M_I = -I_G\,\alpha$ if the body has angular acceleration.
7. **Choose a coordinate system** and apply the three equilibrium equations: $\Sigma F_x = 0,\ \Sigma F_y = 0,\ \Sigma M = 0$.

## 2.2 Types of Forces Encountered on Mechanism FBDs

| Category | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Applied / External Load** | $F_a$ | Known forces from external sources (gas pressure, payload). |
| **Weight** | $W = m g$ | Acts vertically downward through CG. |
| **Joint Reaction** | $R_x, R_y$ | Forces transmitted through pin, slider, cam, gear contacts. |
| **Friction** | $f = \mu N$ | Opposes relative motion at contacts. |
| **Inertia Force** | $F_I = m\,a_G$ | Fictitious force, opposite to CG acceleration. |
| **Inertia Couple** | $M_I = I_G\,\alpha$ | Fictitious couple, opposite to angular acceleration. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Governing Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Sigma \vec{F}_{ext} = m\, \vec{a}_G$ | Newton's 2nd Law (translation) | Any rigid body in motion |
| 2 | $\Sigma M_G = I_G\,\alpha$ | Moment about CG (Euler) | Rotation / plane motion |
| 3 | $\Sigma \vec{F}_{ext} + \vec{F}_I = 0$ | D'Alembert's form (translation) | Dynamic equilibrium on FBD |
| 4 | $\Sigma M_G + M_I = 0$ | D'Alembert's form (rotation) | FBD with rotational inertia |
| 5 | $\vec{F}_I = -m\,\vec{a}_G$ | Inertia force (vector form) | At CG, opposite to $\vec{a}_G$ |
| 6 | $M_I = -I_G\,\alpha$ | Inertia couple | Opposite to angular acceleration |
| 7 | $F_R = \dfrac{F_{net}}{\cos\phi}$ | Connecting rod force | Slider-crank FBD |
| 8 | $a_P \approx r\omega^{2}\!\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{r}{l}\cos 2\theta\right)$ | Approx. piston acceleration | Reciprocating mass inertia |
| 9 | $I_G = m\,k_G^{2}$ | Mass moment of inertia | $k_G$ = radius of gyration about CG |
| 10 | $\vec{a}_B = \vec{a}_A + \vec{\alpha}\times\vec{r}_{B/A} - \omega^{2}\vec{r}_{B/A}$ | Acceleration of point B on rigid body | Linking motion between CG and joints |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Free Body Diagrams are the **universal language of engineering mechanics** and are used extensively in:

- **Internal Combustion Engines:** FBDs of the connecting rod, crank, and piston to compute bearing reactions, side thrust, and turning moment.
- **Robotics & Manipulators:** FBDs of each link of a serial chain to compute joint torques via Newton–Euler or Lagrangian methods.
- **Vehicle Dynamics:** FBDs of wheels and suspension members for traction, braking, and cornering-force studies.
- **Aerospace Structures:** FBDs of control surfaces to compute hinge moments and actuator loads.
- **Civil Structures:** FBDs of beams, trusses, and frames for support reactions and internal forces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** Nearly every numerical problem in *Dynamics of Machinery* (slider-crank, four-bar, cam-follower, gyroscope) **begins with an FBD**. Marks are reserved specifically for a neat, complete, and correctly-labelled diagram.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of D'Alembert's Principle (from Newton's Second Law)

Consider a rigid body of mass $m$ whose centre of gravity $G$ has an instantaneous linear acceleration $\vec{a}_G$ and whose angular acceleration about $G$ is $\alpha$.

**Step 1 — Newton's Second Law for translation:**

$$\Sigma \vec{F}_{ext} = m\, \vec{a}_G$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to bring all terms to one side (equilibrium form):**

$$\Sigma \vec{F}_{ext} - m\, \vec{a}_G = 0$$

**Step 3 — Define the inertia force** $\vec{F}_I$ as a fictitious force equal in magnitude but opposite in direction to the product $m\,\vec{a}_G$:

$$\vec{F}_I = -m\, \vec{a}_G$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the equilibrium form:**

$$\Sigma \vec{F}_{ext} + \vec{F}_I = 0$$

**Step 5 — Apply the same logic to rotation about the CG:**

$$\Sigma M_G + M_I = 0 \quad \text{where} \quad M_I = -I_G\, \alpha$$

> This is **D'Alembert's Principle**: a dynamic problem is mathematically equivalent to a static equilibrium problem once the inertia force and inertia couple are introduced on the FBD. **Hence, FBDs can be used to solve dynamics problems using only statics equations.**

## 3.2 Derivation of Inertia Force on a Slider-Crank Piston

For a slider-crank with crank radius $r$, connecting-rod length $l$, crank angle $\theta$ from inner dead centre, and constant angular velocity $\omega$:

**Step 1 — Piston displacement** (from crank centre, along cylinder axis):

$$x = r\cos\theta + l\cos\phi$$

**Step 2 — Geometric constraint** (sine rule in the slider-crank triangle):

$$\frac{\sin\phi}{r} = \frac{\sin\theta}{l} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \sin\phi = \frac{r}{l}\sin\theta$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $x$ with respect to time:**

$$\dot{x} = -r\omega\sin\theta - l\dot\phi\sin\phi$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate again to get piston acceleration:**

$$\ddot{x} = -r\omega^{2}\cos\theta - r\alpha\sin\theta - l\ddot\phi\sin\phi - l\dot\phi^{2}\cos\phi$$

**Step 5 — For approximate analysis ($r/l \ll 1$), substitute $\cos\phi \approx 1$ and the small-angle approximations for $\phi$ derivatives. After algebraic reduction:**

$$a_P \approx r\omega^{2}\!\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{l}\cos 2\theta\right)$$

**Step 6 — Piston inertia force (D'Alembert):**

$$\boxed{F_I = m_P \cdot a_P = m_P\, r\omega^{2}\!\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{l}\cos 2\theta\right)}$$

This force is directed **opposite to the piston acceleration** and acts at the piston CG.

## 3.3 Derivation of Connecting-Rod Force from the FBD of the Piston

Consider the FBD of the reciprocating piston. The forces acting on it are:

| Symbol | Force | Direction |
|---|---|---|
| $F_g$ | Gas pressure force $= P\,A_p$ | Along cylinder axis, towards crank |
| $F_R$ | Force from connecting rod | Along rod axis |
| $N$ | Side thrust from cylinder wall | $\perp$ to cylinder axis |
| $F_I$ | Piston inertia force | Opposite to piston acceleration |

**Resolve along cylinder axis ($x$):**

$$\Sigma F_x = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; F_g - F_R\cos\phi - F_I = 0$$

$$\boxed{F_R = \frac{F_g - F_I}{\cos\phi}}$$

**Resolve perpendicular to cylinder axis ($y$):**

$$\Sigma F_y = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; N - F_R\sin\phi = 0$$

$$\boxed{N = (F_g - F_I)\,\tan\phi}$$

These two boxed results are the **foundation** for crank-shaft torque and side-thrust calculations in IC engines.

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example — Uniform Rod Rotating about a Hinge

**Problem:** A uniform slender rod $AB$, length $L = 1.2$ m, mass $m = 4$ kg, is hinged at $A$ and rotates in a vertical plane with **constant** angular velocity $\omega = 5$ rad/s. The rod makes angle $\theta = 30°$ with the downward vertical. Determine the hinge reactions.

**Step 1 — Locate the CG:** Since the rod is uniform, the CG lies at the geometric centre, distance $L/2$ from $A$.

**Step 2 — Acceleration of CG:** Because $\omega$ is constant, $\alpha = 0$. The CG has only **centripetal acceleration** of magnitude $\omega^{2}(L/2)$ directed from $G$ toward $A$:

$$a_{Gx} = -\omega^{2}\,\frac{L}{2}\,\sin\theta = -25 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 0.5 = -7.5 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

$$a_{Gy} = +\omega^{2}\,\frac{L}{2}\,\cos\theta = +25 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 0.866 = +12.99 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Inertia force components** (opposite to acceleration):

$$F_{Ix} = -m\,a_{Gx} = -4 \cdot (-7.5) = +30 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{Iy} = -m\,a_{Gy} = -4 \cdot (+12.99) = -51.96 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Apply dynamic equilibrium on the FBD:**

$$\Sigma F_x = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; A_x + F_{Ix} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; A_x = -30 \text{ N}$$

$$\Sigma F_y = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; A_y - mg + F_{Iy} = 0$$

$$A_y = mg - F_{Iy} = 4 \cdot 9.81 - (-51.96) = 39.24 + 51.96 = +91.20 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Resultant hinge reaction:**

$$R = \sqrt{A_x^{2} + A_y^{2}} = \sqrt{30^{2} + 91.20^{2}} = \sqrt{900 + 8317.44}$$

$$R = \sqrt{9217.44} \approx 96.01 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6 — Direction of reaction:**

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{\vert A_x \vert}{A_y} = \frac{30}{91.20} = 0.329 \;\Longrightarrow\; \alpha \approx 18.2°$$

The hinge reaction is **96.01 N at 18.2° from the vertical** (tilted away from the rod).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Diagram — Step-by-Step FBD Construction Procedure

The following **block-level flowchart** captures the systematic procedure used in KTU board examinations to construct a correct Free Body Diagram for a moving link in a mechanism.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Identify the link to analyse] --> B[Step 2: Isolate the link by drawing a clear boundary]
    B --> C[Step 3: Replace each kinematic pair with its reaction force]
    C --> D[Step 4: Mark the centre of gravity G of the link]
    D --> E[Step 5: Add the weight W equals m times g at G downward]
    E --> F[Step 6: Determine motion type: Translation Rotation or Plane Motion]
    F --> G[Step 7: Compute acceleration aG and angular acceleration alpha]
    G --> H[Step 8: Apply DAlembert Principle]
    H --> I[Step 9: Draw inertia force F I equals minus m times aG at G]
    I --> J[Step 10: Draw inertia couple M I equals minus IG times alpha if rotating]
    J --> K[Step 11: Choose coordinate axes and label all vectors]
    K --> L[Step 12: Write equilibrium equations Sigma Fx equals 0 and Sigma Fy equals 0 and Sigma M equals 0]
    L --> M[Step 13: Solve for unknown reactions and internal forces]
    M --> N[End: Validated FBD with all forces labelled]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Force Interactions on a Mechanism Link

The following diagram illustrates how the **six classes of forces/couples** interact on a single isolated link during dynamic force analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS[External Inputs to the Link]
        AP[Applied External Force Fa]
        WT[Self Weight W equals m times g]
        FL[Friction Force f equals mu times N]
    end
    subgraph JOINT[Constraint Forces from Neighbouring Links]
        JT[Pin Joint Reaction R sub x and R sub y]
        SD[Slider Normal Reaction N]
        CM[Cam or Gear Contact Force]
    end
    subgraph INERTIA[D Alembert Inertia System]
        IF[Inertia Force FI equals minus m times aG]
        IC[Inertia Couple MI equals minus IG times alpha]
    end
    INPUTS --> LINK[Isolated Mechanism Link]
    JOINT --> LINK
    INERTIA --> LINK
    LINK --> EQ[Dynamic Equilibrium Equations]
    EQ --> OUT[Unknown Reactions and Internal Forces]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each of the three input streams (External, Constraint, Inertia) feeds into the isolated link. The link then obeys the three equilibrium equations, producing the unknown reaction forces and moments. **Missing any single block invalidates the entire analysis** — a common KTU pitfall.

## 4.3 Skeleton FBD of a Connecting Rod (Text-Schematic)

Although a true pictorial FBD requires hand-drawing, the skeleton below captures every force that **must** appear on the FBD of a connecting rod in a slider-crank mechanism:

```
                F_I  ↓  (Inertia force at G, opposite to a_G)
                W  ↓  (mg at G)
                ●─────────────● ─── Connecting Rod ───
                ▲             ▲
              R_Ax        R_Bx
              R_Ay        R_By
        (Crank pin C)   (Wrist pin P)
        M_IA = −I_G·α (inertia couple)
```

Where:
- $R_{Ax}, R_{Ay}$ = reaction at crank-pin end $A$
- $R_{Bx}, R_{By}$ = reaction at wrist-pin end $B$
- $F_I$ = inertia force at CG $G$
- $W$ = self-weight at $G$
- $M_{IA}$ = inertia couple about $G$

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** 
**Define a Free Body Diagram. List any four types of forces (or couples) that may appear on the FBD of a moving link in a mechanism.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Free Body Diagram (FBD)** is a graphical representation of a body or sub-system of a mechanism, isolated from its surroundings, showing **all** external forces, support reactions, weights, and inertia effects acting on it.

Four common forces/couples appearing on a moving-link FBD are:

1. **Applied / External Load** ($F_a$) — e.g., gas pressure force on a piston.
2. **Self-Weight** ($W = m g$) — acts vertically downward through the CG.
3. **Joint Reaction Forces** ($R_x, R_y$) — at pin, slider, cam, or gear contacts.
4. **Inertia Force** ($F_I = m a_G$) — fictitious force at CG, opposite to acceleration.
*(A fifth is **Inertia Couple** $M_I = I_G \alpha$, opposite to angular acceleration.)*

---

### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`** 
**State D'Alembert's Principle. Explain how it helps in solving dynamic problems using Free Body Diagrams.**

**Model Answer:**
**D'Alembert's Principle** states that a body in dynamic equilibrium can be treated as if it were in static equilibrium under the action of the applied external forces **plus** an **inertia force** $\vec{F}_I = -m\vec{a}_G$ at the centre of gravity and an **inertia couple** $M_I = -I_G \alpha$ about the CG.

**Utility with FBDs:** By adding these fictitious inertia terms to the FBD, the governing equations become identical to the static equilibrium form:
- $\Sigma F_x = 0$, $\Sigma F_y = 0$, $\Sigma M = 0$.

This lets the engineer use **simple statics techniques** on moving systems such as connecting rods, four-bar couplers, and cam followers, drastically simplifying the mathematics.

---

## PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`**

#### **(a) Derive D'Alembert's principle from Newton's second law. Define inertia force and inertia couple with proper sign conventions. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Newton's Second Law for translation of CG:**

$$\Sigma \vec{F}_{ext} = m\, \vec{a}_G$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange (subtract $m\vec{a}_G$ from both sides):**

$$\Sigma \vec{F}_{ext} - m\, \vec{a}_G = 0$$

**Step 3 — Define inertia force** (fictitious force opposite to acceleration):

$$\vec{F}_I = -m\, \vec{a}_G$$

**Step 4 — Substitute to obtain D'Alembert's equation:**

$$\Sigma \vec{F}_{ext} + \vec{F}_I = 0$$

**Step 5 — Moment equation about CG (Euler's form):**

$$\Sigma M_G = I_G\, \alpha \;\Longrightarrow\; \Sigma M_G - I_G\, \alpha = 0$$

**Step 6 — Define inertia couple** (opposite to angular acceleration):

$$M_I = -I_G\, \alpha$$

**Step 7 — Final D'Alembert rotational form:**

$$\Sigma M_G + M_I = 0$$

**Sign Conventions:**
- $\vec{F}_I$ is **opposite in direction** to $\vec{a}_G$.
- $M_I$ is **opposite in sense** to $\alpha$.
- If $a_G = 0$ (pure translation at constant velocity), $F_I = 0$.
- If $\alpha = 0$ (constant $\omega$), $M_I = 0$.

---

#### **(b) A uniform rod $AB$ of length $L = 1.2$ m and mass $m = 4$ kg is hinged at $A$ and rotates in a vertical plane with constant angular velocity $\omega = 5$ rad/s. Draw the FBD when the rod makes $30°$ with the downward vertical and find the hinge reactions. (7 marks)**

**Given:** $L = 1.2$ m, $m = 4$ kg, $\omega = 5$ rad/s, $\theta = 30°$, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 1 — CG position** (uniform rod): at $L/2 = 0.6$ m from $A$.

**Step 2 — Acceleration of CG** (constant $\omega$, so $\alpha = 0$): centripetal only.

$$a_{Gx} = -\omega^{2}\,\frac{L}{2}\sin\theta = -25 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 0.5 = -7.5 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

$$a_{Gy} = +\omega^{2}\,\frac{L}{2}\cos\theta = +25 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 0.866 = +12.99 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Inertia force components:**

$$F_{Ix} = -m\,a_{Gx} = -4(-7.5) = +30 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{Iy} = -m\,a_{Gy} = -4(+12.99) = -51.96 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — FBD has three forces:** hinge reactions $A_x, A_y$ at $A$; weight $mg = 39.24$ N at $G$ (downward); inertia force $(30,\ -51.96)$ N at $G$.

**Step 5 — Apply $\Sigma F_x = 0$:**

$$A_x + F_{Ix} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{A_x = -30 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 6 — Apply $\Sigma F_y = 0$:**

$$A_y - mg + F_{Iy} = 0$$

$$A_y = mg - F_{Iy} = 39.24 - (-51.96)$$

$$\boxed{A_y = +91.20 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 7 — Resultant reaction magnitude:**

$$R = \sqrt{A_x^{2} + A_y^{2}} = \sqrt{30^{2} + 91.20^{2}} \approx 96.01 \text{ N}$$

**Step 8 — Direction from vertical:**

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\vert A_x \vert}{A_y}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{30}{91.20}\right) \approx 18.2°$$

**Final Answer:** The hinge exerts a reaction of **96.01 N at 18.2° from the vertical** (directed away from the rod).

---

### **Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) With the help of a neat sketch, derive the inertia force on the piston of a slider-crank mechanism using the FBD approach. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Sketch elements:** Slider-crank with crank $OA = r$, connecting rod $AB = l$, piston at $B$, crank angle $\theta$, connecting-rod angle $\phi$, gas force $F_g = P A_p$ on piston, inertia force $F_I = m_P a_P$ opposite to acceleration.

**Step 1 — Piston displacement from crank centre:**

$$x = r\cos\theta + l\cos\phi$$

**Step 2 — Geometric constraint:**

$$\sin\phi = \frac{r}{l}\sin\theta$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate twice with respect to time:**

$$\dot{x} = -r\omega\sin\theta - l\dot\phi\sin\phi$$

$$\ddot{x} = -r\omega^{2}\cos\theta - r\alpha\sin\theta - l\ddot\phi\sin\phi - l\dot\phi^{2}\cos\phi$$

**Step 4 — Approximate for $r/l \ll 1$** (standard IC-engine assumption $\cos\phi \approx 1$):

$$a_P \approx r\omega^{2}\!\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{l}\cos 2\theta\right)$$

**Step 5 — Inertia force on piston** (D'Alembert, opposite to acceleration):

$$\boxed{F_I = m_P\, r\omega^{2}\!\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{l}\cos 2\theta\right)}$$

This force appears on the FBD of the piston and connecting rod, and is **subtracted from the gas force** when computing the net force transmitted to the crankpin.

---

#### **(b) Two masses $m_1 = 8$ kg and $m_2 = 12$ kg are connected by a light inextensible string passing over a frictionless pulley. The system is released from rest. Draw the FBD of each mass and find the acceleration and the string tension. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — FBD of mass $m_1 = 8$ kg** (moves upward):
- Tension $T$ (upward), weight $m_1 g$ (downward), inertia force $m_1 a$ (downward, opposite to upward acceleration).

Equilibrium (D'Alembert):

$$T - m_1 g - m_1 a = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; T = m_1(g + a) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 2 — FBD of mass $m_2 = 12$ kg** (moves downward):
- Weight $m_2 g$ (downward), tension $T$ (upward), inertia force $m_2 a$ (upward, opposite to downward acceleration).

Equilibrium:

$$m_2 g - T - m_2 a = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; T = m_2(g - a) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3 — Equate (i) and (ii):**

$$m_1(g + a) = m_2(g - a)$$

$$m_1 g + m_1 a = m_2 g - m_2 a$$

$$(m_1 + m_2)\, a = (m_2 - m_1)\, g$$

**Step 4 — Solve for acceleration:**

$$a = \frac{(m_2 - m_1)\, g}{m_1 + m_2} = \frac{(12 - 8) \cdot 9.81}{8 + 12}$$

$$a = \frac{4 \cdot 9.81}{20} = \frac{39.24}{20}$$

$$\boxed{a = 1.962 \text{ m/s}^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Tension:**

$$T = m_1(g + a) = 8 \cdot (9.81 + 1.962) = 8 \cdot 11.772$$

$$\boxed{T = 94.18 \text{ N}}$$

**Verification:** $T = m_2(g - a) = 12 \cdot (9.81 - 1.962) = 12 \cdot 7.848 = 94.18$ N ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the inertia force on the FBD** of a moving link — *deducts 2–3 marks instantly*. For dynamics problems, the FBD must show $F_I = m a_G$ at the CG.
> 2. **Wrong direction of inertia force** — students often draw $F_I$ *along* the acceleration. Remember: $F_I$ is **opposite** to $\vec{a}_G$.
> 3. **Omitting the inertia couple** when the link has angular acceleration $\alpha \neq 0$. The couple $M_I = I_G \alpha$ must appear on the FBD.
> 4. **Sign errors** in the equilibrium equations — choose a coordinate system and stick to it; mark every force with its sign.
> 5. **No neat diagram** — KTU examiners explicitly award marks for a clear, fully-labelled FBD. A textual description without a sketch loses **2–4 marks** even if the math is correct.
> 6. **Confusing mass $m$ with weight $W = mg$** — the inertia force uses mass $m$, while gravity uses weight $mg$.
> 7. **Forgetting to consider rotational inertia** of a rigid body — for slender rods, $I_G = m L^{2}/12$; for discs, $I_G = m r^{2}/2$. Always write the formula explicitly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- A **Free Body Diagram (FBD)** is an *isolated, fully-labelled* sketch of a body showing **every** force acting on it.
- **FBDs are mandatory first step** for every dynamics problem in KTU Module 1 — marks are explicitly reserved for them.
- **D'Alembert's Principle** converts a dynamic problem into a static-equilibrium problem by adding **inertia force** $F_I = -m a_G$ and **inertia couple** $M_I = -I_G \alpha$ on the FBD.
- **Direction rule:** Inertia force is **always opposite** to the acceleration of the CG; inertia couple is **always opposite** to angular acceleration.
- **Newton–Euler equations on an FBD:** $\Sigma F_x = m a_{Gx}$, $\Sigma F_y = m a_{Gy}$, $\Sigma M_G = I_G \alpha$.
- **In a slider-crank**, the piston inertia force $F_I = m_P\, r\omega^{2}\!\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{l}\cos 2\theta\right)$ must appear on the FBD of the piston and on the FBD of the connecting rod.
- **Connecting-rod force** $F_R = (F_g - F_I)/\cos\phi$ — always **subtract** the inertia force from the gas force for the net load on the crankpin.
- **Side thrust** on the cylinder wall: $N = (F_g - F_I)\,\tan\phi$.
- **Three equilibrium equations** are sufficient for any single rigid body in plane motion — use them after the FBD is complete.
- **Units:** Force in **N**, mass in **kg**, length in **m or mm**, acceleration in **m/s²**, $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- **Common FBD omissions to avoid:** weight (forgot to include), inertia force (dynamic problems), reaction components at supports, inertia couple for rotating links.
- **For IC engines**, the net force transmitted to the crankpin is $F_{net} = F_g - F_I$ along the cylinder axis; the tangential component at the crankpin produces the **turning moment** $T = F_{net} \cdot r \sin(\theta + \phi)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Superposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT527 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Principle of Superposition # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Principle of Superposition (KTU 2024 Module 1 Definition):** > *In a linear vibrating system, the total response (displacement, velocity, or acceleration) produced by two or more simultaneously acting forces (or initial conditions) is equal to the algebraic sum of the individual responses produced by each force (or condition) acting alone, provided the system obeys the laws of linear elasticity and Hooke's Law.* Mathematically, if a system governed by a linear differential equation receives inputs $F_1(t)$, $F_2(t)$, …, $F_n(t)$ producing individual responses $x_1(t)$, $x_2(t)$, …, $x_n(t)$, then the combined response is: $$ x(t) = x_1(t) + x_2(t) + \dots + x_n(t) $$ where each $x_i(t)$ is the solution to the equation when only $F_i(t)$ is applied. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Swimming Pool Analogy:** > Imagine you are standing in a still swimming pool. If friend A jumps in from the left side, waves travel toward you. If friend B jumps in from the right side a moment later, *another set* of waves travels toward you. The total disturbance you feel is simply the **sum** of the two wave patterns. They do not "fight" each other or create a third mysterious wave — they just overlap (superpose) and add up wherever they meet. In the same way, when multiple harmonic forces act on a machine component: - Each force would cause its own **independent vibration pattern** if acting alone. - Because the system is *linear* (force $\propto$ displacement, no plastic deformation), the actual vibration is just the **arithmetic sum** of these individual patterns. - This is only valid while stresses stay within the **elastic limit** of the material (typically below the yield stress, e.g., for mild steel ≈ **250 MPa**). ## 1.3 Why Superposition Matters in Dynamics of Machinery > [!TIP] > **Engineering Significance:** > - Allows engineers to **predict complex vibration** by breaking it into simple harmonic components (Fourier's idea). > - Simplifies analysis of **multi-cylinder engines**, **multi-mass systems**, and **torsional systems** with several torque inputs. > - Used in **modal analysis** (each natural mode vibrates independently and can be superposed). > - Foundation of the **Duhamel's Integral** and **impulse response methods**. ## 1.4 Conditions of Validity The Principle of Superposition **holds ONLY if** the system is: | # | Condition | Physical Meaning | |---|-----------|------------------| | 1 | **Linear** | Equation of motion contains no products or higher powers of $x$ or $\dot{x}$ | | 2 | **Hookean** | Spring force $F_s = kx$ (linear spring constant) | | 3 | **No Coulomb friction** | No dry friction (since $\mu N \cdot \text{sgn}(\dot{x})$ is non-linear) | | 4 | **Constant parameters** | $m$, $c$, $k$ do not change with time or $x$ | | 5 | **Small oscillations** | Displacements remain small (geometric non-linearities ignored) | If any of these is violated, the principle **fails** and numerical integration must be used. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Linear vs. Non-Linear Systems A system is **linear** if it satisfies two mathematical properties: 1. **Homogeneity (Scaling):** If input $F(t)$ produces output $x(t)$, then input $\alpha F(t)$ produces $\alpha x(t)$. 2. **Additivity (Superposition):** If $F_1 \to x_1$ and $F_2 \to x_2$, then $(F_1 + F_2) \to (x_1 + x_2)$. Any system that fails either property is **non-linear** (e.g., systems with large deflections, dry friction, square-law damping, or material yielding). ## 2.2 Application of Superposition to Free Vibration For a single-DOF undamped system with **non-zero initial conditions** (released from $x_0$ with velocity $v_0$), the equation of motion is: $$ m\ddot{x} + kx = 0, \quad x(0) = x_0, \quad \dot{x}(0) = v_0 $$ > **Key Insight:** Instead of solving this as an initial-value problem directly, superposition allows us to treat it as **two separate problems**: > - **Problem A:** System released from rest at $x(0) = x_0$, zero velocity. Solution: $x_A(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega_n t)$ > - **Problem B:** System released from equilibrium at rest, with $\dot{x}(0) = v_0$. Solution: $x_B(t) = \dfrac{v_0}{\omega_n} \sin(\omega_n t)$ By superposition, the complete solution is: $$ x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega_n t) + \frac{v_0}{\omega_n} \sin(\omega_n t) $$ This is the **most-tested KTU application** of the principle in Module 1. ## 2.3 Application to Forced Vibration For a system subjected to **two harmonic forces** $F_1 \sin(\omega_1 t)$ and $F_2 \sin(\omega_2 t)$: $$ m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_1 \sin(\omega_1 t) + F_2 \sin(\omega_2 t) $$ By linearity, the steady-state response is: $$ x(t) = \underbrace{\frac{F_1}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega_1^2)^2 + (c\omega_1)^2}} \sin(\omega_1 t - \phi_1)}_{x_1(t)} + \underbrace{\frac{F_2}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega_2^2)^2 + (c\omega_2)^2}} \sin(\omega_2 t - \phi_2)}_{x_2(t)} $$ Each component vibrates at **its own excitation frequency** $\omega_i$, with **its own phase lag** $\phi_i$. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 — Must Memorize** | # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | |---|---------|-------------|----------------| | 1 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural (undamped) frequency | $\text{rad/s}$ | | 2 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural frequency in Hertz | $\text{Hz}$ | | 3 | $x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega_n t) + \dfrac{v_0}{\omega_n} \sin(\omega_n t)$ | Free vibration (general IC) | $\text{m}$ | | 4 | $x(t) = X \sin(\omega_n t + \phi)$ | Compact amplitude-phase form | $\text{m}$ | | 5 | $X = \sqrt{x_0^2 + \left(\dfrac{v_0}{\omega_n}\right)^2}$ | Resultant amplitude | $\text{m}$ | | 6 | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega_n x_0}{v_0}\right)$ | Initial phase angle | $\text{rad}$ | | 7 | $x_{ss,i}(t) = \dfrac{F_i/m}{\sqrt{(\omega_n^2 - \omega_i^2)^2 + (2\zeta\omega_n\omega_i)^2}} \sin(\omega_i t - \phi_i)$ | Forced response (damped) | $\text{m}$ | | 8 | $\phi_i = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2\zeta\omega_n\omega_i}{\omega_n^2 - \omega_i^2}\right)$ | Phase lag for $i$-th input | $\text{rad}$ | | 9 | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2m\omega_n} = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless | | 10 | $x_{total}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i(t)$ | **Superposition rule** | $\text{m}$ | ## 2.5 Real-World Utility > [!TIP] > **Where Engineers Apply This:** > - **Multi-cylinder reciprocating engines:** Each cylinder creates its own inertia force; the net force on the block is the superposition of all. > - **Rotating machinery with multiple unbalances:** Each disc contributes independently; the resultant is the vector sum. > - **Earthquake engineering:** Multiple ground motion components are superposed. > - **Acoustic systems:** Multiple sound sources combine linearly in air (low amplitude). > - **Bridge / structural dynamics:** When multiple vehicles cross a bridge, their load effects superpose (used in influence line diagrams). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Free Vibration as Superposition of Two Sub-Problems **Given:** A mass $m$ attached to a spring of stiffness $k$, released from $x(0) = x_0$ with initial velocity $\dot{x}(0) = v_0$. **Step 1 — Write the governing equation** $$ m\ddot{x}(t) + kx(t) = 0 $$ **Step 2 — Split the problem into two initial-value sub-problems** > We are allowed to do this **only** because the differential operator $L[\cdot] = m\dfrac{d^2}{dt^2} + k$ is **linear**. **Sub-problem A (Pure Initial Displacement):** $$ m\ddot{x}_A + kx_A = 0, \quad x_A(0) = x_0, \quad \dot{x}_A(0) = 0 $$ Assume $x_A = A\cos(\omega_n t) + B\sin(\omega_n t)$. Apply $\dot{x}_A(0) = 0$: $$ \dot{x}_A = -A\omega_n\sin(\omega_n t) + B\omega_n\cos(\omega_n t) \Rightarrow B\omega_n = 0 \Rightarrow B = 0 $$ Apply $x_A(0) = x_0$: $$ A\cos(0) = A = x_0 $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{x_A(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega_n t)} $$ **Sub-problem B (Pure Initial Velocity):** $$ m\ddot{x}_B + kx_B = 0, \quad x_B(0) = 0, \quad \dot{x}_B(0) = v_0 $$ Assume $x_B = C\cos(\omega_n t) + D\sin(\omega_n t)$. Apply $x_B(0) = 0$: $$ C\cos(0) = C = 0 $$ Apply $\dot{x}_B(0) = v_0$: $$ \dot{x}_B = D\omega_n \cos(\omega_n t) \big\vert_{t=0} = D\omega_n = v_0 \Rightarrow D = \frac{v_0}{\omega_n} $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{x_B(t) = \frac{v_0}{\omega_n} \sin(\omega_n t)} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the Principle of Superposition** $$ x(t) = x_A(t) + x_B(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega_n t) + \frac{v_0}{\omega_n} \sin(\omega_n t) $$ **Step 4 — Verify by substitution** Differentiating twice: $$ \dot{x}(t) = -\omega_n x_0 \sin(\omega_n t) + v_0 \cos(\omega_n t) $$ $$ \ddot{x}(t) = -\omega_n^2 x_0 \cos(\omega_n t) - \omega_n v_0 \sin(\omega_n t) $$ Substitute into $m\ddot{x} + kx$: $$ \begin{aligned} m\ddot{x} + kx &= m\left[-\omega_n^2 x_0 \cos(\omega_n t) - \omega_n v_0 \sin(\omega_n t)\right] + k\left[x_0 \cos(\omega_n t) + \frac{v_0}{\omega_n} \sin(\omega_n t)\right] \\ &= \left[-m\omega_n^2 + k\right] x_0 \cos(\omega_n t) + \left[-m\omega_n v_0 + \frac{k v_0}{\omega_n}\right] \sin(\omega_n t) \end{aligned} $$ Since $k = m\omega_n^2$ (from $\omega_n^2 = k/m$), **both brackets vanish** $\Rightarrow$ Equation is satisfied. ✔️ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Compact Form (Amplitude-Phase) Starting from: $$ x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega_n t) + \frac{v_0}{\omega_n} \sin(\omega_n t) $$ **Step 1 — Factor the amplitude** Let $X\cos\phi = x_0$ and $X\sin\phi = \dfrac{v_0}{\omega_n}$. Then: $$ x(t) = X\cos\phi \cos(\omega_n t) + X\sin\phi \sin(\omega_n t) = X\cos(\omega_n t - \phi) $$ **Step 2 — Solve for amplitude and phase** Squaring and adding: $$ X^2\cos^2\phi + X^2\sin^2\phi = x_0^2 + \left(\frac{v_0}{\omega_n}\right)^2 $$ $$ \Rightarrow \boxed{X = \sqrt{x_0^2 + \left(\frac{v_0}{\omega_n}\right)^2}} $$ Dividing: $$ \frac{X\sin\phi}{X\cos\phi} = \tan\phi = \frac{v_0/\omega_n}{x_0} $$ $$ \Rightarrow \boxed{\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{v_0}{\omega_n x_0}\right)} $$ ## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical) > **Problem:** A spring-mass system has $m = 10$ kg, $k = 4000$ N/m. It is displaced $x_0 = 0.05$ m from equilibrium and released with an initial velocity $v_0 = 2$ m/s. Find the equation of motion using superposition. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute natural frequency** $$ \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{4000}{10}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad/s} $$ **Step 2 — Apply superposition formula directly** $$ x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega_n t) + \frac{v_0}{\omega_n} \sin(\omega_n t) $$ Substitute values: $$ x(t) = 0.05 \cos(20 t) + \frac{2}{20} \sin(20 t) $$ $$ \boxed{x(t) = 0.05 \cos(20 t) + 0.10 \sin(20 t) \;\; \text{m}} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to single-sinusoid form** $$ X = \sqrt{(0.05)^2 + (0.10)^2} = \sqrt{0.0025 + 0.01} = \sqrt{0.0125} = 0.1118 \text{ m} $$ $$ \phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.10}{0.05}\right) = \tan^{-1}(2) = 63.43^\circ = 1.107 \text{ rad} $$ $$ \boxed{x(t) = 0.1118 \sin(20 t + 1.107) \;\; \text{m}} $$ **Step 4 — Sanity check at $t = 0$** Direct formula: $x(0) = 0.05\cos(0) + 0.10\sin(0) = 0.05$ m ✔️ matches $x_0$. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: Linear System Input–Output Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Input F1 of t"]:::inp --> C["Linear System
m d2x/dt2 + c dx/dt + kx = F"] B["Input F2 of t"]:::inp --> C D["Input F3 of t"]:::inp --> C C --> E["Response x1 of t"]:::out C --> F["Response x2 of t"]:::out C --> G["Response x3 of t"]:::out E --> H["Total Response
x of t = x1 + x2 + x3"]:::final F --> H G --> H classDef inp fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0a1f33,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef out fill:#3d5a80,stroke:#1f3a5f,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef final fill:#ee6c4d,stroke:#bb3a1f,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Superposition Applied to Free Vibration with ICs ```mermaid flowchart TB Start(["Initial Conditions
x of 0 = x0 AND dx/dt of 0 = v0"]) --> Split subgraph SubProblemA["Sub-Problem A — Initial Displacement Only"] A1["v0 = 0"] --> A2["Solve: m x'' + kx = 0
with x0 ≠ 0"] --> A3["xA of t = x0 cos omega_n t"] end subgraph SubProblemB["Sub-Problem B — Initial Velocity Only"] B1["x0 = 0"] --> B2["Solve: m x'' + kx = 0
with v0 ≠ 0"] --> B3["xB of t = v0 over omega_n times sin omega_n t"] end Split --> SubProblemA Split --> SubProblemB A3 --> Sum["Summation Block
+"] B3 --> Sum Sum --> Final(["x of t = x0 cos omega_n t + v0 over omega_n sin omega_n t"]) Final --> Compact["Compact Form
X sin omega_n t + phi"] Final --> Verify["Substitute back into EOM
to confirm"] classDef condBox fill:#293241,stroke:#0d1b2a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef subBox fill:#98c1d9,stroke:#3d5a80,stroke-width:2px,color:#0d1b2a classDef resultBox fill:#ee6c4d,stroke:#bb3a1f,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff class Start condBox class SubProblemA,SubProblemB subBox class Sum,Final,Compact,Verify resultBox ``` ## 4.3 Validity Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{"Is the system linear?"} -->|Yes| Q2{"Is damping viscous
(c·dx/dt)?"} Q1 -->|No| Fail["Superposition FAILS
Use numerical integration
e.g., Runge–Kutta 4th order"] Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{"Is the spring Hookean
(F = k·x)?"} Q2 -->|No| Fail Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{"Are parameters
m, c, k constant?"} Q3 -->|No| Fail Q4 -->|Yes| Pass["Superposition APPLIES
x total = Σ xi of t"] Q4 -->|No| Fail classDef yesDec fill:#3d5a80,stroke:#1f3a5f,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef noDec fill:#ee6c4d,stroke:#bb3a1f,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef outcome fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#0a3a35,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff class Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4 yesDec class Fail noDec class Pass outcome ``` ## 4.4 Schematic: Two Force Inputs on a Single-DOF System ```mermaid flowchart LR Wall1["Fixed Wall"]:::wall --- K1["Spring k"]:::spring --- M1["Mass m
Position x of t"]:::mass --- C1["Damper c"]:::damper --- Wall2["Fixed Wall"]:::wall F1A["F1 sin omega1 t"]:::force --> M1 F2A["F2 sin omega2 t"]:::force --> M1 M1 --> Out["EOM: m x'' + c x' + kx
= F1 sin omega1 t + F2 sin omega2 t"]:::eom Out --> SS["Steady-State Response
x of t = x1 of t + x2 of t"]:::ss classDef wall fill:#6c757d,stroke:#343a40,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef spring fill:#06a77d,stroke:#03503d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef mass fill:#ee6c4d,stroke:#bb3a1f,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef damper fill:#8338ec,stroke:#3a0a73,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef force fill:#ffd166,stroke:#a8761f,stroke-width:2px,color:#0d1b2a classDef eom fill:#3d5a80,stroke:#1f3a5f,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef ss fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#0a3a35,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff class Wall1,Wall2 wall class K1 spring class M1 mass class C1 damper class F1A,F2A force class Out eom class SS ss ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]** ### **Q1. State the Principle of Superposition as applied to vibrating systems. Under what conditions is it valid?** `[3 Marks]` **Model Answer:** The Principle of Superposition states that in a *linear* vibrating system, the total response produced by two or more simultaneously acting forces (or initial conditions) is equal to the **algebraic sum of the individual responses** produced by each force (or condition) acting alone. **Validity conditions:** 1. The system must be **linear** (homogeneous + additive). 2. The spring must obey **Hooke's Law** ($F = kx$). 3. Damping, if present, must be **viscous** (linear in velocity). 4. System parameters $m$, $c$, $k$ must be **constant** (time-invariant). 5. Oscillations must remain **small** (no geometric non-linearity). > **[CO1 · Bloom Level: Remember/Understand]** --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** ### **Q2. A spring-mass system has $m = 5$ kg, $k = 500$ N/m. It is pulled down by 0.04 m and released from rest. Using superposition, write the equation of motion.** `[3 Marks]` **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Natural frequency:** $$ \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{500}{5}} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ rad/s} $$ **Step 2 — Given:** $x_0 = 0.04$ m, $v_0 = 0$ m/s (released from rest). **Step 3 — Apply superposition formula:** $$ x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega_n t) + \frac{v_0}{\omega_n}\sin(\omega_n t) $$ $$ \boxed{x(t) = 0.04 \cos(10 t) \;\; \text{m}} $$ > **[CO2 · Bloom Level: Apply]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** ### **Question A (Choice 1):** > A vibrating system consists of a mass $m = 20$ kg, spring stiffness $k = 8000$ N/m, and viscous damping coefficient $c = 80$ N·s/m. The mass is subjected to **two simultaneous harmonic forces**: > $F_1(t) = 200 \sin(30 t)$ N and $F_2(t) = 150 \sin(60 t)$ N. > > **(a)** **[7 Marks]** State the Principle of Superposition and justify its applicability to this system. > > **(b)** **[7 Marks]** Determine the total steady-state response of the system using superposition. --- #### **Solution to Q-A (a):** **Statement of the Principle:** > The Principle of Superposition states that the total response of a *linear* system to multiple simultaneous inputs is equal to the **algebraic sum of the individual responses** caused by each input acting independently. **Justification of applicability:** The given system is governed by: $$ m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_1(t) + F_2(t) $$ The principle is applicable because: | # | Condition | Check for Given System | |---|-----------|------------------------| | 1 | Linear operator | Yes — equation has no products of $x$ or $\dot{x}$ | | 2 | Hookean spring | Yes — restoring force is $kx$ | | 3 | Viscous damping | Yes — damping force is $c\dot{x}$ | | 4 | Constant parameters | $m = 20$ kg, $c = 80$ N·s/m, $k = 8000$ N/m are constants | | 5 | Small oscillations | Assumed (no mention of large deflections) | Therefore, the principle **holds**, and: $$ x_{ss}(t) = x_1(t) + x_2(t) $$ where $x_1$ is the response to $F_1$ alone and $x_2$ is the response to $F_2$ alone. > **[CO1 · Bloom Level: Understand/Apply]** --- #### **Solution to Q-A (b):** **Step 1 — Compute system parameters** $$ \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{8000}{20}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad/s} $$ $$ \zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \frac{80}{2\sqrt{8000 \times 20}} = \frac{80}{2\sqrt{160000}} = \frac{80}{2 \times 400} = \frac{80}{800} = 0.1 $$ So this is an **underdamped** system ($\zeta = 0.1 < 1$). **Step 2 — Response to $F_1(t) = 200 \sin(30 t)$ N** Frequency ratio $r_1 = \omega_1 / \omega_n = 30/20 = 1.5$ Amplitude: $$ X_1 = \frac{F_1/k}{\sqrt{(1 - r_1^2)^2 + (2\zeta r_1)^2}} = \frac{200/8000}{\sqrt{(1 - 2.25)^2 + (2 \times 0.1 \times 1.5)^2}} $$ $$ X_1 = \frac{0.025}{\sqrt{(-1.25)^2 + (0.3)^2}} = \frac{0.025}{\sqrt{1.5625 + 0.09}} = \frac{0.025}{\sqrt{1.6525}} = \frac{0.025}{1.2855} $$ $$ X_1 = 0.01945 \text{ m} \approx 19.45 \text{ mm} $$ Phase lag: $$ \phi_1 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2\zeta r_1}{1 - r_1^2}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.3}{-1.25}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-0.24) $$ Since $1 - r_1^2 < 0$, the system is *above resonance* and the phase lag is in the second quadrant: $$ \phi_1 = 180^\circ - 13.5^\circ = 166.5^\circ = 2.906 \text{ rad} $$ **Step 3 — Response to $F_2(t) = 150 \sin(60 t)$ N** Frequency ratio $r_2 = \omega_2 / \omega_n = 60/20 = 3.0$ Amplitude: $$ X_2 = \frac{F_2/k}{\sqrt{(1 - r_2^2)^2 + (2\zeta r_2)^2}} = \frac{150/8000}{\sqrt{(1 - 9)^2 + (2 \times 0.1 \times 3)^2}} $$ $$ X_2 = \frac{0.01875}{\sqrt{64 + 0.36}} = \frac{0.01875}{\sqrt{64.36}} = \frac{0.01875}{8.0225} = 0.002337 \text{ m} \approx 2.34 \text{ mm} $$ Phase lag: $$ \phi_2 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.6}{-8}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-0.075) = 180^\circ - 4.29^\circ = 175.71^\circ = 3.067 \text{ rad} $$ **Step 4 — Total Response by Superposition** $$ \boxed{x_{ss}(t) = 0.01945 \sin(30 t - 2.906) + 0.00234 \sin(60 t - 3.067) \;\; \text{m}} $$ > **[CO2 & CO3 · Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze]** --- ### **Question B (Choice 2):** > A single-DOF undamped spring-mass system has $m = 4$ kg and $k = 1600$ N/m. The mass is pulled aside by $0.03$ m from equilibrium and simultaneously given an initial velocity of $4$ m/s. > > **(a)** **[7 Marks]** Using the **Principle of Superposition**, derive the equation of motion by treating the initial displacement and initial velocity as two separate sub-problems. > > **(b)** **[7 Marks]** Express the motion in compact amplitude-phase form and identify the natural frequency and the time period. --- #### **Solution to Q-B (a):** **Step 1 — Equation of motion** $$ m\ddot{x} + kx = 0 \Rightarrow 4\ddot{x} + 1600 x = 0 $$ **Step 2 — Natural frequency** $$ \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{1600}{4}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad/s} $$ **Step 3 — Sub-Problem A (Initial displacement only)** Initial conditions: $x_A(0) = 0.03$ m, $\dot{x}_A(0) = 0$. Solution form: $x_A(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega_n t)$ $$ \boxed{x_A(t) = 0.03 \cos(20 t) \;\; \text{m}} $$ **Step 4 — Sub-Problem B (Initial velocity only)** Initial conditions: $x_B(0) = 0$, $\dot{x}_B(0) = 4$ m/s. Solution form: $x_B(t) = \dfrac{v_0}{\omega_n} \sin(\omega_n t)$ $$ \boxed{x_B(t) = \frac{4}{20} \sin(20 t) = 0.20 \sin(20 t) \;\; \text{m}} $$ **Step 5 — Superpose the two solutions** $$ x(t) = x_A(t) + x_B(t) = 0.03 \cos(20 t) + 0.20 \sin(20 t) \;\; \text{m} $$ > **[CO2 · Bloom Level: Apply]** --- #### **Solution to Q-B (b):** **Step 1 — Resultant amplitude** $$ X = \sqrt{x_0^2 + \left(\frac{v_0}{\omega_n}\right)^2} = \sqrt{(0.03)^2 + (0.20)^2} $$ $$ X = \sqrt{0.0009 + 0.04} = \sqrt{0.0409} = 0.2022 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2 — Phase angle** $$ \phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega_n x_0}{v_0}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{20 \times 0.03}{4}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.6}{4}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.15) $$ $$ \phi = 8.53^\circ = 0.1489 \text{ rad} $$ **Step 3 — Compact form of motion** $$ \boxed{x(t) = 0.2022 \sin(20 t + 0.1489) \;\; \text{m}} $$ **Step 4 — Natural frequency and time period** $$ \omega_n = 20 \text{ rad/s} $$ $$ f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{20}{2\pi} = 3.183 \text{ Hz} $$ $$ T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = \frac{2\pi}{20} = 0.3142 \text{ s} $$ > **[CO3 · Bloom Level: Analyze]** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the $v_0/\omega_n$ factor in initial-velocity sub-problem.** Marks are lost in Step 4 of Q-B(a) if the student writes $x_B = v_0 \sin(\omega_n t)$ instead of $x_B = (v_0/\omega_n)\sin(\omega_n t)$. > 2. **Sign error in phase angle formula.** For Q-A(b), above-resonance case ($r > 1$), the phase must be in the *second quadrant* (between 90° and 180°), not the *fourth* quadrant. Add **180°** to the raw arctan value. > 3. **Confusing superposition with squaring amplitudes.** Total amplitude is **not** $\sqrt{X_1^2 + X_2^2}$ — that is for **same-frequency** vector addition. For **different frequencies**, the response is left as a sum of sines (you cannot combine them into one sine). > 4. **Misapplying superposition to non-linear cases.** If the question states "dry friction" or "square damping," the principle **fails**. Watch for keywords: *Coulomb*, *air damping* ($\propto \dot{x}^2$). > 5. **Unit inconsistency in numericals.** Always keep $m$ in kg, $k$ in N/m, $c$ in N·s/m. Force amplitudes in **N** (not kN) and time in **seconds**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Principle of Superposition** - **Definition:** Total response of a *linear* system = algebraic sum of individual responses to each input or initial condition. - **Five Validity Conditions:** Linear · Hookean spring · Viscous damping · Constant parameters · Small oscillations. - **Two classic applications in Module 1:** 1. Free vibration with general initial conditions $x_0$ and $v_0$: $$ x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega_n t) + \frac{v_0}{\omega_n} \sin(\omega_n t) $$ 2. Forced vibration with multiple harmonic forces (different frequencies kept separate). - **Standard compact form:** $x(t) = X \sin(\omega_n t + \phi)$, with: - $X = \sqrt{x_0^2 + (v_0/\omega_n)^2}$ - $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega_n x_0 / v_0)$ - **Cannot combine** different-frequency sinusoids into one — leave as a sum. - **Use $X$ in metres** (not mm) unless specified; convert carefully. - **Damping ratio** $\zeta = c / (2\sqrt{km}) < 1$ for underdamped free response; $\zeta \geq 1$ is *overdamped* or *critically damped* (no oscillation → superposition less commonly applied). - **Phase lag** in forced response: 0° below resonance, 90° at resonance, 180° far above resonance. - **Real engineering examples:** multi-cylinder engines, multi-disc rotor balancing, earthquake loads, acoustic superposition, multi-axle vehicle loading on bridges. - **Key formula to memorize:** $$ \boxed{x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega_n t) + \frac{v_0}{\omega_n} \sin(\omega_n t)} $$ - **Trap questions:** "A system with Coulomb friction..." → Superposition **fails** (write this and you get full credit for the conceptual part). - **Units trap:** $\omega_n$ is in **rad/s**; $f_n$ is in **Hz**; never interchange them.

topics.title: Static Force Analysis of Four Bar Mechanism and Slider-Crank Mechanism without Friction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT527 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Static Force Analysis of Four Bar Mechanism and Slider-Crank Mechanism without Friction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Static Force Analysis** is the study of forces acting on the links of a mechanism when it is in **equilibrium** (either at rest or moving at constant velocity). In KTU 2024 Scheme terms, it is the application of **principle of superposition** and **free-body diagrams (FBDs)** to determine the bearing reactions, internal forces, and the effort required at the input link to overcome a known external load (resistance) — assuming **frictionless joints** (i.e., pin joints transmit only forces, not moments).

For a **Four-Bar Mechanism**, the analysis is performed link-by-link using the equations of static equilibrium ($\Sigma F_x = 0$, $\Sigma F_y = 0$, and $\Sigma M = 0$).

For a **Slider-Crank Mechanism** (the backbone of IC engines, pumps, and compressors), the applied load is the **resistance offered by the piston**, and the goal is to determine the **thrust on the cylinder walls**, **crank effort**, and **reactions at the main bearings and crank-pin bearings**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are trying to push a heavy lawn roller using a bent handle. The handle represents the connecting rod, your hands provide the effort at the crank, and the grass/ground offers resistance at the roller. You intuitively lean and apply force in a particular direction to overcome the resistance.

In machinery terms:
- The **piston (slider)** is the roller — it pushes back against a load (e.g., combustion pressure in an engine, water in a pump).
- The **connecting rod (coupler)** transmits this force obliquely.
- The **crank** rotates to do the work.
- We want to find out **how hard you must push (crank effort)** and **how the bearings take the load**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Assumptions

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Free Body Diagram (FBD)** | An isolated sketch of a single link showing all forces and couples acting on it. |
| **Bearing Reaction ($R$)** | The contact force at a frictionless pin joint, resolved into $R_x$ and $R_y$. |
| **Crank Effort ($T$ or $F_P$)** | The tangential force at the crank-pin required to balance the external load. |
| **Piston Thrust ($F_N$)** | The side-thrust of the slider against the cylinder wall (normal to the line of stroke). |
| **Effort Angle ($\phi$)** | The angle between the connecting rod and the line of stroke. |
| **Crank Angle ($\theta$)** | The angle of the crank measured from the inner dead center (IDC). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption in KTU 2024 Module 1:** All pin joints are **frictionless** — therefore they cannot transmit any couple (torque). Only translational forces pass through pins. The weights of links are usually **neglected** unless explicitly stated.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Analytical Logic

The **principle of superposition** states: *If a system is in equilibrium under several forces, it is also in equilibrium under each of them acting independently.* In mechanism analysis, this means we:
1. Remove the mechanism from its frame.
2. Replace each pin joint by two unknown reaction components ($R_x$, $R_y$).
3. Draw the FBD of each link.
4. Write equilibrium equations for each link.
5. Solve the resulting system of linear algebraic equations.

> [!NOTE]
> For a four-bar mechanism with $n$ links, each with 3 unknowns (two forces + one moment, where moment is zero for frictionless binary links), we get a system of $3n$ equations. Superposition reduces this to a manageable set by isolating the **effort** and the **load (resistance)**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam tested**. Memorize the notation, not just the formula.

#### A. Four-Bar Mechanism — Static Force Analysis

Consider a four-bar mechanism: Frame (1), Input link/crank (2), Coupler (3), Output link/Rocker (4). Let $F$ be the **applied effort** at the crank-pin perpendicular to the crank, and $F_R$ be the **resistance load** on the coupler/output link.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Effort required at crank | Determined by virtual-work or direct equilibrium: $F \cdot v_{F} = F_R \cdot v_{R}$ | N |
| 2 | Virtual work principle | $F \cdot \omega_2 \cdot \dfrac{\partial \theta_2}{\partial \phi} = F_R \cdot \omega_4 \cdot \dfrac{\partial \theta_4}{\partial \phi}$ | — |
| 3 | Bearing reaction at joint $i$ | $R_{ix} = \sum F_x \text{ on link containing } i$ | N |
| 4 | Bearing reaction at joint $i$ | $R_{iy} = \sum F_y \text{ on link containing } i$ | N |
| 5 | Resultant at joint $i$ | $R_i = \sqrt{R_{ix}^2 + R_{iy}^2}$ | N |
| 6 | Direction of $R_i$ | $\tan \alpha_i = \dfrac{R_{iy}}{R_{ix}}$ | degrees |

#### B. Slider-Crank Mechanism — Static Force Analysis

Let $P$ = net load on the piston (resistance), $F$ = crank effort (tangential), $N$ = side thrust on cylinder.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Inertia force (from $P$) | $F_I = P$ (treating $P$ as net force on piston, opposed by inertia) | N |
| 2 | Connecting rod inclination | $\sin \phi = r \sin \theta \big/ \ell$ | rad |
| 3 | Force along connecting rod | $F_Q = P / \cos \phi$ | N |
| 4 | **Crank effort (tangential)** | $F = P \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos \phi}$ | N |
| 5 | Effective load along stroke | $F_E = P \cdot \tan \phi$ (when effort is applied at crank-pin) | N |
| 6 | **Thrust on cylinder walls** | $N = P \tan \phi$ | N |
| 7 | Crank-pin bearing reaction | $R_C = \sqrt{F^2 + (P \tan \phi)^2} = P / \cos \phi$ | N |
| 8 | Side of thrust | Same side as crank centre w.r.t. line of stroke | — |
| 9 | Crank effort in terms of crank angle | $F = P \left( \sin \theta + \dfrac{r \sin 2\theta}{2 \ell} \right) \text{ (approx.)}$ | N |
| 10 | Turning moment on crank | $T = F \cdot r$ | N·m |

> [!WARNING]
> **KR Trap:** The thrust $N = P \tan \phi$ is **not** the same as $F_Q = P / \cos \phi$. The first is perpendicular to the stroke; the second is along the connecting rod. Mixing them is the #1 cause of zero marks in KTU exams.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility

- **Engine Design:** Determines the required **crank-shaft diameter** from peak tangential force $F$.
- **Bearing Selection:** Side thrust $N$ governs the **piston-skirt** and **gudgeon-pin** bushing design.
- **Frame Sizing:** Main-bearing reactions guide **crankcase** and **bedplate** structural design.
- **Compressors & Pumps:** Same analysis predicts **starting torque** and **overturning moment** on foundations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Crank Effort in a Slider-Crank Mechanism

We will derive the **crank effort $F$** in terms of piston load $P$ and the geometry $(r, \ell, \theta, \phi)$.

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry.**
The slider $C$ lies on the line of stroke, $B$ is the crank-pin at crank angle $\theta$ from IDC, $A$ is the crank centre. The connecting rod $AB$ has length $\ell$, the crank has length $r$.

From the slider-crank loop closure (vertical projection):
$$\ell \sin \phi = r \sin \theta$$

Therefore:
$$\sin \phi = \frac{r \sin \theta}{\ell}$$

**Step 2 — Free body of the slider (piston).**
The piston load $P$ acts along the line of stroke (resisting the motion). The connecting rod exerts a compressive force $F_Q$ along the rod, making angle $\phi$ with the line of stroke.

Resolving forces on the piston **along the line of stroke**:
$$F_Q \cos \phi = P$$

Hence the force **along the connecting rod** is:
$$F_Q = \frac{P}{\cos \phi}$$

**Step 3 — Free body of the connecting rod.**
Since pin joints are frictionless, the rod is a **two-force member**. Therefore, the force at the crank-pin $B$ must be equal in magnitude, opposite in direction, and along the rod:
$$F_Q = \frac{P}{\cos \phi}$$

**Step 4 — Free body of the crank.**
The crank rotates about $A$. The force $F_Q$ acts on the crank-pin $B$ along the line of the rod (angle $(\theta + \phi)$ from the line of stroke).

We resolve $F_Q$ into:
- **Tangential component** (perpendicular to crank $AB$): $F_T = F_Q \sin(\theta + \phi)$
- **Radial component** (along the crank): $F_R = F_Q \cos(\theta + \phi)$

The **tangential component** is balanced by the crank effort $F$ applied tangentially at the crank-pin.

**Step 5 — Substitute $F_Q$.**
$$F = F_Q \sin(\theta + \phi) = \frac{P \sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos \phi}$$

$$\boxed{F = P \cdot \frac{\sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos \phi}}$$

**Step 6 — Side thrust on cylinder walls.**
The component of $F_Q$ **perpendicular to the line of stroke** is the side thrust $N$:
$$N = F_Q \sin \phi = \frac{P \sin \phi}{\cos \phi}$$

$$\boxed{N = P \tan \phi}$$

**Step 7 — Approximate form for small $\phi$ (long rod assumption).**
When $\ell \gg r$, $\phi$ is small and $\sin \phi \approx r \sin \theta / \ell$.

Using $\sin(\theta + \phi) = \sin \theta \cos \phi + \cos \theta \sin \phi$, and approximating $\cos \phi \approx 1$:

$$F \approx P \left( \sin \theta + \frac{r \sin \theta \cos \theta}{\ell} \right) = P \left( \sin \theta + \frac{r \sin 2\theta}{2\ell} \right)$$

This is the **approximate crank-effort equation** often used in KTU derivations.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Static Force Analysis of a Four-Bar Mechanism (Method of Superposition)

Consider a four-bar mechanism $ABCB'$ with:
- $A$ = fixed pivot of input link
- $B$ = pivot between input link and coupler
- $C$ = pivot between coupler and output link
- $B'$ = fixed pivot of output link

A known **resistance $F_R$** acts on link $CB$ (coupler or output). We wish to find the **effort $F$** on the input crank $AB$ to keep the mechanism in static equilibrium.

**Step 1 — Decompose the problem (Superposition).**
$$\text{State (a): Mechanism under } F_R \text{ alone, with input link free.} \quad \Rightarrow \text{Turns by } \theta_2^{(a)}$$
$$\text{State (b): Mechanism under } F \text{ alone, with input link free.} \quad \Rightarrow \text{Turns by } \theta_2^{(b)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the principle of virtual work.**
For the input link to be in static equilibrium, the net virtual work done by both forces on the input link must be zero.

Let the virtual angular displacement of the input link under $F$ be $\delta\theta_2$. Then the virtual work of $F$ is $F \cdot a \cdot \delta\theta_2$, where $a$ is the perpendicular distance of $F$ from $A$. Similarly, the resistance $F_R$ does virtual work $F_R \cdot c \cdot \delta\theta_4$ on the output link, where $\delta\theta_4$ is the corresponding angular displacement of the output link.

Setting the total virtual work to zero:
$$F \cdot a \cdot \delta\theta_2 + F_R \cdot c \cdot \delta\theta_4 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Express displacement ratios using velocity coefficients.**
From kinematics of the four-bar mechanism, let:
$$\frac{\delta\theta_4}{\delta\theta_2} = -\frac{l_1 \, l_4}{l_2 \, l_3} \cdot \frac{\sin \beta}{\sin \alpha}$$
where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the transmission angles, and $l_1, l_2, l_3, l_4$ are link lengths.

**Step 4 — Solve for effort $F$.**
$$F = -F_R \cdot \frac{c \cdot \delta\theta_4}{a \cdot \delta\theta_2} = F_R \cdot \frac{c}{a} \cdot \frac{l_1 \, l_4}{l_2 \, l_3} \cdot \frac{\sin \beta}{\sin \alpha}$$

$$\boxed{F = F_R \cdot \frac{l_1 \, l_4}{l_2 \, l_3} \cdot \frac{c}{a} \cdot \frac{\sin \beta}{\sin \alpha}}$$

**Step 5 — Determine bearing reactions (after $F$ is found).**
Apply $\Sigma F_x = 0$ and $\Sigma F_y = 0$ to the FBD of each link, using the known force directions. Solve sequentially starting from the link with the fewest unknowns (typically the coupler if it is a two-force member).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Architecture: Static Force Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START: Given Mechanism] --> A1[Identify Links and Joints]
    A1 --> A2[Identify Applied External Forces: F and F_R]
    A2 --> A3[Decide Method: Superposition or Direct Equilibrium]
    A3 --> B1[Draw FBD of Each Link]
    B1 --> B2[Replace Pin Joints by R_ix and R_iy]
    B2 --> C1[For Slider-Crank: Resolve F_Q along rod]
    C1 --> C2[F_Q = P / cos phi]
    C2 --> C3[Tangential Crank Effort F = F_Q sin theta + phi]
    C3 --> C4[Side Thrust N = P tan phi]
    C4 --> D1[Compute Bearing Reactions R_C and R_P]
    D1 --> D2[Check Equilibrium: Sigma Fx = 0, Sigma Fy = 0]
    D2 --> E1[Report Crank Effort, Thrust, and Bearing Loads]
    E1 --> F0[END]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Force Flow in a Slider-Crank

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EXT[External Load P on Piston] --> SR[Slider Resolves P along Line of Stroke]
    SR --> RO[Connecting Rod Two Force Member F_Q = P / cos phi]
    RO --> CR[Crank Resolves F_Q into Tangential F and Radial R]
    CR --> TN[Tangential F equals Crank Effort]
    CR --> RA[Radial R taken by Crank Pin Bearing]
    SR --> NW[Normal Component N = P tan phi on Cylinder Wall]
    TN --> TM[Turning Moment T = F times r on Crank Shaft]
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Four-Bar FBD Linkage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FRAME[Fixed Frame Link 1] -->|A is pinned to ground| L2
    L2[Input Link 2 with Effort F at B] -->|B is moving pin| L3
    L3[Coupler Link 3 with Reaction R_B and R_C] -->|C is moving pin| L4
    L4[Output Link 4 with Resistance F_R at C] -->|B' is pinned to ground| FRAME
    L3 -->|Two force member if no external force| FORCE[F_B = F_C along BC]
    L2 -->|Free body: R_A and R_B, F applied| FB2
    L4 -->|Free body: R_Bdash and R_C, F_R applied| FB4
```

### 4.4 Vector Diagram (Textual Schematic)

```
            N
            ↑
   ┌────────P────────┐ ← Piston (slider) on line of stroke
   │        ╲
   │         ╲ F_Q (along rod)
   │          ╲
   │           ╲
   │            ↘
   │             B ← crank-pin
   │            ╱
   │           ╱
   │          ╱  tangential F ⟂ crank
   │         ╱
   │   A    ╱  ← crank centre (pivot)
   └──────╱
          radial R along crank
```

**Caption:** Force resolution at the piston. $P$ is opposed by $F_Q \cos \phi$ along the stroke; the perpendicular component $F_Q \sin \phi$ pushes the piston sideways against the cylinder wall, producing the side-thrust $N$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define static force analysis. State the assumptions made during the static force analysis of mechanisms (frictionless case).*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Static force analysis is the determination of forces acting on the various links of a mechanism when it is in equilibrium (either at rest or moving at constant velocity), neglecting inertia effects. The objective is to find the effort required at the input link to overcome a given external load, as well as the bearing reactions at all joints.
- **Assumptions (1 mark):** (i) All pin joints are frictionless and transmit only forces, not couples. (ii) The weights of the links are neglected unless specified. (iii) The mechanism is in static equilibrium (no inertia forces).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
