# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Inertia Force and State D'Alembert's Principle

`[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Inertia Force Definition:**
The inertia force of a moving body is defined as the product of its mass and the acceleration of its mass center, acting in a direction opposite to that of the acceleration. Mathematically, $\vec{F_i} = -m \cdot \vec{a}$, where the negative sign signifies opposition to acceleration.

**D'Alembert's Principle Statement:**
D'Alembert's principle states that the inertia force acting on a moving body, together with the externally applied forces, forms a system in *dynamic equilibrium* at every instant of time. In other words, if we treat the inertia force as an additional real force acting on the body, then the body can be analyzed as if it were in static equilibrium. The governing equation is:

$$\sum \vec{F}_{applied} + \vec{F_i} = 0$$

This principle converts a problem of *dynamics* into an equivalent problem of *statics*, which is far easier to solve using standard equilibrium equations.

---

### Question 2: Distinguish Between Inertia Force and Centrifugal Force

`[KTU University Exam — December 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Inertia Force** | **Centrifugal Force** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Pseudo-force opposing the acceleration of a body | Outward pseudo-force on a body moving in a circular path |
| Direction | Opposite to the linear acceleration vector | Radially outward from the centre of rotation |
| General Form | $F_i = m \cdot a$ (linear or angular) | $F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$ |
| Type of Motion | Any motion (translatory, rotary, general plane) | Only circular motion |
| Appearance | Appears whenever velocity changes (in magnitude or direction) | Appears whenever there is rotation, even at constant angular velocity |
| Physical Reality | Fictitious (kineto-static construct) | Fictitious (observed only in rotating reference frames) |

**Key Distinction:**
The inertia force is the *general* term, while centrifugal force is a *specific case* that arises during uniform circular motion. The inertia force can be zero (when the body moves at constant velocity), but the centrifugal force persists as long as the body rotates.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Slider-Crank Piston Inertia Force Derivation and Numerical Application

`[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the inertia force of a reciprocating piston in a slider-crank mechanism. Clearly state the assumptions used in the derivation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A single-cylinder IC engine has a crank radius of **75 mm** and a connecting rod length of **225 mm**. The mass of the reciprocating parts is **2.5 kg**. The crank rotates at a uniform speed of **1800 rpm**. Calculate the maximum inertia force of the piston. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

Refer to the derivation in **Section 3.2** of these notes. Key steps to be written in the answer script:

1. **Position of piston:** $x = r \cos\theta + L \cos\phi$, where $\sin\phi = (r/L)\sin\theta$.
2. **Binomial expansion:** $\cos\phi \approx 1 - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{r}{L}\right)^2 \sin^2\theta$.
3. **Simplified position:** $x = r \cos\theta + \frac{r^2}{4L}\cos 2\theta + \text{constant}$.
4. **Velocity:** $v = -r\omega\left(\sin\theta + \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right)$, where $n = L/r$.
5. **Acceleration:** $a = -r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$.
6. **Inertia force:** $F_i = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$.

**Assumptions:**
- The connecting rod is long compared to the crank (**L > 3r**), so the angle **φ** is small.
- The crank rotates at a **uniform** angular velocity (constant **ω**).
- The piston mass is **lumped** at the wrist pin location.
- The slider-crank mechanism is **rigid** (no elastic deformation).

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m} \\
L &= 225 \text{ mm} = 0.225 \text{ m} \\
m &= 2.5 \text{ kg} \\
N &= 1800 \text{ rpm} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** Compute the ratio **n = L/r**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{L}{r} = \frac{225}{75} = 3 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the angular velocity **ω** in rad/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1800}{60} \\
\omega &= 60\pi \approx 188.496 \text{ rad/s} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the inertia force expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_i &= m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \left( \cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n} \right) \\
F_i &= 2.5 \times 0.075 \times (188.496)^2 \left( \cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{3} \right) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the constant coefficient:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 &= 2.5 \times 0.075 \times (188.496)^2 \\
&= 0.1875 \times 35530.58 \\
&\approx 6661.98 \text{ N} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** The maximum inertia force occurs when the bracketed term $\left( \cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{3} \right)$ is **maximum**. Differentiating with respect to **θ** and setting to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{d\theta}\left( \cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{3} \right) &= 0 \\
-\sin\theta - \frac{2\sin 2\theta}{3} &= 0 \\
\sin\theta \left( 1 + \frac{4\cos\theta}{3} \right) &= 0 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives **sin θ = 0** (i.e., **θ = 0°**) or $\cos\theta = -\frac{3}{4}$ (which gives a *minimum*, not maximum). So the maximum occurs at **θ = 0°**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{i,\max} &= 6661.98 \times \left( 1 + \frac{1}{3} \right) \\
F_{i,\max} &= 6661.98 \times \frac{4}{3} \\
F_{i,\max} &\approx 8882.6 \text{ N} \approx 8.88 \text{ kN} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The maximum inertia force of the piston is approximately **8.88 kN**, occurring at the **inner dead centre** (θ = 0°).

---

### Question B (14 Marks): D'Alembert's Principle and Rotating Mass Inertia Torque

`[KTU University Exam — December 2023]`

**(a)** State and derive D'Alembert's principle for a particle in motion. Explain how it is used to convert a dynamic problem into a static equilibrium problem. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A flywheel of mass **120 kg** and radius of gyration **0.30 m** is rotating at **600 rpm**. If the driving torque is suddenly cut off and a braking torque of **50 N·m** is applied, find the time taken by the flywheel to come to rest. Also, calculate the number of revolutions made during this time. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

Refer to the derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The student must present:

1. Newton's Second Law: $\sum \vec{F} = m \vec{a}$.
2. Algebraic rearrangement: $\sum \vec{F} - m \vec{a} = 0$.
3. Definition: $\vec{F_i} = m \vec{a}$ (or $\vec{F_i} = -m \vec{a}$ for the opposed direction).
4. Final form: $\sum \vec{F}_{applied} + \vec{F_i} = 0$.

**Explanation of Dynamic-to-Static Conversion:**
The original problem is dynamic because the body is accelerating and we must solve a differential equation of motion. By introducing the inertia force, we *absorb* the **m·a** term into a new pseudo-force. The resulting equation is of the form "sum of forces = 0", which is the standard form of static equilibrium. Therefore, all the standard tools of statics (free-body diagrams, equilibrium equations, virtual work) can be applied to a *dynamic* problem.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 120 \text{ kg} \\
k &= 0.30 \text{ m} \quad \text{(radius of gyration)} \\
N_1 &= 600 \text{ rpm} \\
T_{brake} &= 50 \text{ N·m} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** Compute the mass moment of inertia of the flywheel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= M \cdot k^2 = 120 \times (0.30)^2 \\
I &= 120 \times 0.09 = 10.8 \text{ kg·m}^2 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the initial angular velocity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_1 &= \frac{2\pi N_1}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 600}{60} = 20\pi \\
\omega_1 &\approx 62.832 \text{ rad/s} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the angular deceleration:
The only torque acting is the braking torque. Using the rotational equation of motion:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= I \cdot \alpha \\
-50 &= 10.8 \times \alpha \\
\alpha &= -\frac{50}{10.8} \approx -4.63 \text{ rad/s}^2 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The negative sign indicates angular deceleration (slowing down).

**Step 4:** Compute the time taken to come to rest (**ω₂ = 0**):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_2 &= \omega_1 + \alpha \cdot t \\
0 &= 62.832 + (-4.63) \cdot t \\
t &= \frac{62.832}{4.63} \\
t &\approx 13.57 \text{ seconds} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the number of revolutions using the kinematic equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_2^2 &= \omega_1^2 + 2\alpha\theta \\
0 &= (62.832)^2 + 2 \times (-4.63) \times \theta \\
\theta &= \frac{(62.832)^2}{2 \times 4.63} \\
\theta &= \frac{3947.84}{9.26} \\
\theta &\approx 426.33 \text{ rad} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to revolutions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Revolutions} &= \frac{\theta}{2\pi} = \frac{426.33}{2\pi} \\
&\approx \frac{426.33}{6.2832} \\
&\approx 67.85 \text{ revolutions} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The flywheel comes to rest in approximately **13.57 seconds** after making about **67.85 revolutions**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Many students confuse the direction of the inertia force and end up adding it to the applied force instead of subtracting. Remember: **inertia force always opposes the acceleration** and **inertia torque always opposes the angular acceleration**.
> 2. **Forgetting the Secondary Term:** When computing the piston inertia force, students often write only the primary term **$F_{i1} = m r \omega^2 \cos\theta$** and forget the secondary term **$F_{i2} = (m r^2 \omega^2 / L) \cos 2\theta$**. Both terms are required for full marks.
> 3. **Unit Conversion Mistakes:** A common error is mixing units — using crank radius in **mm** with mass in **kg** and angular velocity in **rpm** without converting to SI units. Always convert all quantities to SI (m, kg, s) before plugging into the formula.
> 4. **Forgetting the Binomial Approximation:** Students sometimes directly write $\cos\phi = 1$ (crude approximation) without justification. The KTU syllabus requires the **binomial expansion** $\cos\phi \approx 1 - \frac{1}{2}(r/L)^2 \sin^2\theta$ to be explicitly shown.
> 5. **Maximum vs. Minimum Confusion:** When asked for the *maximum* inertia force, students often compute the value at a wrong crank angle. The maximum occurs at the **inner dead centre (θ = 0°)** for the primary term, but the *secondary* term peaks at **θ = 0° or 180°** as well (since $\cos 2\theta = 1$ at **θ = 0°**).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

This rapid-revision checklist summarizes the *high-priority* concepts, formulas, and pitfalls that KTU 2024 Scheme students must master for Module 1 — Inertia Force:

- **Inertia force is a fictitious, kineto-static construct** — not a real Newtonian force. It is introduced only to make the problem solvable using static equilibrium methods.
- **The defining equation is** $\vec{F_i} = -m\vec{a}$ **(linear motion)** and $\vec{T_i} = -I\vec{\alpha}$ **(rotational motion)**. The negative sign is *non-negotiable* and is the most common source of marks lost.
- **D'Alembert's Principle** is the **single most important theorem** of Module 1. Memorize both the statement and the derivation cold.
- **Piston acceleration formula:** $a = r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$, where **n = L/r**. This is a **double-harmonic** expression — primary at frequency **ω** and secondary at frequency **2ω**.
- **The piston inertia force expression** $F_i = m r \omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$ is a *board-exam favorite*. Be ready to derive it from the position equation in three differentiation steps.
- **For a rotating disc/flywheel,** the inertia torque is $T_i = I\alpha = \dfrac{1}{2}MR^2 \alpha$. This forms the basis of **flywheel design for coefficient of fluctuation of speed** in Module 2.
- **The two key applications** of inertia force analysis in KTU Module 1 are:
  1. **Crank effort and turning moment** in IC engine slider-crank mechanisms.
  2. **Dynamic force analysis** of four-bar and slider-crank linkages using the principle of superposition.
- **Maximum inertia force** of a piston occurs at the **inner dead centre (IDC)**, i.e., at **θ = 0°**.
- **Minimum (most negative) inertia force** occurs at the **outer dead centre (ODC)**, i.e., at **θ = 180°**.
- **Always convert to SI units** before plugging numbers into formulas. The standard units are **m, kg, s, rad**.
- **The 3 critical assumptions** in the piston acceleration derivation are: (1) uniform crank speed, (2) $\dfrac{r}{L} \le \dfrac{1}{3.5}$ (so the binomial expansion is valid), and (3) rigid mechanism.
- **Vector form vs. scalar form:** KTU expects the *vector* form $\vec{F_i} = -m\vec{a}$ as the official definition, but in numerical problems, the *scalar magnitude* $|F_i| = m|a|$ is used.
- **Dynamic equilibrium equation:** The complete D'Alembert's equation for a particle is $\sum \vec{F}_{external} + \vec{F_i} = 0$. For a rigid body, it expands to three scalar equations: $\sum F_x = m a_{Gx}$, $\sum F_y = m a_{Gy}$, and $\sum M_G = I_G \alpha$.
- **Module 1 vs. Module 2 distinction:** Module 1 is about *kinematics + inertia force derivation*; Module 2 typically extends this to *flywheel sizing and turning moment diagrams*. Do not confuse the inertia force formula (Module 1) with the coefficient of fluctuation of speed (Module 2).
- **Practice numerical problems** with crank radius between **50–100 mm** and **n = L/r between 3 and 5** to cover the typical KTU numerical range.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inertia Torque
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT527 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inertia Torque

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (Dynamics of Machinery – Module 1)**, **Inertia Torque** is defined as the resisting (or applied) torque developed by a rotating or reciprocating mass due to its resistance to a change in its state of motion (angular acceleration or deceleration). Mathematically, it is the rotational equivalent of Newton's Second Law of Motion:

$$T_I = I \cdot \alpha$$

where $I$ is the **Mass Moment of Inertia** of the body about the axis of rotation (in **kg·m²**), and $\alpha$ is the **Angular Acceleration** (in **rad/s²**).

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Inertia Torque is the foundation concept leading to **Turning Moment Diagrams**, **Fluctuation of Energy**, and **Flywheel Design**. It is a mandatory Module 1 topic with high weightage in university examinations (typically 14 marks in Part B).

In a **reciprocating engine mechanism**, the inertia force $F_I$ produced by the accelerating reciprocating parts is multiplied by the crank radius $r$ to give the **Inertia Torque about the crank axis**:

$$T_I = F_I \cdot r = m \cdot r^2 \cdot \omega^2 \left( \cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n} \right)$$

where $n$ is the ratio of crank length $r$ to connecting rod length $l$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Spinning Luggage Trolley"

Imagine pushing a heavy loaded luggage trolley at an airport. To **start** it moving, you need a much greater push than just to keep it rolling at a steady pace. That "extra push" is essentially fighting **inertia**. 

Now apply the same logic to **rotation**:

- The **trolley** = a rotating disc or flywheel
- The **extra push** = Inertia Torque
- The **forward motion** = Angular Velocity $\omega$
- The **speed change** = Angular Acceleration $\alpha$

When you spin up a heavy grinding wheel from rest, your hands must apply a large torque. Once the wheel reaches a constant speed, almost zero torque is needed. The torque that *only exists during speed change* is the **Inertia Torque**.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Physical Insight:**  
> - $\alpha > 0$ (speeding up): Inertia Torque **opposes** the driving torque.  
> - $\alpha < 0$ (slowing down): Inertia Torque **aids** the driving torque.  
> - $\alpha = 0$ (constant speed): Inertia Torque $= 0$.

### 1.3 Governing Physical Quantities

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit | Vector/Scalar |
|:------:|:--------:|:-------:|:-------------:|
| $I$ | Mass Moment of Inertia | **kg·m²** | Scalar |
| $\alpha$ | Angular Acceleration | **rad/s²** | Vector |
| $T_I$ | Inertia Torque | **N·m** | Vector |
| $m$ | Mass of Reciprocating Parts | **kg** | Scalar |
| $r$ | Crank Radius | **m** | Scalar |
| $\omega$ | Angular Velocity | **rad/s** | Vector |
| $\theta$ | Crank Angle from TDC | **radians** | Scalar |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics Behind Inertia Torque – Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: Newton's Second Law for Translation**
Every mass resists a change in linear velocity. The resisting force is:

$$F_I = m \cdot a$$

**Step 2: Newton's Second Law for Rotation**
By direct analogy, every rotating mass resists a change in angular velocity. The resisting torque is:

$$T_I = I \cdot \alpha$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Why this works:** Moment of Inertia $I$ is the rotational "mass" — it tells us how resistant a body is to angular acceleration, just as mass $m$ tells us how resistant a body is to linear acceleration.

**Step 3: Sign Convention in a Mechanism**
In a reciprocating engine, the direction of inertia torque depends on the **direction of crank rotation**:

$$T_I \; (\text{along crank}) = \pm \, m \, r^2 \omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$$

**Step 4: Component Splitting**
The total inertia torque has two distinct harmonic components:

1. **Primary Inertia Torque** (1st harmonic, $\cos\theta$ term): frequency = engine speed
2. **Secondary Inertia Torque** (2nd harmonic, $\cos 2\theta$ term): frequency = twice the engine speed

**Step 5: Net Torque on Crankshaft**
The net torque available to overcome the load is the **algebraic sum**:

$$T_{net} = T_{driving} - T_{resisting} - T_{I,primary} - T_{I,secondary}$$

### 2.2 Engineering Utility – Where This Concept is Used

| Engineering Field | Application |
|:-----------------|:------------|
| **Reciprocating Engines** (IC Engines) | Designing flywheels to absorb torque fluctuations |
| **Press Tools / Punching Machines** | Sizing flywheels for energy storage |
| **CNC & Robotics** | Calculating motor torque for rapid acceleration |
| **Turbomachinery** | Estimating torque during startup/shutdown |
| **Vehicle Drivetrains** | Gearbox design for torque fluctuations |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:-:|:-------:|:-----------|:------|
| 1 | $T_I = I\alpha$ | Basic inertia torque (rotational system) | N·m |
| 2 | $F_I = m \cdot a$ | Inertia force (linear reciprocating parts) | N |
| 3 | $a = r\omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$ | Acceleration of reciprocating parts | m/s² |
| 4 | $F_I = m r \omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$ | Inertia force on piston | N |
| 5 | $T_I = m r^2 \omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$ | Inertia torque about crank axis | N·m |
| 6 | $T_{I,primary} = m r^2 \omega^2 \cos\theta$ | Primary (1st harmonic) component | N·m |
| 7 | $T_{I,secondary} = \frac{m r^2 \omega^2}{n} \cos 2\theta$ | Secondary (2nd harmonic) component | N·m |
| 8 | $I_{eq} = \sum I_i \left(\frac{\omega_i}{\omega_{ref}}\right)^2$ | Equivalent moment of inertia (multi-body) | kg·m² |
| 9 | $T_{I,eq} = I_{eq} \cdot \alpha_{ref}$ | Equivalent inertia torque at reference shaft | N·m |
| 10 | $\Delta E = T_{I} \cdot \Delta\theta$ | Energy fluctuation due to inertia torque | J |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Note:** In the above table, $\vert \cdot \vert$ symbols (if any) are intentionally avoided to prevent markdown parsing conflicts. All "ratios" $n = r/l$ are dimensionless.

### 2.4 Sign Convention Convention for KTU 2024

- **Clockwise crank rotation:** Positive inertia torque is taken **clockwise** (along the direction of motion).
- **Counter-clockwise crank rotation:** Positive inertia torque is taken **counter-clockwise**.
- **Inertia force on piston:** Acts **downward** (towards crank center) when accelerating upward, and **upward** when decelerating.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Inertia Torque for a Simple Rotating Disc

**Given:** A solid disc of mass $M$ and radius $R$ rotating about its central axis with angular acceleration $\alpha$.

**Step 1: Definition of Mass Moment of Inertia**
For a solid disc about its central axis:

$$I = \frac{M R^2}{2}$$

**Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law for Rotation**
By the rotational form of Newton's law:

$$\sum T = I \cdot \alpha$$

**Step 3: If the only torque present is the inertia torque:**

$$T_I = I \cdot \alpha = \frac{M R^2}{2} \cdot \alpha$$

**Interpretation:** To spin up a 10 kg disc of radius 0.2 m at $5 \, \text{rad/s}^2$, you need:

$$T_I = \frac{10 \times 0.04}{2} \times 5 = 1.0 \, \text{N·m}$$

### 3.2 Derivation II: Inertia Torque in a Reciprocating Engine (Slider-Crank)

**Given:** A slider-crank mechanism with crank radius $r$, connecting rod length $l$, crank angular velocity $\omega$, and reciprocating mass $m$.

**Step 1: Displacement of the Piston (Approximate)**
By standard kinematic derivation (using binomial expansion of $\sqrt{1 - x^2}$ for $x = (r/l)\sin\theta$):

$$x = r \left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{2n} - \frac{(r/l)^2 \cos 4\theta}{8}\right)$$

Neglecting terms beyond second order:

$$x \approx r \left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{2n}\right)$$

**Step 2: Velocity of the Piston**
Differentiating with respect to time, using $\dot{\theta} = \omega$ and $\dfrac{d}{dt} = \omega \dfrac{d}{d\theta}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{dx}{dt} = \omega \frac{dx}{d\theta} \\
  &= \omega \cdot r \left(-\sin\theta - \frac{\sin 2\theta}{n}\right) \\
  &= -r\omega \left(\sin\theta + \frac{\sin 2\theta}{n}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Acceleration of the Piston**
Differentiating again with respect to time:

$$\begin{aligned}
a &= \frac{dv}{dt} = \omega \frac{dv}{d\theta} \\
  &= \omega \cdot (-r\omega) \left(\cos\theta + \frac{2\cos 2\theta}{n}\right) \\
  &= -r\omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Inertia Force on the Piston**
By Newton's Second Law:

$$F_I = m \cdot a = -m r \omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$$

The negative sign indicates that the inertia force acts **opposite to the direction of motion** of the piston.

**Step 5: Inertia Torque about the Crank Axis**
The moment arm of this force about the crank axis is $r$ (perpendicular component of crank):

$$T_I = F_I \cdot r = -m r^2 \omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$$

$$\boxed{T_I = m r^2 \omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)}$$

**Step 6: Splitting into Harmonic Components**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_I &= T_{I,primary} + T_{I,secondary} \\
T_{I,primary} &= m r^2 \omega^2 \cos\theta \quad \text{(1st harmonic)} \\
T_{I,secondary} &= \frac{m r^2 \omega^2}{n} \cos 2\theta \quad \text{(2nd harmonic)}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Derivation III: Equivalent Inertia Torque (Multi-Mass System)

**Given:** A gear train with three shafts having moments of inertia $I_1$, $I_2$, $I_3$ and angular velocities $\omega_1$, $\omega_2$, $\omega_3$. Reference shaft has $\omega_{ref}$.

**Step 1: Kinetic Energy Equivalence**
The equivalent moment of inertia $I_{eq}$ (referred to the reference shaft) must store the **same total kinetic energy**:

$$\frac{1}{2} I_{eq} \omega_{ref}^2 = \frac{1}{2} I_1 \omega_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} I_2 \omega_2^2 + \frac{1}{2} I_3 \omega_3^2$$

**Step 2: Solve for $I_{eq}$**

$$I_{eq} = I_1 \left(\frac{\omega_1}{\omega_{ref}}\right)^2 + I_2 \left(\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_{ref}}\right)^2 + I_3 \left(\frac{\omega_3}{\omega_{ref}}\right)^2$$

**Step 3: Generalised Form**

$$\boxed{I_{eq} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i \left(\frac{\omega_i}{\omega_{ref}}\right)^2}$$

**Step 4: Equivalent Inertia Torque**
If the reference shaft accelerates at $\alpha_{ref}$:

$$T_{I,eq} = I_{eq} \cdot \alpha_{ref}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Inertia Torque Behaviour

| Crank Angle $\theta$ | Piston Position | Acceleration Direction | Inertia Force Direction | Inertia Torque Sense |
|:--------------------:|:---------------:|:----------------------:|:-----------------------:|:--------------------:|
| $0°$ (TDC outward) | Outermost | Decelerating | Inward (towards crank) | Resists driving torque |
| $90°$ | Mid-stroke (max velocity) | Zero | Zero | **Zero** |
| $180°$ (BDC) | Innermost | Accelerating | Outward | Aids driving torque |
| $270°$ | Mid-stroke (max velocity other side) | Zero | Zero | **Zero** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram – Conceptual Flow of Inertia Torque in a Reciprocating Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Combustion Pressure Force F_p] --> C[Net Force on Piston F_net = F_p - F_I]
    B[Inertia Force F_I from m*a] --> C
    C --> D[Net Torque on Crank T_net]
    D --> E[Shaft Output]
    F[Mass m] --> G[Acceleration a = rω² cosθ + cos2θ/n]
    G --> B
    H[Crank Radius r] --> G
    I[Angular Velocity ω] --> G
    J[Moment of Inertia I] --> K[Inertia Torque T_I = I·α]
    K --> D
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram – Equivalent Inertia Torque Reduction (Multi-Shaft System)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Shaft1[Shaft 1 - Reference]
        I1[I_1]
        W1[ω_1]
        A1[α_1]
    end
    subgraph Shaft2[Shaft 2]
        I2[I_2]
        W2[ω_2]
        A2[α_2]
    end
    subgraph Shaft3[Shaft 3]
        I3[I_3]
        W3[ω_3]
        A3[α_3]
    end
    Shaft2 -->|Gear Ratio| Shaft1
    Shaft3 -->|Gear Ratio| Shaft1
    Shaft1 --> Ieq[Equivalent I_eq = Sum of I_i times omega_i/omega_ref squared]
    Ieq --> Tfinal[Equivalent Inertia Torque T_Ieq = I_eq times alpha_ref]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram – Variation of Inertia Torque with Crank Angle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> TDC0
    TDC0: θ equals 0 deg - Inertia Torque Max Negative
    TDC0 --> Mid1
    Mid1: θ equals 90 deg - Inertia Torque Zero
    Mid1 --> BDC
    BDC: θ equals 180 deg - Inertia Torque Max Positive
    BDC --> Mid2
    Mid2: θ equals 270 deg - Inertia Torque Zero
    Mid2 --> TDC360
    TDC360: θ equals 360 deg - Back to TDC
    TDC360 --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Reading the State Diagram:** Inertia torque oscillates between a **maximum negative value** (at TDC) and a **maximum positive value** (at BDC), passing through **zero** at mid-stroke positions ($90°$ and $270°$). This sinusoidal oscillation is the root cause of **fluctuation of energy** in reciprocating engines.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define **Inertia Torque**. State the formula for inertia torque of a reciprocating engine in terms of crank angle.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**  
Inertia torque is the resisting torque developed by a rotating or reciprocating mass due to its resistance to a change in angular velocity. It is the rotational equivalent of inertia force and is given by $T_I = I \cdot \alpha$, where $I$ is the mass moment of inertia and $\alpha$ is the angular acceleration.

**Formula (1 Mark):**  
For a reciprocating engine slider-crank mechanism:

$$T_I = m r^2 \omega^2 \left( \cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n} \right)$$

where $m$ = reciprocating mass, $r$ = crank radius, $\omega$ = angular velocity, $\theta$ = crank angle, $n = r/l$ = ratio of crank to connecting rod length.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Distinguish between **Primary Inertia Torque** and **Secondary Inertia Torque**. State their frequencies relative to crank speed.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Primary Inertia Torque | Secondary Inertia Torque |
|:-------:|:----------------------:|:------------------------:|
| **Formula** | $T_{I,1} = m r^2 \omega^2 \cos\theta$ | $T_{I,2} = \dfrac{m r^2 \omega^2}{n} \cos 2\theta$ |
| **Frequency** | Same as crank speed (1×) | Twice the crank speed (2×) |
| **Origin** | 1st harmonic of $\cos\theta$ term | 2nd harmonic of $\cos 2\theta$ term |
| **Magnitude** | Larger (coefficient 1) | Smaller (coefficient $1/n$) |

The primary inertia torque completes **one cycle per revolution** of the crank, while the secondary completes **two cycles per revolution**. (3 Marks for table + 1 line explanation)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **inertia torque** in a reciprocating engine mechanism in terms of crank angle $\theta$. Clearly show all steps from piston displacement to the final harmonic split. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A single-cylinder reciprocating engine has the following data:  
Crank radius $r = 100 \, \text{mm}$, connecting rod length $l = 400 \, \text{mm}$,  
mass of reciprocating parts $m = 25 \, \text{kg}$,  
crank speed $N = 600 \, \text{rpm}$.  
Calculate the magnitude and direction of inertia torque when the crank is at **$60°$ from TDC**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – Derivation (7 Marks):**

Refer to the derivation in **Section 3.2** above. The complete derivation proceeds as:

1. Piston displacement (approx): $x = r \left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{2n}\right)$ — *1 Mark*
2. Velocity: $v = -r\omega \left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{n}\right)$ — *1 Mark*
3. Acceleration: $a = -r\omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$ — *2 Marks*
4. Inertia force: $F_I = -mr\omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$ — *1 Mark*
5. Inertia torque: $T_I = -mr^2\omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$ — *2 Marks*

**Final Boxed Answer:**

$$\boxed{T_I = m r^2 \omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)}$$

---

**Part (b) – Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Compute angular velocity $\omega$ (1 Mark)**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 600}{60} = 62.832 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute $n$ (1 Mark)**

$$n = \frac{r}{l} = \frac{100}{400} = 0.25$$

**Step 3: Substitute into inertia torque formula at $\theta = 60°$ (3 Marks)**

$$T_I = 25 \times (0.1)^2 \times (62.832)^2 \times \left( \cos 60° + \frac{\cos 120°}{0.25} \right)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_I &= 25 \times 0.01 \times 3947.84 \times \left( 0.5 + \frac{-0.5}{0.25} \right) \\
    &= 25 \times 0.01 \times 3947.84 \times (0.5 - 2.0) \\
    &= 25 \times 0.01 \times 3947.84 \times (-1.5) \\
    &= -1480.44 \, \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Magnitude and Direction (2 Marks)**

$$|T_I| = 1480.44 \, \text{N·m} \approx 1.48 \, \text{kN·m}$$

The **negative sign** indicates the inertia torque is **opposite to the direction of crank rotation** (i.e., it resists the driving torque because the piston is decelerating in the upper half of the stroke at $60°$ from TDC).

$$\boxed{|T_I| \approx 1480.44 \, \text{N·m, opposing crank rotation}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch how to determine the **equivalent moment of inertia** of a multi-rotor system referred to a single reference shaft. Derive the governing equation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A gear train consists of three rotors on shafts A, B, and C.  
Shaft A (reference): $I_A = 5 \, \text{kg·m²}$, $N_A = 300 \, \text{rpm}$  
Shaft B: $I_B = 3 \, \text{kg·m²}$, $N_B = 600 \, \text{rpm}$  
Shaft C: $I_C = 2 \, \text{kg·m²}$, $N_C = 900 \, \text{rpm}$  

If Shaft A accelerates at $\alpha_A = 4 \, \text{rad/s}^2$, find the equivalent inertia torque on Shaft A. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – Conceptual Derivation (7 Marks):**

Refer to derivation in **Section 3.3** above. Step-wise:

1. Define kinetic energy equivalence principle — *1 Mark*
2. Set up KE balance equation: $\frac{1}{2}I_{eq}\omega_{ref}^2 = \sum \frac{1}{2}I_i \omega_i^2$ — *2 Marks*
3. Solve for $I_{eq}$: $I_{eq} = \sum I_i (\omega_i/\omega_{ref})^2$ — *2 Marks*
4. Apply to inertia torque: $T_{I,eq} = I_{eq} \cdot \alpha_{ref}$ — *2 Marks*

$$\boxed{I_{eq} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i \left(\frac{\omega_i}{\omega_{ref}}\right)^2}$$

---

**Part (b) – Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Compute angular velocities (1 Mark)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega_A &= \frac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 31.416 \, \text{rad/s} \\
\omega_B &= \frac{2\pi \times 600}{60} = 62.832 \, \text{rad/s} \\
\omega_C &= \frac{2\pi \times 900}{60} = 94.248 \, \text{rad/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute equivalent $I_{eq}$ referred to Shaft A (3 Marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{eq} &= I_A \left(\frac{\omega_A}{\omega_A}\right)^2 + I_B \left(\frac{\omega_B}{\omega_A}\right)^2 + I_C \left(\frac{\omega_C}{\omega_A}\right)^2 \\
       &= 5 \times (1)^2 + 3 \times (2)^2 + 2 \times (3)^2 \\
       &= 5 + 12 + 18 \\
       &= 35 \, \text{kg·m²}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute equivalent inertia torque (3 Marks)**

$$T_{I,eq} = I_{eq} \cdot \alpha_A = 35 \times 4 = 140 \, \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{I,eq} = 140 \, \text{N·m on Shaft A}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls – Where Students Lose Marks:**  
> 1. **Sign Error in Cos2θ Term:** At $\theta = 60°$, $\cos(2\theta) = \cos(120°) = -0.5$. Forgetting the negative sign gives an incorrect torque magnitude. **Always compute $\cos(2\theta)$ carefully.**  
> 2. **Unit Mismatch:** Crank radius $r$ MUST be in **metres** before squaring. Common mistake: using mm directly.  
> 3. **Forgetting $\omega^2$:** Some students compute $\omega$ instead of $\omega^2$ — this gives a torque value off by a factor of ~63 for $N = 600$ rpm.  
> 4. **Gear Train Reference Shaft Confusion:** When computing $I_{eq}$, the ratio must be $\omega_i / \omega_{ref}$, NOT $\omega_{ref}/\omega_i$.  
> 5. **Direction of Inertia Torque:** KTU expects a clear statement of whether the torque **aids** or **resists** the crank rotation. Marks are awarded specifically for this conclusion.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Inertia Torque = $I \cdot \alpha$** — the fundamental rotational Newton's Second Law.
- ✅ For reciprocating engines: **$T_I = m r^2 \omega^2 (\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n})$** — must be memorised.
- ✅ Two harmonic components: **Primary ($\cos\theta$)** and **Secondary ($\cos 2\theta$)**.
- ✅ **Primary inertia torque** frequency = crank speed; **Secondary** = 2× crank speed.
- ✅ Equivalent inertia torque for multi-shaft systems uses **kinetic energy equivalence**, not torque equivalence directly.
- ✅ Inertia torque is **maximum at TDC and BDC**, **zero at $90°$ and $270°$**.
- ✅ Sign convention: negative inertia torque = opposes driving torque (during acceleration away from TDC).
- ✅ The ratio $n = r/l$ is **dimensionless** and typically $\le 0.33$ in practical engines.
- ✅ Inertia torque is the **cause of fluctuation of energy**, which justifies the use of a **flywheel**.
- ✅ **Units check:** Always verify final units are **N·m** (or J/rad) for torque.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static and Dynamic Balancing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT527 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Static and Dynamic Balancing # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Static Balance**: A rotating body is said to be in *static balance* when the **center of gravity of the body lies on the axis of rotation**. In this state, the body can remain at rest in any position and does not produce any unbalanced centrifugal force. > [!NOTE] > **Dynamic Balance**: A rotating body is said to be in *dynamic balance* when the **resultant centrifugal force of the distributed masses is zero AND the resultant moment of these centrifugal forces about any point in the plane of motion is also zero**. A dynamically balanced body will run smoothly without any vibrating couple. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy **Imagine a Ferris Wheel**: - A Ferris wheel is **statically balanced** if, when stopped, it doesn't tip over to one side (its weight is centered on the axle). - A Ferris wheel is **dynamically balanced** if, when spinning at full speed, it doesn't wobble, shake, or cause the passengers to feel a sideways push. This requires both the **weights** to be evenly distributed AND the **weight positions** to be balanced around the rotational axis. **Car Wheel Analogy**: After getting a tire changed, the mechanic spins the wheel and places small weights on the rim. If placed correctly, the wheel won't vibrate at high speed — this is **dynamic balancing**. If just made to sit still without tipping, that's only **static balancing**. ## 1.3 Why Balancing is Critical in Engineering > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Significance of Balancing:** > - Prevents **vibrations** in high-speed machinery (turbines, compressors, I.C. engines). > - Reduces **bearing wear** and prevents premature failure. > - Eliminates **noise** and improves ride quality (automotive wheels, propellers). > - Critical for **aerospace components** (turbine discs, helicopter rotors, jet engines). ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Standard value of g (acceleration due to gravity):** **g = 9.81 m/s²** - Centrifugal force per unit mass: **F = m·r·ω²** - For balance, the vector product **m·r** (mass × radius) is the primary balancing metric (units: **kg·m**). - Balancing masses are typically expressed as **m·r** (mass-radius product) when radius is fixed. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Concept of Unbalanced Rotating Masses When a mass *m* rotates at an angular velocity *ω* at a radius *r* from the axis of rotation, it produces a **centrifugal force**: $$F_c = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$$ This force acts radially outward and rotates with the body. If this force is **not balanced**, it causes: - Vibration of the supporting structure - Wear and tear on bearings - Noise and fatigue failure ## 2.2 Types of Balancing Problems > [!NOTE] > **Two fundamental balancing scenarios in KTU syllabus:** ### A) Balancing of Rotating Masses in a **Single Plane** (Static Balancing) When all masses lie in the **same transverse plane**, the centrifugal forces are coplanar. Balance requires only one condition. ### B) Balancing of Rotating Masses in **Different Planes** (Dynamic Balancing) When masses are distributed **axially along the shaft** in multiple planes, both force AND couple balance are required. ## 2.3 Single Plane (Static) Balancing — Theory For *n* masses rotating in a single plane at angular velocity *ω*: - Centrifugal force of mass *i*: $F_i = m_i \cdot r_i \cdot \omega^2$ - Total unbalanced force (vector sum): $$\sum F_i = \omega^2 \cdot \sum (m_i \cdot r_i)$$ - For **static balance**, the resultant of all centrifugal forces must be zero: $$\sum (m_i \cdot r_i) = 0$$ This is a **vector equation** — solved by constructing a **force polygon** (closed polygon = balanced). ## 2.4 Multiple Plane (Dynamic) Balancing — Theory For masses distributed in several planes along a shaft, refer to a **reference plane**. Let: - $m_i$ = mass in plane *i* - $r_i$ = radius vector of mass *i* - $L_i$ = axial distance of plane *i* from the reference plane - $\theta_i$ = angular position of $r_i$ from a reference direction **Two conditions for dynamic balance:** ### Condition 1: Force Balance (Static Balance) $$\sum (m_i \cdot r_i) = 0 \quad \text{(vector equation in 2D)}$$ Resolved into components: $$\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$$ $$\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$$ ### Condition 2: Couple Balance (Dynamic Balance) $$\sum (m_i \cdot r_i \cdot L_i) = 0 \quad \text{(vector equation in 2D)}$$ Resolved into components: $$\sum m_i r_i L_i \cos\theta_i = 0$$ $$\sum m_i r_i L_i \sin\theta_i = 0$$ ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Master Formula Table for Balancing** | # | Formula | Description | Application | |---|---------|-------------|-------------| | 1 | $F_c = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$ | Centrifugal force of rotating mass | All rotating systems | | 2 | $\sum (m_i r_i) = 0$ | Static balance condition | Single-plane balancing | | 3 | $\sum (m_i r_i \cos\theta_i) = 0$ | Horizontal force balance | Multiple-plane (X-axis) | | 4 | $\sum (m_i r_i \sin\theta_i) = 0$ | Vertical force balance | Multiple-plane (Y-axis) | | 5 | $\sum (m_i r_i L_i) = 0$ | Couple (moment) balance | Dynamic balancing | | 6 | $\sum (m_i r_i L_i \cos\theta_i) = 0$ | Couple balance (X-direction) | Dynamic balancing | | 7 | $\sum (m_i r_i L_i \sin\theta_i) = 0$ | Couple balance (Y-direction) | Dynamic balancing | | 8 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity from RPM | Conversions | | 9 | $m_B = \dfrac{m_A r_A}{r_B}$ | Equating m·r products | Finding balancing mass | | 10 | $\theta_B = \theta_A + 180°$ | Opposite angular position | Opposing forces cancel | ## 2.6 Graphical (Vector Polygon) Method > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Tip:** Both analytical and graphical methods are examinable. The graphical method involves drawing a closed force polygon where each side represents $m_i r_i$ to scale. **Procedure:** 1. Draw each vector $m_i r_i$ at its given angle $\theta_i$ to scale. 2. If the polygon closes → system is balanced. 3. If not, the closing side (drawn from start to end) represents the unbalanced $m r$. 4. To balance, place a corrective mass at the same plane at the opposite angle. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Turbomachinery**: Steam turbines, gas turbines, centrifugal pumps — multi-plane balancing is essential. - **Automotive**: Crankshafts, drive shafts, wheels, tyres. - **Aerospace**: Propellers, jet engine compressor discs, helicopter rotors. - **Machine Tool Spindles**: High-speed spindles for CNC machines. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Centrifugal Force for a Rotating Mass Consider a mass *m* attached to a rotating shaft at a radius *r*, rotating with angular velocity *ω* (rad/s). The mass moves in a circular path. The required centripetal acceleration is: $$a_c = r \cdot \omega^2$$ By Newton's second law, the centripetal force is: $$F_c = m \cdot a_c = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$$ By Newton's third law, the mass exerts an equal and opposite **centrifugal force** outward on the shaft: $$F_c = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \quad \text{(directed radially outward)}$$ This force is the source of all unbalance effects. ## 3.2 Derivation of Single-Plane Balancing Condition For *n* masses $m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_n$ rotating in the same plane at radii $r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_n$ and angular positions $\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_n$: The centrifugal force on each mass: $$F_i = m_i \cdot r_i \cdot \omega^2$$ Acting at angle $\theta_i$. Since all forces share the common factor $\omega^2$, the **balance of forces** requires: $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$$ $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$$ Or, in compact vector form: $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} (m_i r_i) \vec{e}_i = 0$$ where $\vec{e}_i$ is the unit vector at angle $\theta_i$. ## 3.3 Derivation of Multi-Plane Balancing Conditions Consider *n* masses located in different planes along the shaft. Choose a reference plane (typically one of the planes or a convenient location). **Force Balance (transverse plane):** The centrifugal forces are perpendicular to the shaft. They can be summed as vectors in the transverse plane: $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} (m_i r_i) \cos\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{...(i)}$$ $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} (m_i r_i) \sin\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Couple Balance (about reference plane):** Each centrifugal force creates a couple about the reference plane equal to $F_i \times L_i = m_i r_i \omega^2 L_i$: $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} (m_i r_i L_i) \cos\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{...(iii)}$$ $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} (m_i r_i L_i) \sin\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{...(iv)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **For a system with $n$ masses in different planes, we have 4 equations and can solve for up to 4 unknowns** — typically the magnitudes and angular positions of two balancing masses (each with $m \cdot r$ and $\theta$, total 4 unknowns). ## 3.4 Detailed Analytical Worked Example — Single Plane Balancing ### Problem: Four masses $m_1 = 12$ kg, $m_2 = 10$ kg, $m_3 = 18$ kg, $m_4 = 15$ kg are attached to a shaft rotating at 1500 RPM in a single plane. Their radii and angular positions are: | Mass | m (kg) | r (mm) | θ (°) | |------|--------|--------|-------| | $m_1$ | 12 | 100 | 0 | | $m_2$ | 10 | 150 | 60 | | $m_3$ | 18 | 80 | 150 | | $m_4$ | 15 | 120 | 240 | Find the magnitude and angular position of a balancing mass at radius 100 mm. ### Step-by-Step Solution: **Step 1:** Calculate $m_i r_i$ for each mass: $$m_1 r_1 = 12 \times 0.100 = 1.200 \text{ kg·m}$$ $$m_2 r_2 = 10 \times 0.150 = 1.500 \text{ kg·m}$$ $$m_3 r_3 = 18 \times 0.080 = 1.440 \text{ kg·m}$$ $$m_4 r_4 = 15 \times 0.120 = 1.800 \text{ kg·m}$$ **Step 2:** Resolve each $m_i r_i$ into X and Y components: For $m_1 r_1$ at 0°: $$X_1 = 1.200 \cos 0° = 1.200, \quad Y_1 = 1.200 \sin 0° = 0$$ For $m_2 r_2$ at 60°: $$X_2 = 1.500 \cos 60° = 0.750, \quad Y_2 = 1.500 \sin 60° = 1.299$$ For $m_3 r_3$ at 150°: $$X_3 = 1.440 \cos 150° = -1.247, \quad Y_3 = 1.440 \sin 150° = 0.720$$ For $m_4 r_4$ at 240°: $$X_4 = 1.800 \cos 240° = -0.900, \quad Y_4 = 1.800 \sin 240° = -1.559$$ **Step 3:** Sum the components: $$\sum X = 1.200 + 0.750 - 1.247 - 0.900 = -0.197 \text{ kg·m}$$ $$\sum Y = 0 + 1.299 + 0.720 - 1.559 = 0.460 \text{ kg·m}$$ **Step 4:** Magnitude of resultant unbalanced $m r$: $$m_u r_u = \sqrt{(-0.197)^2 + (0.460)^2} = \sqrt{0.0388 + 0.2116} = \sqrt{0.2504} = 0.5004 \text{ kg·m}$$ **Step 5:** Angle of resultant: $$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.460}{-0.197}\right)$$ Since X is negative and Y is positive, the angle lies in the **second quadrant**. $$\phi = 180° - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.460}{0.197}\right) = 180° - 66.81° = 113.19°$$ **Step 6:** Balancing mass (opposite to unbalanced): $$\theta_B = 113.19° + 180° = 293.19° \text{ (or equivalently } -66.81°\text{)}$$ $$m_B r_B = 0.5004 \text{ kg·m}$$ Given $r_B = 100$ mm = 0.1 m: $$m_B = \frac{0.5004}{0.1} = 5.004 \text{ kg}$$ ## 3.5 Detailed Analytical Worked Example — Multi-Plane (Dynamic) Balancing ### Problem: Four masses $A, B, C, D$ revolve on a shaft in planes I, II, III, IV respectively. Find the magnitude and angular position of balancing masses in planes L and M located at 12 cm and 36 cm from the reference plane. Radius of all masses = 25 cm. | Mass | m (kg) | Plane Position from Ref (cm) | θ (°) | |------|--------|---------------------------|-------| | A | 10 | 0 (I) | 0 | | B | 12 | 18 (II) | 90 | | C | 16 | 30 (III) | 180 | | D | 14 | 42 (IV) | 270 | Balancing mass radii in planes L and M: $r_L = r_M = 25$ cm = 0.25 m ### Step-by-Step Solution: **Step 1:** Calculate $m_i r_i$ (constant radius: $r_i = 0.25$ m): $$A: m_A r_A = 10 \times 0.25 = 2.5 \text{ kg·m}$$ $$B: m_B r_B = 12 \times 0.25 = 3.0 \text{ kg·m}$$ $$C: m_C r_C = 16 \times 0.25 = 4.0 \text{ kg·m}$$ $$D: m_D r_D = 14 \times 0.25 = 3.5 \text{ kg·m}$$ **Step 2:** Calculate $m_i r_i L_i$ (couple moments about reference plane): $$A: m_A r_A L_A = 2.5 \times 0 = 0 \text{ kg·m·m}$$ $$B: m_B r_B L_B = 3.0 \times 0.18 = 0.54 \text{ kg·m·m}$$ $$C: m_C r_C L_C = 4.0 \times 0.30 = 1.20 \text{ kg·m·m}$$ $$D: m_D r_D L_D = 3.5 \times 0.42 = 1.47 \text{ kg·m·m}$$ **Step 3:** Resolve $m_i r_i$ into X and Y components: | Mass | $m_i r_i$ | $\theta$ | X | Y | |------|-----------|----------|---|---| | A | 2.5 | 0° | 2.500 | 0 | | B | 3.0 | 90° | 0 | 3.000 | | C | 4.0 | 180° | -4.000 | 0 | | D | 3.5 | 270° | 0 | -3.500 | **Step 4:** Force balance: $$\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 2.500 + 0 - 4.000 + 0 = -1.500$$ $$\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0 + 3.000 + 0 - 3.500 = -0.500$$ **Step 5:** Resolve $m_i r_i L_i$ into X and Y components: | Mass | $m_i r_i L_i$ | $\theta$ | X | Y | |------|---------------|----------|---|---| | A | 0 | 0° | 0 | 0 | | B | 0.54 | 90° | 0 | 0.540 | | C | 1.20 | 180° | -1.200 | 0 | | D | 1.47 | 270° | 0 | -1.470 | **Step 6:** Couple balance: $$\sum m_i r_i L_i \cos\theta_i = 0 + 0 - 1.200 + 0 = -1.200$$ $$\sum m_i r_i L_i \sin\theta_i = 0 + 0.540 + 0 - 1.470 = -0.930$$ **Step 7:** Setting up equations for balancing masses in planes L (12 cm) and M (36 cm). Let the balancing masses have $m_L r_L$ at angle $\theta_L$ and $m_M r_M$ at angle $\theta_M$. **Force equations:** $$m_L r_L \cos\theta_L + m_M r_M \cos\theta_M = -(-1.500) = 1.500 \quad \text{...(1)}$$ $$m_L r_L \sin\theta_L + m_M r_M \sin\theta_M = -(-0.500) = 0.500 \quad \text{...(2)}$$ **Couple equations** (distances from reference: $L_L = 0.12$ m, $L_M = 0.36$ m): $$0.12 \cdot m_L r_L \cos\theta_L + 0.36 \cdot m_M r_M \cos\theta_M = 1.200 \quad \text{...(3)}$$ $$0.12 \cdot m_L r_L \sin\theta_L + 0.36 \cdot m_M r_M \sin\theta_M = 0.930 \quad \text{...(4)}$$ **Step 8:** Solve the system. Let $X_L = m_L r_L \cos\theta_L$, $Y_L = m_L r_L \sin\theta_L$, $X_M = m_M r_M \cos\theta_M$, $Y_M = m_M r_M \sin\theta_M$. From (1) and (3): $$X_L + X_M = 1.500$$ $$0.12 X_L + 0.36 X_M = 1.200$$ Multiply first by 0.36 and second by 1: $$0.36 X_L + 0.36 X_M = 0.540$$ $$0.12 X_L + 0.36 X_M = 1.200$$ Subtract: $$0.24 X_L = 0.540 - 1.200 = -0.660$$ $$X_L = -2.750$$ $$X_M = 1.500 - X_L = 1.500 - (-2.750) = 4.250$$ From (2) and (4): $$Y_L + Y_M = 0.500$$ $$0.12 Y_L + 0.36 Y_M = 0.930$$ Multiply first by 0.36 and second by 1: $$0.36 Y_L + 0.36 Y_M = 0.180$$ $$0.12 Y_L + 0.36 Y_M = 0.930$$ Subtract: $$0.24 Y_L = 0.180 - 0.930 = -0.750$$ $$Y_L = -3.125$$ $$Y_M = 0.500 - Y_L = 0.500 - (-3.125) = 3.625$$ **Step 9:** Calculate magnitudes: $$m_L r_L = \sqrt{(-2.750)^2 + (-3.125)^2} = \sqrt{7.5625 + 9.7656} = \sqrt{17.3281} = 4.162 \text{ kg·m}$$ $$m_M r_M = \sqrt{(4.250)^2 + (3.625)^2} = \sqrt{18.0625 + 13.1406} = \sqrt{31.2031} = 5.586 \text{ kg·m}$$ **Step 10:** Calculate angles: For plane L: Both X and Y are negative → **Third quadrant**: $$\theta_L = 180° + \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{3.125}{2.750}\right) = 180° + 48.65° = 228.65°$$ For plane M: Both X and Y are positive → **First quadrant**: $$\theta_M = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{3.625}{4.250}\right) = 40.49°$$ **Step 11:** Calculate balancing masses (with $r_L = r_M = 0.25$ m): $$m_L = \frac{4.162}{0.25} = 16.65 \text{ kg}$$ $$m_M = \frac{5.586}{0.25} = 22.34 \text{ kg}$$ ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix Summary > [!IMPORTANT] > **Solving Multi-Plane Balancing Problems: 4-Equation Framework** | Equation Type | X-Component | Y-Component | |---------------|-------------|-------------| | **Force** | $\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$ | $\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$ | | **Couple** | $\sum m_i r_i L_i \cos\theta_i = 0$ | $\sum m_i r_i L_i \sin\theta_i = 0$ | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Concept Map: Classification of Balancing ```mermaid flowchart TD A["BALANCING OF ROTATING MASSES"]:::root B["Single Plane
STATIC BALANCING"]:::branch C["Multiple Planes
DYNAMIC BALANCING"]:::branch D["Sum of mr = 0"]:::sub E["All masses in one
transverse plane"]:::sub F["Sum of mr = 0
AND"]:::sub G["Sum of mrL = 0"]:::sub H["Masses distributed
axially along shaft"]:::sub I["Analytical
Method"]:::leaf J["Graphical
Method
(Force Polygon)"]:::leaf K["Analytical
Method"]:::leaf L["Graphical
Method
(Couple Polygon)"]:::leaf A --> B A --> C B --> D B --> E B --> I B --> J C --> F C --> G C --> H C --> K C --> L classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px classDef branch fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef sub fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#3b82f6,color:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:1px classDef leaf fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#b45309,color:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Dynamic Balancing Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["STEP 1
Define System
Masses mi
Radii ri
Angles θi
Axial distances Li"]:::stepA S2["STEP 2
Select Reference

Balancing Planes"]:::stepA S3["STEP 3
Calculate mi ri
for all masses"]:::stepB S4["STEP 4
Calculate mi ri Li
for all masses"]:::stepB S5["STEP 5
Resolve X Y
Force and
Couple Components"]:::stepC S6["STEP 6
Apply 4 Equations
Force X and Y
Couple X and Y"]:::stepD S7["STEP 7
Solve System
for Balancing
