# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define static and dynamic balancing. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Static Balancing:** A rotating system is said to be statically balanced if the center of mass of the system lies on the axis of rotation. The condition is $\sum m_i r_i = 0$ (vector sum). It eliminates the radial forces on the bearings but not the couples.
>
> **Dynamic Balancing:** A rotating system is said to be dynamically balanced if it is statically balanced AND the resultant couple of all centrifugal forces about any reference plane is zero. The conditions are:
>
> $$\sum m_i r_i = 0 \quad \text{(static balance)}$$
> $$\sum m_i r_i L_i = 0 \quad \text{(dynamic balance, couple condition)}$$
>
> A dynamically balanced system produces no resultant force OR couple on the bearings, ensuring smooth operation at all speeds.

---

### **Question 2: What is the difference between balancing of masses in the same plane and in different planes? [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Aspect | Same Plane | Different Planes |
> |:---|:---|:---|
> | **Mass distribution** | All masses in one transverse plane | Masses distributed along the shaft |
> | **Condition** | Only $\sum m_i r_i = 0$ | Both $\sum m_i r_i = 0$ AND $\sum m_i r_i L_i = 0$ |
> | **Balancing masses** | Single balancing mass | Two balancing masses in two planes |
> | **Method** | Force polygon only | Force polygon + Couple polygon |
> | **Vibration type addressed** | Static (radial) | Static + Dynamic (wobble) |
>
> Same-plane balancing is a 2-D problem; different-plane balancing is a 4-D problem (2-D force + 2-D couple).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

### **Question A (14 Marks): Multi-Plane Balancing Problem** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Question:** A shaft carries four masses $A$, $B$, $C$, and $D$ in four different planes. The details are:
>
> | Mass | Magnitude (kg) | Radius (mm) | Plane Position (mm) | Angular Position |
> |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
> | A | 20 | 80 | 0 | 0° |
> | B | 30 | 100 | 100 | 90° |
> | C | 25 | 90 | 250 | 180° |
> | D | 35 | 70 | 400 | 270° |
>
> Find the magnitudes and angular positions of balancing masses to be placed in planes (i) 500 mm and (ii) 700 mm from the left end, if their radii of rotation are 100 mm each.

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Determine the resultant force and couple, and the mass in plane at 500 mm.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $m_i r_i$ values:**

$$m_A r_A = 20 \times 80 = 1600 \text{ kg·mm at } 0°$$
$$m_B r_B = 30 \times 100 = 3000 \text{ kg·mm at } 90°$$
$$m_C r_C = 25 \times 90 = 2250 \text{ kg·mm at } 180°$$
$$m_D r_D = 35 \times 70 = 2450 \text{ kg·mm at } 270°$$

**Step 2 — Resolve into X and Y components:**

$$X_A = 1600 \cos 0° = 1600, \quad Y_A = 0$$
$$X_B = 3000 \cos 90° = 0, \quad Y_B = 3000$$
$$X_C = 2250 \cos 180° = -2250, \quad Y_C = 0$$
$$X_D = 2450 \cos 270° = 0, \quad Y_D = -2450$$

**Resultant force components:**
$$\sum X = 1600 - 2250 = -650 \text{ kg·mm}$$
$$\sum Y = 3000 - 2450 = 550 \text{ kg·mm}$$

**Magnitude of resultant force:**
$$R = \sqrt{(-650)^2 + (550)^2} = \sqrt{422500 + 302500} = \sqrt{725000} \approx 851.5 \text{ kg·mm}$$

**Angle of resultant:**
$$\theta_R = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{550}{-650}\right) = 180° - 40.24° = 139.76°$$

**Step 3 — Compute the couple about plane at 0 mm (left end):**

Couple $C_i = m_i r_i \cdot L_i$

$$C_A = 1600 \times 0 = 0$$
$$C_B = 3000 \times 100 = 300000 \text{ kg·mm}^2 \text{ at } 90°$$
$$C_C = 2250 \times 250 = 562500 \text{ kg·mm}^2 \text{ at } 180°$$
$$C_D = 2450 \times 400 = 980000 \text{ kg·mm}^2 \text{ at } 270°$$

**Components of couple:**
$$X_{C,sum} = 0 + 0 - 562500 + 0 = -562500 \text{ kg·mm}^2$$
$$Y_{C,sum} = 0 + 300000 + 0 - 980000 = -680000 \text{ kg·mm}^2$$

**Magnitude of resultant couple:**
$$C = \sqrt{(-562500)^2 + (-680000)^2} = \sqrt{3.164 \times 10^{11} + 4.624 \times 10^{11}} = \sqrt{7.788 \times 10^{11}}$$
$$C \approx 882,500 \text{ kg·mm}^2$$

**Angle of resultant couple:**
$$\theta_C = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-680000}{-562500}\right) = 180° + 50.4° = 230.4°$$

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Calculate the balancing masses in planes at 500 mm and 700 mm.**

**Model Solution:**

**Distance between the two balancing planes:**
$$L = 700 - 500 = 200 \text{ mm}$$

**Balancing mass-radius product at plane 1 (500 mm from left end):**
$$m_1 r_1 = R = 851.5 \text{ kg·mm}$$

$$m_1 = \frac{851.5}{100} = 8.515 \text{ kg}$$

**Angle of $m_1$:**
$$\theta_1 = \theta_R + 180° = 139.76° + 180° = 319.76°$$

**Balancing mass-radius product at plane 2 (700 mm from left end):**
$$m_2 r_2 = \frac{C}{L} = \frac{882500}{200} = 4412.5 \text{ kg·mm}$$

$$m_2 = \frac{4412.5}{100} = 44.125 \text{ kg}$$

**Angle of $m_2$:**
$$\theta_2 = \theta_C + 180° = 230.4° + 180° = 410.4° \equiv 50.4°$$

> **Final Answer:**
> - **Plane at 500 mm:** Mass = **8.515 kg** at angle **319.76°** (or equivalently 360° − 40.24°)
> - **Plane at 700 mm:** Mass = **44.125 kg** at angle **50.4°**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

> **Question:** Three masses of 10 kg, 18 kg, and 25 kg are attached to a shaft at radii 120 mm, 100 mm, and 80 mm respectively. The angular positions of the masses, measured from a certain reference, are 60°, 150°, and 240° respectively. The axial positions of the masses are at 0, 150 mm, and 300 mm.
>
> **(a)** Find the magnitude and position of the balancing mass to be placed in the plane of the 18 kg mass, if the shaft is to be statically balanced. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** If the shaft is also to be dynamically balanced, find the magnitudes and positions of two balancing masses placed at axial positions 100 mm and 400 mm. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — Static Balance Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $m_i r_i$ vectors:**

$$m_1 r_1 = 10 \times 120 = 1200 \text{ kg·mm at } 60°$$
$$m_2 r_2 = 18 \times 100 = 1800 \text{ kg·mm at } 150°$$
$$m_3 r_3 = 25 \times 80 = 2000 \text{ kg·mm at } 240°$$

**Step 2 — Components:**

$$X_1 = 1200 \cos 60° = 600, \quad Y_1 = 1200 \sin 60° = 1039.2$$
$$X_2 = 1800 \cos 150° = -1558.8, \quad Y_2 = 1800 \sin 150° = 900$$
$$X_3 = 2000 \cos 240° = -1000, \quad Y_3 = 2000 \sin 240° = -1732.1$$

**Resultant of original masses:**
$$\sum X = 600 - 1558.8 - 1000 = -1958.8 \text{ kg·mm}$$
$$\sum Y = 1039.2 + 900 - 1732.1 = 207.1 \text{ kg·mm}$$

**Magnitude:**
$$R = \sqrt{(-1958.8)^2 + (207.1)^2} = \sqrt{3836900 + 42890} = \sqrt{3879790} \approx 1969.7 \text{ kg·mm}$$

**Angle:**
$$\theta_R = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{207.1}{-1958.8}\right) = 180° - 6.04° = 173.96°$$

**Step 3 — Balancing mass in plane of $m_2$ (at 150 mm):**

For static balance, the balancing mass-radius product must be **equal and opposite** to $R$:

$$m_b r_b = 1969.7 \text{ kg·mm}$$

Given that the balancing mass is in the plane of $m_2$ (at 150 mm), we can choose a suitable radius. If $r_b = 120$ mm:

$$m_b = \frac{1969.7}{120} = 16.41 \text{ kg}$$

**Angle of $m_b$:**
$$\theta_b = 173.96° + 180° = 353.96° \equiv -6.04° \text{ (or } 354°\text{)}$$

#### **Part (b) — Dynamic Balance Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the couple about plane 1 (0 mm):**

$$C_1 = m_1 r_1 L_1 = 1200 \times 0 = 0$$
$$C_2 = 1800 \times 150 = 270000 \text{ kg·mm}^2 \text{ at } 150°$$
$$C_3 = 2000 \times 300 = 600000 \text{ kg·mm}^2 \text{ at } 240°$$

**Components of couple:**
$$X_{C,sum} = 0 - 233827 + (-300000) = -533827$$
$$Y_{C,sum} = 0 + 135000 - 519615 = -384615$$

**Note:** $C_2 X = 1800 \cos 150° \times 150 = -1558.8 \times 150 = -233827$
and $C_2 Y = 1800 \sin 150° \times 150 = 900 \times 150 = 135000$

For $C_3$: $X = 2000 \cos 240° \times 300 = -1000 \times 300 = -300000$ and $Y = 2000 \sin 240° \times 300 = -1732.1 \times 300 = -519615$

**Magnitude of couple:**
$$C = \sqrt{(-533827)^2 + (-384615)^2} = \sqrt{2.85 \times 10^{11} + 1.479 \times 10^{11}} = \sqrt{4.329 \times 10^{11}} \approx 658,000 \text{ kg·mm}^2$$

**Angle:**
$$\theta_C = 180° + \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{384615}{533827}\right) = 180° + 35.78° = 215.78°$$

**Step 2 — Compute balancing masses in planes at 100 mm and 400 mm:**

Distance:
$$L = 400 - 100 = 300 \text{ mm}$$

The two balancing masses must satisfy:
$$m_1 r_1 + m_2 r_2 = R \text{ (vector sum)}$$
$$m_1 r_1 \cdot 100 + m_2 r_2 \cdot 400 = C \text{ (vector sum about reference)}$$

**Solving (using the method of equivalent transfer):**

The balancing mass-radius product at plane 1 (100 mm):
$$m_1 r_1 = R \cdot \frac{L - L_1}{L} \text{ ... (varies by problem formulation)}$$

A cleaner approach: The system of masses $m_1$ at 100 mm and $m_2$ at 400 mm must be equivalent to a force $R$ at any reference plane and a couple $C$ at the same plane.

If we place $m_1$ at 100 mm and $m_2$ at 400 mm, the equivalent system at the 0 mm reference plane is:
- Force: $m_1 r_1 + m_2 r_2 = R$ (vector equation)
- Couple: $100 \cdot m_1 r_1 + 400 \cdot m_2 r_2 = C$ (vector equation)

These are two vector equations = 4 scalar equations. We can solve for the two unknown vectors $m_1 r_1$ and $m_2 r_2$.

**From the force equation:** $m_2 r_2 = R - m_1 r_1$

**Substituting into the couple equation:**
$$100 \cdot m_1 r_1 + 400 \cdot (R - m_1 r_1) = C$$
$$-300 \cdot m_1 r_1 + 400 R = C$$
$$m_1 r_1 = \frac{400 R - C}{300}$$

**Computing (using complex numbers with X and Y components):**

$400 R$ components: $400 \times (-1958.8) = -783520$ and $400 \times 207.1 = 82840$

$400 R - C$ components: $-783520 - (-533827) = -249693$ and $82840 - (-384615) = 467455$

$$m_1 r_1 = \frac{-249693 - j \cdot 467455}{300} = -832.3 + j \cdot 1558.2 \text{ kg·mm}$$

**Magnitude of $m_1 r_1$:**
$$|m_1 r_1| = \sqrt{(-832.3)^2 + (1558.2)^2} = \sqrt{692724 + 2427988} = \sqrt{3120712} \approx 1766.6 \text{ kg·mm}$$

**Angle:** $\theta_1 = 180° - \tan^{-1}(1558.2 / 832.3) = 180° - 61.88° = 118.12°$

**Mass:** $m_1 = 1766.6 / 100 = 17.67$ kg (taking $r_1 = 100$ mm)

**For $m_2 r_2$:** $m_2 r_2 = R - m_1 r_1 = (-1958.8 - (-832.3)) + j(207.1 - 1558.2) = -1126.5 - j \cdot 1351.1$

**Magnitude:** $|m_2 r_2| = \sqrt{1269005 + 1825471} = \sqrt{3094476} \approx 1759.1$ kg·mm

**Angle:** $\theta_2 = 180° + \tan^{-1}(1351.1 / 1126.5) = 180° + 50.18° = 230.18°$

**Mass:** $m_2 = 1759.1 / 100 = 17.59$ kg (taking $r_2 = 100$ mm)

> **Final Answer:**
> - **Plane at 100 mm:** $m_1 = 17.67$ kg at angle $118.12°$
> - **Plane at 400 mm:** $m_2 = 17.59$ kg at angle $230.18°$

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to add 180° to the balancing mass angle.** The balancing mass is always on the **opposite side** of the resultant. Many students compute $\theta_R$ correctly but forget to add 180°, leading to a WRONG direction and full mark deduction.
>
> 2. **Mixing up signs when computing couple components.** $C_i = m_i r_i \cdot L_i$ — the $L_i$ multiplies both X and Y components. A sign error in $L_i$ (e.g., using a negative distance incorrectly) cascades through the entire problem.
>
> 3. **Using $\omega$ or $\omega^2$ in the balance equations.** Since $\omega$ is common to ALL masses, it cancels out. Use $m_i r_i$ (NOT $m_i r_i \omega^2$) for polygon construction.
>
> 4. **Choosing the wrong reference plane for the couple calculation.** The couple is **not** a free vector — its value depends on the chosen reference plane. Always state the reference plane explicitly.
>
> 5. **Not verifying the force polygon closure.** A common check: if $\sum X \neq 0$ and $\sum Y \neq 0$ after balancing, there's a calculation error.
>
> 6. **Confusing angle conventions.** KTU expects angles measured counter-clockwise from the positive X-axis (standard math convention). Some textbooks use clockwise; stick to the KTU standard.
>
> 7. **Forgetting units.** Distances must be consistent: if $m_i r_i$ is in kg·mm and $L_i$ is in mm, then $C$ is in kg·mm². Be consistent throughout.
>
> 8. **Skipping the verification step.** Always verify: $\sum m_i r_i = 0$ AND $\sum m_i r_i L_i = 0$ after adding balancing masses. This earns "methodology" marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Multi-Plane Balancing:**

- [x] **Static balance** = $\sum m_i r_i = 0$ (force polygon closes)
- [x] **Dynamic balance** = $\sum m_i r_i = 0$ **AND** $\sum m_i r_i L_i = 0$ (force AND couple polygon close)
- [x] Centrifugal force = $m_i r_i \omega^2$, but $\omega^2$ cancels out in balance equations
- [x] Use $m_i r_i$ vectors (NOT forces) for polygon construction
- [x] Angular velocity: $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ rad/s
- [x] Couple about reference plane = $m_i r_i \cdot L_i$ (where $L_i$ is the axial distance from the reference)
- [x] Balancing mass is always on the **opposite side** of the resultant (add 180° to angle)
- [x] **Two balancing masses** are needed for multi-plane balancing, placed in two reference planes
- [x] Distance between balancing planes ($L$) directly affects the balancing mass calculation
- [x] Choose balancing radii to keep masses reasonable (typically same as existing radii)
- [x] **Verification:** After adding balancing masses, both the force sum AND the couple sum must be zero
- [x] **Engineer’s rule:** A statically balanced rotor can still wobble; a dynamically balanced rotor runs smoothly at any speed
- [x] **Real-world applications:** Crankshafts, turbogenerators, helicopter rotors, marine propellers, high-speed turbines
- [x] **Graphical method:** Construct the force polygon and couple polygon on the same diagram; both must close
- [x] **Analytical method:** Resolve all vectors into X and Y components, sum separately, then compute resultant magnitudes and angles using Pythagoras and $\tan^{-1}$
- [x] **Quadrant check:** Always verify the sign of X and Y components to determine the correct quadrant for the angle
- [x] **Common KTU marks-distribution pattern:** 3-mark questions test definitions; 14-mark questions require complete numerical solutions with diagrams

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule for KTU Exams:** Always draw the polygon or at least state the magnitude and direction of all vectors. Examiners award "methodology" marks for clear, step-by-step presentation even if a final numerical answer has a minor arithmetic error.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analytical and/or Graphical Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT527 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analytical and/or Graphical Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Module 2 – Dynamics of Machinery**, the topic *"Analytical and/or Graphical Methods"* refers to the two principal techniques employed in **cam profile synthesis** — the design of the working surface of a cam so that a follower executes a prescribed motion law as the cam rotates at constant angular velocity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition – Analytical Method**
> The *analytical method* of cam profile synthesis is the **mathematical procedure** in which the follower's displacement $y(\theta)$, velocity $v(\theta)$, and acceleration $a(\theta)$ are expressed as **continuous, differentiable functions of the cam rotation angle $\theta$**. The cam profile coordinates $(x_c, y_c)$ are then computed analytically using these functions, eliminating the need for physical construction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition – Graphical Method**
> The *graphical method* is a **geometric construction technique** in which the cam pitch curve (and hence the cam profile) is drawn on paper using straight-edges, dividers, and protractors. The displacement values $y(\theta)$ are first plotted against $\theta$ on a **displacement diagram**, and then projected onto the base circle of the cam to generate the pitch curve point-by-point.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Understanding

Imagine you are designing a **roller-coaster track**. You want the car to climb a hill smoothly, pause at the top, and descend gently.

- **Analytical method** is like giving the *mathematical equation of the hill*: $y(x) = \dfrac{h}{2}\left(1 - \cos \dfrac{\pi x}{L}\right)$. The hill's shape is *computed* precisely from the formula — fast, accurate, and reproducible.
- **Graphical method** is like a sculptor *drawing the hill freehand* on a piece of paper using rulers and protractors, dividing the height into 8 equal steps, then projecting those heights radially outward to mark the hill's contour — slower, but visually intuitive and excellent for visualization.

In a cam mechanism, the *hill* is replaced by the **cam pitch curve**, and the *coaster car* is replaced by the **follower** that rides on the cam.

## 1.3 Why Both Methods?

> [!NOTE]
> - **Analytical methods** dominate in modern **CAD/CAM software** (e.g., Mastercam, SolidWorks Motion). They allow precision, parametric optimization, and direct CNC tool-path generation.
> - **Graphical methods** are still taught in KTU because they build *engineering intuition*. They illustrate the relationship between the **displacement diagram** and the **cam profile geometry**, making concepts like *pressure angle* and *radius of curvature* visually obvious.
> - KTU examiners frequently pose questions requiring *both* — students must derive the analytical equations **and** sketch the construction procedure.

## 1.4 Key Notation Used Throughout This Module

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|:------:|:--------|:------------:|
| $r_0$ | Base-circle radius | mm |
| $r_p$ | Prime-circle radius | mm |
| $h$ | Maximum lift (stroke) of follower | mm |
| $\beta$ | Cam rotation angle (variable) | rad or deg |
| $\beta_0$ | Outflow (rise or return) angular interval | rad or deg |
| $\omega$ | Angular velocity of cam | rad/s |
| $R$ | Roller radius (if roller follower) | mm |
| $e$ | Offset of follower axis | mm |
| $\alpha$ | Pressure angle | deg |
| $y, v, a$ | Displacement, velocity, acceleration of follower | mm, mm/s, mm/s² |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cam-Follower System — Terminology Inventory

A **cam** is a rotating or sliding member having an irregular shape (a *profile*) which imparts a prescribed motion to another member called the **follower**. The fundamental terminology — appearing repeatedly in KTU exam answers — is tabulated below.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Terminology Box** — These terms are **must-know** for any 3-mark or 14-mark question.

- **Cam Profile** — The actual working surface in contact with the follower.
- **Pitch Curve** — The path traced by a point on the follower (the *trace point*) relative to the cam, assumed as a knife-edge follower. It is the *ideal* theoretical curve.
- **Prime Circle** — The smallest circle drawn tangent to the pitch curve.
- **Base Circle** — The smallest circle drawn tangent to the cam profile. For a knife-edge follower, base circle = prime circle.
- **Trace Point** — A reference point on the follower whose motion defines the pitch curve (e.g., the tip of a knife-edge follower, or the centre of a roller).
- **Pressure Angle ($\alpha$)** — The angle between the direction of motion of the follower and the *normal* to the pitch curve at the point of contact. It governs the side-thrust on the follower.
- **Angle of Ascent (Outflow Angle, $\beta_0$)** — The angular displacement of the cam during which the follower *rises* through the full lift $h$.
- **Dwell** — A period during which the follower remains stationary while the cam continues to rotate.

## 2.2 Classification of Followers (Examination-Relevant)

Followers are classified by:
1. **Motion type** — Translating (sliding) or Oscillating (pivoting).
2. **Follower axis orientation** — Radial, Offset, or In-line.
3. **Follower contact shape** — **Knife-edge**, **Roller**, **Flat-faced**, **Spherical (mushroom)**.

The **roller follower** is the most common industrial design because rolling contact drastically reduces wear compared to sliding contact.

## 2.3 Standard Follower Motion Laws (KTU Repeatedly Asked)

KTU questions almost always involve one of the four classical motion laws below. Each law specifies how the lift $h$ is distributed over the angular outflow $\beta_0$.

### Law 1 — Uniform Velocity (UV)

The follower lifts with constant velocity. Although simple, it causes **infinite acceleration at the start and end of the stroke** — unacceptable for high-speed machines.

### Law 2 — Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)

The follower's acceleration is sinusoidal. Produces smooth motion, but **high accelerations in the middle** of the stroke.

### Law 3 — Uniform Acceleration and Retardation (UAR)

Acceleration is **constant (positive)** during the first half of the stroke and **constant (negative)** during the second half. Causes a sudden **jerk** (discontinuity in acceleration) at the mid-point — must be avoided at high speeds.

### Law 4 — Cycloidal Motion

Both velocity and acceleration are smooth, with **zero jerk at both ends** of the stroke. Considered the **best motion law** for high-speed cams.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **single most important resource** for solving Module 2 cam problems in the KTU 2024 University Examination. **Memorize the displacement equations**; derive velocity and acceleration by differentiation.

| Motion Law | Displacement $y(\theta)$ | Velocity $v = \omega\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}$ | Acceleration $a = \omega^{2}\dfrac{d^{2}y}{d\theta^{2}}$ | Max Acceleration |
|:----------:|:------------------------:|:------------------------------------------:|:--------------------------------------------------------:|:----------------:|
| **Uniform Velocity** | $y = \dfrac{h}{\beta_0}\,\theta$ | $v = \dfrac{h\omega}{\beta_0}$ (const.) | $a = 0$ (interior); infinite at start/end | $\infty$ (impractical) |
| **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)** | $y = \dfrac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta_0}\right)\right]$ | $v = \dfrac{h\pi\omega}{2\beta_0}\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta_0}\right)$ | $a = \dfrac{h\pi^{2}\omega^{2}}{2\beta_0^{2}}\cos\left(\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta_0}\right)$ | $\dfrac{h\pi^{2}\omega^{2}}{2\beta_0^{2}} \approx 4.93\,\dfrac{h\omega^{2}}{\beta_0^{2}}$ |
| **Uniform Accel. & Retard. (UAR)** — Rise half ($0 \le \theta \le \beta_0/2$) | $y = \dfrac{2h}{\beta_0^{2}}\,\theta^{2}$ | $v = \dfrac{4h\omega}{\beta_0^{2}}\,\theta$ | $a = \dfrac{4h\omega^{2}}{\beta_0^{2}}$ (constant) | $\dfrac{4h\omega^{2}}{\beta_0^{2}}$ |
| **Uniform Accel. & Retard. (UAR)** — Return half ($\beta_0/2 \le \theta \le \beta_0$) | $y = h - \dfrac{2h}{\beta_0^{2}}(\beta_0-\theta)^{2}$ | $v = \dfrac{4h\omega}{\beta_0^{2}}(\beta_0-\theta)$ | $a = -\dfrac{4h\omega^{2}}{\beta_0^{2}}$ (constant) | $\dfrac{4h\omega^{2}}{\beta_0^{2}}$ |
| **Cycloidal Motion** | $y = h\left[\dfrac{\theta}{\beta_0} - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sin\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_0}\right)\right]$ | $v = \dfrac{h\omega}{\beta_0}\left[1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_0}\right)\right]$ | $a = \dfrac{2\pi h\omega^{2}}{\beta_0^{2}}\sin\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_0}\right)$ | $\dfrac{2\pi h\omega^{2}}{\beta_0^{2}} \approx 6.28\,\dfrac{h\omega^{2}}{\beta_0^{2}}$ |

### 2.5 Critical Pressure-Angle Formulas

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Pressure angle is the **second most-asked topic** in KTU DoM Module 2. It determines whether a cam can be driven at the specified speed without jamming.

For a **radial translating follower** (no offset, $e = 0$):

$$
\tan \alpha \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}}{r_0 + y}
$$

For an **offset translating follower** (offset distance $e$):

$$
\tan \alpha \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{dy}{d\theta} - e}{r_0 + y}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Limit** — The maximum permissible pressure angle for **translating followers** is approximately $\mathbf{30^{\circ}}$. Beyond this, side-thrust and wear become unacceptable. For **oscillating followers**, the limit is relaxed to $\mathbf{35^{\circ}}$–$\mathbf{40^{\circ}}$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive engine camshafts** — Analytical cam profiles (often *polynomial* or *cycloidal* based) dictate valve timing in IC engines, directly affecting fuel economy and emissions.
- **Printing and packaging machinery** — Cam-driven cutters must reach **uniform velocity** during the dwell to produce clean cuts.
- **Textile machinery (looms)** — Cams actuate the shuttle release and beating-up mechanisms with **SHM-type motion** for smooth yarn handling.
- **Robotic actuators** — Modern cam design uses analytical synthesis embedded in CAD software (SolidWorks, CATIA) for high-precision cam profiles.

## 2.7 Graphical Method — Step-by-Step Construction (Procedure)

For a knife-edge follower executing **SHM rise** over outflow angle $\beta_0$:

1. Draw the **base circle** of radius $r_0$ with centre $O$.
2. Draw the **prim circle** (here coincident with the base circle for a knife-edge follower).
3. Mark the **starting point** $A$ on the base circle and the **end point** $B$ after rotating the cam through the outflow angle $\beta_0$.
4. Draw a line representing the **lift $h$** vertically to the right of $A$. Divide it into (typically) **8 equal parts** $P_1, P_2, \dots, P_8$.
5. Draw a **semi-circle** on $h/2$ as diameter; project horizontally from $P_1, \dots, P_8$ onto the semi-circle to obtain the SHM displacement ordinates $y_1, y_2, \dots, y_8$.
6. Divide the arc $AB$ (of angle $\beta_0$) into **8 equal angular parts** $A_1, A_2, \dots, A_8$.
7. From $A_1, A_2, \dots, A_8$ on the arc, draw **radii**; from the corresponding points $y_1, y_2, \dots, y_8$ on the lift line, draw **horizontal lines**.
8. Their **intersections** are points on the **pitch curve**. Join them with a smooth curve.
9. For a **roller follower**, draw roller circles of radius $R$ at each pitch-curve point and trace their envelope — this gives the actual **cam profile**.

This construction procedure is what KTU examiners expect in any "graphical method" sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Convention** — In a 14-mark derivation question, students are expected to start from first principles (kinematic equations), arrive at the displacement function, and then differentiate to get velocity and acceleration. **Every step must be justified.** Skipping integration/differentiation steps costs marks.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) Displacement Law

### Statement of the Problem
A knife-edge follower is to rise through a total lift $h$ over an angular interval $\beta_0$ of cam rotation, following **simple harmonic motion**. Starting from the SHM projection principle, derive expressions for $y$, $v$, and $a$.

### Geometric Premise
In SHM, the displacement $y$ is the **projection on the diameter** of a point moving on a **circle** of diameter $h$ with uniform angular velocity $\pi\omega / \beta_0$ (in units compatible with $\theta$).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let the auxiliary point move on a circle of diameter $h$ centred at $C$, with the leftmost position as the starting reference. Let the angle swept by this point be $\phi$. Then by SHM projection:

$$
y \;=\; \frac{h}{2} - \frac{h}{2}\cos\phi
$$

This simplifies to:

$$
y \;=\; \frac{h}{2}\left(1 - \cos\phi\right)
$$

The cam's rotation angle $\theta$ and the auxiliary angle $\phi$ are linked by proportionality. When $\theta = \beta_0$, the auxiliary point completes half a circle, so $\phi = \pi$:

$$
\frac{\theta}{\beta_0} \;=\; \frac{\phi}{\pi} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \phi \;=\; \frac{\pi\theta}{\beta_0}
$$

Substituting $\phi$:

$$
y \;=\; \frac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\beta_0}\right)\right]
$$

**Velocity** — Differentiate $y$ with respect to time $t$. Note $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega$:

$$
v \;=\; \frac{dy}{dt} \;=\; \frac{dy}{d\theta}\cdot\frac{d\theta}{dt} \;=\; \omega\,\frac{dy}{d\theta}
$$

First compute $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}$:

$$
\frac{dy}{d\theta} \;=\; \frac{h}{2}\cdot\left(\frac{\pi}{\beta_0}\right)\cdot\sin\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\beta_0}\right) \;=\; \frac{h\pi}{2\beta_0}\sin\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\beta_0}\right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
v \;=\; \frac{h\pi\omega}{2\beta_0}\sin\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\beta_0}\right)
$$

**Acceleration** — Differentiate once more:

$$
a \;=\; \omega\,\frac{dv}{d\theta} \;=\; \omega^{2}\,\frac{d^{2}y}{d\theta^{2}}
$$

Compute the second derivative:

$$
\frac{d^{2}y}{d\theta^{2}} \;=\; \frac{h}{2}\cdot\left(\frac{\pi}{\beta_0}\right)^{2}\cdot\cos\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\beta_0}\right) \;=\; \frac{h\pi^{2}}{2\beta_0^{2}}\cos\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\beta_0}\right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
a \;=\; \frac{h\pi^{2}\omega^{2}}{2\beta_0^{2}}\cos\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\beta_0}\right)
$$

### Verification (Boundary Conditions)

At $\theta = 0$ (start of rise):
- $y = \dfrac{h}{2}(1 - 1) = 0$ ✓
- $v = 0$ ✓ (smooth start)
- $a = \dfrac{h\pi^{2}\omega^{2}}{2\beta_0^{2}}$ — finite maximum acceleration

At $\theta = \beta_0$ (end of rise):
- $y = \dfrac{h}{2}(1 - (-1)) = h$ ✓
- $v = 0$ ✓ (smooth finish)
- $a = -\dfrac{h\pi^{2}\omega^{2}}{2\beta_0^{2}}$ — symmetric about mid-stroke

At $\theta = \beta_0/2$ (mid-stroke):
- $y = h/2$, $v = \dfrac{h\pi\omega}{2\beta_0}$ (max velocity), $a = 0$ (zero acceleration) ✓

All boundary conditions are satisfied — the derivation is **complete and correct**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Uniform Acceleration and Retardation (UAR) Law

### Statement
A follower rises through lift $h$ over an outflow angle $\beta_0$, with **constant positive acceleration $a_1$ during the first half** ($\beta_0/2$) and **constant negative acceleration $a_2$ during the second half** ($-\beta_0/2$). The magnitudes of $a_1$ and $a_2$ are equal so that the velocity is **continuous at the mid-stroke** (otherwise the follower would experience an infinite jerk).

### Phase 1 — Acceleration Phase ($0 \le \theta \le \beta_0/2$)

The acceleration $a_1$ is constant. From kinematics, with $\theta = \omega t$ (so $t = \theta/\omega$):

$$
a_1 \;=\; \omega^{2}\,\frac{d^{2}y}{d\theta^{2}} \;=\; \text{constant}
$$

Integrate once with respect to $\theta$ (with initial velocity zero at $\theta = 0$):

$$
\omega^{2}\,\frac{dy}{d\theta} \;=\; a_1\,\theta + C_1
$$

At $\theta = 0$, $v = 0$ so $C_1 = 0$. Therefore:

$$
\frac{dy}{d\theta} \;=\; \frac{a_1}{\omega^{2}}\,\theta
$$

Integrate again (with $y = 0$ at $\theta = 0$):

$$
y \;=\; \frac{a_1}{2\omega^{2}}\,\theta^{2} + C_2
$$

$C_2 = 0$. At the **mid-stroke**, $\theta = \beta_0/2$ and $y = h/2$:

$$
\frac{h}{2} \;=\; \frac{a_1}{2\omega^{2}}\left(\frac{\beta_0}{2}\right)^{2} \;=\; \frac{a_1\beta_0^{2}}{8\omega^{2}}
$$

Solve for $a_1$:

$$
a_1 \;=\; \frac{4h\omega^{2}}{\beta_0^{2}}
$$

Substitute back into $y$ and $v$:

$$
y \;=\; \frac{4h\omega^{2}}{2\omega^{2}\beta_0^{2}}\,\theta^{2} \;=\; \frac{2h}{\beta_0^{2}}\,\theta^{2}
$$

$$
v \;=\; \omega\,\frac{dy}{d\theta} \;=\; \frac{4h\omega}{\beta_0^{2}}\,\theta
$$

### Phase 2 — Retardation Phase ($\beta_0/2 \le \theta \le \beta_0$)

By symmetry, the deceleration magnitude is the same: $a_2 = -a_1 = -\dfrac{4h\omega^{2}}{\beta_0^{2}}$. Working in the shifted variable $\theta' = \beta_0 - \theta$:

$$
y \;=\; h - \frac{2h}{\beta_0^{2}}(\beta_0-\theta)^{2}
$$

$$
v \;=\; \frac{4h\omega}{\beta_0^{2}}(\beta_0-\theta)
$$

### Continuity Check at $\theta = \beta_0/2$
- $y$ (Phase 1) = $\dfrac{2h}{\beta_0^{2}}\cdot\dfrac{\beta_0^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{h}{2}$ ✓
- $y$ (Phase 2) = $h - \dfrac{2h}{\beta_0^{2}}\cdot\dfrac{\beta_0^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{h}{2}$ ✓
- $v$ (Phase 1) = $\dfrac{4h\omega}{\beta_0^{2}}\cdot\dfrac{\beta_0}{2} = \dfrac{2h\omega}{\beta_0}$
- $v$ (Phase 2) = $\dfrac{4h\omega}{\beta_0^{2}}\cdot\dfrac{\beta_0}{2} = \dfrac{2h\omega}{\beta_0}$ ✓

Velocity is continuous at mid-stroke — the derivation is consistent.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cycloidal Motion Law

### Statement
Cycloidal motion is the **smoothest standard motion law** for high-speed cams. Its defining feature is that the **acceleration is sinusoidal and the jerk (rate of change of acceleration) is zero at both ends** of the stroke.

### Premise
The displacement $y$ can be constructed as the **sum of a uniform velocity term and a sinusoidal correction term** chosen so that $y$, $v$, and $a$ all vanish at $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \beta_0$.

Assume:

$$
y \;=\; A\theta + B\sin\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_0}\right) + C
$$

### Boundary Conditions
At $\theta = 0$: $y = 0 \Rightarrow C = 0$ (since $\sin 0 = 0$).

At $\theta = 0$: $v = 0$. Differentiate:

$$
\frac{dy}{d\theta} \;=\; A + B\cdot\frac{2\pi}{\beta_0}\cos\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_0}\right)
$$

At $\theta = 0$, $\cos 0 = 1$, so:

$$
0 \;=\; A + \frac{2\pi B}{\beta_0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \;=\; -\frac{2\pi B}{\beta_0}
$$

At $\theta = \beta_0$: $y = h$. Use $\sin(2\pi) = 0$:

$$
h \;=\; A\beta_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \;=\; \frac{h}{\beta_0}
$$

Equating the two expressions for $A$:

$$
\frac{h}{\beta_0} \;=\; -\frac{2\pi B}{\beta_0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad B \;=\; -\frac{h}{2\pi}
$$

### Final Displacement Equation

$$
y \;=\; \frac{h\theta}{\beta_0} - \frac{h}{2\pi}\sin\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_0}\right)
$$

$$
y \;=\; h\left[\frac{\theta}{\beta_0} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_0}\right)\right]
$$

### Velocity and Acceleration

$$
v \;=\; \omega\,\frac{dy}{d\theta} \;=\; \frac{h\omega}{\beta_0}\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_0}\right)\right]
$$

$$
a \;=\; \omega^{2}\,\frac{d^{2}y}{d\theta^{2}} \;=\; \frac{2\pi h\omega^{2}}{\beta_0^{2}}\sin\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_0}\right)
$$

### Verification
- At $\theta = 0$: $y = 0$, $v = 0$ (since $\cos 0 = 1$), $a = 0$ ✓
- At $\theta = \beta_0$: $y = h$, $v = 0$ (since $\cos 2\pi = 1$), $a = 0$ ✓
- **Jerk** $j = \dfrac{da}{dt} = \omega^{3}\dfrac{d^{3}y}{d\theta^{3}} = \dfrac{4\pi^{2}h\omega^{3}}{\beta_0^{3}}\cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_0}\right)$ — equals $j_{max}$ at $\theta = 0$ but is **continuous** (no discontinuity).

This confirms cycloidal motion has **finite, continuous jerk** — making it ideal for high-speed cam applications.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Pressure Angle for a Radial Translating Follower

### Setup
Consider a disc cam rotating anticlockwise about centre $O$ with a radial translating follower at distance $r_0$ (base circle radius) above $O$. Let the trace point be at $P$, displaced by $y$ above the prime circle.

At any instant, the follower translates vertically upward with velocity $v = dy/dt = \omega\,dy/d\theta$. The cam surface exerts a force $F$ on the follower along the common normal $N$ at the contact point. The **pressure angle $\alpha$** is the angle between the direction of motion of the follower (vertical) and this normal $N$.

### Force Triangle
Let $F_n$ = the normal force exerted by the cam. It has two components:
- Tangential to follower motion: $F_t = F_n \cos\alpha$ — this drives the follower.
- Perpendicular (side-thrust on follower guide): $F_s = F_n \sin\alpha$ — undesirable.

The driving force must overcome the load $W$ on the follower, so $F_t = W$. Therefore:

$$
F_n\cos\alpha \;=\; W \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_n \;=\; \frac{W}{\cos\alpha}
$$

The **side-thrust** is:

$$
F_s \;=\; W\tan\alpha
$$

This shows **why $\alpha$ must be minimised** — it directly multiplies the side-load on the follower guide.

### Geometry — Finding $\tan\alpha$ in Terms of $y$ and $dy/d\theta$

Draw the radial line from $O$ to the trace point $P$ at the moment of interest. The horizontal distance from $O$ to the follower axis is $r_0 + y$ (since the trace point has moved up by $y$). The horizontal velocity component (in the rotating frame of the cam) of the trace point due to follower translation alone is:

$$
v_x \;=\; v \;=\; \omega\,\frac{dy}{d\theta}
$$

The slope of the pitch curve at $P$ is therefore $v_x$ (vertical) divided by the cam's rotation rate $\omega$ (horizontal), i.e., $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}$. The normal to the pitch curve makes angle $\alpha$ with the radial line at $P$, and the well-known geometry gives:

$$
\tan\alpha \;=\; \frac{\text{Vertical velocity of trace point}}{\text{Horizontal distance to centre}} \;=\; \frac{\omega\,\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}}{r_0 + y}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\tan\alpha \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}}{r_0 + y}
$$

For an **offset follower** (offset distance $e$ measured perpendicular to the follower axis), the geometric analysis yields:

$$
\tan\alpha \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{dy}{d\theta} - e}{r_0 + y}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU tip — In a numerical problem, find $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}$ by differentiating the displacement law, then plug into the pressure-angle formula. The **maximum pressure angle occurs at the steepest portion of the displacement curve**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Finding Maximum Pressure Angle

**Problem:** A radial translating knife-edge follower moves with SHM, rising through $h = 40$ mm in an outflow angle $\beta_0 = 60^{\circ}$. The base circle radius is $r_0 = 30$ mm. The cam rotates at $N = 300$ rpm. Determine the **maximum velocity and acceleration of the follower** and the **maximum pressure angle**.

**Given:** $h = 40$ mm, $\beta_0 = 60^{\circ} = \pi/3$ rad, $r_0 = 30$ mm, $N = 300$ rpm.

**Solution:**

Angular velocity $\omega$:

$$
\omega \;=\; \frac{2\pi N}{60} \;=\; \frac{2\pi(300)}{60} \;=\; 10\pi \text{ rad/s} \;\approx\; 31.416 \text{ rad/s}
$$

From the SHM formulas:

$$
v_{max} \;=\; \frac{h\pi\omega}{2\beta_0} \;=\; \frac{40 \times \pi \times 10\pi}{2 \times (\pi/3)} \;=\; \frac{400\pi^{2}}{2\pi/3} \;=\; \frac{400\pi^{2}\cdot 3}{2\pi} \;=\; 600\pi \text{ mm/s}
$$

$$
v_{max} \;\approx\; 600 \times 3.14159 \;\approx\; 1884.96 \text{ mm/s} \;\approx\; 1.885 \text{ m/s}
$$

Maximum acceleration (at $\theta = 0$ or $\theta = \beta_0$):

$$
a_{max} \;=\; \frac{h\pi^{2}\omega^{2}}{2\beta_0^{2}} \;=\; \frac{40 \times \pi^{2} \times (10\pi)^{2}}{2 \times (\pi/3)^{2}}
$$

Compute step by step:

$$
a_{max} \;=\; \frac{40 \times \pi^{2} \times 100\pi^{2}}{2 \times \pi^{2}/9} \;=\; \frac{4000\pi^{4}}{2\pi^{2}/9} \;=\; \frac{4000\pi^{4} \cdot 9}{2\pi^{2}} \;=\; 18000\pi^{2}
$$

$$
a_{max} \;\approx\; 18000 \times 9.8696 \;\approx\; 177{,}652 \text{ mm/s}^{2} \;\approx\; 177.65 \text{ m/s}^{2}
$$

For maximum pressure angle, evaluate $\tan\alpha_{max} = \dfrac{dy/d\theta}{r_0 + y}$ at the steepest point.

For SHM, the function $\dfrac{dy/d\theta}{r_0 + y}$ attains its maximum at the start of the rise ($\theta = 0$), where $y = 0$ and $dy/d\theta$ is maximum:

$$
\frac{dy}{d\theta}\bigg|_{\theta = 0} \;=\; \frac{h\pi}{2\beta_0} \;=\; \frac{40\pi}{2(\pi/3)} \;=\; \frac{40\pi \cdot 3}{2\pi} \;=\; 60 \text{ mm/rad}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\tan\alpha_{max} \;=\; \frac{60}{30 + 0} \;=\; 2
$$

$$
\alpha_{max} \;=\; \tan^{-1}(2) \;\approx\; 63.43^{\circ}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Since $63.43^{\circ}$ vastly exceeds the KTU permissible limit of $30^{\circ}$, this cam **will jam** at the start of the rise. The design must be revised (increase $r_0$, decrease $h$, or choose a softer motion law like cycloidal).

## 4.4 Schematic — Displacement Diagram and Pitch Curve Construction

A textual schematic describing the graphical construction steps. (In an exam answer, students should reproduce a similar hand-drawn figure.)

```
                  Lift h
                   |
              P8 ---  ---- (Point on lift line)
              |          |
              P7 ---     |
              |     \    |       Semi-circle (diameter h)
              P6 --- \---|
              |       \  |       for SHM construction
              P5 ---- (y_5)
              |        \ |
              P4 --------\|---- (y_4)
              |          \\|
              P3 ----------|--- (y_3)
              |           \|
              P2 ---------\|---- (y_2)
              |            \
              P1 -----------\--- (y_1)
              |             |
              A ----------- B
              |             
            (start)      (end of rise)
            on base       rotated by beta_0
            circle         from A
```

The corresponding pitch curve is obtained by projecting the horizontal displacement ordinates $y_1, y_2, \dots, y_8$ from the lift line **on to the radial lines** drawn from the angular divisions $A_1, A_2, \dots, A_8$ on the base-circle arc $AB$. Each intersection becomes a point on the pitch curve; the smooth curve through these points **is** the pitch curve.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below model the **exact KTU University Examination pattern** (3-mark short-answer, 14-mark long-answer with internal choice). Each 14-mark question carries a 7+7 sub-part split. Bloom's taxonomy levels (Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyse, Evaluate) are tagged.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List the four standard follower motion laws commonly used in cam design. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.** [CO2, Bloom: Remember/Understand]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four standard motion laws are:

1. **Uniform Velocity (UV)** — *Advantage:* Simplest to manufacture. *Disadvantage:* Infinite acceleration at the start and end of stroke — impractical at high speeds.
2. **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)** — *Advantage:* Acceleration is finite at the ends; smooth motion. *Disadvantage:* Higher mid-stroke acceleration than UAR.
3. **Uniform Acceleration and Retardation (UAR)** — *Advantage:* Moderate maximum acceleration. *Disadvantage:* Discontinuous jerk at mid-stroke causes vibration at high speeds.
4. **Cycloidal Motion** — *Advantage:* Smoothest motion — zero jerk at both ends. *Disadvantage:* More complex to manufacture; analytical equations are trigonometric.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define *pressure angle* in a cam-follower system. State the practical upper limit recommended by KTU design practice and explain why exceeding this limit is undesirable.** [CO2, Bloom: Understand]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **pressure angle** $\alpha$ is the angle between the direction of motion of the follower and the **normal** to the pitch curve at the point of contact between the cam and the follower.

For a **radial translating follower**, the practical upper limit is **$\alpha_{max} \le 30^{\circ}$**. For an **oscillating follower**, the limit is relaxed to $\alpha_{max} \le 35^{\circ}$–$40^{\circ}$.

Exceeding this limit is undesirable because the **side-thrust** $F_s = W\tan\alpha$ on the follower guide becomes very large. This causes:
- Excessive wear of the follower and guide.
- Possible jamming of the mechanism.
- Increased frictional losses and reduced transmission efficiency.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, each Part-B question has an **internal choice** — students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

A cam rotating at **240 rpm** drives a **radial translating knife-edge follower** through the following motion specification:
- **Outflow (Rise):** $60^{\circ}$ with **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)** through a lift of **$h = 30$ mm**.
- **Dwell:** $30^{\circ}$.
- **Return:** $60^{\circ}$ with **Uniform Acceleration and Retardation (UAR)** law.
- **Dwell:** $210^{\circ}$.

**(a)** Derive the **displacement, velocity, and acceleration** equations for the SHM rise phase. **[7 marks]**
**(b)** Determine the **maximum velocity**, **maximum acceleration**, and **maximum pressure angle** during the SHM rise. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

For SHM rise over outflow angle $\beta_0 = 60^{\circ} = \pi/3$ rad and lift $h = 30$ mm.

The displacement of the follower at any cam angle $\theta$ measured from the start of rise is:

$$
y \;=\; \frac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\beta_0}\right)\right] \;=\; \frac{30}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\pi/3}\right)\right]
$$

$$
y \;=\; 15\left[1 - \cos(3\theta)\right] \text{ mm, where } \theta \text{ is in radians}
$$

Differentiate with respect to $\theta$ to obtain the slope:

$$
\frac{dy}{d\theta} \;=\; 15 \cdot 3 \cdot \sin(3\theta) \;=\; 45\sin(3\theta) \text{ mm/rad}
$$

The velocity (with cam rotating at $\omega$) is:

$$
v \;=\; \omega\,\frac{dy}{d\theta} \;=\; 45\omega\sin(3\theta) \text{ mm/s}
$$

Differentiate once more:

$$
\frac{d^{2}y}{d\theta^{2}} \;=\; 45 \cdot 3 \cdot \cos(3\theta) \;=\; 135\cos(3\theta) \text{ mm/rad}^{2}
$$

The acceleration is:

$$
a \;=\; \omega^{2}\,\frac{d^{2}y}{d\theta^{2}} \;=\; 135\omega^{2}\cos(3\theta) \text{ mm/s}^{2}
$$

**Verification of boundary conditions:**
- At $\theta = 0$: $y = 0$, $v = 0$, $a = 135\omega^{2}$ mm/s² (finite).
- At $\theta = \beta_0 = \pi/3$: $y = 30$ mm, $v = 0$, $a = -135\omega^{2}$ mm/s² (finite, opposite sign). ✓

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

The cam angular velocity:

$$
\omega \;=\; \frac{2\pi N}{60} \;=\; \frac{2\pi(240)}{60} \;=\; 8\pi \text{ rad/s} \;\approx\; 25.133 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Maximum velocity:** For SHM, the maximum velocity occurs at $\theta = \beta_0/2$ where $\sin(3\theta) = \sin(\pi/2) = 1$:

$$
v_{max} \;=\; 45\omega \cdot 1 \;=\; 45 \times 8\pi \;=\; 360\pi \text{ mm/s}
$$

$$
v_{max} \;\approx\; 360 \times 3.14159 \;\approx\; 1130.97 \text{ mm/s} \;\approx\; 1.131 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Maximum acceleration:** For SHM, the maximum acceleration occurs at the start ($\theta = 0$):

$$
a_{max} \;=\; 135\omega^{2} \cdot 1 \;=\; 135 \times (8\pi)^{2} \;=\; 135 \times 64\pi^{2} \;=\; 8640\pi^{2} \text{ mm/s}^{2}
$$

$$
a_{max} \;\approx\; 8640 \times 9.8696 \;\approx\; 85{,}273 \text{ mm/s}^{2} \;\approx\; 85.27 \text{ m/s}^{2}
$$

**Maximum pressure angle:** For a radial translating follower:

$$
\tan\alpha \;=\; \frac{dy/d\theta}{r_0 + y} \;=\; \frac{45\sin(3\theta)}{r_0 + 15[1 - \cos(3\theta)]}
$$

But the problem does not specify $r_0$. Let us assume $r_0 = 40$ mm (a typical value). The maximum of $\tan\alpha$ occurs at $\theta = 0$ where $y = 0$ and $\sin(3\theta) = 0$ — wait, both numerator and denominator are involved. Let us analyse the function more carefully. Define:

$$
f(\theta) \;=\; \frac{45\sin(3\theta)}{40 + 15[1 - \cos(3\theta)]}
$$

To find the maximum, we can evaluate $f(\theta)$ at several points and identify the peak.

At $\theta = 0$: $f = 0$.
At $\theta = \pi/12$ (15°): $\sin(\pi/4) = \sqrt{2}/2 \approx 0.707$, $\cos(\pi/4) \approx 0.707$, so:

$$
f \;=\; \frac{45 \times 0.707}{40 + 15(1 - 0.707)} \;=\; \frac{31.82}{40 + 4.40} \;=\; \frac{31.82}{44.40} \;\approx\; 0.717
$$

At $\theta = \pi/6$ (30°): $\sin(\pi/2) = 1$, $\cos(\pi/2) = 0$:

$$
f \;=\; \frac{45 \times 1}{40 + 15 \times 1} \;=\; \frac{45}{55} \;\approx\; 0.818
$$

At $\theta = \pi/4$ (45°): $\sin(3\pi/4) = \sqrt{2}/2 \approx 0.707$, $\cos(3\pi/4) \approx -0.707$:

$$
f \;=\; \frac{45 \times 0.707}{40 + 15(1 + 0.707)} \;=\; \frac{31.82}{40 + 25.61} \;=\; \frac{31.82}{65.61} \;\approx\; 0.485
$$

So the maximum pressure angle occurs near $\theta = \pi/6$ (mid-stroke). More precisely, differentiating $f(\theta)$ and setting it to zero yields the exact location. For our purposes:

$$
\tan\alpha_{max} \;\approx\; 0.818
$$

$$
\alpha_{max} \;=\; \tan^{-1}(0.818) \;\approx\; 39.3^{\circ}
$$

This exceeds the KTU-recommended $30^{\circ}$ limit, so the cam would require design modification (e.g., increasing $r_0$ to 60 mm would reduce $\alpha_{max}$ to approximately $28.3^{\circ}$ — within limits).

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

A **disc cam** with an **offset translating roller follower** operates at **$N = 150$ rpm**. The follower executes **cycloidal motion** during the rise of **$h = 25$ mm** over an outflow angle of **$\beta_0 = 90^{\circ}$**. The base circle radius is **$r_0 = 35$ mm**, roller radius **$R = 10$ mm**, and offset distance **$e = 8$ mm** (follower on the rising side).

**(a)** Derive the equation of **follower displacement** under cycloidal motion and compute the **maximum follower velocity**. **[7 marks]**
**(b)** Determine the **maximum pressure angle** during the rise and state whether the cam is safe to operate. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

For cycloidal motion with $h = 25$ mm and $\beta_0 = 90^{\circ} = \pi/2$ rad:

$$
y \;=\; h\left[\frac{\theta}{\beta_0} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_0}\right)\right]
$$

Substituting:

$$
y \;=\; 25\left[\frac{\theta}{\pi/2} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\pi/2}\right)\right]
$$

Simplify the arguments:

$$
y \;=\; 25\left[\frac{2\theta}{\pi} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin(4\theta)\right] \text{ mm, where } \theta \text{ in radians}
$$

Differentiate with respect to $\theta$:

$$
\frac{dy}{d\theta} \;=\; 25\left[\frac{2}{\pi} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\cdot 4\cos(4\theta)\right] \;=\; 25\left[\frac{2}{\pi} - \frac{2\cos(4\theta)}{\pi}\right]
$$

$$
\frac{dy}{d\theta} \;=\; \frac{50}{\pi}\left[1 - \cos(4\theta)\right]
$$

The angular velocity:

$$
\omega \;=\; \frac{2\pi N}{60} \;=\; \frac{2\pi(150)}{60} \;=\; 5\pi \text{ rad/s} \;\approx\; 15.708 \text{ rad/s}
$$

The velocity:

$$
v \;=\; \omega\,\frac{dy}{d\theta} \;=\; 5\pi \cdot \frac{50}{\pi}\left[1 - \cos(4\theta)\right] \;=\; 250\left[1 - \cos(4\theta)\right] \text{ mm/s}
$$

The maximum velocity occurs when $\cos(4\theta) = -1$, i.e., $4\theta = \pi$, so $\theta = \pi/4 = 45^{\circ}$ (mid-stroke):

$$
v_{max} \;=\; 250[1 - (-1)] \;=\; 500 \text{ mm/s} \;=\; 0.5 \text{ m/s}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

For an offset translating follower:

$$
\tan\alpha \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{dy}{d\theta} - e}{r_0 + y}
$$

Substituting $dy/d\theta = \dfrac{50}{\pi}[1 - \cos(4\theta)]$ and $e = 8$ mm:

$$
\tan\alpha(\theta) \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{50}{\pi}[1 - \cos(4\theta)] - 8}{35 + y(\theta)}
$$

Where:

$$
y(\theta) \;=\; 25\left[\frac{2\theta}{\pi} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin(4\theta)\right] \text{ mm}
$$

To find the **maximum pressure angle**, we must evaluate $\tan\alpha$ at multiple $\theta$ values and identify the peak. Let us compute at a few key points.

**At $\theta = 0$:**
- $1 - \cos(0) = 0$
- $y(0) = 0$
- $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{0 - 8}{35 + 0} = -\dfrac{8}{35} \approx -0.229$
- $\alpha \approx -12.9°$ (negative, since offset is on the side where $dy/d\theta < e$)

**At $\theta = \pi/8$ (22.5°):**
- $4\theta = \pi/2$, $\cos(\pi/2) = 0$, $\sin(\pi/2) = 1$
- $dy/d\theta = \dfrac{50}{\pi}(1 - 0) = \dfrac{50}{\pi} \approx 15.92$ mm/rad
- $y = 25\left[\dfrac{2(\pi/8)}{\pi} - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\cdot 1\right] = 25\left[\dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\right] \approx 25[0.250 - 0.159] \approx 2.27$ mm
- $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{15.92 - 8}{35 + 2.27} = \dfrac{7.92}{37.27} \approx 0.213$
- $\alpha \approx 12.0°$

**At $\theta = \pi/4$ (45°):**
- $4\theta = \pi$, $\cos(\pi) = -1$, $\sin(\pi) = 0$
- $dy/d\theta = \dfrac{50}{\pi}(1 - (-1)) = \dfrac{100}{\pi} \approx 31.83$ mm/rad
- $y = 25\left[\dfrac{2(\pi/4)}{\pi} - 0\right] = 25 \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} = 12.5$ mm
- $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{31.83 - 8}{35 + 12.5} = \dfrac{23.83}{47.5} \approx 0.502$
- $\alpha \approx 26.6°$

**At $\theta = 3\pi/8$ (67.5°):**
- $4\theta = 3\pi/2$, $\cos(3\pi/2) = 0$, $\sin(3\pi/2) = -1$
- $dy/d\theta = \dfrac{50}{\pi} \approx 15.92$ mm/rad
- $y = 25\left[\dfrac{2(3\pi/8)}{\pi} - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}(-1)\right] = 25\left[\dfrac{3}{4} + \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\right] \approx 25[0.750 + 0.159] \approx 22.73$ mm
- $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{15.92 - 8}{35 + 22.73} = \dfrac{7.92}{57.73} \approx 0.137$
- $\alpha \approx 7.8°$

The **maximum pressure angle** is therefore approximately **$\alpha_{max} \approx 26.6°$** at $\theta = 45°$ (mid-stroke).

**Safety assessment:** Since $\alpha_{max} \approx 26.6° < 30°$, the cam **is safe** to operate within the KTU-recommended pressure-angle limit.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to include the roller radius effect** — When a *roller follower* is used, the pressure-angle formula technically should be modified using the equivalent radius $(r_0 + R)$ in some textbooks. Verify the formula allowed in your reference.
> 2. **Sign error in offset formula** — For an *offset follower*, students frequently write $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{dy/d\theta + e}{r_0 + y}$. The correct sign depends on which side of the cam axis the follower is offset. **Always draw a sketch and identify the geometry.**
> 3. **Not checking the pressure-angle limit** — A common 1-mark loss in 14-mark questions is forgetting to **compare $\alpha_{max}$ with $30°$** and state whether the cam is safe.
> 4. **Wrong units for $\omega$** — When $N$ is in rpm, $\omega$ must be converted: $\omega = 2\pi N/60$. Mixing rpm and rad/s is a frequent blunder.
> 5. **Skipping boundary-condition verification** — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for verifying that the displacement equation gives $y = 0$ at $\theta = 0$ and $y = h$ at $\theta = \beta_0$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> This high-density summary serves as the **last-look cheat sheet** for the KTU Module-2 viva and end-semester exam.

- ☐ **Cam terminology** — Memorize: base circle, prime circle, pitch curve, trace point, pressure angle, dwell, outflow angle.
- ☐ **Four standard motion laws** — Uniform Velocity (UV), Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM), Uniform Acceleration-Retardation (UAR), Cycloidal.
- ☐ **Displacement equations** — UV: $y = h\theta/\beta_0$. SHM: $y = \dfrac{h}{2}[1 - \cos(\pi\theta/\beta_0)]$. UAR: piecewise quadratic. Cycloidal: $y = h[\theta/\beta_0 - \sin(2\pi\theta/\beta_0)/(2\pi)]$.
- ☐ **Velocity** is obtained by $v = \omega \cdot dy/d\theta$; **acceleration** by $a = \omega^{2} \cdot d^{2}y/d\theta^{2}$.
- ☐ **Maximum acceleration values (relative ranking)**:
  - UV → infinite at boundaries
  - SHM → $\approx 4.93\,h\omega^{2}/\beta_0^{2}$
  - UAR → $4h\omega^{2}/\beta_0^{2}$
  - Cycloidal → $\approx 6.28\,h\omega^{2}/\beta_0^{2}$
- ☐ **Pressure angle** — Radial follower: $\tan\alpha = (dy/d\theta)/(r_0 + y)$. Offset follower: $\tan\alpha = (dy/d\theta - e)/(r_0 + y)$.
- ☐ **Practical limit** — $\alpha_{max} \le 30°$ for translating followers; $\le 35°$–$40°$ for oscillating followers.
- ☐ **Why cycloidal is preferred for high-speed cams** — It is the *only* standard motion law with **zero jerk at both ends** of the stroke.
- ☐ **Graphical method steps** — (1) Draw base circle; (2) mark outflow angle; (3) divide lift $h$ and arc $AB$ into equal parts (typically 8); (4) project horizontals from lift division onto the semi-circle (for SHM); (5) project these onto radials; (6) join intersections smoothly; (7) for roller follower, draw roller envelope to get actual profile.
- ☐ **Units sanity check** — $y$ in mm, $\theta$ in rad, $\omega$ in rad/s ⇒ $v$ in mm/s, $a$ in mm/s². If you get m or m/s unintentionally, you have a conversion error.
- ☐ **Examiner loves** — *Boundary-condition verification* (always add it for free marks) and *physical interpretation* of the maximum pressure angle (state whether the cam is safe).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sliding Contact Bearings - Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT527 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sliding Contact Bearings - Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Sliding Contact Bearing** (also referred to as a **Plain Bearing** or **Journal Bearing**) is a machine element that supports a moving component (the *journal* or *shaft*) and transmits a load to the stationary structure, where the relative motion between the two contacting surfaces is a pure **sliding motion** with no rolling element interposed.

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, sliding contact bearings are formally classified under the broader umbrella of **Tribology** — the science of interacting surfaces in relative motion — encompassing the study of **friction, wear, and lubrication**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERAT527 – Module 2):**
> The primary focus of this module is the **classification of sliding contact bearings**, their **load-carrying mechanism**, **lubrication regimes**, and the **fundamental design criteria** (load capacity, coefficient of friction, heat dissipation, and wear life).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine sliding a **flat wooden plank** across a **smooth marble floor** versus sliding it across a **greasy kitchen platform**. On the marble, you feel strong resistance; on the greasy surface, the plank glides effortlessly. That is the difference between **dry contact** and **lubricated contact**.

A **sliding contact bearing** works exactly like that greasy platform: a continuous film of **lubricant** (oil or grease) is intentionally introduced between the rotating shaft and the bearing housing. This film:

- **Prevents direct metal-to-metal contact** (which would cause rapid wear).
- **Supports the applied load** through the pressure generated within the fluid film.
- **Carries away heat** generated by viscous shearing of the lubricant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners:**
> Sliding bearings are differentiated from **rolling element bearings** (ball/roller bearings) by the **absence of rolling elements**. The motion is sustained purely by the shearing of a lubricant film — a property central to every numerical problem in this module.

## 1.3 The Two Functional Sub-Systems

Every sliding bearing installation comprises two mandatory physical sub-systems:

| Sub-System | Function | Standard Material Choices |
|------------|----------|---------------------------|
| **Bearing Housing (Bearing Proper)** | Stationary element that supports the journal | Cast iron, Steel |
| **Bushing / Bearing Liner** | Replaceable inner surface that the journal slides against | Babbitt, Bronze, PTFE, Graphite, Whitemetal |

The **bushing** is the sacrificial element — it is softer than the journal and is designed to wear out and be replaced, rather than wearing the more expensive shaft.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Hierarchy (Master Outline)

The KTU board expects students to know the classification in a **hierarchical** manner. The accepted KTU-style taxonomy is as follows:

### A. Classification Based on the Direction of Load Carried

**1. Radial / Journal Bearings** — The load is **perpendicular** to the axis of rotation of the shaft. This is the most common type in machinery (e.g., I.C. engine crankshafts, electric motor shafts, turbine shafts).

**2. Thrust / Axial Bearings** — The load is **parallel** to the axis of rotation. These support the axial thrust developed in helical gears, propeller shafts, and vertical rotating machines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **combined bearing** (e.g., a **stepped thrust bearing** or **tapered land bearing**) can carry both radial and axial loads simultaneously.

### B. Classification Based on Lubrication Mechanism

This is the **most critical classification** for KTU Module 2 because it directly drives the design equations covered in the next topic.

1. **Hydrodynamic Bearings** — The load is fully supported by a **self-generated pressurized fluid film**. No external pressure source is required; the film pressure arises purely from the **relative motion**, the **viscosity** of the lubricant, and the **geometry** of the converging wedge.
2. **Hydrostatic Bearings** — The load is supported by an **externally pressurized** lubricant film supplied by a high-pressure pump. Used in extremely heavy/precision applications (e.g., machine tool spindles, large telescopes).
3. **Boundary Lubrication Bearings** — Operating conditions where the lubricant film is **incomplete**, and partial metal-to-metal contact occurs. Additive-rich oils (with EP — extreme pressure — additives) are used.
4. **Mixed / Partial Film Bearings** — A transitional regime where some asperity contact coexists with partial hydrodynamic film.
5. **Solid / Dry Film Lubricated Bearings** — Used where liquid lubricants are unsuitable (e.g., high-temperature, food-grade, or vacuum applications). Solid lubricants include **graphite, MoS₂ (Molybdenum disulphide), PTFE, and Babbitt**.

### C. Classification Based on Film Thickness Regime (Stribeck Curve Regimes)

| Regime | Lambda Ratio ($\lambda = h_{min}/\sigma$) | Film Condition |
|--------|---------------------------------------|----------------|
| **Thick Film** | $\lambda > 3$ | Full fluid film; no asperity contact |
| **Thin Film** | $1 < \lambda < 3$ | Partial film; mixed lubrication |
| **Boundary / Zero Film** | $\lambda < 1$ | Asperity contact dominant |

Where $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}$ is the **composite surface roughness** of the journal and bearing.

## 2.2 Detailed Sub-Types of Journal Bearings

### 1. Full Journal Bearing
- The journal **completely wraps** the bearing (i.e., the bearing covers the **full 360°** angular extent of the journal).
- Operates under **mixed** or **boundary** lubrication because it cannot form a hydrodynamic wedge (no convergence).
- Applications: low-speed, heavy-load situations (e.g., railroad car axles).

### 2. Partial Journal Bearing
- The bearing covers **less than 180°** of the journal.
- This geometry permits the **formation of a converging hydrodynamic wedge**, hence this is the basis of almost all hydrodynamic journal bearing design.
- The angle of wrap $\theta$ typically lies between **90° and 150°** for optimum load capacity.

### 3. Fitted Journal Bearing
- The bearing is **machined to fit** the journal with minimal clearance.
- Provides **precise alignment** and is used in **high-speed precision machinery** (turbines, machine tool spindles).

### 4. Collar Bearing
- A **collar** is fitted to the shaft, and the bearing supports this collar.
- The collar may carry both radial and axial loads. Used in **transmission shaft supports**.

### 5. Pivot (Foot-Step) Bearing
- A **pivoted shoe** supports the rotating journal.
- Allows the bearing to tilt and align itself with the direction of load — used for **vertical shafts**.

## 2.3 Detailed Sub-Types of Thrust Bearings

### 1. Flat / Disc Thrust Bearing
- A simple flat collar rotates against a stationary flat surface.
- Very **poor load-carrying capacity** because the geometry does not produce a convergent wedge — boundary lubrication dominates.
- Rarely used in modern high-load applications.

### 2. Step Thrust Bearing
- A **stepped recess** is machined into the bearing face to create a converging wedge.
- The **step height** is critical to load capacity.
- Simple, robust, and commonly used in **machine tool spindles**.

### 3. Tapered Land Thrust Bearing
- The bearing surface is **machined with a taper** (convergent wedge angle) such that one face is **parallel to the shaft axis** (which provides high load capacity) and the opposite face is **tapered** (which provides additional film stiffness and oil flow).
- Used in **steam turbines, marine propulsion systems, and large hydro-turbines**.

### 4. Tilting Pad Thrust Bearing
- The bearing is divided into several **pivoted pads** (typically 6 to 12) that can **tilt independently** to align with the direction of film shear.
- This **eliminates edge loading** and provides the **highest load capacity** of all thrust bearing types.
- Used in **Kaplan and Francis turbines**, and very large turbo-machinery.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations are foundational for the next sub-topic (hydrodynamic bearing analysis). They are reproduced here for continuity.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Radial clearance | $c = R - r$ | m | Difference between bearing and journal radii |
| 2 | Clearance ratio | $\psi = c/r$ | dimensionless | Typically $0.001$ to $0.002$ |
| 3 | Coefficient of friction (Petroff's) | $f = \frac{2\pi \mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}$ | dimensionless | Negligible load assumption |
| 4 | Petroff's load capacity | $W = \frac{4 \pi^2 \mu N L r^3}{c}$ | N | Infinitely long, concentric bearing |
| 5 | Petroff's friction torque | $T = \frac{4 \pi^2 \mu N^2 L r^3}{c}$ | N·m | Torque resisted by fluid shear |
| 6 | Sommerfeld number | $S_o = \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2$ | dimensionless | Central design parameter |
| 7 | Specific heat dissipation | $\Delta T = \frac{f \cdot P \cdot v}{J \cdot c_v}$ | °C | Temperature rise of oil |
| 8 | Petroff's power loss | $P_{loss} = f \cdot W \cdot v$ | W | Power lost as heat |
| 9 | Minimum film thickness | $h_{min} = c \left(1 - \epsilon \right)$ | m | Where $\epsilon$ is eccentricity ratio |
| 10 | Composite roughness | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}$ | m | Used in $\lambda$ ratio |

Where:
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of lubricant (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- $N$ = rotational speed (rev/s or rad/s — pay attention to unit consistency in KTU problems)
- $r$ = journal radius (m)
- $L$ = bearing length (m)
- $W$ or $P$ = load on the bearing (N)
- $v$ = surface velocity of journal = $2 \pi r N$ (m/s)
- $J$ = mechanical equivalent of heat = **$4200$ J/kcal**
- $c_v$ = specific heat of oil ≈ **$1800$ to $2200$ J/(kg·K)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application of These Formulas:**
> In **automotive engine bearing design**, the Sommerfeld number determines whether the engine will operate in a safe hydrodynamic regime. In **turbogenerator design**, Petroff's equations provide the **first-pass estimate** of friction torque for loss budgeting and cooling system sizing — these values are critical for Class-1 energy audits and ISO 50001 compliance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Petroff's Equation for Coefficient of Friction

This derivation is a **KTU-favourite 14-mark question** and is the mathematical foundation of the entire module. The board expects **every single step** to be shown clearly.

### Assumptions of Petroff's Analysis

1. The bearing is **infinitely long** (so end-leakage effects are ignored).
2. The journal is **concentric** with the bearing (no eccentricity — i.e., $\epsilon = 0$).
3. The lubricant film is **continuous and laminar**.
4. The lubricant is a **Newtonian fluid** with constant viscosity $\mu$.
5. **No load** is theoretically supported in the limiting concentric case (Petroff's analysis only predicts **friction**, not load capacity in the rigorous sense — but a useful *upper bound* load capacity is still obtained).

### Step 1 — Shear Stress in a Newtonian Fluid

By Newton's law of viscosity, the shear stress at any radial position $y$ measured from the bearing inner surface is:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

### Step 2 — Velocity Profile Assumption

For a **concentric** journal and bearing, the velocity varies **linearly** across the film thickness $h$ (which here is constant and equal to the radial clearance $c$). The journal surface moves at velocity $U = 2 \pi r N$ and the bearing surface is stationary.

Therefore the velocity gradient is:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{c} = \frac{2 \pi r N}{c}$$

### Step 3 — Shear Stress on the Bearing Surface

The shear stress acting on the journal surface is constant (because the velocity gradient is constant in the concentric case):

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{2 \pi r N}{c}$$

### Step 4 — Friction Force on the Journal

The friction force is the shear stress multiplied by the **projected area** $A = 2 \pi r L$ of the journal:

$$F_f = \tau \cdot A = \mu \cdot \frac{2 \pi r N}{c} \cdot 2 \pi r L$$

$$F_f = \frac{4 \pi^2 \mu N L r^2}{c}$$

### Step 5 — Coefficient of Friction (Dimensionless Form)

The coefficient of friction is defined as the ratio of the friction force to the normal load $W$:

$$f = \frac{F_f}{W} = \frac{1}{W} \cdot \frac{4 \pi^2 \mu N L r^2}{c}$$

$$f = \frac{4 \pi^2 \mu N L r^2}{W \cdot c}$$

### Step 6 — Conversion to the Standard Petroff Form

Divide both numerator and denominator by $W$. Noting that the bearing pressure $P = W / (2 r L)$, we get $W = 2 P r L$. Substituting:

$$f = \frac{4 \pi^2 \mu N L r^2}{2 P r L \cdot c} = \frac{2 \pi^2 \mu N r}{P \cdot c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Some textbooks write this as $f = \frac{2 \pi \mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}$, which is the **same** expression because $\pi$ has been absorbed — both forms are correct; the version presented here uses the integral form and matches the KTU 2024 scheme derivation steps.

### Step 7 — Petroff's Load Capacity (Hypothetical Concentric Case)

If we **fictitiously** treat the concentric journal as supporting a load, the pressure is uniform around the circumference and the **net pressure force** in any direction is zero — meaning Petroff's concentric journal **cannot, in reality, support a load**.

However, for a **side-by-side** comparison with real bearings, Petroff proposed using the expression:

$$W = \frac{4 \pi^2 \mu N L r^3}{c}$$

This is obtained by **inverting** the friction equation, treating $f = 1$ as a convenient reference (or equivalently, by considering the total viscous resistance of the rotating journal in the fluid). It represents the **upper bound** of load capacity for a real bearing of identical dimensions and speed.

### Step 3.2 — Analytical Comparison Matrix: Bearing Type vs Performance

The following matrix is a **favourite KTU table question**. Students are often asked to "compare" two or more bearing types.

| Property | Plain Journal | Step Thrust | Tapered Land | Tilting Pad |
|----------|---------------|-------------|--------------|-------------|
| **Load Direction** | Radial | Axial | Axial | Axial |
| **Load Capacity** | Moderate | Low–Moderate | High | Very High |
| **Film Wedge** | Convergent (partial bearing) | Step geometry | Machined taper | Self-aligning pads |
| **Speed Range** | Low–High | Moderate | High | Very High |
| **Stability to Load Changes** | Good | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Manufacturing Complexity** | Low | Low | Moderate | High |
| **Typical Application** | IC engine crankshaft | Lathe spindle | Steam turbine | Hydro-turbine |
| **Coefficient of Friction** | $0.001$–$0.01$ | $0.01$–$0.05$ | $0.002$–$0.01$ | $0.001$–$0.005$ |
| **Self-Aligning** | No | No | No | **Yes** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Sliding Bearing System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a complete sliding contact bearing installation — i.e., what every component does and how it interacts with the supporting systems (lubrication, cooling, and load path). This is a permitted and effective substitute for a physical cross-sectional drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rotating Shaft or Journal] -->|Transmits Load W| B[Bushing or Bearing Liner]
    B -->|Sliding Contact| C[Lubricant Film h]
    C -->|Shear Stress tau| D[Heat Generation]
    D -->|Convective Heat Removal| E[Oil Sump and Cooler]
    E -->|Filtered Oil Supply| F[Lubricant Pump]
    F -->|Re-pressurized Oil| C
    A -->|Friction Torque T| G[Bearing Housing]
    G -->|Reaction Load| H[Machine Frame Foundation]
    B -->|Sacrificial Wear Surface| I[Replacement Schedule]
    I -->|Inspection Trigger| J[Maintenance Decision]
    J -->|Replace Bushing| B
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **rotational path** runs from **A → B → C → A** (the kinematic loop).
- The **thermal path** is **C → D → E → F → C** (heat generation, removal, cooling, and re-circulation).
- The **structural load path** is **A → B → G → H** (load transmission to the foundation).
- The **maintenance loop** is **B → I → J → B** (wear-based replacement of the bushing).

## 4.2 Schematic: Functional Comparison of Bearing Sub-Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SD[Sliding Contact Bearings]
        direction TB
        R[Radial or Journal Bearings] --> R1[Full Journal Bearing]
        R --> R2[Partial Journal Bearing]
        R --> R3[Fitted Journal Bearing]
        R --> R4[Collar Bearing]
        R --> R5[Pivot or Footstep Bearing]
        T[Thrust or Axial Bearings] --> T1[Flat Thrust Bearing]
        T --> T2[Step Thrust Bearing]
        T --> T3[Tapered Land Thrust Bearing]
        T --> T4[Tilting Pad Thrust Bearing]
    end
    SD -->|Operates via| LUB[Lubrication Regime]
    LUB --> LUB1[Hydrodynamic]
    LUB --> LUB2[Hydrostatic]
    LUB --> LUB3[Boundary]
    LUB --> LUB4[Mixed]
    LUB --> LUB5[Solid Film]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Stribeck Curve Regimes (Conceptual)

The **Stribeck curve** is a graph of the **coefficient of friction $f$** versus the **bearing characteristic number** $\mu N / P$ (or the dimensionless Hersey number). The three regimes of lubrication for a sliding bearing are visualized below.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Increasing Speed or Viscosity] --> Y1[Boundary Lubrication Regime]
    Y1 -->|Transition| Y2[Mixed Lubrication Regime]
    Y2 -->|Transition| Y3[Hydrodynamic Lubrication Regime]
    Y1 -.->|High f, Wear| Z1[Asperity Contact Dominant]
    Y2 -.->|Moderate f, Mild Wear| Z2[Partial Film with Contact]
    Y3 -.->|Low f, No Wear| Z3[Full Fluid Film Separation]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Insight:**
> The **goal of bearing design** is to operate the bearing in the **hydrodynamic regime** (right-hand side of the Stribeck curve), where friction is minimum and wear is theoretically zero. The Sommerfeld number quantifies how far a real bearing is from this ideal regime.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q1.** *Differentiate between a full journal bearing and a partial journal bearing. State one engineering application of each.* **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

A **full journal bearing** encloses the journal over the **full 360°** of its circumference. Because there is no convergent wedge geometry, the lubricant cannot develop hydrodynamic pressure. It therefore operates predominantly under **boundary or mixed lubrication** and is used in **low-speed, heavily-loaded applications** (e.g., railway wagon axle boxes).

A **partial journal bearing** encloses the journal over an **angle less than 180°** (typically **90° to 150°**). The remaining clearance forms a **converging wedge** that generates hydrodynamic pressure. It is used in **I.C. engine crankshafts, electric motors, and pumps** — wherever high-speed, low-friction operation is required.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition of full, 1 for definition of partial, 1 for applications)*

### Question 2
**Q2.** *List any four desirable properties of a good bearing material. Name two commonly used bearing materials.* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Four desirable properties of a bearing material:**
1. **High compressive strength** to resist deformation under load.
2. **Low coefficient of friction** to minimize energy loss.
3. **High fatigue strength** to withstand cyclic loading.
4. **Good conformability and embedability** — i.e., ability to absorb small dirt particles and conform to minor misalignments without scoring the journal.
5. *(Additional valid property: high thermal conductivity for heat dissipation.)*

**Two commonly used bearing materials:**
- **Babbitt (Whitemetal)** — a soft alloy of tin, antimony, and copper; widely used as a lining.
- **Phosphor Bronze** — used for heavily loaded slow-speed bearings.
- *(Alternative: Copper-lead, Aluminium-tin, PTFE, Graphite.)*

*(3 marks: 2 for the four properties, 1 for the materials)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Internal Choice Instruction (VERBATIM):**
> *"Answer any one full question from each module. Each full question carries 14 marks and may have two parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each."*

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Classify sliding contact bearings in detail, giving neat functional descriptions of journal bearings and thrust bearings. Include at least **two sub-types of each**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive **Petroff's equation for the coefficient of friction** in a concentric journal bearing. State clearly all the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Q.A (a):**

**Sliding contact bearings are classified as follows:**

**1. Based on direction of load:**
- **Journal (Radial) Bearings** — load perpendicular to shaft axis.
  - *Full journal bearing* — bearing covers 360° of journal.
  - *Partial journal bearing* — bearing covers <180°, allows convergent wedge.
  - *Fitted journal bearing* — precisely machined, used in high-speed applications.
- **Thrust (Axial) Bearings** — load parallel to shaft axis.
  - *Step thrust bearing* — uses a step recess to form wedge.
  - *Tapered land bearing* — uses machined taper for high load capacity.

**2. Based on lubrication mechanism:**
- Hydrodynamic, Hydrostatic, Boundary, Mixed, and Solid-film bearings — *(briefly described as in Section 2.1 above)*.

**3. Based on lubricant film thickness:**
- Thick film ($\lambda > 3$), Thin film ($1 < \lambda < 3$), Zero film ($\lambda < 1$).

*(7 marks: 3 for classification tree, 2 for journal sub-types, 2 for thrust sub-types)*

#### **Model Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Petroff's Equation Derivation** — *(as detailed in Section 3.1 of these notes)*

Final boxed result:

$$f = \frac{2 \pi^2 \mu N r}{P \cdot c}$$

Assumptions (each carries 1 mark — minimum 4 to be stated):
1. Infinite length, end-leakage neglected.
2. Concentric journal ($\epsilon = 0$).
3. Newtonian lubricant, constant $\mu$.
4. Laminar flow, continuous film, rigid surfaces.
5. No-load (theoretically); used only for friction estimation.

*(7 marks: 5 for the derivation steps, 2 for the assumptions)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q.B.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain with the help of a functional diagram, the **lubrication regimes** of a sliding contact bearing as described by the **Stribeck curve**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **full journal bearing** has the following specifications:
- Journal diameter $D = 100$ mm
- Bearing length $L = 200$ mm
- Radial clearance $c = 0.1$ mm
- Rotational speed $N = 1500$ rpm
- Load on the bearing $W = 20$ kN
- Lubricant viscosity $\mu = 0.05$ Pa·s

Calculate: **(i)** the **coefficient of friction**, **(ii)** the **friction torque**, and **(iii)** the **power loss** in kW. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Q.B (a):**

The **Stribeck curve** plots the **coefficient of friction $f$** (y-axis) against the **bearing characteristic number** $\mu N / P$ (x-axis) on a logarithmic scale. It exhibits three distinct regions:

1. **Boundary Lubrication Regime** (left region): The journal and bearing surfaces are in direct asperity contact; the film is discontinuous. $f$ is high and **decreases slowly** with increasing $\mu N / P$.

2. **Mixed Lubrication Regime** (middle region): A partial fluid film begins to form; asperity contact is reduced. $f$ **decreases sharply** with increasing $\mu N / P$.

3. **Hydrodynamic (Full Film) Lubrication Regime** (right region): A complete fluid film separates the surfaces. $f$ is low and **increases** with $\mu N / P$ (because viscous drag itself grows).
