The **minimum point** of the Stribeck curve is called the **transition point**, marking the boundary between mixed and hydrodynamic regimes.

*(7 marks: 2 for axes/labels, 3 for the three regimes, 2 for the transition and engineering significance)*

#### **Model Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Given data:**

$$D = 100 \text{ mm} \implies r = 0.05 \text{ m}$$

$$L = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}, \quad c = 0.1 \text{ mm} = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

$$N = 1500 \text{ rpm} = \frac{1500}{60} = 25 \text{ rev/s}$$

$$W = 20 \text{ kN} = 20000 \text{ N}, \quad \mu = 0.05 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Step (i) — Coefficient of friction using Petroff's equation:**

$$f = \frac{2 \pi^2 \mu N r}{P \cdot c}$$

Substituting:

$$f = \frac{2 \pi^2 \times 0.05 \times 25 \times 0.05}{P \times 1 \times 10^{-4}}$$

The bearing pressure $P$ is defined as:

$$P = \frac{W}{2 r L} = \frac{20000}{2 \times 0.05 \times 0.2} = \frac{20000}{0.02} = 1 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2 = 1 \text{ MPa}$$

Substituting back:

$$f = \frac{2 \times 9.8696 \times 0.05 \times 25 \times 0.05}{1 \times 10^6 \times 1 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$f = \frac{1.2337}{100} = 0.01234$$

$$\boxed{f \approx 0.0123}$$

**Step (ii) — Friction torque:**

$$T = f \cdot W \cdot r = 0.01234 \times 20000 \times 0.05$$

$$T = 12.34 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 12.34 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step (iii) — Power loss:**

The surface velocity of the journal is:

$$v = 2 \pi r N = 2 \pi \times 0.05 \times 25 = 7.854 \text{ m/s}$$

The power loss is:

$$P_{loss} = f \cdot W \cdot v = 0.01234 \times 20000 \times 7.854$$

$$P_{loss} = 1938.5 \text{ W} \approx 1.94 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{loss} \approx 1.94 \text{ kW}}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for unit conversions, 1 for $P$, 2 for $f$, 1 for $T$, 1 for $P_{loss}$)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in $N$:** Students forget to convert **rpm → rev/s** (or rad/s), leading to $f$ values that are off by a factor of 60 or $2\pi$. Always state "$N$ in rev/s" clearly in the substitution step.
> 2. **Confusion between Petroff's $f$ and the real bearing $f$:** Petroff's equation applies ONLY to the **concentric, no-load** limiting case. It gives an **upper bound** for friction and is **not** valid for the design of real eccentric bearings. Examiners WILL deduct marks if you do not state this.
> 3. **Bearing Pressure $P$ vs. Load $W$:** The symbol $P$ in Petroff's equation refers to the **projected bearing pressure** $W / (2 r L)$ — **not** the unit of load. Mixing these two is the single most common error.
> 4. **Forgetting "Infinite Length" Assumption:** Petroff's equation assumes an infinitely long bearing. For a finite bearing, a correction factor of approximately $0.75$ to $0.9$ must be applied — KTU students are expected to **state the assumption**, not correct for it.
> 5. **Bearing Material Misnaming:** Calling **Babbitt** a "bearing alloy" without specifying it is a **tin-based whitemetal** loses 0.5 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Sliding contact bearing** = bearing with **no rolling element**; load supported by **fluid film shear**.
- [x] Two mandatory parts: **bearing housing** + **bushing/liner** (replaceable).
- [x] **Classification by load direction:** Radial (Journal) vs Axial (Thrust).
- [x] **Classification by lubrication:** Hydrodynamic, Hydrostatic, Boundary, Mixed, Solid-film.
- [x] **Classification by film thickness:** Thick ($\lambda>3$), Thin ($1<\lambda<3$), Boundary ($\lambda<1$).
- [x] **Journal sub-types:** Full, Partial, Fitted, Collar, Pivot — *partial* is the basis of all hydrodynamic analysis.
- [x] **Thrust sub-types:** Flat, Step, Tapered Land, Tilting Pad — *tilting pad* offers the **highest load capacity** and is **self-aligning**.
- [x] **Petroff's coefficient of friction:** $f = \dfrac{2 \pi^2 \mu N r}{P \cdot c}$ — **concentric, infinite-length, no-load** assumption.
- [x] **Petroff's load capacity (upper bound):** $W = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu N L r^3}{c}$.
- [x] **Sommerfeld number:** $S_o = \dfrac{\mu N}{P} \left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2$ — **central design parameter** for hydrodynamic bearings.
- [x] **Stribeck curve** has three regimes: Boundary (high $f$) → Mixed (decreasing $f$) → Hydrodynamic (low $f$, then rising).
- [x] **Bearing material examples:** Babbitt (whitemetal), Phosphor Bronze, PTFE, Graphite, Aluminium-tin.
- [x] **Key dimensionless group:** $\lambda = h_{min} / \sigma$ — the **specific film thickness**.
- [x] **Unit conversions:** $N$ in rev/s; $P$ in Pa; lengths in metres — be **meticulous**.
- [x] **Engineering applications:** IC engine crankshafts (partial journal), turbines (tilting pad thrust), machine tools (step thrust), railway axles (full journal), pumps and motors (fitted journal).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lubrication in Bush Bearings, Thick and Thin Film Lubrication, Coefficient of Friction in Boundary and Hydrodynamic Regions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT527 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lubrication in Bush Bearings, Thick and Thin Film Lubrication, Coefficient of Friction in Boundary and Hydrodynamic Regions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Bush (Journal) Bearing?

A **bush bearing**, also called a **plain journal bearing** or **sleeve bearing**, is the simplest and most widely used type of bearing in mechanical engineering. It consists of a cylindrical shaft (journal) rotating inside a closely fitting cylindrical surface (bearing bush or liner). The relative motion is sliding, with no rolling elements involved.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A bush bearing is a machine element that supports a rotating shaft through a thin film of lubricant, transferring radial load from the shaft to the supporting structure while permitting relative rotational motion.

The clearance between the journal (diameter **D** or **d**) and the bush (diameter **D + 2c**) is extremely small — typically, the **radial clearance (c)** is in the order of **0.001 to 0.002 times the journal diameter**.

## 1.2 What is Lubrication?

**Lubrication** is the process of introducing a viscous fluid (lubricant) between two sliding surfaces to:
- Reduce direct metal-to-metal contact
- Minimize **frictional resistance**
- Dissipate the **heat of friction**
- Prevent **wear and seizure**
- Protect against **corrosion**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** Without lubrication, a steel shaft rotating inside a steel bush at even moderate speeds would weld itself to the bush (a phenomenon called **seizure**) within seconds. Lubrication is what allows modern machinery to operate reliably for thousands of hours.

## 1.3 The Real-World Analogy — A Skater on Ice

Imagine pushing a heavy metal block across a concrete floor — it resists motion strongly. Now, place a thin film of oil on a metal plate and slide the block again — it moves much more easily.

A better analogy is a **skater on an ice rink**:
- **No lubrication (dry friction):** The skater is essentially walking in sand — enormous resistance, high wear on shoes, intense heat.
- **Boundary lubrication (thin film):** Only a thin film of water exists between the skate blade and the ice — friction is reduced but the blades still touch asperities (tiny peaks) of the surface.
- **Hydrodynamic lubrication (thick film):** The skater goes fast enough that the ice melts instantly under pressure, creating a continuous water cushion — the blade never touches the ice.

This is exactly what happens in a journal bearing: as speed increases, the journal "floats" on a wedge of oil.

## 1.4 The Three Lubrication Regimes

The behaviour of friction in a bush bearing is governed by a famous relationship called the **Stribeck Curve**, which shows the coefficient of friction (μ) plotted against the dimensionless bearing characteristic number (Z·N/P), where:
- **Z** = absolute viscosity of lubricant (Pa·s or reyns)
- **N** = speed (rev/s or rev/min)
- **P** = bearing pressure (N/mm²)

The curve has three distinct zones:

> [!NOTE]
> **Zone 1 — Boundary Lubrication (Thin Film):**
> Very low speed or high load. The lubricant film is **thinner than the surface roughness**. Metal-to-metal contact occurs at asperities. μ is **high (0.1 to 0.4)** and **decreases** as speed increases. Wear is significant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Zone 2 — Mixed (Semi-Fluid) Lubrication:**
> The film thickness is comparable to surface roughness. Partial asperity contact exists alongside partial fluid film. μ reaches its **minimum value (μ_min ≈ 0.001 to 0.01)**. This is the **transition region**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Zone 3 — Hydrodynamic (Thick Film) Lubrication:**
> The film thickness is much greater than surface roughness. Complete separation of surfaces. μ is **low (0.001 to 0.01)** and **increases** with speed (because of viscous drag in the oil film). No wear, long life.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| Absolute Viscosity of Oil | Z or μ | $0.001$ to $0.1$ Pa·s (1 to 100 cP) |
| Kinematic Viscosity | ν | $10$ to $100$ cSt at 40°C |
| Coefficient of Friction (boundary) | μ | $0.1$ to $0.4$ |
| Coefficient of Friction (hydrodynamic) | μ | $0.001$ to $0.01$ |
| Radial Clearance | c | $0.001D$ to $0.002D$ |
| Specific Bearing Pressure | p | $5$ to $20$ N/mm² for industrial bearings |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Lubrication Works — The Physics

A rotating journal inside a stationary bush experiences a wedge-shaped converging gap when loaded. The shaft, due to the adhesion of oil molecules to its surface, drags oil into this converging wedge. The oil cannot escape fast enough, so pressure builds up — this pressure supports the load.

This is the **hydrodynamic principle** discovered by **Osborne Reynolds (1886)** and experimentally verified by **Beauchamp Tower (1885)**.

## 2.2 Modes of Lubrication (Detailed)

### 2.2.1 Thick Film Lubrication (Hydrodynamic / Fluid-Film)

- The journal and bush are **completely separated** by an oil film.
- The minimum film thickness $h_0$ >> combined surface roughness $R_a$.
- The bearing carries load purely through the **fluid pressure** generated in the film.
- **No metal-to-metal contact** — zero wear in theory.
- Coefficient of friction is governed by **viscous shear** in the oil, not by surface interaction.
- The film pressure is generated by:
  1. **Squeeze effect** (at start-up/shut-down)
  2. **Wedging action** (steady-state hydrodynamic wedge)
  3. **Viscous drag** (rotation dragging oil into the convergent zone)

### 2.2.2 Thin Film Lubrication (Boundary Lubrication)

- The oil film is **thinner than the surface roughness**.
- Load is supported partly by asperity contact and partly by boundary film.
- The boundary film is a **few molecular layers thick** of polar molecules (e.g., fatty acids in the oil) that adhere strongly to the metal surface.
- This regime occurs at:
  - **Start-up** and **shut-down** of machinery
  - **High loads** or **very low speeds**
  - **High temperatures** where oil viscosity drops
- Friction follows the law: $F = \mu W$, where $\mu$ depends on the **shear strength of the boundary film**, not on viscosity.

### 2.2.3 Mixed Lubrication (Semi-Fluid)

- A **transition** between boundary and hydrodynamic.
- Asperity contact exists intermittently.
- Coefficient of friction reaches its **lowest value** here.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **directly testable** in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). Memorize the symbol meaning, the units, and the conditions of applicability.

### A. Petroff's Equation (Friction Torque in Lightly-Loaded Bearings)

$$F_t = \frac{4 \pi^2 \mu Z N R^3 L}{c}$$

$$T_f = F_t \cdot R = \frac{4 \pi^2 \mu Z N R^4 L}{c}$$

$$\mu_f = \frac{F_t}{W} = \frac{4 \pi^2 \mu Z N R^3 L}{W c}$$

where the **Petroff coefficient of friction** is:

$$\mu_f = 2 \pi^2 \frac{\mu Z N}{p} \cdot \frac{R}{c}$$

### B. Hydrodynamic Pressure Equation (Reynolds Equation — Simplified)

$$\frac{dp}{dx} = 6 \mu U \frac{h - h_m}{h^3}$$

### C. Load Carrying Capacity (Sommerfeld / Raimondi-Boyd Charts)

$$W = \frac{2 \pi \mu U L R^3}{c} \cdot S$$

where $S$ is the **Sommerfeld number** (read from charts).

### D. Coefficient of Friction — Hydrodynamic Regime

$$\mu_f = \frac{F_t}{W} = \left(\frac{R}{c}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\mu U}{P}\right) \cdot f\left(\frac{L}{D}, \epsilon\right)$$

The function $f(L/D, \epsilon)$ is obtained from the **Raimondi-Boyd charts**.

### E. Coefficient of Friction — Boundary Regime (Bowden-Tabor Model)

$$\mu_b = \frac{\tau_s}{p_y}$$

where $\tau_s$ = shear strength of boundary film, $p_y$ = yield pressure of asperity contact.

### F. Stribeck Number (Bearing Characteristic Number)

$$\text{Stribeck Number} = \frac{\mu N}{P}$$

(also written as $ZN/P$ in older texts).

### G. Heat Dissipated in Bearing

$$H = \mu_f W U \;\; (\text{Watts})$$

### H. Petroff's Friction Variable (Alternative Form)

For Petroff's lightly-loaded bearing:

$$f \cdot \frac{r}{c} = 2 \pi^2 \frac{Z N}{P}$$

### Master Formula Table

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_t = \dfrac{4\pi^2 \mu Z N R^3 L}{c}$ | Petroff friction force | N |
| 2 | $T_f = \dfrac{4\pi^2 \mu Z N R^4 L}{c}$ | Petroff friction torque | N·m |
| 3 | $\mu_f = 2\pi^2 \dfrac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \dfrac{R}{c}$ | Petroff coefficient of friction | dimensionless |
| 4 | $S = \dfrac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \dfrac{R}{c}$ | Sommerfeld number | dimensionless |
| 5 | $H = \mu_f W U$ | Heat generated in bearing | W |
| 6 | $W = \dfrac{2\pi \mu U L R^3}{c} \cdot S$ | Hydrodynamic load capacity | N |
| 7 | $h_0 = c(1 - \epsilon)$ | Minimum film thickness | m |
| 8 | $h_0 = c(1 + \epsilon)$ | Maximum film thickness | m |
| 9 | $\mu_b = \tau_s / p_y$ | Boundary friction coefficient | dimensionless |
| 10 | $P = W/(L \cdot D)$ | Bearing pressure | N/m² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The coefficient of friction in the hydrodynamic regime is **inversely proportional to the bearing pressure P**. This is why slow-speed, heavily-loaded bearings experience high friction, and why we use the dimensionless **Sommerfeld number** to scale bearing performance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Petroff's Equation (Lightly-Loaded Bearing)

Petroff considered a **hypothetical, lightly-loaded bearing** in which the journal is **concentric** with the bush (no eccentricity, no load). The oil film is purely **viscous shear** in a uniform clearance.

### Step 1: Set Up the Geometry

Consider a journal of radius **R** rotating at **N rev/s** inside a bush with radial clearance **c**. Since the journal is concentric, the film thickness is uniform and equal to **c** everywhere.

### Step 2: Velocity of the Journal Surface

The surface velocity of the journal at the oil-bush interface is:

$$U = 2\pi R N \;\; (\text{m/s})$$

where **N** is the rotational speed in revolutions per second.

### Step 3: Velocity Gradient in the Oil Film

Because the bush is stationary and the journal is moving, the velocity in the oil film varies linearly from **U** at the journal surface to **0** at the bush surface. The velocity gradient (shear rate) is:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{c} = \frac{2\pi R N}{c}$$

### Step 4: Shear Stress in the Oil Film

From **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = \mu \cdot \frac{2\pi R N}{c}$$

where $\mu$ (sometimes written $Z$) is the **absolute dynamic viscosity** of the lubricant.

### Step 5: Total Friction Force on the Bearing

Integrating the shear stress around the journal surface of length **L** and circumference $2\pi R$:

$$F_t = \tau \cdot (2\pi R L) = \mu \cdot \frac{2\pi R N}{c} \cdot 2\pi R L$$

$$\boxed{F_t = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu Z N R^3 L}{c}}$$

### Step 6: Friction Torque

Multiplying force by radius:

$$\boxed{T_f = F_t \cdot R = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu Z N R^4 L}{c}}$$

### Step 7: Coefficient of Friction

The bearing load is $W = P \cdot L \cdot D = P \cdot L \cdot 2R$ (where P is the bearing pressure). So:

$$\mu_f = \frac{F_t}{W} = \frac{\mu \cdot 4\pi^2 N R^3 L / c}{P \cdot 2 R L}$$

$$\boxed{\mu_f = 2\pi^2 \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{R}{c}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Petroff's Insight:** The coefficient of friction is directly proportional to **Z (viscosity)**, **N (speed)**, and **R/c (inverseness of clearance)**, and inversely proportional to **P (bearing pressure)**. This single equation already captures the essence of the **Stribeck curve** in the hydrodynamic regime — friction increases with speed and viscosity in the thick-film zone.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Hydrodynamic Load Capacity (Reynolds Approach)

### Step 1: Assumed Velocity Distribution (Couette + Poiseuille)

For a thin oil film of thickness $h(x)$ with the journal moving at velocity $U$ and a pressure gradient $\frac{dp}{dx}$ along the direction of motion, the velocity profile is the **superposition of Couette flow (linear) and Poiseuille flow (parabolic)**:

$$u(y) = U \cdot \frac{y}{h} - \frac{1}{2\mu} \frac{dp}{dx} \cdot y (h - y)$$

### Step 2: Volume Flow Rate Per Unit Width

Integrating across the film thickness:

$$q = \int_0^h u \, dy$$

$$q = U \cdot \frac{h}{2} - \frac{h^3}{12\mu} \cdot \frac{dp}{dx}$$

### Step 3: Apply Continuity (No Side Leakage — Approximation)

For an infinitely wide bearing, the volume flow rate is **constant** along $x$:

$$\frac{dq}{dx} = 0$$

$$\frac{U}{2} \cdot \frac{dh}{dx} - \frac{1}{12\mu} \frac{d}{dx}\left(h^3 \frac{dp}{dx}\right) = 0$$

### Step 4: Reynolds Equation in 1D

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(h^3 \frac{dp}{dx}\right) = 6\mu U \frac{dh}{dx}$$

Integrating:

$$\boxed{\frac{dp}{dx} = 6\mu U \frac{h - h_m}{h^3}}$$

where $h_m$ is the film thickness at maximum pressure ($dp/dx = 0$).

### Step 5: Load Carrying Capacity

The total vertical load supported by the pressure distribution is obtained by integrating pressure over the projected bearing area:

$$W = L \int_0^{2\pi R} p \cos\theta \, Rd\theta$$

This integral is evaluated using the **Raimondi-Boyd charts** for given values of $L/D$ and eccentricity ratio $\epsilon$.

## 3.3 Coefficient of Friction in the Boundary Regime

In boundary lubrication, the surfaces touch at microscopic asperities. The real area of contact is small but the local pressure is enormous — sufficient to cause **plastic deformation**.

### Step 1: Yield of Asperities

The real contact area $A_r$ under load $W$ with asperity yield pressure $p_y$ is:

$$A_r = \frac{W}{p_y}$$

### Step 2: Shear of Boundary Film

The boundary film has a shear strength $\tau_s$ (much lower than the metal's shear strength). The friction force is:

$$F_t = A_r \cdot \tau_s = \frac{W \cdot \tau_s}{p_y}$$

### Step 3: Coefficient of Friction

$$\boxed{\mu_b = \frac{F_t}{W} = \frac{\tau_s}{p_y}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** In boundary lubrication, the coefficient of friction is **independent of load, speed, and viscosity**. It depends only on the **material properties** of the boundary film and the metal surfaces. This is why boundary friction is "stick-slip" and noisy — adding more oil does not reduce μ.

## 3.4 Behaviour of Coefficient of Friction in the Three Regions (Analytical Matrix)

| Parameter | Boundary (Thin Film) | Mixed | Hydrodynamic (Thick Film) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Film thickness $h_0$ | < combined surface roughness | ≈ surface roughness | >> surface roughness |
| Load support mechanism | Asperity contact | Mixed | Fluid pressure |
| Coefficient of friction μ | High (0.1 – 0.4) | Minimum (0.001 – 0.01) | Low (0.001 – 0.01) |
| Effect of speed on μ | μ decreases with N | μ at minimum | μ increases with N |
| Effect of viscosity on μ | Independent | μ at minimum | μ increases with Z |
| Effect of load on μ | μ decreases with W (initially) | — | μ decreases with P |
| Governing equation | $\mu = \tau_s / p_y$ | Empirical / Stribeck | $\mu = f(S, L/D, \epsilon)$ |
| Wear | Significant | Moderate | Negligible |
| Heat generated | High (conduction at contact) | Moderate | Steady (carried by oil) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hydrodynamic Bush Bearing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rotating Journal - Radius R, Speed N] --> B[Oil Inlet at High Pressure Region]
    B --> C[Converging Wedge Region - Film thickness h decreases from h0 to hmin]
    C --> D[Maximum Pressure Point - dp/dx equals 0]
    D --> E[Diverging Wedge Region - Film thickness increases from hmin to h0]
    E --> F[Oil Outlet at Low Pressure Region]
    F --> G[Lubricant Return to Sump via Side Leakage]
    D --> H[Load W supported by Pressure Distribution]
    H --> I[Shaft Floats on Oil Film - Eccentricity e]
    I --> J[Film thickness h0 = c minus e, hmin = c minus e_critical]
    C --> K[Viscous Shear generates Friction Force F_t]
    K --> L[Friction Torque T_f equals F_t times R]
    L --> M[Heat Generation H equals F_t times U]
    M --> N[Heat removed by Oil Flow and Conduction to Bush]
```

## 4.2 The Stribeck Curve — Three Lubrication Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Boundary Lubrication Region] --> P2[Z N over P increases]
    P2 --> P3[Mixed Lubrication Region]
    P3 --> P4[Z N over P increases further]
    P4 --> P5[Hydrodynamic Lubrication Region]
    P1 -.->|High friction 0.1 to 0.4| P3
    P3 -.->|Minimum friction 0.001 to 0.01| P5
    P1 --> A1[Thin film - Asperity contact - Wear occurs]
    P3 --> A2[Transition - Partial contact]
    P5 --> A3[Thick film - No contact - Long life]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Coefficient of Friction Variation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start] --> T{Bearing Operating Condition}
    T -->|Very low speed or High load| U[Boundary Lubrication - Thin Film]
    T -->|Moderate speed and load| V[Mixed Lubrication]
    T -->|Sufficient speed and load| W[Hydrodynamic Lubrication - Thick Film]
    U --> X1[High mu - 0.1 to 0.4 - Decreases with speed]
    V --> X2[Lowest mu - 0.001 to 0.01 - Minimum point]
    W --> X3[Low mu - 0.001 to 0.01 - Increases with speed]
    X1 --> Y[mu - Z N over P curve is decreasing]
    X2 --> Y
    X3 --> Z[mu - Z N over P curve is increasing]
    Y --> End1[Stribeck Curve Descending Branch]
    Z --> End2[Stribeck Curve Ascending Branch]
```

## 4.4 Lubrication Regime Decision Tree (KTU Examiner-Favourite Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Journal and Bush Relative Motion] --> A{Velocity Sufficient to form Film?}
    A -->|No| B1[Boundary Lubrication]
    A -->|Yes| B2[Check Load and Viscosity]
    B2 --> C{Film Thickness greater than Roughness?}
    C -->|No| D1[Mixed Lubrication]
    C -->|Yes| D2[Hydrodynamic Lubrication]
    B1 --> E1[Friction equals tau_s over p_y]
    D1 --> E2[Friction interpolated from Stribeck]
    D2 --> E3[Friction equals f of Sommerfeld number]
    E1 --> F[KTU Exam Point - Identify Regime]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**Q: Differentiate between thick film lubrication and thin film lubrication.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Thick Film (Hydrodynamic) Lubrication | Thin Film (Boundary) Lubrication |
|---|---|---|
| **Film thickness** | $h_0$ >> surface roughness $R_a$ | $h_0$ < $R_a$ |
| **Separation** | Journal and bush completely separated | Metal-to-metal contact at asperities |
| **Load support** | By fluid pressure in oil film | By boundary film and asperity contact |
| **Coefficient of friction** | $0.001$ to $0.01$ | $0.1$ to $0.4$ |
| **Wear** | Negligible | Significant |
| **Speed range** | High | Low / at start-up |
| **Governing equation** | $\mu = 2\pi^2 (ZN/P)(R/c)$ | $\mu = \tau_s / p_y$ |
| **Effect of viscosity** | Friction increases with Z | Friction independent of Z |
| **Example** | Steady-state operation of engine crankshaft | Cold start of an engine |

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: Define the Stribeck curve. What is the significance of the bearing characteristic number $ZN/P$?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Stribeck curve** is a plot of the **coefficient of friction (μ)** of a journal bearing as a function of the **bearing characteristic number** $\lambda = ZN/P$ (also called the **Stribeck number**), where:
- $Z$ = absolute viscosity of lubricant (Pa·s or reyns)
- $N$ = speed of journal (rev/s or rev/min)
- $P$ = bearing pressure (N/m²)

**Significance:**
- The Stribeck curve identifies the three lubrication regimes: **boundary**, **mixed**, and **hydrodynamic**.
- The **minimum point** on the curve indicates the transition between mixed and hydrodynamic regimes — the **optimum operating point**.
- A bearing should normally operate on the **ascending (right) branch** of the curve, in the hydrodynamic regime, to ensure long life and low wear.
- The bearing characteristic number combines all the most important operating parameters (viscosity, speed, load) into a single dimensionless number, making bearing selection and scale-up straightforward.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each question in Part B carries **14 marks**, typically split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. The KTU ESE pattern allows the student to choose between two full questions (each with both sub-parts) within the same module.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / May 2024 Model]**

A full journal bearing has the following specifications:
- Journal diameter $D = 100$ mm
- Bearing length $L = 80$ mm
- Radial clearance $c = 0.1$ mm
- Absolute viscosity of oil $Z = 0.05$ Pa·s
- Speed $N = 1500$ rev/min
- Load $W = 25$ kN

Assume the bearing operates in the hydrodynamic regime and use **Petroff's equation** for the initial estimate.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Calculate the coefficient of friction and the friction torque using Petroff's equation. Also calculate the heat generated.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert all quantities to SI units.**

$$R = \frac{D}{2} = 0.050 \text{ m}$$

$$N = \frac{1500}{60} = 25 \text{ rev/s}$$

$$W = 25{,}000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Petroff's coefficient of friction formula.**

$$\mu_f = 2\pi^2 \cdot \frac{Z \cdot N}{P} \cdot \frac{R}{c}$$

**Step 3: Compute the bearing pressure $P$.**

$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{25{,}000}{0.080 \times 0.100} = 3{,}125{,}000 \text{ N/m}^2 = 3.125 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Substitute values into Petroff's formula.**

$$\mu_f = 2 \times (3.1416)^2 \times \frac{0.05 \times 25}{3{,}125{,}000} \times \frac{0.050}{0.0001}$$

**Step 5: Evaluate step by step.**

$$2 \pi^2 = 2 \times 9.8696 = 19.7392$$

$$\frac{Z N}{P} = \frac{0.05 \times 25}{3{,}125{,}000} = \frac{1.25}{3{,}125{,}000} = 4 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s/m}^2$$

$$\frac{R}{c} = \frac{0.050}{0.0001} = 500$$

$$\mu_f = 19.7392 \times 4 \times 10^{-7} \times 500 = 19.7392 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} = 3.948 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{\mu_f \approx 0.00395}$$

**Step 6: Friction force.**

$$F_t = \mu_f \cdot W = 0.00395 \times 25{,}000 = 98.75 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7: Friction torque.**

$$T_f = F_t \cdot R = 98.75 \times 0.050 = 4.94 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_f \approx 4.94 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 8: Heat generated.**

$$U = 2\pi R N = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 0.050 \times 25 = 7.854 \text{ m/s}$$

$$H = F_t \cdot U = 98.75 \times 7.854 = 775.6 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{H \approx 776 \text{ W} \approx 0.776 \text{ kW}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**If the bearing is required to operate strictly in the hydrodynamic regime with a coefficient of friction not exceeding 0.002, determine the minimum viscosity of oil required. State the lubrication regime in which the original bearing operates.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Set the maximum allowable $\mu_f$ and solve for $Z$.**

From Petroff's equation:

$$\mu_f = 2\pi^2 \cdot \frac{Z N}{P} \cdot \frac{R}{c}$$

Solving for $Z$:

$$Z = \frac{\mu_f \cdot P \cdot c}{2\pi^2 \cdot N \cdot R}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$Z = \frac{0.002 \times 3{,}125{,}000 \times 0.0001}{19.7392 \times 25 \times 0.050}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate numerator.**

$$\text{Numerator} = 0.002 \times 3{,}125{,}000 \times 0.0001 = 0.625 \text{ N·s/m}^2$$

**Step 4: Evaluate denominator.**

$$\text{Denominator} = 19.7392 \times 25 \times 0.050 = 24.674$$

**Step 5: Final value of $Z$.**

$$Z = \frac{0.625}{24.674} = 0.02533 \text{ Pa·s}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{\min} \approx 0.0253 \text{ Pa·s} = 25.3 \text{ cP}}$$

**Step 6: Identify the lubrication regime.**

Since the computed $\mu_f = 0.00395$ in the original bearing is in the range $0.001 - 0.01$, the bearing operates in the **hydrodynamic (thick film) regime**. However, this is a Petroff estimate (concentric journal). The Raimondi-Boyd analysis with actual eccentricity would give a more accurate — and slightly different — value.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q: [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]**

A journal bearing is required to support a load of **20 kN** at a speed of **1200 rev/min**. The journal diameter is **80 mm** and the bearing length is **60 mm**. The radial clearance ratio is $c/R = 0.001$ and the oil viscosity is $Z = 0.04$ Pa·s.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Determine the friction torque, the coefficient of friction, and the power loss in the bearing using Petroff's equation. Comment on whether the bearing operates in the hydrodynamic regime.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: SI conversions.**

$$R = 0.040 \text{ m}, \quad L = 0.060 \text{ m}, \quad N = 20 \text{ rev/s}, \quad W = 20{,}000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the radial clearance.**

$$c = 0.001 \times R = 0.001 \times 0.040 = 0.00004 \text{ m} = 0.04 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the bearing pressure.**

$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{20{,}000}{0.060 \times 0.080} = 4{,}166{,}667 \text{ N/m}^2 \approx 4.17 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the coefficient of friction.**

$$\mu_f = 2\pi^2 \cdot \frac{Z N}{P} \cdot \frac{R}{c}$$

$$\mu_f = 19.7392 \times \frac{0.04 \times 20}{4{,}166{,}667} \times \frac{0.040}{0.00004}$$

$$\frac{ZN}{P} = \frac{0.8}{4{,}166{,}667} = 1.92 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$\frac{R}{c} = 1000$$

$$\mu_f = 19.7392 \times 1.92 \times 10^{-7} \times 1000 = 19.7392 \times 1.92 \times 10^{-4} = 3.79 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{\mu_f \approx 0.00379}$$

**Step 5: Friction force.**

$$F_t = \mu_f \cdot W = 0.00379 \times 20{,}000 = 75.8 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6: Friction torque.**

$$T_f = F_t \cdot R = 75.8 \times 0.040 = 3.03 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_f \approx 3.03 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 7: Power loss.**

$$U = 2\pi R N = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 0.040 \times 20 = 5.027 \text{ m/s}$$

$$P_{\text{loss}} = T_f \cdot \omega = T_f \cdot 2\pi N = 3.03 \times 2 \times 3.1416 \times 20 = 380.7 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{loss}} \approx 0.381 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 8: Regime identification.**

Since $\mu_f = 0.00379$ falls in the range $0.001 - 0.01$, the bearing operates in the **hydrodynamic (thick film) regime**. ✓

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Using the Stribeck curve approach, comment on what happens to the coefficient of friction if:**
**(i) The load is doubled while speed and viscosity remain constant.**
**(ii) The speed is halved while load and viscosity remain constant.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Recall the Stribeck number.**

The Stribeck number is $\lambda = \dfrac{Z N}{P}$.

**Case (i): Load doubled, $W \rightarrow 2W$, $P \rightarrow 2P$, $N$ and $Z$ constant.**

$$\lambda_{\text{new}} = \frac{Z N}{2P} = \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

The Stribeck number **decreases by half**. The operating point moves **to the left** on the Stribeck curve.

- If we were operating in the **hydrodynamic regime** (right branch), the new point is closer to the **minimum**, so $\mu_f$ **decreases slightly**.
- However, the friction force $F_t = \mu_f W$ actually **doubles** approximately (because $W$ doubled and $\mu_f$ changed only slightly).

**Case (ii): Speed halved, $N \rightarrow N/2$, $P$ and $Z$ constant.**

$$\lambda_{\text{new}} = \frac{Z (N/2)}{P} = \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

Again, the Stribeck number **decreases by half**. Same qualitative behaviour as Case (i).

- The operating point moves **toward the boundary region**.
- In the **hydrodynamic regime**, $\mu_f \propto N$ (from Petroff: $\mu_f = 2\pi^2 (ZN/P)(R/c)$). So halving $N$ **halves $\mu_f$**.
- Friction force $F_t = \mu_f W$ is also **halved**.
- The bearing approaches the **danger zone of boundary lubrication** where wear becomes severe.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A reduction in speed (or a reduction in $Z$ through cold oil, or an increase in load) moves the bearing toward the boundary regime. This is why **warm-up of machinery is essential** — the viscosity must be in the correct range so that $\lambda$ places the operating point on the hydrodynamic branch.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in KTU Exams — Where You Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Forgetting to convert $N$ from rev/min to rev/s, or $D$ from mm to m. This single error can give a 10× wrong answer. **Always write the SI conversion as Step 1.**
> 2. **Confusion between $Z$ and $\nu$:** $Z$ (or $\mu$) is **absolute/dynamic viscosity** in Pa·s. $\nu$ is **kinematic viscosity** in m²/s. Petroff's equation uses **dynamic viscosity**. If the problem gives kinematic viscosity, convert using $Z = \nu \times \rho$ (where $\rho$ is oil density ≈ 900 kg/m³ for mineral oil).
> 3. **Forgetting $2\pi$ in surface velocity:** $U = 2\pi R N$, not $U = R N$ or $U = \pi D N$.
> 4. **Confusing Petroff (concentric) with Raimondi-Boyd (eccentric):** Petroff assumes a concentric journal — useful only as a first estimate. Raimondi-Boyd charts use the actual eccentricity ratio $\epsilon$ and the $L/D$ ratio.
> 5. **Misidentifying the lubrication regime:** If the calculated $\mu_f$ is in the range $0.001 - 0.01$, the answer is **hydrodynamic**. If $0.1 - 0.4$, it is **boundary**. If in between, it is **mixed**. The question often asks you to identify this explicitly.
> 6. **Heat dissipation units:** $H = F_t \cdot U$ gives heat in **Watts**. The examiner expects W or J/s — not N·m/s (which is the same but ambiguous).
> 7. **Sommerfeld number substitution:** The Sommerfeld number $S = (\mu N / P)(R/c)$ is dimensionless. Some students forget the $(R/c)$ term and use $S = \mu N / P$ — this is wrong.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision Points (Save This For The Night Before Your KTU ESE):**
>
> ☐ **Bush bearing** = plain journal bearing with sliding contact; clearance $c = 0.001D$ to $0.002D$.
> ☐ **Lubrication** reduces friction, wear, and heat.
> ☐ **Thick film (hydrodynamic) lubrication** — surfaces fully separated by oil; low μ (0.001-0.01); μ **increases** with $Z$, $N$, and $R/c$; **decreases** with $P$.
> ☐ **Thin film (boundary) lubrication** — asperity contact; high μ (0.1-0.4); μ **independent** of $N$ and $Z$; governed by $\mu = \tau_s / p_y$.
> ☐ **Mixed lubrication** — transition zone; μ reaches **minimum value**.
> ☐ **Stribeck curve** plots μ vs $ZN/P$ — three regimes visible.
> ☐ **Petroff's equation** (concentric, light-load estimate):
>       $F_t = 4\pi^2 Z N R^3 L / c$
>       $T_f = 4\pi^2 Z N R^4 L / c$
>       $\mu_f = 2\pi^2 (ZN/P)(R/c)$
> ☐ **Hydrodynamic load:** $W = 2\pi Z U L R^3 S / c$ where $S$ is Sommerfeld number from Raimondi-Boyd charts.
> ☐ **Reynolds equation** generates pressure in the converging wedge of the oil film.
> ☐ **Friction torque and heat:** $T_f = \mu_f W R$, $H = F_t U = T_f \omega$.
> ☐ **Operating regime** is identified by μ value: $\mu < 0.01$ = hydrodynamic; $\mu > 0.1$ = boundary; in between = mixed.
> ☐ **Key dimensionless groups:** Stribeck number $\lambda = ZN/P$; Sommerfeld number $S = (ZN/P)(R/c)$.
> ☐ **Shaft surface velocity:** $U = \pi D N$ (where N is rev/s); circumference swept per second.
> ☐ **For high loads or low speeds**, viscosity must be increased to maintain hydrodynamic regime.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrostatic and Hydrodynamic Bearings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT527 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hydrostatic and Hydrodynamic Bearings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Bearings in Rotating Machinery

A **bearing** is a machine element that constrains relative motion between two parts and reduces friction between moving components, typically by allowing smooth sliding or rolling motion. In the context of KTU's *Dynamics of Machinery*, bearings form the foundation of all rotating systems, ranging from small electric motors to massive turbine shafts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Bearings are broadly classified into two families based on the **Lubrication Mechanism**:
> 1. **Hydrostatic Bearings** (Externally Pressurized)
> 2. **Hydrodynamic Bearings** (Self-Acting / Fluid Film)

---

## 1.2 Hydrostatic Bearings — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> A **hydrostatic bearing** is a bearing in which the lubricant is supplied to the bearing surfaces under pressure from an *external source* (such as a high-pressure pump), and the load is supported entirely by this pressurized fluid film — *irrespective of any relative motion* between the surfaces.

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — The Hovercraft Principle

Imagine an **air hockey table**: tiny jets of air push the puck upward so it floats with zero contact. Now replace the air with oil, and the puck with a heavy rotating shaft. That is exactly how a hydrostatic bearing works — an external pump forces oil into recessed pockets in the bearing, creating a pressure field that lifts the shaft and supports it without any rotation required. The shaft can be perfectly stationary and still be floating on a film of oil.

### 1.2.2 Construction Features

- **Recessed pockets (compensators)** are machined into the bearing liner.
- Each pocket is connected to a **metering device** (capillary, orifice, or constant-flow valve).
- A **high-pressure pump** supplies clean, filtered oil.
- **Throttle valves** maintain constant flow under varying loads.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Engineering Constant:** Standard oil viscosity for industrial hydrostatic bearings: **μ = 0.01 – 0.05 Pa·s (at 40 °C)**, with supply pressures between **5 – 35 bar**.

---

## 1.3 Hydrodynamic Bearings — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> A **hydrodynamic bearing** (also called a *self-acting* or *fluid-film bearing*) is one in which the pressure required to separate the journal and the bearing is **generated by the relative motion of the surfaces themselves**, causing the lubricant to be dragged into a converging wedge of the clearance space.

### 1.3.1 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Skier Principle

Picture a water-skier gliding on a single ski: as the boat pulls them forward, water is dragged *under* the ski, building up pressure that lifts the skier above the surface. If the skier stops moving, they sink. Similarly, a hydrodynamic journal bearing needs the journal to **rotate** to drag oil into the narrowing gap, where the converging geometry generates a pressure spike that lifts the shaft. **No motion = no lift = metal-to-metal contact.**

### 1.3.2 Physical Mechanism — The Wedge Effect

For pressure to develop, the oil film thickness **h** must *vary* in the direction of motion. This is achieved by:

1. **Offsetting the journal** slightly above (or below) the geometric center → creates a converging-diverging wedge.
2. **Tilting pad geometry** → each pad acts as a separate wedge.
3. **Step or tapered thrust collars** → for axial hydrodynamic support.

The maximum pressure develops where the film is **minimum** (vis-à-vis the converging side), exactly as dictated by the **Reynolds Equation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — Stribeck Curve Region:**
> Hydrodynamic bearings operate in the **full-fluid-film lubrication regime**, where the lambda ratio (λ = h/σ) is greater than **3**, ensuring complete surface separation. The coefficient of friction here is in the range **f = 0.001 – 0.01**.

---

## 1.4 Comparative Snapshot

| Feature | Hydrostatic Bearing | Hydrodynamic Bearing |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure source | External pump | Self-generated by motion |
| Requires rotation? | **No** | **Yes (mandatory)** |
| Starting friction | Very low | High (boundary contact at start) |
| Speed range | 0 – very high | Medium to very high |
| Load capacity | Very high | High |
| Cost & complexity | Expensive (pump, filters) | Simple & cheap |
| Typical applications | Heavy roll mills, machine-tool spindles, telescopes | I.C. engines, turbines, electric motors |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hydrostatic Bearing — Working Theory

### 2.1.1 Operating Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. A high-pressure pump delivers lubricant through a filter to the bearing's supply manifold.
2. The oil passes through a **metering element** (orifice, capillary, or restrictor).
3. The flow enters the **recessed pocket**, where the gap (h₀) is much larger than the land gap (h).
4. A hydrostatic pressure is established across the recess — this pressure supports the applied load.
5. The journal "floats" on the oil film; the load-carrying capacity depends on supply pressure, recess area, and the restrictor geometry.

### 2.1.2 Load-Carrying Capacity of a Circular Step Bearing

For a simple thrust bearing with circular recess of radius **R**, land radius **R₁**, recess depth **h₀**, and film thickness **h** under the land:

$$W = \frac{\pi \mu Q}{2 h^{2}} \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{R}{R_1} \right)^{4} \right]$$

Where:
- **W** = Load capacity (N)
- **Q** = Volumetric flow (m³/s)
- **μ** = Dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- **h** = Land film thickness (m)
- **R, R₁** = Recess and land radii (m)

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this work?**
> The orifice creates a *pressure drop* proportional to flow rate. As load W increases, the journal moves closer, flow Q decreases (because flow ∝ h³), and pressure rises automatically — creating a **self-regulating system**.

### 2.1.3 Engineering Applications

- **Rolling mills** for steel (load > 10⁷ N)
- **Turbine generators** (vertical shaft support)
- **CNC machine tool spindles** (precision < 1 µm)
- **Radio telescopes and antenna pedestals** (zero-speed positioning)

---

## 2.2 Hydrodynamic Bearing — Theory of Lubrication

### 2.2.1 The Two Pillars of Fluid-Film Theory

The entire mathematical framework of hydrodynamic bearings rests on two equations:

1. **Reynolds Equation** — Governs the pressure distribution in the oil film.
2. **Petroff's Equation** — Provides the friction in a perfectly concentric (no-load) journal.

### 2.2.2 Operational Logic of a Journal Bearing

1. The journal is supported inside a cylindrical bearing with a small radial clearance **c**.
2. Under load, the journal **eccentrically displaces** by an amount **e**, creating a minimum film thickness **h₀ = c − e** and a maximum film thickness **h_max = c + e**.
3. As the journal rotates at angular velocity **ω**, viscous oil is dragged into the converging wedge.
4. A pressure distribution develops, with **peak pressure** at the location of minimum film thickness.
5. The integrated pressure over the bearing surface equals the applied load.

### 2.2.3 Petroff's Friction Equation (Concentric Journal, No Load)

Assuming a perfectly centered journal, the velocity profile in the film is linear (simple Couette flow):

$$\tau = \mu \frac{U}{h}$$

The friction torque per unit length:

$$T = \frac{4 \pi^{2} \mu N r^{3} L}{60 \, c}$$

The coefficient of friction (where **P = W/(L·D)**):

$$f = 2 \pi^{2} \frac{\mu N r}{P \, c} \times \frac{1}{60}$$

Simplified form using **U = πDN/60**:

$$f = \frac{2 \pi^{3} \mu r^{3} N}{P \, c} \times \frac{1}{30}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Petroff's Key Result:** Friction is **independent of load** (P cancels from numerator and denominator for an unloaded bearing). It depends *linearly* on viscosity and speed.

### 2.2.4 Reynolds Equation — The Master Equation

For an incompressible Newtonian fluid in a thin film, the 2D steady-state Reynolds equation is:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left( h^{3} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left( h^{3} \frac{\partial p}{\partial z} \right) = 6 \mu U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

This is the governing equation for all hydrodynamic bearings, including journal and thrust bearings.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Variables & Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Velocity of journal surface | $U = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s; D(m), N(rpm) |
| 2 | Mean film pressure | $P = \dfrac{W}{L \cdot D}$ | Pa |
| 3 | Petroff's friction coefficient | $f = \dfrac{2 \pi^{2} \mu N r}{P c}$ × $\dfrac{1}{60}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Petroff's friction power loss | $H_{loss} = f \cdot W \cdot U$ | W (watts) |
| 5 | Friction torque (Petroff) | $T = \dfrac{4 \pi^{2} \mu N r^{3} L}{60 c}$ | N·m |
| 6 | Radial clearance | $c = R - r$ | m |
| 7 | Eccentricity ratio | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{e}{c}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 8 | Minimum film thickness | $h_0 = c(1 - \varepsilon)$ | m |
| 9 | **Sommerfeld Number** | $S = \dfrac{r}{c} \cdot \dfrac{\mu N}{P}$ × $\dfrac{2\pi}{60}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Sommerfeld (alternate) | $S = \dfrac{\mu N}{P}\left( \dfrac{r}{c} \right)^{2}$ × $\dfrac{2\pi}{60}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Reynolds Equation (2D steady) | $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\left( h^{3} \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} \right) + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}\left( h^{3} \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial z} \right) = 6 \mu U \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x}$ | Pa |
| 12 | Hydrostatic load capacity | $W = \dfrac{\pi \mu Q}{2 h^{2}} \left[ 1 - \left( \dfrac{R}{R_1} \right)^{4} \right]$ | N |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Use of the Sommerfeld Number:**
> It is a *dimensionless performance index*. For a safe journal-bearing design, the operating Sommerfeld number must lie in the range **0.001 ≤ S ≤ 1.0**, depending on the chosen **ε ratio** from the Sommerfeld chart.

---

## 2.4 Why These Theories Matter in Real Engineering

| **Theory** | **Real-World Use** |
|---|---|
| Petroff's Law | Provides the *lower bound* on friction; used in highly loaded, lightly loaded bearings like electric motor shafts. |
| Reynolds Equation | Forms the basis of **CFD/FEA software** used by SKF, Timken, and Schaeffler for bearing design. |
| Sommerfeld Number | Standard design parameter quoted on bearing datasheets worldwide. |
| Hydrostatic Theory | Enables *zero-speed load support* in machine tools, where static friction would otherwise cause positional drift. |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Reynolds Equation (2D, Steady State)

This is the **most important derivation** in the entire Module 2 syllabus and is a guaranteed 7–8 mark question in every KTU University Exam.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Navier-Stokes Equations

For a Newtonian fluid with constant viscosity, incompressible, with negligible body forces:

$$\rho \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} \right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \left( \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial z^{2}} \right) + \rho g_x$$

### 3.1.2 Standard Assumptions (KTU High-Yield — must be memorized)

1. **Steady flow** → ∂/∂t = 0.
2. **Incompressible fluid** → ρ = constant.
3. **Newtonian fluid** → constant μ.
4. **Thin film** → h ≪ L and h ≪ B. Therefore, ∂p/∂y = 0 (pressure does not vary across the film).
5. **No slip** at the bearing surfaces.
6. **Laminar flow** (no turbulence).
7. **Negligible body forces** → ρg terms dropped.
8. **Inertia terms are negligible** compared to viscous terms (creeping flow, very small Reynolds number in the film).
9. **No flow across the bearing boundaries** (sealed sides) → w = 0 at boundaries, but ∂p/∂z ≠ 0 inside.
10. **Constant viscosity** across the film (isothermal operation).

### 3.1.3 Simplified Navier-Stokes Equation

After applying the assumptions, the x-momentum equation reduces to:

$$0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}}$$

Similarly for the z-direction:

$$0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} + \mu \frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial y^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> These can each be integrated *twice* with respect to **y** to give the velocity profiles.

### 3.1.4 Integration of x-Momentum

Integrating once:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{1}{\mu} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} y + C_1$$

Integrating again:

$$u = \frac{1}{2\mu} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} y^{2} + C_1 y + C_2$$

### 3.1.5 Applying Boundary Conditions

**BC 1:** At the bearing surface (y = 0), the surface is stationary, so **u = 0**.

$$0 = 0 + 0 + C_2 \implies C_2 = 0$$

**BC 2:** At the journal surface (y = h), the journal moves with velocity U in the x-direction, so **u = U**.

$$U = \frac{1}{2\mu} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} h^{2} + C_1 h$$

$$C_1 = \frac{U}{h} - \frac{1}{2\mu} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} h$$

### 3.1.6 Velocity Profile — Final Form

Substituting C₁ and C₂ back:

$$u = \frac{1}{2\mu} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} (y^{2} - y h) + \frac{U y}{h}$$

The first term is the **Poiseuille (pressure-driven) flow**; the second is the **Couette (drag-driven) flow**.

### 3.1.7 Volumetric Flow Rate per Unit Width

The flow rate per unit width in the x-direction:

$$q_x = \int_0^h u \, dy = \int_0^h \left[ \frac{1}{2\mu} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} (y^{2} - y h) + \frac{U y}{h} \right] dy$$

$$q_x = \frac{1}{2\mu} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \left[ \frac{y^{3}}{3} - \frac{y^{2} h}{2} \right]_0^h + \frac{U}{h} \left[ \frac{y^{2}}{2} \right]_0^h$$

$$q_x = \frac{1}{2\mu} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \left( \frac{h^{3}}{3} - \frac{h^{3}}{2} \right) + \frac{U h}{2}$$

$$q_x = -\frac{h^{3}}{12 \mu} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{U h}{2}$$

Similarly, for the z-direction (no surface motion in z, so the Couette term vanishes):

$$q_z = -\frac{h^{3}}{12 \mu} \frac{\partial p}{\partial z}$$

### 3.1.8 Continuity Equation — The Critical Step

For an incompressible fluid, **mass (volume) is conserved** in the control volume of the bearing. The net flow out of an elemental slice **dx · dz** must equal the rate of change of film volume:

$$\frac{\partial q_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial q_z}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial h}{\partial t} = 0$$

### 3.1.9 Substituting and Simplifying

Differentiating qx and qz and substituting:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left( -\frac{h^{3}}{12\mu} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{U h}{2} \right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left( -\frac{h^{3}}{12\mu} \frac{\partial p}{\partial z} \right) + \frac{\partial h}{\partial t} = 0$$

Multiplying through by **−12 μ**:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left( h^{3} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left( h^{3} \frac{\partial p}{\partial z} \right) = 6 \mu U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} + 12 \mu \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}$$

### 3.1.10 Final Reynolds Equation (2D, Unsteady, Incompressible)

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left( h^{3} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left( h^{3} \frac{\partial p}{\partial z} \right) = 6 \mu U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} + 12 \mu \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}}$$

For **steady state** (∂h/∂t = 0):

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left( h^{3} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left( h^{3} \frac{\partial p}{\partial z} \right) = 6 \mu U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning:** The right-hand side is the *forcing function* — it is non-zero **only if there is surface motion AND a converging wedge** (i.e., ∂h/∂x ≠ 0). No motion OR no wedge → no pressure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Petroff's Equation

### 3.2.1 Geometry & Assumption

Journal of radius **r**, bearing radius **R**, radial clearance **c = R − r ≪ r**. The journal is **perfectly concentric** (no load, ε = 0). The film thickness is uniform: **h = c**.

The journal rotates at **N rpm** → surface velocity U = πDN/60 = πdN/60.

### 3.2.2 Shear Stress in the Film

Because pressure is constant (no load) and only Couette flow exists:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = \mu \frac{U}{c}$$

### 3.2.3 Friction Force on Journal Surface

Per unit area, the journal surface experiences shear **τ**. For a length L of bearing:

$$\text{Friction force} = \tau \times (2 \pi r L) = \frac{2 \pi \mu U r L}{c}$$

### 3.2.4 Friction Torque

$$T = F_{friction} \times r = \frac{2 \pi \mu U r^{2} L}{c}$$

Substituting U = πDN/60 (and noting D = 2r):

$$T = \frac{2 \pi \mu L r^{2}}{c} \cdot \frac{\pi (2r) N}{60} = \frac{4 \pi^{2} \mu L r^{3} N}{60 \, c}$$

### 3.2.5 Coefficient of Friction

$$f = \frac{F_{friction}}{W} = \frac{2 \pi \mu U r L / c}{P \cdot L \cdot D} = \frac{2 \pi \mu U r}{P \cdot c \cdot D}$$

Substituting U = πDN/60 and D = 2r:

$$f = \frac{2 \pi \mu r}{P c (2r)} \cdot \frac{\pi (2r) N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi^{2} \mu r N}{P c \cdot 60}$$

$$\boxed{f = \frac{2 \pi^{2} \mu r N}{60 \, P \, c}}$$

### 3.2.6 Analytical Insight

- **f ∝ μN/Pc** — high viscosity or high speed increases friction, while high load reduces it.
- The result is *independent of bearing length L* — Petroff's law treats the bearing as infinitely long.
- Petroff's law is a **lower bound** on friction; real bearings (with eccentricity) have slightly higher friction.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Style)

**Problem:**
A 360° hydrodynamic journal bearing has the following data:
- Journal diameter: D = 100 mm
- Radial clearance: c = 0.1 mm
- Length: L = 100 mm
- Speed: N = 1440 rpm
- Viscosity: μ = 0.05 Pa·s
- Load: W = 5000 N

**Calculate** the Petroff coefficient of friction and the power loss.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Journal radius:
$$r = D/2 = 0.050 \text{ m}$$

Step 2 — Surface velocity:
$$U = \frac{\pi D N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.1 \times 1440}{60} = 7.5398 \text{ m/s}$$

Step 3 — Mean bearing pressure:
$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{5000}{0.1 \times 0.1} = 5 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 0.5 \text{ MPa}$$

Step 4 — Petroff's coefficient of friction:
$$f = \frac{2 \pi^{2} \mu r N}{60 \, P \, c} = \frac{2 \pi^{2} \times 0.05 \times 0.050 \times 1440}{60 \times 5 \times 10^{5} \times 0.0001}$$

$$f = \frac{2 \times 9.8696 \times 0.05 \times 0.050 \times 1440}{60 \times 5 \times 10^{5} \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$f = \frac{71.061}{3000} = 0.0237$$

Step 5 — Friction force:
$$F_f = f \cdot W = 0.0237 \times 5000 = 118.5 \text{ N}$$

Step 6 — Power loss:
$$H = F_f \times U = 118.5 \times 7.5398 = 893.5 \text{ W} \approx 0.894 \text{ kW}$$

**Answer:** f ≈ 0.0237, Power loss ≈ 0.894 kW.

> [!TIP]
> **Self-Check:** For typical KTU numericals, the answer for f should be in the range **0.001 to 0.05**. If you get something wildly different, recheck your decimal placement in the pressure and clearance terms.
