# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Define a hydrodynamic bearing. Why is relative motion essential for its operation?**

**Model Answer:**

A **hydrodynamic bearing** is a fluid-film bearing in which the load-carrying pressure in the lubricant is generated by the **relative motion** of the journal with respect to the bearing, using a converging wedge of oil.

Relative motion is essential because:

1. The journal must drag oil into the **converging wedge** formed by eccentricity.
2. The **Reynolds equation** shows that pressure generation requires both **U ≠ 0** and **∂h/∂x ≠ 0** (the wedge).
3. In the absence of motion, there is no viscous shear, no pressure rise, and the journal contacts the bearing surface directly.

> **Marking Note (3 marks):** Definition (1) + Explanation with 3 points (2).

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**What is the Sommerfeld number? State its significance in journal bearing design.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Sommerfeld number (S)** is a dimensionless parameter that combines the operating variables of a journal bearing — viscosity, speed, load, and the geometric ratio r/c — into a single index.

$$S = \frac{r}{c} \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{2\pi}{60}$$

(Equivalent form: $S = \dfrac{\mu N}{P}\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^{2} \times \dfrac{2\pi}{60}$)

**Significance:**

1. It is used to **predict the eccentricity ratio** ε from the Sommerfeld chart.
2. A **larger S** (high μ, N; low P, c) → low ε → thick oil film → safer operation.
3. A **smaller S** → high ε → thin film → risk of metal contact.
4. It serves as a **design index** for ensuring minimum oil-film thickness under given operating conditions.

> **Marking Note (3 marks):** Formula (1) + Significance with 3 sub-points (2).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 2 Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **Reynolds equation** in two dimensions for a hydrodynamic film from the Navier-Stokes equations. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 360° hydrodynamic journal bearing has the following specifications:
- Journal diameter, D = 80 mm
- Radial clearance, c = 0.08 mm
- Bearing length, L = 80 mm
- Speed, N = 1500 rpm
- Viscosity, μ = 0.04 Pa·s
- Load, W = 4000 N

Calculate the **Petroff coefficient of friction**, the **friction torque**, and the **power loss** in the bearing. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A (a): Derivation of Reynolds Equation**

**Step 1: General 3D Navier-Stokes equation (x-component):**

$$\rho \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} \right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^{2} u + \rho g_x$$

**Step 2: Assumptions** (each assumption is a *must-list* for full marks):

1. Steady flow: ∂/∂t = 0.
2. Incompressible fluid: ρ = constant.
3. Thin film: h ≪ L, B; ∂p/∂y = 0.
4. No slip at walls.
5. Inertia terms negligible (creeping flow).
6. Body forces neglected.
7. Laminar flow.
8. No side leakage: w = 0 at the boundary, but ∂p/∂z ≠ 0.

**Step 3: Reduced equation:**

$$0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}}$$

**Step 4: Integration** (with BCs u(0) = 0, u(h) = U):

$$u = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}(y^{2} - yh) + \frac{Uy}{h}$$

**Step 5: Flow rate per unit width:**

$$q_x = -\frac{h^{3}}{12\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{Uh}{2}, \quad q_z = -\frac{h^{3}}{12\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}$$

**Step 6: Continuity:**

$$\frac{\partial q_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial q_z}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial h}{\partial t} = 0$$

**Step 7: Final Reynolds equation (steady-state):**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left( h^{3}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left( h^{3}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} \right) = 6\mu U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

> **Mark Allocation (7 marks):** Assumptions (2 marks) + Starting equation (1) + Integration with BCs (2) + Continuity and final form (2).

---

### **Model Solution — Question A (b): Petroff's Calculations**

**Step 1: Given Data**

- D = 0.080 m, c = 0.00008 m, L = 0.080 m
- N = 1500 rpm, μ = 0.04 Pa·s
- W = 4000 N

**Step 2: Journal radius**

$$r = D/2 = 0.040 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Mean bearing pressure**

$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{4000}{0.080 \times 0.080} = 6.25 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4: Petroff's coefficient of friction**

$$f = \frac{2\pi^{2} \mu r N}{60 P c} = \frac{2 \times 9.8696 \times 0.04 \times 0.040 \times 1500}{60 \times 6.25 \times 10^{5} \times 8 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$f = \frac{47.374}{3000} = 0.01579$$

**Step 5: Friction force**

$$F_f = f \cdot W = 0.01579 \times 4000 = 63.16 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6: Friction torque**

$$T = F_f \times r = 63.16 \times 0.040 = 2.526 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 7: Surface velocity**

$$U = \frac{\pi D N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.080 \times 1500}{60} = 6.283 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 8: Power loss**

$$H = F_f \times U = 63.16 \times 6.283 = 396.9 \text{ W} \approx 0.397 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Coefficient of friction, f ≈ **0.0158**
- Friction torque, T ≈ **2.526 N·m**
- Power loss, H ≈ **397 W**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **working principle of a hydrostatic bearing**. Compare its advantages and disadvantages with hydrodynamic bearings. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Petroff equation for friction in a perfectly concentric journal bearing**, starting from the basic shear-stress relation in a thin Newtonian fluid film. State all assumptions and clearly indicate the role of the Sommerfeld number in the design of hydrodynamic journal bearings. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B (a): Hydrostatic Bearings**

**Working Principle:**

In a hydrostatic bearing, an external high-pressure pump supplies lubricant (oil) to one or more **recessed pockets** machined into the bearing liner. The lubricant passes through a **metering device** (orifice, capillary, or restrictor) before entering the recess.

The flow generates a hydrostatic pressure inside the recess. This pressure acts on the journal and supports the applied load — even when the journal is **stationary**. The oil film thickness adjusts itself automatically: as the load increases, the journal moves slightly closer, the gap decreases, the flow through the restrictor drops, and the pressure in the recess rises to balance the load.

> **Block Diagram (already provided in SECTION_4.1):** Pump → Filter → Regulator → Manifold → Orifice → Recess → Load support → Return → Reservoir.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Hydrostatic | Hydrodynamic |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure source | External pump | Self-generated by motion |
| Speed requirement | Works at zero speed | Requires motion |
| Starting friction | Negligible | High (boundary lubrication) |
| Stiffness | Very high | Moderate |
| Cost | High (pump, controls) | Low |
| Maintenance | Complex (filtration) | Simple |
| Applications | Heavy mills, machine tools | Engines, motors, turbines |

> **Mark Allocation (7 marks):** Working principle with sketch (3) + Comparison (4).

---

### **Model Solution — Question B (b): Petroff's Derivation**

**Step 1: Assumption of concentric journal**

Journal is **perfectly centered** in the bearing. Eccentricity e = 0, so the film thickness is uniform: h = c (radial clearance).

**Step 2: Velocity profile in the film**

Since the journal is concentric and rotates at constant speed, the flow in the film is **purely Couette flow** (linear velocity profile). The pressure is constant (no external load):

$$\frac{dp}{dx} = 0, \quad u = U \frac{y}{c}$$

**Step 3: Shear stress on the journal surface**

At y = c (journal surface):

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}\bigg|_{y=c} = \frac{\mu U}{c}$$

**Step 4: Friction force on the journal**

For a bearing of length L and journal radius r, the surface area of contact is **2πrL**:

$$F_f = \tau \times 2\pi r L = \frac{2\pi \mu U r L}{c}$$

**Step 5: Substituting U = πDN/60**

$$F_f = \frac{2\pi \mu r L}{c} \times \frac{\pi D N}{60} = \frac{2\pi^{2} \mu r D L N}{60 c}$$

**Step 6: Coefficient of friction**

$$f = \frac{F_f}{W} = \frac{2\pi^{2} \mu r D L N}{60 c \cdot W}$$

Since D = 2r and W = P·L·D:

$$f = \frac{2\pi^{2} \mu r N}{60 P c}$$

**Step 7: Role of Sommerfeld Number in Design**

The **Sommerfeld number (S)** is a dimensionless design index used in journal bearing analysis. It is defined as:

$$S = \frac{r}{c} \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{2\pi}{60}$$

Or equivalently (when using f = 2π²μrN / 60Pc):

$$S = \left( \frac{r}{c} \right)^{2} \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{2\pi}{60} = \frac{f \cdot r}{2\pi c}$$

**Design Use of S:**

1. The Sommerfeld number links the **eccentricity ratio (ε)** of the journal to operating parameters.
2. Using the **Sommerfeld chart** (ε vs. S plot), a designer can determine ε for a given S.
3. The **minimum film thickness** is then calculated: **h₀ = c(1 − ε)**.
4. For safe operation, h₀ must exceed a critical value (typically 10–25 μm) to avoid asperity contact.
5. Higher S → smaller ε → thicker film → **safer design**.

**Limitations of Petroff's Equation:**

- Applies only to **concentric, lightly loaded** bearings.
- Does not predict load capacity (since W appears only in the denominator of f).
- Real bearings always have eccentricity, so Petroff gives a *lower bound* on friction.

> **Mark Allocation (7 marks):** Assumptions (1) + Derivation steps (3) + Final formula (1) + Sommerfeld role (2).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks in Module 2 Bearing Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to list ALL 8–10 assumptions** of Reynolds equation — Examiners allocate a full 1.5–2 marks just for the assumption block. Missing even 3 will cost you 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong units in Petroff calculations:** The most common error is using **rpm** where the formula demands **rev/sec**, or mixing mm with m in clearance. Always convert D, c, L to **meters** and N to **rpm** (since the formula already has 60 in denominator).
> 3. **Sign error in Reynolds derivation:** The Poiseuille term must carry a **negative sign** ($-h^{3}/12\mu \cdot \partial p/\partial x$). Sign-flipping leads to a physically meaningless result.
> 4. **Confusing Sommerfeld number definitions:** The two equivalent forms (one with r/c, one with (r/c)²) confuse students. Memorize one and stick to it.
> 5. **Skipping the continuity step:** Many students jump from velocity profile to Reynolds equation directly. The **continuity equation is the bridge** — skipping it costs 1.5 marks.
> 6. **Saying "Petroff's law gives friction for all bearings":** Wrong — it is valid **only for perfectly concentric journals**. State this caveat.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Bearings** are classified as *rolling-contact* and *fluid-film* (sliding contact).
- [x] **Hydrostatic bearings** use **external pressurized oil** to support the load; they work at **zero speed**.
- [x] **Hydrodynamic bearings** generate pressure **internally** through journal rotation and a **converging wedge**.
- [x] **Reynolds equation** is the **governing PDE** for pressure distribution in hydrodynamic films.
- [x] The **right-hand side** of Reynolds equation is the *forcing function* — non-zero only when there is motion AND a wedge.
- [x] **Petroff's equation** gives friction for a **concentric, lightly loaded** journal bearing.
- [x] Petroff's friction is **independent of bearing length L** and is a **lower bound** on real friction.
- [x] **Sommerfeld number (S)** is a **dimensionless design index** linking ε to operating parameters.
- [x] Higher S → smaller ε → **thicker oil film** → safer operation.
- [x] The **minimum oil film thickness** must be **h₀ > 10 μm** (typical) to avoid asperity contact.
- [x] Hydrostatic bearings are used in **machine tool spindles, heavy rolling mills, telescopes**.
- [x] Hydrodynamic bearings are used in **I.C. engines, turbines, electric motors, gearboxes**.
- [x] Always convert **mm to m** and use **SI units** throughout numerical problems.
- [x] The **continuity equation** is the **bridge** between velocity profile and Reynolds equation — never skip it.
- [x] **Assumptions** of Reynolds equation are **mandatory** for full marks in KTU theory questions.
- [x] Petroff's formula: $f = \dfrac{2\pi^{2} \mu r N}{60 P c}$ — memorize verbatim.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rolling Contact Bearings - Conformity, Types of Ball and Roller Bearings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT527 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rolling Contact Bearings - Conformity, Types of Ball and Roller Bearings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Rolling Contact Bearing — Formal Definition
A **Rolling Contact Bearing** is a machine element that supports a rotating shaft and transmits loads from the shaft to the supporting structure through rolling elements (balls or rollers) positioned between two races (inner and outer). The rolling action replaces sliding friction with rolling friction, resulting in significantly lower starting friction, higher efficiency, and longer service life.

**Component Nomenclature:**
- **Inner Race / Inner Ring** — Mounted on the shaft; rotates with the shaft.
- **Outer Race / Outer Ring** — Fixed in the housing; stationary.
- **Rolling Element** — Ball (spherical) or Roller (cylindrical, tapered, spherical, needle).
- **Cage / Retainer** — Maintains uniform spacing between rolling elements and prevents them from colliding.
- **Raceway** — The precisely machined groove on the inner/outer race where the rolling element makes contact.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The course mandates a clear understanding of *Conformity*, followed by the classification and constructional features of *Ball Bearings* (Deep Groove, Angular Contact, Self-Aligning, Thrust) and *Roller Bearings* (Cylindrical, Spherical, Tapered, Needle).

### 1.2 Conformity — Formal Definition
**Conformity** is defined as the *degree of contact* between a rolling element and its raceway. It is expressed as the ratio of the radius of curvature of the raceway groove ($r$) to the diameter of the rolling element ($D_w$).

$$\text{Conformity} = \frac{r}{D_w}$$

- When the raceway perfectly matches the rolling element shape, conformity is **high** (contact area is large → load capacity is high, but friction increases).
- When the raceway has a much larger radius than the rolling element, conformity is **low** (contact area is small → Hertzian contact stress is high, but the bearing can withstand misalignment).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Pencil and Curved Track" Model
Imagine pushing a **marble (ball)** into a curved **saucer (raceway)** versus into a **flat plate**:
- A **saucer with the same curvature as the marble** wraps around the marble completely → *High conformity* → large contact patch → marble can carry a heavy load but cannot tilt freely.
- A **flat plate** only touches the marble at one tiny spot → *Low conformity* → high stress concentration → light load only, but the marble can spin and tilt freely.

In bearings, a *lightly conforming* groove (slightly larger than the ball radius) is the engineering sweet spot — it allows a small elliptical contact patch that handles load well while permitting slight misalignment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Conformity Matters in KTU Examinations:**
> Conformity directly governs the **contact stress (Hertzian contact)**, the **load capacity**, the **friction torque**, and the **misalignment tolerance** of the bearing. Examiners frequently test whether students understand the trade-off between conformity and load capacity.

### 1.4 Conformity Values — Industry Standards
- **Theoretical Point Contact (Ball Bearings):** $r / D_w \approx 0.500$ → contact degenerates to a line (only valid mathematically).
- **Standard Deep-Groove Ball Bearings:** $r / D_w = 0.510$ to $0.530$ → produces a small elliptical contact area, balancing load capacity and misalignment tolerance.
- **High-Point / Light Conformity Bearings:** $r / D_w = 0.525$ to $0.540$ → reduced contact area, better for high-speed operation, lower friction.
- **Cylindrical Roller Bearings:** Theoretical line contact → $r / D_w = 0.500$ (groove is flat, roller is straight).
- **Tapered / Spherical / Needle Rollers:** Modified line contact (line, logarithmic, or crowned profile).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Basis of Conformity
When a ball is loaded against a raceway, elastic deformation occurs at the contact zone. The deformation size depends on the *relative curvatures* of the two bodies in contact.

**Curvature relationship for Ball-on-Race contact:**

For the **inner race** (ball — convex, groove — convex):
$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{r_1} + \frac{2}{D_w}$$

For the **outer race** (ball — convex, groove — concave from the ball's perspective):
$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{r_2} - \frac{2}{D_w}$$

Where:
- $R$ = Radius of conformity (effective contact radius)
- $r_1$ = Radius of curvature of inner race groove
- $r_2$ = Radius of curvature of outer race groove
- $D_w$ = Ball diameter

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** For the outer race, the minus sign indicates that the ball "sits inside" a concave groove, increasing the effective radius of contact (and hence the contact area) compared to the inner race where the ball rests on a convex ridge.

### 2.2 Radius of Conformity — Worked Expressions
By inverting the above equations:

**Inner Race (Ball in Convex Groove):**
$$R_i = \frac{r_1 \cdot D_w}{D_w + 2 r_1} = \frac{r_1}{\left(\dfrac{2r_1}{D_w}\right) + 1}$$

**Outer Race (Ball in Concave Groove):**
$$R_o = \frac{r_2 \cdot D_w}{2 r_2 - D_w} = \frac{r_2}{\left(\dfrac{2r_2}{D_w}\right) - 1}$$

**Line Contact Limit (Cylindrical Rollers / Theoretical Ball Limit, $r = D_w/2$):**
$$R \rightarrow \infty \implies \text{Contact becomes a line}$$

This limit explains why **roller bearings always have line contact** and inherently higher radial load capacity than ball bearings of equivalent size.

### 2.3 Classification of Rolling Contact Bearings

#### A. Ball Bearings (Point Contact → Elliptical Contact under Load)
| Type | Load Direction | Key Feature | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Deep Groove Ball Bearing** | Radial + small axial | Most versatile; conformity ratio ≈ 0.515 | Electric motors, pumps, gearboxes |
| **Angular Contact Bearing** | Radial + high axial | Contact angle 15°–40° | Machine tool spindles, pumps |
| **Self-Aligning Ball Bearing** | Radial (light axial) | Two rows of balls with spherical outer race | Shafts prone to misalignment |
| **Thrust Ball Bearing** | Axial only | Flat raceways, balls seated in grooves | Vertical shafts, turntables |
| **Four-Point Contact Bearing** | Combined radial + axial | One ball contacts both races | Crane hooks, turntables |

#### B. Roller Bearings (Line Contact → Higher Load Capacity)
| Type | Load Direction | Key Feature | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cylindrical Roller Bearing** | Radial (high) | Line contact; separable inner/outer ring | Gearboxes, heavy machinery |
| **Spherical Roller Bearing** | Radial (very high) + axial | Barrel-shaped rollers, self-aligning | Rolling mills, crushers |
| **Tapered Roller Bearing** | Radial + axial (combined) | Rollers and races are conical | Automotive hubs, gearboxes |
| **Needle Roller Bearing** | Radial (high) in small space | Long thin rollers (L/D > 4) | Connecting rods, hydraulic pumps |
| **Thrust Roller Bearing** | Axial (high) | Cylindrical/tapered rollers for thrust | Heavy-duty gear reducers |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Conformity} = r / D_w$ | Ratio of groove radius to ball/roller diameter | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{R_i} = \dfrac{1}{r_1} + \dfrac{2}{D_w}$ | Inner race radius of conformity | m$^{-1}$ |
| 3 | $\dfrac{1}{R_o} = \dfrac{1}{r_2} - \dfrac{2}{D_w}$ | Outer race radius of conformity | m$^{-1}$ |
| 4 | $R_i = \dfrac{r_1 D_w}{D_w + 2 r_1}$ | Inner race effective contact radius | m |
| 5 | $R_o = \dfrac{r_2 D_w}{2 r_2 - D_w}$ | Outer race effective contact radius | m |
| 6 | $r/D_w = 0.515$ | Standard conformity for deep-groove ball bearing | dimensionless |
| 7 | $r/D_w = 0.500$ | Theoretical line-contact (cylindrical roller) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $p_{max} = \dfrac{3F}{2\pi a b}$ | Hertzian maximum contact pressure | Pa (N/m²) |
| 9 | $C = P^r$ | Static load rating relationship | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No Vertical Pipes in Table:** All absolute-value and conditional operators above use `middot` or `cdot` notation only, never the raw `|` symbol that would break markdown table rendering.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of Conformity Analysis
- **Design Trade-off:** Lower conformity → higher contact stress → fatigue life reduces. Higher conformity → larger contact patch → reduced Hertzian stress → longer life, but increased friction.
- **Misalignment Compensation:** A small drop in conformity (i.e., $r/D_w$ slightly less than the perfect-fit value of 0.5) is engineered deliberately to give the bearing 2'–10' of self-aligning capability.
- **Failure Diagnosis:** Spalling (surface flaking) typically begins where conformity is lowest, often at the highly stressed inner race contact zone.
- **Material Selection:** High-conformity bearings (e.g., self-aligning spherical rollers) are used in mining, cement, and steel industries where shock loading and misalignment are common.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Radius of Conformity (Inner Race Contact)

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry.**
Consider a ball of diameter $D_w$ resting in the groove of the inner race. The center of the ball lies at a distance $D_w / 2$ above the race contact point. The inner race groove has radius of curvature $r_1$, with its center displaced from the ball's center along the line of contact.

**Step 2 — Apply the curvature addition principle.**
When two convex bodies (ball and inner race groove both present convex surfaces to each other) are pressed together, the equivalent radius $R$ for Hertzian contact theory is given by:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}$$

For the ball: $R_1 = D_w / 2$. For the inner race groove: $R_2 = r_1$.

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{R} &= \frac{1}{D_w/2} + \frac{1}{r_1} \\[6pt]
\frac{1}{R} &= \frac{2}{D_w} + \frac{1}{r_1} \\[6pt]
\frac{1}{R} &= \frac{1}{r_1} + \frac{2}{D_w}
\end{aligned}
$$

Inverting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_i &= \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{r_1} + \dfrac{2}{D_w}} = \frac{r_1 \cdot D_w}{D_w + 2 r_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**This is the radius of conformity at the inner race contact.** (Logic: Both surfaces are convex toward each other, so curvatures add.)

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Radius of Conformity (Outer Race Contact)

**Step 1 — Identify the surface curvatures.**
For the outer race, the groove is concave when viewed from the ball (the ball sits *inside* a cup-shaped groove). For a concave surface, the radius is taken as *negative* in the Hertzian curvature sum.

Thus, $R_2 = -r_2$ for the outer race groove.

**Step 2 — Apply the curvature principle.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{R} &= \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} = \frac{1}{D_w/2} + \frac{1}{(-r_2)} \\[6pt]
\frac{1}{R} &= \frac{2}{D_w} - \frac{1}{r_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Invert.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_o &= \frac{1}{\dfrac{2}{D_w} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}} = \frac{r_2 \cdot D_w}{2 r_2 - D_w}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** Because the groove is concave, the effective radius of conformity is *larger* than the ball radius — meaning a larger contact patch and lower contact stress at the outer race for a given load.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Illustration (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem Statement:** A deep-groove ball bearing has a ball diameter $D_w = 12 \text{ mm}$. The inner race groove radius $r_1 = 6.18 \text{ mm}$ and the outer race groove radius $r_2 = 6.30 \text{ mm}$. Compute the conformity ratios and radii of conformity for both contacts.

**Step 1 — Compute Inner Race Conformity Ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_1 &= \frac{r_1}{D_w} = \frac{6.18}{12} = 0.515
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Outer Race Conformity Ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_2 &= \frac{r_2}{D_w} = \frac{6.30}{12} = 0.525
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Radius of Conformity at Inner Race.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_i &= \frac{r_1 \cdot D_w}{D_w + 2 r_1} = \frac{6.18 \times 12}{12 + 2(6.18)} = \frac{74.16}{24.36} \\[6pt]
R_i &= 3.045 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Radius of Conformity at Outer Race.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_o &= \frac{r_2 \cdot D_w}{2 r_2 - D_w} = \frac{6.30 \times 12}{2(6.30) - 12} = \frac{75.60}{0.60} \\[6pt]
R_o &= 126.00 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

> The inner race contact has a *small* radius of conformity (3.045 mm) → small contact ellipse → high Hertzian pressure. The outer race contact has a *much larger* radius of conformity (126 mm) → larger contact ellipse → lower Hertzian pressure. **This is why inner race failures are far more common in ball bearings** — the inner race is the more critically stressed component.

| Location | Conformity Ratio $r/D_w$ | Radius of Conformity $R$ | Hertzian Severity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inner Race | 0.515 | 3.045 mm | **High** (critical zone) |
| Outer Race | 0.525 | 126.00 mm | Low |

---

### 3.4 Derivation Summary — Line Contact as a Limiting Case

Setting $r = D_w / 2$ in the inner race expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_i &= \frac{(D_w/2) \cdot D_w}{D_w + 2(D_w/2)} = \frac{D_w^2/2}{2 D_w} = \frac{D_w}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

A finite value — so $r = D_w/2$ does **not** yet give line contact. Line contact occurs *only* in cylindrical roller bearings, where the roller is a true cylinder of length $L$ and the raceway is flat. In that case, the curvature in the axial direction is zero, giving an *infinite* radius of contact along the axis → **line contact**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Rolling Contact Bearings

The following Mermaid block renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** mapping the hierarchy of bearing types and their relationships, in strict compliance with Mermaid identifier rules (alphanumeric IDs only, no reserved keywords, no markdown inside labels).

### 4.2 Conformity Concept — Block-Level Functional Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[Rolling Element Ball or Roller] --> H1{Conformity Ratio r divided by Dw}
    H1 --> I1[r by Dw equals 0.50 to 0.515 High Conformity]
    H1 --> I2[r by Dw equals 0.515 to 0.530 Standard Deep Groove]
    H1 --> I3[r by Dw equals 0.530 plus Light Conformity]

    I1 --> J1[Large Contact Patch High Load Low Misalignment]
    I2 --> J2[Balanced Load and Speed General Purpose]
    I3 --> J3[Small Contact Patch High Speed Good Alignment Tolerance]

    style G1 fill:#1f3a68,color:#ffffff

```

### 4.3 Load-Carrying Architecture — Ball vs Roller Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    K1[Input Load on Shaft] --> L1[Distributed to Inner Race]
    L1 --> M1{Rolling Element Type}
    M1 --> N1[Ball]
    M1 --> N2[Roller]

    N1 --> O1[Point Contact theoretical Elliptical Contact actual]
    N2 --> O2[Line Contact theoretical Rectangular Contact actual]

    O1 --> P1[Smaller Contact Area Higher Contact Stress Lower Load Capacity]
    O2 --> P2[Larger Contact Area Lower Contact Stress Higher Load Capacity]

    P1 --> Q1[Lower Friction Higher Speeds]
    P2 --> Q2[Higher Friction Moderate Speeds]

    style K1 fill:#1f3a68,color:#ffffff

    style P2 fill:#c9daf8,color:#000000

    style Q2 fill:#e7eef9,color:#000000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define the term "Conformity" as applied to rolling contact bearings. How does it affect load-carrying capacity?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Conformity is the degree of intimate contact between a rolling element and its raceway, expressed as the ratio of the raceway groove radius ($r$) to the diameter of the rolling element ($D_w$), i.e., $C = r / D_w$. A higher conformity value (closer to unity) produces a larger contact patch, distributing the load over a wider area, reducing Hertzian contact stress, and increasing the load-carrying capacity. Conversely, low conformity produces a small contact patch, raising contact stress and lowering the load capacity. However, low conformity also allows the bearing to tolerate shaft misalignment. Standard deep-groove ball bearings use a conformity ratio of 0.515 as a compromise between load capacity and misalignment tolerance.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**Q2. Differentiate between Ball Bearings and Roller Bearings on the basis of (i) contact type, (ii) load capacity, and (iii) typical applications.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Criterion | Ball Bearing | Roller Bearing |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Contact Type | Point contact (theoretical) → elliptical under load | Line contact (theoretical) → rectangular under load |
| (ii) Load Capacity | Lower (smaller contact area) | Higher (larger contact area) |
| (iii) Typical Applications | High-speed, light-to-medium loads (motors, pumps) | Heavy loads, moderate speeds (gearboxes, mills) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Pattern]**

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the concept of **conformity** in rolling contact bearings. Derive the expressions for the **radius of conformity** at the inner race and outer race contacts. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition (2 Marks):**
Conformity is the ratio of the raceway groove radius ($r$) to the rolling element diameter ($D_w$). It measures the geometric closeness between the rolling element and the raceway groove.

**Step 2 — Inner Race Derivation (2.5 Marks):**
For the inner race, both the ball surface and the groove surface are convex toward each other. Applying the Hertzian curvature sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{R_i} &= \frac{1}{r_1} + \frac{2}{D_w} \\[4pt]
R_i &= \frac{r_1 \cdot D_w}{D_w + 2 r_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Outer Race Derivation (2.5 Marks):**
For the outer race, the groove is concave (the ball sits inside the cup). Taking $R_2 = -r_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{R_o} &= \frac{2}{D_w} - \frac{1}{r_2} \\[4pt]
R_o &= \frac{r_2 \cdot D_w}{2 r_2 - D_w}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The outer race has a much larger radius of conformity for identical groove geometry, which is why outer race contacts sustain less Hertzian stress than inner race contacts.

---

**(b)** With the help of neat sketches, describe the **construction and working of a Deep Groove Ball Bearing** and an **Angular Contact Ball Bearing**. Compare their axial load capacities. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Deep Groove Ball Bearing (3.5 Marks):**
- **Construction:** It consists of an inner race, an outer race, a set of balls held by a cage, and a deep uninterrupted groove on both races conforming closely to the ball. The conformity ratio is typically 0.510–0.530.
- **Working:** When the shaft rotates, the balls roll on the deep grooves, transmitting radial load primarily, with a small axial load capacity arising from the contact angle being near zero.
- **Features:** Simple, robust, suitable for high speeds, can be fitted in any orientation.

**Angular Contact Ball Bearing (3.5 Marks):**
- **Construction:** The outer race has a relieved shoulder on one side, while the inner race has a high shoulder. This asymmetry creates a definite contact angle $\alpha$ (15°, 25°, 30°, or 40°). The balls are guided along the line of action through the contact angle.
- **Working:** The contact angle ensures that the line of force transmission is inclined, so that a portion of the radial load is converted into a thrust component that the bearing can carry.
- **Features:** Higher axial load capacity than deep groove; can support combined radial + axial loads; usually mounted in pairs (back-to-back, face-to-face, or tandem) for heavy axial loads.

**Comparison of Axial Load Capacity:**

| Parameter | Deep Groove | Angular Contact |
|---|---|---|
| Contact Angle | ≈ 0° (nominal) | 15°–40° |
| Axial Load Capacity | Low | High |
| Speed Capability | Very high | Moderate to high |
| Mounting | Single or paired | Almost always in pairs |

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the various types of **roller bearings** with neat sketches. Compare them based on load direction and load capacity. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Cylindrical Roller Bearing (1.5 Marks):**
Rollers are straight cylinders (length ≈ diameter). Raceways are flat → true line contact. Very high radial load capacity, but **zero axial load capacity**. Used in gearboxes and heavy machinery.

**2. Spherical Roller Bearing (1.5 Marks):**
Rollers are barrel-shaped. The outer race is a common spherical race shared by both rows of rollers. Excellent self-aligning capability (typically ±1.5°). Can carry very heavy radial loads and moderate axial loads. Used in rolling mills, crushers, paper machines.

**3. Tapered Roller Bearing (1.5 Marks):**
Rollers, inner race, and outer race are all conical with matching apex angles. The contact angle is fixed (typically 10°–30°). The bearing transmits combined radial + axial loads in one direction. Always used in pairs (opposing arrangements). Used in automotive wheel hubs and gearboxes.

**4. Needle Roller Bearing (1.5 Marks):**
Rollers are long and thin ($L/D > 4$). Used where radial space is limited but radial load is high. No cage in many designs ("full complement"). Used in connecting rods, hydraulic pumps, transmissions.

**5. Thrust Roller Bearing (1 Mark):**
Cylindrical or tapered rollers arranged on a flat washer-like track. Carries pure axial (thrust) loads. Used in gear reducers, crane slew bearings.

**Comparison Table:**

| Bearing Type | Load Direction | Load Capacity | Self-Aligning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cylindrical Roller | Radial only | Very High | No |
| Spherical Roller | Radial + light axial | Very High | Yes (±1.5°) |
| Tapered Roller | Radial + Axial (combined) | High | No |
| Needle Roller | Radial only | High (in small space) | No |
| Thrust Roller | Axial only | Very High | No |

---

**(b)** Describe the **construction and working of a Self-Aligning Ball Bearing** and a **Tapered Roller Bearing** with neat diagrams. State any two applications of each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Self-Aligning Ball Bearing (3.5 Marks):**
- **Construction:** Two rows of balls running on a *common spherical outer race*. The inner race has two grooved raceways aligned with the spherical outer surface. The spherical center of the outer race coincides with the bearing axis.
- **Working:** When the shaft tilts or misaligns (typically up to ±2.5° to ±3°), the balls and inner race can swivel as a unit inside the spherical outer race, automatically compensating for the misalignment.
- **Applications:** (i) Agricultural machinery shafts. (ii) Long transmission shafts prone to deflection. (iii) Textile machinery.

**Tapered Roller Bearing (3.5 Marks):**
- **Construction:** Consists of a cone (inner race), a cup (outer race), tapered rollers, and a cage. All components — cone, cup, and rollers — have matching tapers that meet at a common apex point on the bearing axis. The rollers are guided by the contact angle (typically 10°–30°).
- **Working:** The tapered geometry ensures that the rollers roll true on the raceways without sliding. The radial component of load is borne by the line contact, while the inclined contact line also resists axial thrust in one direction.
- **Applications:** (i) Automobile wheel hubs. (ii) Gearbox input/output shafts. (iii) Rolling mill reduction gears.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Sign Error in Outer Race:** Students often write $1/R_o = 1/r_2 + 2/D_w$ instead of the minus sign. The minus sign arises because the outer race groove is *concave* from the ball's perspective. **Loss: 1–2 marks per question.**
> - **Confusing $D_w$ with $D$ (Bore):** $D_w$ is the *ball diameter*; $D$ is the *bore diameter*. Examiners deduct marks for interchange.
> - **Forgetting to state the conformity ratio numerically:** Always quote a value (e.g., 0.515) when describing a deep-groove bearing.
> - **Neglecting "line contact" distinction:** In roller bearings, say *explicitly* "true line contact" or "modified line contact" depending on roller geometry — examiners are strict.
> - **Sketch Quality:** Neat, labelled diagrams with the contact angle marked distinctly fetch 1–2 extra marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (must memorize before KTU exam):**

- **Conformity** is the ratio of groove radius to ball diameter: $C = r / D_w$. Standard value for deep-groove ball bearings: **0.515**.
- **Theoretical point contact** in ball bearings; **theoretical line contact** in cylindrical roller bearings.
- **Inner race formula:** $1/R_i = 1/r_1 + 2/D_w$ (both surfaces convex → curvatures add).
- **Outer race formula:** $1/R_o = 1/r_2 - 2/D_w$ (groove concave → curvature subtracts).
- **Outer race has a *larger* contact patch** than the inner race → lower stress → inner race fails first in service.
- **Ball Bearings carry radial + light axial loads** at high speeds. **Roller Bearings carry heavier radial (or combined) loads** at moderate speeds.
- **Angular contact bearings** have an explicit contact angle (15°–40°) and carry high axial loads.
- **Self-aligning bearings** (ball or spherical roller) compensate for shaft misalignment up to ±1.5° to ±3°.
- **Tapered roller bearings** always operate in *opposed pairs* (back-to-back or face-to-face).
- **Needle roller bearings** use rollers with $L/D > 4$ for high load in compact radial space.
- **Hertzian contact theory** governs contact stress; higher conformity = lower stress = longer fatigue life.
- **Conformity trade-off:** High conformity → high load + low misalignment tolerance; Low conformity → low load + good misalignment tolerance.
- **Engineering rule of thumb:** Use deep-groove ball bearings as the *default*; switch to roller bearings only when load exceeds ball bearing capacity; switch to self-aligning types when shaft deflection or misalignment exceeds 0.001 rad.
- **KTU Module 2 weightage:** This topic typically appears as a 7-mark sub-part of a 14-mark Part B question and as a 3-mark direct Part A item.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Angular Velocity Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT527 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Angular Velocity Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Angular Velocity Ratio (AVR)** is defined as the ratio of the angular velocity of the driving member (input) to the angular velocity of the driven member (output) in a kinematic chain. It is a dimensionless quantity that quantifies how rotational motion is transmitted and modified between two shafts.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$
AVR = \frac{\omega_{driver}}{\omega_{driven}} = \frac{N_{driver}}{N_{driven}}
$$

Where:
- $\omega$ = Angular velocity (rad/s)
- $N$ = Rotational speed (rpm)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is AVR Important in Dynamics of Machinery?**
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PERAT527), the Angular Velocity Ratio is the **primary performance parameter** for evaluating any power transmission system — gear trains, belt drives, chain drives, and epicyclic (planetary) gearboxes. It directly determines the **mechanical advantage**, **torque transmission**, and **kinematic inversions** of mechanisms.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

Imagine a **bicycle gear system** — when you shift to a **larger rear sprocket** (more teeth on the driven gear), pedaling becomes **easier** (more torque) but you cover **less distance per pedal revolution** (less speed). Conversely, shifting to a **smaller rear sprocket** makes pedaling harder but the wheel spins **faster**.

| Pedal (Driver) | Rear Sprocket (Driven) | Result | AVR (Driver/Driven) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 Revolution | 2 Revolutions | Speed up, torque down | 0.5 (Speed Multiplier) |
| 1 Revolution | 0.5 Revolutions | Speed down, torque up | 2.0 (Torque Multiplier) |

**Key Intuition:** Angular Velocity Ratio tells you *how the rotational "energy effort" is traded off* between the two shafts. A value greater than **1** means **torque amplification** (reduction); a value less than **1** means **speed amplification** (overdrive).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard unit of angular velocity ($\omega$):** rad/s
> - **Standard unit of rotational speed ($N$):** rpm (revolutions per minute)
> - **Conversion factor:** $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s
> - **AVR is strictly dimensionless** — no SI unit is assigned.
> - **Module (m):** For meshing gears, the pitch circle must share the same *circular module* ($m = D/T$), where $D$ is the pitch diameter and $T$ is the number of teeth.

---

## 1.4 Alternate Terminologies Used in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Students must recognize the following synonymous terms used across the KTU 2024 syllabus and standard textbooks:
> 1. **Velocity Ratio (VR)** — Same as AVR; used interchangeably in Indian textbooks.
> 2. **Speed Ratio (SR)** — The reciprocal of AVR in some conventions; verify with the problem statement.
> 3. **Train Value (TV)** — Often used in older texts to denote AVR when > 1 (i.e., a "reduction" gearing). It is computed as: $TV = \dfrac{N_{driver}}{N_{driven}}$.
> 4. **Mechanical Advantage (MA)** — In an ideal (lossless) transmission, AVR is equivalent to the MA. In real systems, MA < AVR due to frictional losses.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Logic of Angular Velocity Ratio

The derivation of AVR stems from a single, elegant physical principle: **the pitch point velocity must be identical for two meshing components** (no slipping in pure gear/belt contact).

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown:

1. **Identify the constraint:** At the pitch point of two meshing gears, the linear velocity of both gear pitch circles must be equal (assuming no slip).
2. **Express linear velocity:** Linear velocity at pitch circle = $\omega \times r$, where $r$ is the pitch circle radius.
3. **Equate:** $\omega_1 \times r_1 = \omega_2 \times r_2$
4. **Rearrange:** $\dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{r_2}{r_1}$
5. **Substitute $r = mT/2$ (with same module $m$):** $\dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$
6. **Conclude:** Since rpm $N$ is directly proportional to $\omega$, the ratio of speeds equals the ratio of teeth, but **inverted**.

---

## 2.2 AVR Across Different Transmission Systems

### A) Simple Gear Train (Two External Gears in Mesh)

For two spur gears with teeth $T_1$ (driver) and $T_2$ (driven) sharing the same module $m$:

$$
AVR = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{D_2}{D_1}
$$

> **Why inverted?** A smaller driver must spin **faster** to cover the same arc length as a larger driven gear in one meshing cycle.

### B) Compound Gear Train (Multiple Intermediate Shafts)

In a compound train, gears are mounted on intermediate shafts that share rigid connections. The overall AVR is the **product of individual mesh ratios**:

$$
AVR_{compound} = \frac{N_{input}}{N_{output}} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} \times \frac{T_4}{T_3} \times \frac{T_6}{T_5} \times \ldots
$$

> **Logic:** Each mesh multiplies the AVR, allowing for very high or very low overall ratios in compact geometries.

### C) Reverted Gear Train (Coaxial Input/Output)

When the input and output shafts lie on the **same axis** (e.g., automotive transmissions), a special compound configuration is used. The constraint of equal center distance for both meshes gives:

$$
T_1 + T_2 = T_3 + T_4
$$

$$
AVR = \frac{T_2 \times T_4}{T_1 \times T_3}
$$

### D) Belt or Rope Drive

For a belt drive connecting two pulleys, the **belt velocity is the same** on both sides (assuming no slip):

$$
AVR = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{D_2}{D_1}
$$

### E) Epicyclic (Planetary) Gear Train — Willis Equation

In epicyclic trains, gears orbit as well as rotate. The Willis Equation provides the AVR when the **arm is fixed**:

$$
\frac{N_1 - N_{arm}}{N_2 - N_{arm}} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} \times \frac{T_4}{T_3} \times \ldots
$$

> This is the foundation of automatic gearboxes and is a **favourite KTU Module 3 question** for 14-mark problems.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S. No. | Transmission Type | AVR Formula | Key Constraint | Direction Rule |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | **Simple Gear Train** (External) | $AVR = T_2 / T_1$ | Same module $m$ | Output **opposite** to input |
| 2 | **Simple Gear Train** (Internal/Ring) | $AVR = T_{ring} / T_{sun}$ | Concentric axes | Output **same** as input |
| 3 | **Compound Gear Train** | $AVR = (T_2 \cdot T_4) / (T_1 \cdot T_3)$ | Rigid intermediate shafts | Multi-step rule |
| 4 | **Reverted Gear Train** | $AVR = (T_2 \cdot T_4) / (T_1 \cdot T_3)$ | $T_1 + T_2 = T_3 + T_4$ | Input/Output coaxial |
| 5 | **Belt / Rope Drive** | $AVR = D_2 / D_1$ | No slip, same belt speed | Output same as input |
| 6 | **Chain Drive** | $AVR = T_2 / T_1$ | Equal pitch | Output opposite to input |
| 7 | **Epicyclic (Willis Eq.)** | $(N_1 - N_a) / (N_2 - N_a) = \pm (T_2 T_4) / (T_1 T_3)$ | One member fixed | Sign convention critical |
| 8 | **Conversion** | $\omega = (2\pi N) / 60$ | rad/s to rpm | $\omega$ proportional to $N$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Reminder:** All AVR values are dimensionless. $\omega$ is in **rad/s**, $N$ is in **rpm**, $D$ is in **mm** or **m**, $T$ is a pure count.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Industry | Typical AVR Range |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Automotive gearbox | Automotive | 3:1 to 5:1 (per stage) |
| Wind turbine gearbox | Renewable Energy | 1:50 to 1:100 |
| Industrial conveyor drives | Manufacturing | 5:1 to 20:1 |
| Watch / Clock mechanism | Horology | 1:1000 (multi-stage) |
| Robot joint actuators | Robotics | 50:1 to 200:1 (harmonic drives) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: AVR for Two Meshing Spur Gears (Fundamental Proof)

**Given:** Two external spur gears in mesh. Gear 1 (driver) has $T_1$ teeth and rotates at $N_1$ rpm. Gear 2 (driven) has $T_2$ teeth and rotates at $N_2$ rpm. Both gears share the same circular module $m$.

**Objective:** Derive the expression $AVR = T_2 / T_1$.

### Step 1: Express Pitch Circle Diameters

The pitch circle diameter $D$ of a gear is related to its number of teeth $T$ and module $m$ by the standard gear geometry equation:

$$
D = m \cdot T
$$

Therefore, for the two gears:

$$
D_1 = m \cdot T_1 \quad \text{and} \quad D_2 = m \cdot T_2
$$

### Step 2: Apply the No-Slip Condition

At the pitch point (point of contact), the linear velocities of the two gear pitch circles must be **equal** (kinematic constraint for pure rolling contact):

$$
v_1 = v_2
$$

### Step 3: Express Linear Velocity in Terms of Angular Velocity

Linear velocity at the pitch circle radius $r = D/2$ is:

$$
v = \omega \cdot r = \omega \cdot \frac{D}{2}
$$

Substituting for both gears:

$$
\omega_1 \cdot \frac{D_1}{2} = \omega_2 \cdot \frac{D_2}{2}
$$

### Step 4: Simplify by Cancelling the Common Factor

The factor $1/2$ cancels from both sides:

$$
\omega_1 \cdot D_1 = \omega_2 \cdot D_2
$$

### Step 5: Rearrange to Form the Ratio

Dividing both sides by $\omega_2 \cdot D_1$:

$$
\frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \frac{D_2}{D_1}
$$

### Step 6: Substitute the Module Relations

Replacing $D_1 = m T_1$ and $D_2 = m T_2$:

$$
\frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \frac{m \cdot T_2}{m \cdot T_1}
$$

### Step 7: Cancel the Module

Since both gears share the same module $m$, it cancels:

$$
\frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \frac{T_2}{T_1}
$$

### Step 8: Convert to RPM Using the Proportionality $\omega \propto N$

Since $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$, the proportionality constant cancels in the ratio:

$$
AVR = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} \quad \blacksquare
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This derivation is universal — it applies to any pair of meshing gears, whether spur, helical, bevel, or worm (with appropriate geometric adjustments).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Compound Gear Train — Train Value (Multi-Stage)

**Given:** A compound gear train with input shaft (1), intermediate shaft (2), and output shaft (3). Gears: $T_1$ (on shaft 1) meshes with $T_2$ (on shaft 2); $T_3$ (on shaft 2, same rigid shaft as $T_2$) meshes with $T_4$ (on shaft 3).

**Objective:** Derive the overall AVR.

### Step 1: First Mesh — Between Shafts 1 and 2

From Derivation 1:

$$
\frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_2}{T_1}
$$

### Step 2: Second Mesh — Between Shafts 2 and 3

Similarly:

$$
\frac{N_2}{N_3} = \frac{T_4}{T_3}
$$

### Step 3: Multiply the Two Ratios

Multiplying these two equations, the intermediate speed $N_2$ cancels:

$$
\frac{N_1}{N_2} \times \frac{N_2}{N_3} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} \times \frac{T_4}{T_3}
$$

### Step 4: Simplify the Left Side

$$
\frac{N_1}{N_3} = \frac{T_2 \cdot T_4}{T_1 \cdot T_3}
$$

### Step 5: Generalize to n Meshes

Extending to $n$ meshes in a compound train:

$$
AVR_{compound} = \frac{N_{input}}{N_{output}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{T_{driven,i}}{T_{driver,i}}
$$

$$
AVR_{compound} = \frac{T_2 \cdot T_4 \cdot T_6 \cdots T_{2n}}{T_1 \cdot T_3 \cdot T_5 \cdots T_{2n-1}} \quad \blacksquare
$$

> **Engineering Utility:** A two-stage compound train can achieve ratios in the range 1:100 to 1:200 in a compact envelope — essential for **machine tool gearboxes** and **automotive transmissions**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem (KTU 2019 Pattern):** A compound gear train consists of four gears mounted on three parallel shafts. The gears have the following number of teeth: $T_1 = 20$, $T_2 = 50$, $T_3 = 25$, $T_4 = 75$. The driver (gear 1) rotates at $720$ rpm. Calculate:
1. The Angular Velocity Ratio (Train Value).
2. The speed of the output shaft.
3. The speed of the intermediate shaft.

**Solution:**

### Step 1: Identify the Driver and Driven Teeth

Driver teeth: $T_1, T_3$ (input side of each mesh)
Driven teeth: $T_2, T_4$ (output side of each mesh)

### Step 2: Apply the Compound Train Formula

$$
AVR = \frac{T_2 \cdot T_4}{T_1 \cdot T_3}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
AVR = \frac{50 \times 75}{20 \times 25}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Numerator and Denominator

$$
AVR = \frac{3750}{500} = 7.5
$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Output Speed

From $AVR = N_1 / N_3$:

$$
N_3 = \frac{N_1}{AVR} = \frac{720}{7.5} = 96 \text{ rpm}
$$

### Step 5: Calculate the Intermediate Shaft Speed

Using the first mesh only:

$$
N_2 = N_1 \times \frac{T_1}{T_2} = 720 \times \frac{20}{50} = 288 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Verification via second mesh:** $N_2 \times (T_3 / T_4) = 288 \times (25/75) = 288 \times (1/3) = 96$ rpm ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Direction Analysis:** For an external-external-external compound train, the output shaft rotates in the **same direction as the input** (two inversions = identity). This is a common 2-mark sub-question in KTU exams.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Simple Train | Compound Train | Reverted Train | Epicyclic Train |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Number of Shafts** | 2 | $\geq 3$ | 3 (input-output coaxial) | Variable |
| **AVR Magnitude** | Limited ($<10:1$) | High ($>100:1$ possible) | High | Very High |
| **Compactness** | Bulky for high ratio | Compact | Most compact | Extremely compact |
| **Direction of Output** | Opposite to input | Same (if 2 meshes) | Same | Configurable |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| **Common Mistake** | Forgetting inversion sign | Missing intermediate shaft constraint | Confusing center distance | Willis equation sign error |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Angular Velocity Ratio Analysis

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **decision-based analytical flow** that an engineer (or a KTU student) follows when classifying a transmission problem to compute the AVR.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Identify Transmission Type] --> B{Is it a Belt or Chain Drive?}
    B -- YES --> C[Apply Belt Rule: AVR = D2 by D1]
    B -- NO --> D{Is it a Planetary or Epicyclic Train?}
    D -- YES --> E[Apply Willis Equation with Arm Speed Na]
    D -- NO --> F{How many shafts in the train?}
    F -- TWO SHAFTS --> G[Apply Simple Gear Rule: AVR = T2 by T1]
    F -- THREE OR MORE SHAFTS --> H{Are input and output coaxial?}
    H -- YES --> I[Reverted Train: AVR = T2T4 by T1T3, check T1+T2 = T3+T4]
    H -- NO --> J[Compound Train: AVR = Product of Driven Teeth by Product of Driver Teeth]
    C --> K[END: Report AVR Value and Direction]
    E --> K
    G --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Start at node **A**, answer the decision diamonds (rhombuses) sequentially, and arrive at the correct governing formula at one of the terminal nodes (**G, I, J, C, or E**) before reporting the final AVR at node **K**.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Velocity Flow in a Compound Gear Train

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Input Shaft 1, N1 rpm, Gear T1] --> MESH1[Mesh Point 1: T1 with T2]
    MESH1 --> MID2[Intermediate Shaft 2, N2 rpm, Gears T2 and T3]
    MID2 --> MESH2[Mesh Point 2: T3 with T4]
    MESH2 --> OUT3[Output Shaft 3, N3 rpm, Gear T4]
    IN1 -.AVR equals T2T4 by T1T3.-> OUT3
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram visually reinforces that the **input speed $N_1$** is first transformed to **$N_2$** at the first mesh, and then to **$N_3$** at the second mesh. The overall AVR is the product of the two individual ratios.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Epicyyclic (Planetary) Gear Train Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SUN[Sun Gear T1, Rotates at N1] --> MESH_EPI[Internal Mesh Point]
    PLANET[Planet Gears T2 mounted on Carrier Arm, Rotates at N_planet] --> MESH_EPI
    MESH_EPI --> RING[Ring or Annulus Gear T3, Rotates at N3]
    ARM[Arm or Carrier, Rotates at Na] -.Revolves all planets.-> PLANET
    SUN -.Willis Equation governs.-> RING
```

> **Note on Epicyclic Systems:** In the Willis Equation, $\omega_{arm}$ (or $N_{arm}$) is the *reference frame velocity*. To use the equation, **fix the arm** ($N_{arm} = 0$) mentally and compute relative speeds, then return to the absolute frame.

---

## 4.4 Engineering Architecture: Speed Reduction Unit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MOTOR[Motor Input: 1440 rpm] --> STAGE1[Stage 1: AVR1 = 3 to 1]
    STAGE1 --> STAGE2[Stage 2: AVR2 = 4 to 1]
    STAGE2 --> OUTPUT[Final Output: 1440 divided by 12 = 120 rpm]
    MOTOR -.Overall AVR equals 12 to 1.-> OUTPUT
```

> **Interpretation:** A **two-stage compound reduction gearbox** with individual stage ratios of 3:1 and 4:1 produces an overall AVR of 12:1, reducing the motor speed from 1440 rpm to 120 rpm — a typical industrial conveyor drive specification.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define Angular Velocity Ratio. State its significance in the design of gear trains.**

**Model Answer:**
Angular Velocity Ratio (AVR) is defined as the ratio of the angular velocity of the driving member to that of the driven member in a kinematic chain. For a pair of meshing gears, it is given by $AVR = N_1 / N_2 = T_2 / T_1$, where $N$ and $T$ denote the rotational speed (rpm) and number of teeth respectively.

**Significance:**
1. It determines the **torque multiplication** and **speed reduction/amplification** of the system.
2. It governs the **mechanical advantage** in power transmission.
3. It is essential for **sizing gears, selecting materials**, and ensuring **kinematic compatibility** in compound, reverted, and epicyclic gear trains.
4. In the design of gearboxes, AVR dictates the **stage count and gear geometry** to meet output speed requirements.

*(Marks distribution: Definition - 1, Formula - 1, Significance - 1)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Differentiate between a simple gear train and a compound gear train with neat sketches.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Simple Gear Train | Compound Gear Train |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Number of Shafts** | Two (input + output) | Three or more |
| **Number of Gears** | Two (or more on one shaft) | At least four |
| **AVR Range** | Limited (typically $<10:1$) | High (can exceed 100:1) |
| **Geometry** | All gears share the same module, arranged linearly | Intermediate shaft carries two or more gears |
| **Speed Calculation** | Direct: $AVR = T_2/T_1$ | Product rule: $AVR = (T_2 T_4)/(T_1 T_3)$ |
| **Application** | Low-ratio systems, clocks | Machine tool gearboxes, automotive |
| **Output Direction** | Opposite to input | Same as input (for 2 external meshes) |

*(Marks distribution: 3 key differences - 1 each, with one supporting point)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module 3 Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3 Choice 1]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
A simple gear train has a driver gear with 18 teeth rotating at 1200 rpm. The driven gear has 72 teeth.
1. Calculate the Angular Velocity Ratio.
2. Determine the speed of the driven gear.
3. Find the pitch circle diameter of the driven gear, given module $m = 4$ mm.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Given Data**
- Driver teeth: $T_1 = 18$
- Driven teeth: $T_2 = 72$
- Driver speed: $N_1 = 1200$ rpm
- Module: $m = 4$ mm

**Step 2: Calculate AVR**
Using $AVR = T_2 / T_1$:

$$
AVR = \frac{72}{18} = 4
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Driven Speed**
Using $AVR = N_1 / N_2$:

$$
N_2 = \frac{N_1}{AVR} = \frac{1200}{4} = 300 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Pitch Circle Diameter of Driven Gear**
Using $D = m \cdot T$:

$$
D_2 = 4 \times 72 = 288 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Answer:** $AVR = 4:1$, $N_2 = 300$ rpm, $D_2 = 288$ mm.
The output shaft rotates in the **opposite direction** to the input (external mesh).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
Define **Train Value**. A compound gear train has the following data:
- Shaft 1 (Input): Gear with $T_1 = 20$ teeth, $N_1 = 600$ rpm
- Shaft 2 (Intermediate): Gear with $T_2 = 60$ teeth (meshes with $T_1$) and $T_3 = 18$ teeth (on same shaft)
- Shaft 3 (Output): Gear with $T_4 = 54$ teeth (meshes with $T_3$)

Find: (i) Overall Train Value, (ii) Speed of output shaft, (iii) Direction of rotation relative to input.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Definition**
> **Train Value (TV)** is the reciprocal of the velocity ratio when defined in the speed-multiplication sense, and is the same as the AVR when defined in the torque-multiplication sense. It represents the ratio of input speed to output speed and is the most commonly used performance metric for reduction gearboxes.

**Step 2: Identify Driver and Driven Teeth**
- Mesh 1: Driver $T_1 = 20$, Driven $T_2 = 60$
- Mesh 2: Driver $T_3 = 18$, Driven $T_4 = 54$

**Step 3: Calculate Overall Train Value**
Using the compound train formula:

$$
TV = \frac{T_2 \cdot T_4}{T_1 \cdot T_3} = \frac{60 \times 54}{20 \times 18}
$$

$$
TV = \frac{3240}{360} = 9
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Output Speed**

$$
N_3 = \frac{N_1}{TV} = \frac{600}{9} = 66.67 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 5: Direction of Rotation**
For two external meshes in a compound train, the output direction is the **same as the input** (two inversions = identity). If an idler or internal gear is included, the direction must be traced mesh by mesh.

**Final Answer:** $TV = 9$, $N_3 = 66.67$ rpm, direction **same as input**.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3 Choice 2]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
A **reverted gear train** is used in an automobile gearbox. The gears have the following data:
- Gear 1 (on input shaft, coaxial with output): $T_1 = 20$ teeth
- Gear 2 (meshes with $T_1$): $T_2 = 40$ teeth
- Gear 3 (on same shaft as $T_2$, coaxial with input): $T_3 = 25$ teeth
- Gear 4 (meshes with $T_3$, on output shaft): $T_4 = ?$

If the input rotates at 800 rpm and the output is required to be 200 rpm:
1. Calculate the required number of teeth on Gear 4.
2. Verify the center distance constraint.
3. Compute the Train Value.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the Train Value from Speeds**

$$
TV = \frac{N_1}{N_4} = \frac{800}{200} = 4
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Reverted Train Formula**

$$
TV = \frac{T_2 \cdot T_4}{T_1 \cdot T_3}
$$

Substituting known values:

$$
4 = \frac{40 \cdot T_4}{20 \cdot 25}
$$

**Step 3: Solve for $T_4$**

$$
4 = \frac{40 \cdot T_4}{500}
$$

$$
T_4 = \frac{4 \times 500}{40} = 50 \text{ teeth}
$$

**Step 4: Verify the Center Distance Constraint**
For a reverted train, $T_1 + T_2 = T_3 + T_4$:

$$
T_1 + T_2 = 20 + 40 = 60
$$

$$
T_3 + T_4 = 25 + 50 = 75
$$

**Critical Issue:** $60 \neq 75$ — the center distance constraint is **NOT satisfied**.

**Correction:** To satisfy $T_1 + T_2 = T_3 + T_4$, either $T_3$ or $T_4$ must be adjusted. The problem implicitly assumes a common module across both meshes, which is **physically impossible** with these teeth counts for a true reverted configuration.

> **Engineering Resolution:** In practice, slightly different modules are used for the two meshes, with the input and output shafts connected by bearings that accommodate the minor axial offset. For an *ideal* reverted train, redesign with $T_4 = 35$ (then $T_3 + T_4 = 60$ ✓).

**Final Answer:** $T_4 = 50$ (as calculated, with center distance caveat noted), $TV = 4$.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2022]`
Two pulleys are connected by a cross-belt drive. The driver pulley has a diameter of 250 mm and rotates at 900 rpm. The driven pulley has a diameter of 500 mm. The driven pulley is now replaced by a pulley of 750 mm diameter.
1. Calculate the original speed of the driven pulley.
2. Calculate the new speed after replacement.
3. Comment on the direction of rotation in each case.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the Configuration**
A **cross-belt drive** reverses the direction of rotation (in contrast to an open-belt drive which preserves direction).

**Step 2: Original Configuration — Compute Original Driven Speed**
Using $AVR = D_2 / D_1$:

$$
N_2 = N_1 \times \frac{D_1}{D_2} = 900 \times \frac{250}{500}
$$

$$
N_2 = 900 \times 0.5 = 450 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 3: New Configuration — Compute New Driven Speed**

$$
N_2^{new} = N_1 \times \frac{D_1}{D_2^{new}} = 900 \times \frac{250}{750}
$$

$$
N_2^{new} = 900 \times \frac{1}{3} = 300 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 4: Direction of Rotation**
- **Cross-belt drive:** Output rotates in the **opposite direction** to the input.
- This direction **reversal property remains unchanged** after pulley replacement.

**Final Answer:** Original $N_2 = 450$ rpm; New $N_2 = 300$ rpm; Direction = **opposite to input** (in both cases).

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
>
> 1. **Sign Confusion in AVR:** Students often write $AVR = T_1 / T_2$ instead of $T_2 / T_1$. Remember: **smaller driver spins faster than larger driven** — the speed ratio inverts the teeth ratio.
>
> 2. **Direction Tracking in Compound Trains:** Each external mesh **reverses** the direction. With $n$ external meshes, output direction = input direction if $n$ is even, opposite if $n$ is odd. **Draw a free-body diagram** for multi-stage problems.
>
> 3. **Reverted Train Center Distance:** The constraint $T_1 + T_2 = T_3 + T_4$ is **mandatory** for a true coaxial design. Failing to check this loses **2 marks instantly** in KTU valuation.
>
> 4. **Willis Equation Sign Error:** In epicyclic trains, the sign of the term $(N - N_{arm})$ depends on the **relative rotation direction**. Always fix the arm first ($N_{arm} = 0$) and assign signs based on the **viewer's reference frame**.
>
> 5. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing $N$ in rpm and $\omega$ in rad/s in the same equation is a **silent mark killer**. Convert all speeds to the same unit before substituting.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### High-Density Revision Checklist — Angular Velocity Ratio
>
> - [x] **Definition:** AVR = $\omega_{driver} / \omega_{driven} = N_{driver} / N_{driven}$ — **dimensionless**.
> - [x] **Fundamental Principle:** Linear velocity at the pitch point is **equal** for both meshing members.
> - [x] **Simple Gear Train:** $AVR = T_2 / T_1 = D_2 / D_1$ (inverse relationship with teeth).
> - [x] **Compound Train:** $AVR = (T_2 \cdot T_4) / (T_1 \cdot T_3)$ — product rule for multi-stage.
> - [x] **Reverted Train:** Add constraint $T_1 + T_2 = T_3 + T_4$ for coaxial input/output.
> - [x] **Belt Drive:** $AVR = D_{driven} / D_{driver}$; **cross-belt reverses** direction.
> - [x] **Epicyclic Train:** Use **Willis Equation** — fix arm, compute ratios in relative frame.
> - [x] **Direction Rule:** External mesh = reversal; Internal mesh (ring) = same direction.
> - [x] **Speed Conversion:** $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ — convert **before** substituting.
> - [x] **Mechanical Advantage:** In ideal (lossless) systems, $MA = AVR$.
> - [x] **KTU Module Weightage:** This topic typically carries **14 marks** in ESE Module 3 questions.
> - [x] **Most Tested Sub-Topics:** Compound train speed calculation, Reverted train design, Willis equation application.
> - [x] **Common Traps:** Sign errors in Willis, missing center distance check, unit mixing.

---

> **End of Module 3 — Angular Velocity Ratio Study Notes**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme | PERAT527 | Dynamics of Machinery*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Slip
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT527 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effect of Slip

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **belt drives**, **slip** is defined as the relative motion between the belt and the pulley due to insufficient frictional grip. It occurs because the belt never perfectly matches the surface speed of either the driving or the driven pulley. There is always a small relative displacement between the belt and the pulley surface as the belt fails to maintain absolute contact without micro-slip.

Mathematically, slip is quantified as the percentage difference between the linear velocity of the belt and the peripheral (rim) velocity of the pulley.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Standard):** *"Slip is the difference between the linear velocities of the belt and the rim of the pulley, expressed as a percentage of the rim velocity of the driving pulley."*

### Types of Slip

- **Positive Slip (Slip of the Driving Pulley):** The driving pulley advances more belt than the driven pulley actually receives. The belt lags behind the driving pulley.
- **Negative Slip (Slip of the Driven Pulley):** The driven pulley receives slightly more belt length than the driving pulley advances, due to the stretching/contraction behavior of the belt as it transitions between tight and slack sides. In effect, the driven pulley runs slightly *faster* than the ideal no-slip prediction.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two wet wheels rolling on each other — like a motorcycle wheel spinning on a greasy patch of road. The wheel **wants** to move the bike forward, but because of the lack of friction, the contact patch slips. The bike moves slower than the wheel's rotation would suggest.

In belt drives, it's the same idea:
- The **driving pulley** spins and tries to drag the belt along.
- The **belt** tries to drag the **driven pulley**.
- But because friction is finite, the belt creeps slightly on both pulleys. The driven pulley thus rotates **slower** (in cross-belt/open belt, considering only positive slip) than the ideal theoretical speed ratio.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants / Metrics (in Bold):**
> - **Coefficient of friction (μ):** Typically **0.25 – 0.35** for leather belts on cast iron pulleys.
> - **Slip (s):** Expressed as a **percentage (1% – 4%)** in real industrial drives.
> - **Standard slip value assumed in KTU problems:** **s = 1.5% or 2%** unless otherwise specified.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Why Does Slip Occur? (The "Why" Behind the Concept)

1. **Insufficient Frictional Grip:** The belt material (leather, rubber, fabric) and the pulley material (cast iron, steel) cannot generate infinite friction. The driving tangential force required to transmit power sometimes exceeds the available frictional capacity, causing micro-creep.
2. **Belt Elasticity:** The belt stretches on the tight side and contracts on the slack side. This length change means the belt does not advance a full arc-length per revolution of the driving pulley.
3. **Pulley Surface Conditions:** Dust, moisture, oil, glazing, and wear on the pulley surface all reduce the effective coefficient of friction.

## How Slip Modifies the Velocity Ratio (The "How")

Without slip, the velocity ratio is purely geometric:

$$\text{Velocity Ratio (no slip)} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{D_2}{D_1}$$

When slip is present (typically 1% to 4%), the driven pulley loses a fraction of the belt's intended motion. Therefore, the **actual velocity ratio increases** (the driven shaft turns *slower* than ideal).

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Slip of belt | $s = \dfrac{v_1 - v_2}{v_1} \times 100$ | % | $v_1$ = rim velocity of driving pulley |
| 2 | Rim velocity (driving) | $v_1 = \dfrac{\pi D_1 N_1}{60}$ | m/s | $D_1$ in m, $N_1$ in rpm |
| 3 | Belt linear velocity | $v = \pi D_1 N_1 \left(1 - \dfrac{s}{100}\right) / 60$ | m/s | Reduced due to slip |
| 4 | Driven pulley rpm (with slip) | $N_2 = \dfrac{D_1 \cdot N_1}{D_2}\left(1 - \dfrac{s}{100}\right) - \dfrac{s \cdot N_1}{100}$ | rpm | Combined effect |
| 5 | Effective velocity ratio (with slip) | $\dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{D_2 + s \cdot D_1}{D_1 (1 - s)}$ | ratio | KTU standard form |
| 6 | Power lost due to slip | $P_{slip} = (T_1 - T_2) v \cdot s$ | W | Approximate loss term |
| 7 | Length of belt (open) | $L = 2C + \dfrac{\pi (D_1 + D_2)}{2} + \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2)^2}{4C}$ | m | Used in design problems |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Critical Notation Rule:** In every formula above, $s$ must be used as a **decimal fraction** (e.g., 0.02) when placed inside algebraic expressions, and as a **percentage** (e.g., 2) only when shown with the % symbol. The standard KTU examiner expects you to state this clearly.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Conveyor systems:** Predicting actual throughput of material handling belts.
- **Automotive accessory drives:** Timing belts, alternator belts, and serpentine belts all suffer slip; engineers must over-design the pulley ratio to compensate.
- **Textile and printing machinery:** Speed synchronization between rollers is critical; slip is the primary cause of misprinted patterns or misaligned fabrics.
- **Industrial fans and pumps:** Designers use slip-adjusted velocity ratios to size motors correctly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Expression for Velocity Ratio Considering Slip

### Given Information
- $D_1$ = Diameter of driving pulley
- $D_2$ = Diameter of driven pulley
- $N_1$ = Speed of driving pulley (rpm)
- $N_2$ = Speed of driven pulley (rpm)
- $s$ = Slip as a percentage (e.g., 2 means 2%)

### Assumptions
1. The belt is inextensible (no stretching under load — the *length* is conserved, but slip allows micro-displacement at contact).
2. Slip occurs on both pulleys. Let $s_1$ = slip on driving pulley, $s_2$ = slip on driven pulley.
3. Total effective slip $s = s_1 + s_2$ (in percentage).

### Step 1: Velocity of the Belt at the Driving Pulley
The rim velocity of the driving pulley is:

$$v_1 = \frac{\pi D_1 N_1}{60} \quad \text{(m/s)}$$

### Step 2: Velocity of the Belt Leaving the Driving Pulley
Due to slip $s_1$ on the driving pulley, the belt moves slower than the rim:

$$v_{belt} = v_1 \left(1 - \frac{s_1}{100}\right) = \frac{\pi D_1 N_1}{60} \left(1 - \frac{s_1}{100}\right)$$

### Step 3: Velocity at Which the Belt Enters the Driven Pulley
The belt arrives at the driven pulley with velocity $v_{belt}$. Due to slip $s_2$ on the driven pulley, the driven rim travels slower than the belt:

$$v_2 = v_{belt} \left(1 - \frac{s_2}{100}\right) = \frac{\pi D_1 N_1}{60} \left(1 - \frac{s_1}{100}\right)\left(1 - \frac{s_2}{100}\right)$$

### Step 4: Also Equating via Driven Pulley Rim Speed
The rim velocity of the driven pulley is $v_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N_2}{60}$. Equating both expressions for $v_2$:

$$\frac{\pi D_2 N_2}{60} = \frac{\pi D_1 N_1}{60} \left(1 - \frac{s_1}{100}\right)\left(1 - \frac{s_2}{100}\right)$$

### Step 5: Simplify the π and 60 Terms
Cancel the common terms $\pi/60$:

$$D_2 N_2 = D_1 N_1 \left(1 - \frac{s_1}{100}\right)\left(1 - \frac{s_2}{100}\right)$$

### Step 6: Approximate (Neglect Second-Order Slip Term)
For small $s_1, s_2$ (typical in practice, both < 5%), we can neglect $s_1 s_2 / 10000$ as a second-order small quantity. Expanding:

$$\left(1 - \frac{s_1}{100}\right)\left(1 - \frac{s_2}{100}\right) \approx 1 - \frac{s_1 + s_2}{100} = 1 - \frac{s}{100}$$

where $s = s_1 + s_2$ is the total effective slip.

### Step 7: Final Velocity Ratio
Rearranging:

$$\frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{D_2}{D_1 \left(1 - \dfrac{s}{100}\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Result:**
> $$\boxed{\dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{D_2}{D_1} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\left(1 - \dfrac{s}{100}\right)}}$$
> This shows that with slip, the velocity ratio **increases** (the driven shaft turns slower than the ideal no-slip ratio $D_2/D_1$).

---

## Derivation 2: Effect of Slip on Belt Length (with Belt Thickness)

### Step 1: Standard Open Belt Length
For a flat belt on pulleys of diameters $D_1, D_2$ with center distance $C$:

$$L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2) + \frac{(D_1 - D_2)^2}{4C}$$

### Step 2: Modification Due to Slip
As the belt slips, the *effective* arc length traversed per revolution decreases by the slip fraction. The effective length used in computing the velocity ratio per revolution is:

$$L_{eff} = L \left(1 - \frac{s}{100}\right) \cdot \frac{N_1}{N_1} = L \left(1 - \frac{s}{100}\right)$$

> This relation is **rarely asked** in KTU exams but is useful for design questions involving **belt life estimation**.

---

## KTU Numerical Pattern: Worked Example

**Problem (KTU University Exam - May 2023 Style):**
A belt drive has two pulleys of diameters 300 mm and 600 mm. The driving pulley runs at 1440 rpm. If there is a slip of 2% between the belt and the pulleys, find:
(a) The speed of the driven pulley.
(b) The linear velocity of the belt.
(c) The velocity ratio.

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 300$ mm = $0.3$ m
- $D_2 = 600$ mm = $0.6$ m
- $N_1 = 1440$ rpm
- $s = 2\%$

### Part (a): Speed of Driven Pulley
Using the standard formula with slip:

$$N_2 = N_1 \cdot \frac{D_1}{D_2} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{s}{100}\right)$$

$$N_2 = 1440 \times \frac{0.3}{0.6} \times (1 - 0.02)$$

$$N_2 = 1440 \times 0.5 \times 0.98$$

$$N_2 = 705.6 \text{ rpm}$$

### Part (b): Linear Velocity of the Belt
The belt velocity is the rim velocity of the driving pulley minus the slip on it:

$$v_{belt} = \frac{\pi D_1 N_1}{60} \left(1 - \frac{s_1}{100}\right)$$

Assuming slip is distributed equally, $s_1 = s_2 = 1\%$:

$$v_{belt} = \frac{\pi \times 0.3 \times 1440}{60} \times (1 - 0.01)$$

$$v_{belt} = \frac{\pi \times 0.3 \times 1440}{60} \times 0.99$$

$$v_{belt} = 7.2\pi \times 0.99 = 22.619 \times 0.99 = 22.39 \text{ m/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Simpler alternative:** Many KTU textbooks directly compute $v = \pi D_1 N_1 (1 - s/100)/60$ using total slip. The result is approximately the same.

### Part (c): Velocity Ratio
$$\text{VR} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{1440}{705.6} = 2.04$$

> Without slip, VR would be $D_2/D_1 = 2.00$. So slip increases VR by **2%**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Slip Phenomenon — Block Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driving Motor: N1 rpm] --> B[Driving Pulley D1 spins]
    B --> C{Belt-Pulley Contact Zone}
    C -->|Slip s1 occurs here| D[Belt linear velocity v_belt]
    D --> E{Belt-Pulley Contact Zone 2}
    E -->|Slip s2 occurs here| F[Driven Pulley D2 spins]
    F --> G[Driven Shaft: N2 rpm reduced]

    H[Friction Coefficient mu] -.-> C
    I[Belt Elasticity] -.-> D
    J[Pulley Surface Condition] -.-> E

    K[Total Slip s = s1 + s2] -.-> G
```

## Diagram 2: Velocity Comparison — With vs. Without Slip

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ideal No-Slip Case] --> A1["v_belt = π D1 N1 / 60"]
    A1 --> A2["N2_ideal = N1 × D1 / D2"]

    B[Real Case with Slip] --> B1["v_belt_eff = π D1 N1 / 60 × 1 - s/100"]
    B1 --> B2["N2_actual = N2_ideal × 1 - s/100"]

    A2 --> C{Velocity Ratio}
    B2 --> C
    C --> D["VR_no_slip = D2 / D1"]
    C --> E["VR_with_slip = D2 / D1 × 1 / 1-s/100"]

    E --> F[VR increases by s percent]
```

## Diagram 3: Forces Acting on the Belt

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Tension on Tight Side: T1] --> C[Frictional Grip at Pulley]
    B[Tension on Slack Side: T2] --> C
    C --> D{Angle of Contact: θ}
    D --> E["T1 = T2 × e^(μθ) — Euler's Belt Friction Equation"]
    E --> F[Resultant Friction Force Limits Slip]
    F --> G{If Friction Force < Tension Difference}
    G -->|Yes| H[SLIP OCCURS]
    G -->|No| I[No Slip - Belt Drives Pulley Fully]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Interpretation Tip:** When the question shows a belt drive diagram with $T_1, T_2, v_1, v_2$ labelled, the examiner expects you to mark the **slip direction** on both pulleys and explicitly state whether the slip is **on the driving or driven pulley** in your answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
