<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*With the help of a neat sketch, define a cross belt drive. State any two of its advantages and disadvantages compared to an open belt drive.*

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

- **Definition:** A cross belt drive is a belt drive in which the belt is made to cross between the two pulleys, forming an "X" pattern. The driver and driven pulleys rotate in opposite directions.
- **Advantages:**
  1. No need for an additional reverse-gear arrangement to achieve opposite rotation.
  2. Larger angle of contact on *both* pulleys increases power transmission capacity for the same pulley geometry.
- **Disadvantages:**
  1. The belt rubs against itself at the crossing, causing rapid wear and reduced belt life.
  2. Higher frictional losses and heat generation at the crossing reduce overall efficiency.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Write the expression for the length of a cross belt, defining all the terms. Mention the condition for the drive to be geometrically feasible.*

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

The length of a cross belt connecting pulleys of radii $R$ and $r$ with centre distance $C$ is given by:

$$L \;=\; 2C \;+\; \pi(R+r) \;+\; \frac{(R+r)^{2}}{C}$$

(Approximate form, valid when $(R+r) \ll C$.)

- **$R$:** Radius of the larger pulley (in m or mm).
- **$r$:** Radius of the smaller pulley (in m or mm).
- **$C$:** Centre distance between the two pulleys (in m or mm).
- **Geometrical feasibility condition:** $C > R + r$ (otherwise the pulleys would physically overlap).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** and typically have an *internal choice*. You are expected to attempt **one** of the two questions given below.

---

### Question B — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the length of a cross belt connecting two pulleys of radii $R$ and $r$ ($R > r$) with centre distance $C$ between them. Assume $(R+r) \ll C$ for the final simplified form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cross belt drive has pulleys of diameters 600 mm and 400 mm. The centre distance is 2 m and the belt runs at 600 m/min. If the coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.25$ and the maximum tension in the belt is 1.5 kN, calculate: **(i)** length of the belt, **(ii)** angle of contact on each pulley, **(iii)** power transmitted. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B Option 1

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Geometry of the cross belt.** *(2 marks)*

Let $O_{1}$ and $O_{2}$ be the centres of the larger and smaller pulleys (radii $R$ and $r$). The belt crosses between them. On each pulley the belt is tangent at two points. The radii to the tangent points are perpendicular to the belt at those points.

**Step 2 — Length of the straight portion of the belt.** *(2 marks)*

Because the radii at the tangent points are perpendicular to the same straight belt-line, they are *parallel*. The perpendicular distance from $O_{1}$ to the belt line is $R$ and from $O_{2}$ is $r$, and since the belt crosses, $O_{1}$ and $O_{2}$ are on **opposite sides** of the belt line. Using the right triangle formed by $O_{1}O_{2}$, the two perpendiculars, and the belt straight portion:

$$L_{\text{straight, one side}} \;=\; \sqrt{C^{2} - (R+r)^{2}}$$

Total straight length:

$$L_{\text{straight, total}} \;=\; 2\sqrt{C^{2} - (R+r)^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Angle of contact on each pulley.** *(2 marks)*

Let $\beta$ be the angle the straight portion makes with the line of centres. Then:

$$\sin\beta \;=\; \frac{R+r}{C} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \beta \;=\; \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{R+r}{C}\right)$$

Each pulley is wrapped by the belt through an angle:

$$\alpha \;=\; \pi + 2\beta \;=\; \pi + 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{R+r}{C}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Total length and approximation.** *(1 mark)*

$$L_{\text{cross}} \;=\; 2\sqrt{C^{2} - (R+r)^{2}} + \pi(R+r) + 2(R+r)\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{R+r}{C}\right)$$

For $(R+r) \ll C$, using $\sqrt{C^{2}-(R+r)^{2}} \approx C - (R+r)^{2}/(2C)$ and $\sin^{-1}((R+r)/C) \approx (R+r)/C$:

$$\boxed{\,L_{\text{cross}} \;\approx\; 2C \;+\; \pi(R+r) \;+\; \frac{(R+r)^{2}}{C}\,}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $D = 600\,\text{mm} \Rightarrow R = 300\,\text{mm}$, $d = 400\,\text{mm} \Rightarrow r = 200\,\text{mm}$, $C = 2\,\text{m} = 2000\,\text{mm}$, $v = 600\,\text{m/min} = 10\,\text{m/s}$, $\mu = 0.25$, $T_{1} = 1500\,\text{N}$.

**(i) Length of the belt:** *(3 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
L \;&=\; 2C + \pi(R+r) + \frac{(R+r)^{2}}{C} \\[3pt]
&=\; 2(2000) + \pi(300+200) + \frac{(300+200)^{2}}{2000} \\[3pt]
&=\; 4000 + 500\pi + \frac{250000}{2000} \\[3pt]
&=\; 4000 + 1570.80 + 125 \;=\; \mathbf{5695.80 \; mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Angle of contact on each pulley:** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin\beta \;&=\; \frac{R+r}{C} \;=\; \frac{500}{2000} \;=\; 0.25 \\[3pt]
\beta \;&=\; \sin^{-1}(0.25) \;=\; 0.2527 \;\text{rad} \\[3pt]
\alpha \;&=\; \pi + 2\beta \;=\; 3.1416 + 2(0.2527) \;=\; 3.6470 \;\text{rad} \\[3pt]
&=\; 3.6470 \times \frac{180°}{\pi} \;\approx\; \mathbf{208.96°}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Power transmitted:** *(2 marks)*

First, the ratio of tensions using the belt-friction (Eytelwein) equation for the *smaller* of the two contact angles. (For a cross belt, both pulleys have the *same* contact angle, so the limiting ratio is the same for both.)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}} \;&=\; e^{\mu\alpha} \;=\; e^{0.25 \times 3.6470} \;=\; e^{0.9118} \;=\; 2.488 \\[3pt]
T_{2} \;&=\; \frac{T_{1}}{2.488} \;=\; \frac{1500}{2.488} \;=\; 602.9 \;\text{N} \\[3pt]
P \;&=\; (T_{1} - T_{2})v \;=\; (1500 - 602.9)(10) \;=\; \mathbf{8971 \; W \; \approx\; 8.97 \; kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Two pulleys of diameters 500 mm and 300 mm are connected by a cross belt. The centre distance between the pulleys is 1.5 m. Determine: **(i)** the length of the belt, and **(ii)** the angle of contact on each pulley. Use the approximate formula. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cross belt drive transmits 7.5 kW at 250 rpm of the larger pulley. The diameters of the pulleys are 400 mm and 200 mm, the centre distance is 1.5 m, and the coefficient of friction is 0.3. Find the length of the belt, the angle of contact, the tensions $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ in the belt, and the initial tension $T_{0}$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B Option 2

#### Part (a) — Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $D = 500\,\text{mm} \Rightarrow R = 250\,\text{mm}$, $d = 300\,\text{mm} \Rightarrow r = 150\,\text{mm}$, $C = 1500\,\text{mm}$.

**(i) Length of the belt:** *(4 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
L \;&=\; 2C + \pi(R+r) + \frac{(R+r)^{2}}{C} \\[3pt]
&=\; 2(1500) + \pi(250+150) + \frac{(250+150)^{2}}{1500} \\[3pt]
&=\; 3000 + 400\pi + \frac{160000}{1500} \\[3pt]
&=\; 3000 + 1256.64 + 106.67 \;=\; \mathbf{4363.31 \; mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Angle of contact:** *(3 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin\beta \;&=\; \frac{R+r}{C} \;=\; \frac{400}{1500} \;=\; 0.2667 \\[3pt]
\beta \;&=\; \sin^{-1}(0.2667) \;=\; 0.2700 \;\text{rad} \;(\approx 15.47°) \\[3pt]
\alpha \;&=\; \pi + 2\beta \;=\; 3.1416 + 0.5399 \;=\; 3.6815 \;\text{rad} \\[3pt]
&=\; \mathbf{210.96°}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $P = 7.5\,\text{kW} = 7500\,\text{W}$, $N_{1} = 250\,\text{rpm}$, $D_{1} = 400\,\text{mm} \Rightarrow R = 200\,\text{mm}$, $D_{2} = 200\,\text{mm} \Rightarrow r = 100\,\text{mm}$, $C = 1500\,\text{mm}$, $\mu = 0.3$.

**Step 1 — Belt length:** *(2 marks)*

$$
L = 2(1500) + \pi(200+100) + \frac{(200+100)^{2}}{1500} = 3000 + 942.48 + 60 = 4002.48\;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Angle of contact:** *(1 mark)*

$$
\sin\beta = \frac{300}{1500} = 0.2, \quad \beta = 0.2014\;\text{rad}, \quad \alpha = \pi + 2\beta = 3.5443\;\text{rad} \;(= 203.07°)
$$

**Step 3 — Belt velocity and tensions:** *(3 marks)*

$$
v = \frac{\pi D_{1} N_{1}}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.4 \times 250}{60} = 5.236\;\text{m/s}
$$

From the power equation:

$$(T_{1} - T_{2})v = P \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_{1} - T_{2} = \frac{7500}{5.236} = 1432.4\;\text{N}$$

From the belt-friction equation (cross belt has the same wrap on both pulleys, so the limiting ratio is the same):

$$
\frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}} = e^{\mu\alpha} = e^{0.3 \times 3.5443} = e^{1.0633} = 2.896
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_{1} = \frac{1432.4 \times 2.896}{2.896 - 1} = \frac{4148.5}{1.896} = 2187.9\;\text{N}
$$

$$
T_{2} = T_{1}/2.896 = 755.5\;\text{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Initial tension $T_{0}$:** *(1 mark)*

For a cross belt, both tight-side and slack-side tensions ($T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$) act on both pulleys. The initial tension is:

$$
T_{0} = \frac{T_{1} + T_{2}}{2} = \frac{2187.9 + 755.5}{2} = \mathbf{1471.7\;\text{N}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing the $(R+r)$ vs $(R-r)$ term:** Many students blindly write the open-belt formula for a cross-belt problem and lose 3–4 marks immediately. **Always re-check the sign** in the third term of the length formula.
> 2. **Forgetting the *unit conversion* on centre distance:** $C$ in mm and radii in mm must be consistent, or you will get a numerical answer that is off by 1000. State units clearly in every step.
> 3. **Mixing up degrees and radians in $\sin^{-1}$:** KTU examiners check whether the angle of contact is in **radians** when you substitute into $e^{\mu\alpha}$ for the tension ratio. A common slip is to substitute the angle in degrees, yielding wildly incorrect tension ratios.
> 4. **Skipping the geometric feasibility check:** Failing to verify $C > R + r$ at the start of a problem costs at least 1 mark on a "validate the configuration" type question.
> 5. **Using $T_{0} = (T_{1} + T_{2})/2$ only for cross belt with *equal* wrap on both pulleys:** This is correct here, but for an *open* belt the relation is $T_{0} = (T_{1} + T_{2} + 2T_{c})/2$ where $T_{c}$ is centrifugal tension. The cross belt can ignore $T_{c}$ only if speeds are low.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Length of Cross Belt**

- **Definition:** Cross belt has its two straight portions crossing between the pulleys, forming an "X" pattern; reverses rotation direction at the driven shaft.
- **Geometric feasibility:** $C > R + r$ (mandatory); typically $C \geq 3(R+r)$ for good design.
- **Exact length formula:** $L = 2\sqrt{C^{2} - (R+r)^{2}} + \pi(R+r) + 2(R+r)\sin^{-1}\!\bigl((R+r)/C\bigr)$.
- **Approximate length formula (most used):** $L \approx 2C + \pi(R+r) + (R+r)^{2}/C$.
- **Wrap angle on each pulley:** $\alpha = \pi + 2\sin^{-1}\!\bigl((R+r)/C\bigr)$ — same on both pulleys (unlike open belt).
- **Belt-friction equation:** $T_{1}/T_{2} = e^{\mu\alpha}$ — use the *smaller* of the two contact angles in the open belt; for the cross belt both are equal, so use either.
- **Belt velocity:** $v = \pi D_{1} N_{1} / 60$ (in m/s when $D$ in m and $N$ in rpm).
- **Power transmitted:** $P = (T_{1} - T_{2})v$.
- **Initial tension (cross belt):** $T_{0} = (T_{1} + T_{2})/2$ (valid when wrap on both pulleys is equal and centrifugal tension is negligible).
- **Velocity ratio:** $N_{2}/N_{1} = D_{1}/D_{2} = R/r$ (independent of crossing — depends only on pulley sizes).
- **Open vs Cross comparison:** "Cross uses PLUS" mnemonic — the term $(R+r)$ replaces $(R-r)$ in both the wrap angle and the length formula.
- **Design warning:** Cross belts wear faster at the crossing point; service life is typically 30–50% of an equivalent open belt drive.
- **Common SI units:** Lengths in **m** or **mm**; tension in **N**; power in **W** (or kW); speed in **rad/s** or **rpm**; $\mu$ is dimensionless.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Angle of Arc of Contact
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT527 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Angle of Arc of Contact

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Angle of Arc of Contact (α)** is the angle (in radians or degrees) subtended at the center of a pulley by that portion of the belt or rope which remains in actual contact with the surface of the pulley. It is also commonly referred to as the **Angle of Lap** or **Angle of Wrap**.

Mathematically, it represents the angular measure of the arc on the pulley surface over which frictional engagement occurs between the belt and the pulley. The magnitude of this angle directly governs the **frictional grip** available for power transmission and the **ratio of belt tensions** on the tight and slack sides.

For a two-pulley belt drive system, there are **two distinct angles of contact** — one for the **smaller (driving/driven) pulley** and one for the **larger pulley**. Since the belt is inextensible, the length in contact with one pulley plus the length in contact with the other must equal the total belt length wrapped around the system.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hugging Hand" 🖐️

Imagine you are holding a cricket ball with your hand. The tighter you wrap your fingers around the ball, the greater the **arc of contact** between your palm and the ball. Now, if you try to pull the ball out of your hand:

- If you wrap only **half** your hand (≈180°), the ball will slip out easily.
- If you wrap your **full hand and wrist** (≈270° or more), the ball will not slip — the grip is much stronger.

**This is exactly how a belt grips a pulley.** The greater the angle of arc of contact, the larger the surface area of frictional engagement, and hence the more torque/power the belt can transmit without slipping. In engineering terms, friction force is proportional to the **exponential of the angle of contact** (Euler's belt friction equation: $\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \alpha}$).

## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Quantities Used in Arc of Contact Analysis:**
> - **α** = Angle of arc of contact (in **radians** for engineering calculations, **degrees** for convenience)
> - **r, R** = Radii of smaller and larger pulleys respectively (in **mm** or **m**)
> - **C** = Center distance between the two pulleys (in **mm** or **m**)
> - **β** = Auxiliary angle formed between the line of centers and the line perpendicular to the common tangent (in **radians** or **degrees**)
> - **μ** = Coefficient of friction between belt and pulley (dimensionless)
> - Standard unit: **Radian (rad)** in SI; however, the **degree (°)** is permitted in KTU board examinations.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

The Angle of Arc of Contact is a **gateway concept** in Dynamics of Machinery. Almost every numerical problem in Module 3 (Belt Drives, Rope Drives, Chain Drives) requires the student to first compute α before proceeding to find belt tensions, power transmitted, belt length, or initial tension. It is a **high-frequency, high-weightage** concept — typically worth **3 to 14 marks** depending on the depth of the problem.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Setup — Open Belt Drive

Consider two pulleys with centers $O_1$ (smaller) and $O_2$ (larger), separated by a center distance $C$. The smaller pulley has radius $r$ and the larger has radius $R$. A belt is wrapped around them in an **open configuration** (both runs parallel, no crossing).

The common internal tangent touches the smaller pulley at point $A$ and the larger pulley at point $B$. Drawing perpendiculars from $O_1$ and $O_2$ to this tangent:

- $O_1 A = r$ (perpendicular to tangent at $A$)
- $O_2 B = R$ (perpendicular to tangent at $B$)

A line drawn parallel to the tangent through $O_1$ meets $O_2 B$ at point $D$. The geometry reveals:

$$O_2 D = O_2 B - O_1 A = R - r$$

In the right triangle $\triangle O_1 D O_2$:

$$\sin(\beta) = \frac{O_2 D}{O_1 O_2} = \frac{R - r}{C}$$

This auxiliary angle $\beta$ is the **foundation** of every arc-of-contact derivation.

## 2.2 Derivation Logic — Why the Formulas Look the Way They Do

- The **line of centers** $O_1 O_2$ subtends an angle of $2\beta$ on the smaller pulley (split equally on the entering and leaving sides) because the belt must deviate by angle $\beta$ to clear the larger pulley.
- Therefore, the belt does **not** wrap around the full $360°$ of the smaller pulley — it loses a total of $2\beta$ from the theoretical $360°$ (or $\pi$ in terms of the half-circle).
- Conversely, the larger pulley **gains** an extra $2\beta$ of contact beyond the standard $180°$, because the belt must reach across the gap to engage the smaller pulley.

## 2.3 The Two Critical Formulas for Open Belt Drive

**Angle of contact on the smaller pulley:**

$$\alpha_s = 180° - 2\beta \quad \text{(or } \pi - 2\beta \text{ in radians)}$$

**Angle of contact on the larger pulley:**

$$\alpha_L = 180° + 2\beta \quad \text{(or } \pi + 2\beta \text{ in radians)}$$

**Verification of internal consistency:**

$$\alpha_s + \alpha_L = (\pi - 2\beta) + (\pi + 2\beta) = 2\pi = 360°$$

This confirms that the two angles sum to the full circumference — as they must, because the belt is a closed loop.

## 2.4 Cross Belt Drive Variation

In a **crossed belt drive**, the belt runs in opposite directions on the two pulleys. The geometry changes because the perpendiculars from $O_1$ and $O_2$ to the **internal common tangent** are on **opposite sides**. This yields:

$$\sin(\beta) = \frac{R + r}{C}$$

And in this case, **both pulleys** have the same angle of contact:

$$\alpha_s = \alpha_L = 180° + 2\beta \quad \text{(or } \pi + 2\beta \text{ in radians)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Memory Hook:** Open belt → **subtract** radii ($R - r$); Cross belt → **add** radii ($R + r$).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Unit | Applicable Drive Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Auxiliary angle (open) | $\sin(\beta) = \dfrac{R - r}{C}$ | dimensionless (angle in rad/°) | Open belt |
| 2 | Auxiliary angle (cross) | $\sin(\beta) = \dfrac{R + r}{C}$ | dimensionless (angle in rad/°) | Cross belt |
| 3 | Angle of contact (smaller pulley, open) | $\alpha_s = \pi - 2\beta$ | rad (or deg) | Open belt |
| 4 | Angle of contact (larger pulley, open) | $\alpha_L = \pi + 2\beta$ | rad (or deg) | Open belt |
| 5 | Angle of contact (both pulleys, cross) | $\alpha = \pi + 2\beta$ | rad (or deg) | Cross belt |
| 6 | Ratio of tensions (Euler) | $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \alpha}$ | dimensionless | Both |
| 7 | Power transmitted | $P = (T_1 - T_2)\,v$ | W (or kW) | Both |
| 8 | Belt velocity | $v = \dfrac{\pi d n}{60}$ | m/s | Both |
| 9 | Centrifugal tension | $T_C = m v^2$ | N | Both |
| 10 | Effective tension | $T_{\text{eff}} = T_1 - T_2 - T_C$ | N | Both |

> **Notation Key:** $T_1$ = tension on tight side; $T_2$ = tension on slack side; $m$ = mass per unit length of belt; $v$ = belt linear velocity; $d$ = pulley diameter; $n$ = pulley speed in rpm.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of arc of contact is **not confined to textbook problems** — it is the design cornerstone of:

- **Automotive serpentine belts** (alternator, AC compressor drives) — designers ensure $\alpha \geq 150°$ on every pulley to prevent slip.
- **Industrial conveyor systems** — minimum wrap angle is mandated by ISO 5048 to avoid material spillage.
- **Elevator and crane hoists** — wire rope arc of contact on the sheave determines safe working load.
- **Bicycle chain and gear systems** — improper arc of contact causes "chain skipping" under high torque.
- **CNC machine tool drives** — timing belt arcs of contact are precisely calculated to achieve micron-level positioning accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Open Belt Drive (Smaller Pulley)

**Geometric Construction:** Let the two pulleys be $P_1$ (smaller, radius $r$, center $O_1$) and $P_2$ (larger, radius $R$, center $O_2$). The center distance is $O_1 O_2 = C$. The upper straight portion of the open belt is tangent to $P_1$ at $A$ and to $P_2$ at $B$.

**Step 1 — Establish the auxiliary triangle:**

Draw $O_1 A \perp AB$ (perpendicular from $O_1$ to the belt). By the property of tangents, $O_1 A = r$. Similarly, draw $O_2 B \perp AB$; then $O_2 B = R$.

**Step 2 — Construct the right triangle:**

Draw a line through $O_1$ parallel to the belt $AB$. This line meets $O_2 B$ at point $D$. Since $O_1 D$ is parallel to $AB$ and both $O_1 A$ and $O_2 B$ are perpendicular to $AB$, the quadrilateral $O_1 A B D$ is a rectangle. Therefore:

$$O_1 D = AB \quad \text{and} \quad O_1 A = D B = r$$

**Step 3 — Compute the side $O_2 D$:**

From the rectangle property:

$$O_2 D = O_2 B - D B = R - r$$

**Step 4 — Apply trigonometry in $\triangle O_1 D O_2$:**

The angle at $O_1$ between the line of centers $O_1 O_2$ and the line $O_1 D$ is $\beta$. Therefore:

$$\sin(\beta) = \frac{\text{Opposite side}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{O_2 D}{O_1 O_2} = \frac{R - r}{C}$$

**Step 5 — Relate $\beta$ to the angle of contact on the smaller pulley:**

The total angle around $O_1$ is $360°$ ($2\pi$ rad). The straight portions of the belt depart from $O_1$ along directions that make an angle $\beta$ on either side of the line $O_1 O_2$. Therefore, the belt wraps the smaller pulley through an angle that is the **straight angle ($180°$) minus twice this deviation**:

$$\alpha_s = 180° - 2\beta$$

Or in radians:

$$\alpha_s = \pi - 2\beta$$

**Step 6 — Angle of contact on the larger pulley:**

By symmetry of the closed belt loop, the larger pulley must "compensate" for the lost $2\beta$ on the smaller pulley:

$$\alpha_L = 180° + 2\beta = \pi + 2\beta$$

**Final consolidated expression for open belt drive:**

$$\alpha_s = \pi - 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{R - r}{C}\right)$$

$$\alpha_L = \pi + 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{R - r}{C}\right)$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Cross Belt Drive

**Geometric Difference:** In a crossed belt, the two straight portions of the belt cross each other between the pulleys. The tangent points are such that the perpendiculars $O_1 A$ and $O_2 B$ are on **opposite sides** of the line of centers.

**Step 1 — Build the auxiliary triangle:**

Following the same construction logic, the line $O_2 D$ now equals the **sum** of the radii (not the difference) because the perpendiculars are on opposite sides:

$$O_2 D = R + r$$

**Step 2 — Trigonometric relation:**

$$\sin(\beta) = \frac{R + r}{C}$$

**Step 3 — Angle of contact:**

Both pulleys are identical in geometry, and both gain an additional $2\beta$ beyond $180°$:

$$\alpha_s = \alpha_L = \pi + 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{R + r}{C}\right)$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Demonstration (Open Belt)

**Problem:** Two pulleys of diameters $300 \text{ mm}$ and $450 \text{ mm}$ are connected by an open belt. The center distance is $1.8 \text{ m}$. Find the angles of contact on both pulleys.

**Given:**
- $d_1 = 300 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow r = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- $d_2 = 450 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow R = 225 \text{ mm} = 0.225 \text{ m}$
- $C = 1.8 \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — Compute the auxiliary angle $\beta$:**

$$\sin(\beta) = \frac{R - r}{C} = \frac{0.225 - 0.15}{1.8} = \frac{0.075}{1.8} = 0.04167$$

$$\beta = \sin^{-1}(0.04167) = 2.389°$$

**Step 2 — Angle of contact on smaller pulley:**

$$\alpha_s = 180° - 2\beta = 180° - 2(2.389°) = 180° - 4.778° = 175.22°$$

**Step 3 — Angle of contact on larger pulley:**

$$\alpha_L = 180° + 2\beta = 180° + 4.778° = 184.78°$$

**Step 4 — Verification:**

$$\alpha_s + \alpha_L = 175.22° + 184.78° = 360° \quad \checkmark$$

**Conversion to radians (required for Euler's equation):**

$$\alpha_s = 175.22° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} = 3.058 \text{ rad}$$

$$\alpha_L = 184.78° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} = 3.225 \text{ rad}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Open vs. Cross Belt

| Property | Open Belt Drive | Cross Belt Drive |
|---|---|---|
| Auxiliary angle formula | $\sin(\beta) = \dfrac{R - r}{C}$ | $\sin(\beta) = \dfrac{R + r}{C}$ |
| Angle on smaller pulley | $\pi - 2\beta$ | $\pi + 2\beta$ |
| Angle on larger pulley | $\pi + 2\beta$ | $\pi + 2\beta$ |
| Symmetry of angles | Asymmetric (pulleys differ) | Symmetric (both equal) |
| Belt length | Shorter (more compact) | Longer (due to crossing) |
| Direction of rotation | Same direction on both pulleys | Opposite direction on both pulleys |
| Wear pattern | Uniform | Concentrated at crossover region |
| Typical application | Most industrial drives | Short-center, reversing applications |
| Slip tendency | Higher on smaller pulley | Reduced due to larger $\alpha$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Geometric Block Diagram — Arc of Contact Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **functional flow** of computing the angle of arc of contact, from input parameters to the final angular outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Parameters: r, R, C] --> B{Determine Drive Type}
    B -->|Open Belt| C[Compute sin beta = R minus r over C]
    B -->|Cross Belt| D[Compute sin beta = R plus r over C]
    C --> E[beta in degrees]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Drive Type Selection}
    F -->|Open Belt| G1[alpha_s = 180 minus 2 beta]
    F -->|Open Belt| G2[alpha_L = 180 plus 2 beta]
    F -->|Cross Belt| H1[alpha_s = 180 plus 2 beta]
    F -->|Cross Belt| H2[alpha_L = 180 plus 2 beta]
    G1 --> I[Convert to radians]
    G2 --> I
    H1 --> I
    H2 --> I
    I --> J[Apply Euler equation T1 over T2 = exp mu alpha]
    J --> K[Compute power transmitted P]
    K --> L[Final Design Output]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Role of Arc of Contact in Belt Drive Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Pulley Geometry: r and R] --> B1[Center Distance C]
    B1 --> C1[Arc of Contact alpha]
    C1 --> D1[Belt Tension Ratio T1 over T2]
    D1 --> E1[Effective Tension]
    E1 --> F1[Power Transmission Capacity]
    F1 --> G1[Design Decision: Pulley Diameter and Center Distance]
    G1 -.feedback.-> B1
```

## 4.3 Visual Schematic of Open Belt Drive (Descriptive Block Form)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph A2 [Smaller Pulley P1]
        A2a[Center O1]
        A2b[Radius r]
        A2c[Contact Arc alpha_s = 180 minus 2 beta]
    end
    subgraph B2 [Larger Pulley P2]
        B2a[Center O2]
        B2b[Radius R]
        B2c[Contact Arc alpha_L = 180 plus 2 beta]
    end
    A2c -->|Tight side T1| B2c
    B2c -->|Slack side T2| A2c
    A2a --- B2a
```

## 4.4 Compact Numerical Worked-Example Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Step1[Given: d1 = 300 mm, d2 = 450 mm, C = 1.8 m] --> Step2[Convert to radii: r = 0.15 m, R = 0.225 m]
    Step2 --> Step3[Compute sin beta = 0.075 over 1.8 = 0.04167]
    Step3 --> Step4[beta = 2.389 degrees]
    Step4 --> Step5a[alpha_s = 175.22 degrees]
    Step4 --> Step5b[alpha_L = 184.78 degrees]
    Step5a --> Step6[Convert to radians: 3.058 rad and 3.225 rad]
    Step5b --> Step6
    Step6 --> Step7[Ready for Euler Belt Friction Analysis]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "Angle of Arc of Contact" as applied to belt drives. Why is it considered a critical design parameter?**

**Model Answer:**
The angle of arc of contact, also called the angle of lap, is the angular measure (in radians or degrees) of the belt that is in contact with the surface of a pulley. For an open belt drive, it is computed as $\alpha_s = 180° - 2\beta$ for the smaller pulley and $\alpha_L = 180° + 2\beta$ for the larger pulley, where $\sin(\beta) = (R - r)/C$.

It is a critical design parameter because the **ratio of belt tensions** is governed by the exponential relationship $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \alpha}$, meaning a small change in $\alpha$ produces a large change in the power-transmitting capacity. A small $\alpha$ leads to slip; hence designers always target $\alpha \geq 150°$ on the smaller pulley.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between the angle of contact in an open belt drive and a cross belt drive. State the formulas.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Open Belt Drive | Cross Belt Drive |
|---|---|---|
| Auxiliary angle $\beta$ | $\sin(\beta) = \dfrac{R - r}{C}$ | $\sin(\beta) = \dfrac{R + r}{C}$ |
| Contact angle on smaller pulley | $180° - 2\beta$ | $180° + 2\beta$ |
| Contact angle on larger pulley | $180° + 2\beta$ | $180° + 2\beta$ |
| Sum of both contact angles | $360°$ | $360°$ |

In an open belt, the two contact angles are different; in a cross belt, both are equal because the geometry is symmetric.

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**An open belt drive connects two pulleys of diameters $400 \text{ mm}$ and $600 \text{ mm}$. The center distance between the pulleys is $2.4 \text{ m}$. The coefficient of friction between the belt and pulley is $0.3$, and the belt mass is $0.9 \text{ kg/m}$. The smaller pulley runs at $1440 \text{ rpm}$.**

**(a)** Determine the angles of contact on both pulleys.
**(b)** If the maximum permissible tension in the belt is $1500 \text{ N}$, calculate the power that can be transmitted.

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Identify given data:**

- $d_1 = 400 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow r = 0.2 \text{ m}$
- $d_2 = 600 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow R = 0.3 \text{ m}$
- $C = 2.4 \text{ m}$
- Drive type: **Open belt**

**Step 2 — Compute auxiliary angle $\beta$:**

$$\sin(\beta) = \frac{R - r}{C} = \frac{0.3 - 0.2}{2.4} = \frac{0.1}{2.4} = 0.04167$$

$$\beta = \sin^{-1}(0.04167) = 2.389°$$

**Step 3 — Angles of contact:**

$$\alpha_s = 180° - 2(2.389°) = 175.22° = 3.058 \text{ rad}$$

$$\alpha_L = 180° + 2(2.389°) = 184.78° = 3.225 \text{ rad}$$

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Belt velocity:**

$$v = \frac{\pi d_1 n}{60} = \frac{\pi (0.4)(1440)}{60} = 30.16 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Centrifugal tension:**

$$T_C = m v^2 = 0.9 \times (30.16)^2 = 0.9 \times 909.6 = 818.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum tight-side tension (limited to $1500 \text{ N}$):**

$$T_1 = 1500 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Use the smaller pulley's contact angle (critical):**

$$\alpha_s = 3.058 \text{ rad}$$

$$\frac{T_1 - T_C}{T_2 - T_C} = e^{\mu \alpha_s} = e^{0.3 \times 3.058} = e^{0.9174} = 2.503$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $T_2$:**

$$\frac{1500 - 818.6}{T_2 - 818.6} = 2.503$$

$$T_2 - 818.6 = \frac{681.4}{2.503} = 272.2 \text{ N}$$

$$T_2 = 1090.8 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6 — Power transmitted:**

$$P = (T_1 - T_2)\,v = (1500 - 1090.8)(30.16) = 409.2 \times 30.16 = 12,341 \text{ W} \approx 12.34 \text{ kW}$$

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**A crossed belt drive connects two pulleys of radii $150 \text{ mm}$ and $250 \text{ mm}$. The center distance is $1.5 \text{ m}$ and the coefficient of friction is $0.25$.**

**(a)** Calculate the angle of contact on each pulley and the length of the belt required.
**(b)** Find the ratio of tensions and the power transmitted if the belt speed is $20 \text{ m/s}$ and the maximum tension is $1200 \text{ N}$.

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute auxiliary angle for cross belt:**

$$\sin(\beta) = \frac{R + r}{C} = \frac{0.25 + 0.15}{1.5} = \frac{0.4}{1.5} = 0.2667$$

$$\beta = \sin^{-1}(0.2667) = 15.47°$$

**Step 2 — Angle of contact (both pulleys equal in cross belt):**

$$\alpha_s = \alpha_L = 180° + 2(15.47°) = 180° + 30.94° = 210.94°$$

In radians: $\alpha = 3.682 \text{ rad}$

**Step 3 — Length of crossed belt:**

$$L = \frac{\pi (d_1 + d_2)}{2} + 2C + \frac{(d_1 + d_2)^2}{4C}$$

$$L = \frac{\pi (0.3 + 0.5)}{2} + 2(1.5) + \frac{(0.3 + 0.5)^2}{4(1.5)}$$

$$L = \frac{\pi (0.8)}{2} + 3.0 + \frac{0.64}{6.0}$$

$$L = 1.2566 + 3.0 + 0.1067 = 4.363 \text{ m}$$

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Ratio of tensions:**

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \alpha} = e^{0.25 \times 3.682} = e^{0.9205} = 2.51$$

**Step 2 — Maximum tight-side tension:**

$$T_1 = 1200 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Slack-side tension (assuming negligible $T_C$ here):**

$$T_2 = \frac{1200}{2.51} = 478.1 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Power transmitted:**

$$P = (T_1 - T_2)\,v = (1200 - 478.1)(20) = 721.9 \times 20 = 14,438 \text{ W} \approx 14.44 \text{ kW}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Trap:** Forgetting to convert $\alpha$ from degrees to **radians** before using Euler's equation $e^{\mu \alpha}$. This alone costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Sign Confusion:** In open belt drives, students often incorrectly add $2\beta$ to the smaller pulley's contact angle. Remember: the **smaller** pulley **loses** contact (use $\pi - 2\beta$), the **larger** pulley **gains** (use $\pi + 2\beta$).
> 3. **Centrifugal Tension Omission:** Many students compute $P = (T_1 - T_2)v$ directly without subtracting the centrifugal tension $T_C$. The correct formula uses the **effective tension**: $P = (T_1 - T_2 - T_C)v$ or equivalently uses the ratio $\dfrac{T_1 - T_C}{T_2 - T_C} = e^{\mu \alpha}$.
> 4. **Cross vs. Open Mix-up:** The single most common error is using $(R - r)$ instead of $(R + r)$ for a cross belt, or vice versa. Always **draw the configuration first**.
> 5. **Forgetting Internal Choice:** KTU ESE 14-mark questions have an **internal choice (OR option)**. Always attempt both alternatives in your preparation.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

- ✅ **Angle of arc of contact** is the angular belt-pulley engagement measure, governing frictional grip.
- ✅ **Open belt:** $\alpha_s = \pi - 2\beta$, $\alpha_L = \pi + 2\beta$, where $\sin(\beta) = (R - r)/C$.
- ✅ **Cross belt:** $\alpha_s = \alpha_L = \pi + 2\beta$, where $\sin(\beta) = (R + r)/C$.
- ✅ **Always** convert the final $\alpha$ to **radians** before applying the Euler belt friction equation.
- ✅ **Verification trick:** The two contact angles must sum to exactly $360°$ — use this as a self-check.
- ✅ **Design rule of thumb:** The smaller pulley always has the **smaller** contact angle, hence the **critical** pulley for slip analysis.
- ✅ **Practical minimum:** For safe industrial operation, $\alpha_s \geq 150°$ on the smaller pulley.
- ✅ **Centrifugal tension** must be **subtracted** from both tight and slack side tensions before computing the tension ratio.
- ✅ **Belt velocity** $v = \pi d n / 60$ — note diameter (not radius).
- ✅ The **tight side** of the belt always leaves the **smaller** pulley on the side that produces greater wrap.
- ✅ Cross belt drives have **higher contact angles** than open belt drives of identical dimensions, but suffer from **faster wear** at the crossover point.
- ✅ The angle of arc of contact directly affects **belt life** — lower $\alpha$ means higher slip, higher heat, lower life.
- ✅ In KTU examinations, always **state the formula first**, then substitute numerical values — this earns **partial marks** even if the final answer is wrong.
- ✅ **SI units** (N, m, m/s, W) are mandatory; mixing units (mm with m) is a guaranteed **mark deduction**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ratio of Belt Tensions
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Ratio of Belt Tensions

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of flat and V-belt drives, the **Ratio of Belt Tensions** refers to the mathematical relationship between the tensions in the tight side (T₁) and slack side (T₂) of the belt as it wraps around the driving and driven pulleys. This ratio is fundamentally governed by the **Euler's Belt Friction Equation** (also called the Eytelwein's formula), which states that the ratio of tensions is an exponential function of the coefficient of friction (μ) between the belt and the pulley, and the angle of wrap (θ) in radians.

Mathematically, the primary relationship is expressed as:

$$
\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}
$$

Where:
- **T₁** = Tension in the tight side of the belt (N)
- **T₂** = Tension in the slack side of the belt (N)
- **μ** = Coefficient of friction between the belt and the pulley surface (dimensionless)
- **θ** = Angle of wrap / angle of contact in radians (rad)
- **e** = Base of natural logarithm (Euler's number ≈ **2.71828**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is a foundational building block for Module 3 of PERAT527. The concept of the tension ratio is a prerequisite for understanding power transmission, maximum power condition, and centrifugal effects in belt drives. KTU examiners frequently test the derivation of this ratio combined with numerical applications involving centrifugal tension.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are pulling a thick rope wrapped around a cylindrical wooden post (like tying a boat to a bollard). You will notice a fascinating phenomenon: even though you are pulling with a small force at one end, the person holding the other end of the rope needs to apply a much larger force to keep the rope from slipping. This is the exact principle behind the **Ratio of Belt Tensions**.

When a belt wraps around a pulley, friction between the belt surface and the pulley surface prevents slippage. The belt on the side approaching the pulley (tight side, T₁) carries significantly more tension than the side leaving the pulley (slack side, T₂). The exponential nature of the ratio implies that a small increase in either the friction coefficient or the wrap angle produces a disproportionately large increase in the tension ratio, allowing the belt to transmit substantial power without slipping.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used in this Module:**
> - Coefficient of friction (μ) for leather belt on cast iron pulley: **0.25 – 0.35**
> - Coefficient of friction (μ) for rubber belt on cast iron pulley: **0.30 – 0.40**
> - Standard mass density of belt material (leather): **1000 kg/m³**
> - Standard mass density of belt material (rubber): **1100 – 1200 kg/m³**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**

## 1.3 Concept of Tight Side and Slack Side

In a belt drive system, the belt has two distinct tension values:

| Parameter | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|--------|------------------|
| Tight Side Tension | T₁ | The side of the belt that delivers pulling force to the driven pulley |
| Slack Side Tension | T₂ | The side of the belt returning from the driven pulley with reduced load |
| Centrifugal Tension | T_c | Additional tension due to the centrifugal effect of belt mass during motion |

The difference (T₁ − T₂) is known as the **effective tension** or **net driving force** available at the pulley rim, which directly determines the power transmitted by the belt drive.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why Does the Ratio Exist?

The concept of the belt tension ratio emerges from the **theory of friction on curved surfaces**. When a flexible element (belt or rope) is in contact with a curved rigid body (pulley) under normal pressure, the friction forces along the contact arc create a cumulative tension differential.

The underlying physics involves:
- **Normal reaction** distributed along the contact arc of the belt
- **Tangential friction force** = μ × (Normal reaction) opposing relative slip
- **Differential equilibrium** of an elemental belt arc of length **ds** = R·dθ

When the belt is on the verge of slipping (impending motion), the friction is at its maximum static value, and the tensions follow the exponential law.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow for the Ratio Derivation

Consider an infinitesimal element of the belt wrapped around the pulley subtending an angle **dθ** at the center:

**Step 1:** Let the tension on one side of the element be **T** and on the other side be **T + dT**.

**Step 2:** The normal reaction **dN** acts radially outward, while the friction force **μ·dN** acts tangentially opposing the impending slip.

**Step 3:** Resolving forces along the tangential direction of the belt element gives the differential equation governing tension variation.

**Step 4:** Integrating this differential equation from 0 to θ (the total angle of wrap) yields the exponential relationship.

**Step 5:** The boundary conditions applied are: at θ = 0, T = T₂ (slack side) and at θ = θ, T = T₁ (tight side).

## 2.3 Centrifugal Tension: A Critical Correction

When the belt moves at high velocity, the mass of the belt itself creates an additional outward radial force on the pulley, which manifests as an additional tension called **Centrifugal Tension (T_c)**.

The centrifugal tension is mathematically expressed as:

$$
T_c = m \cdot v^2
$$

Where:
- **m** = mass of the belt per unit length (kg/m)
- **v** = linear velocity of the belt (m/s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When centrifugal tension is considered, the effective tensions on both sides of the belt must be reduced. The maximum power transmission formula uses the **effective tensions** (T₁ − T_c) and (T₂ − T_c) rather than the raw tensions T₁ and T₂.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | T₁ / T₂ = eᵘᶿ | Basic ratio of belt tensions (Euler's equation) | dimensionless |
| 2 | T₁ − T₂ = P / v | Effective tension from power equation | N |
| 3 | T_c = m · v² | Centrifugal tension due to belt mass | N |
| 4 | m = ρ · A | Mass per unit length of belt | kg/m |
| 5 | v = π · d · N / 60 | Linear belt velocity | m/s |
| 6 | T₁ = T_c + σ · A | Tight side tension (initial tension + centrifugal) | N |
| 7 | T₂ = T_c + σ' · A | Slack side tension | N |
| 8 | (T₁ − T_c) / (T₂ − T_c) = eᵘᶿ | Modified ratio with centrifugal effect | dimensionless |
| 9 | P_max = (T₁ − T₂) · v | Power transmitted by the belt | W |
| 10 | P_max = (T₁ − T_c) [1 − 1/eᵘᶿ] · v | Power with centrifugal correction | W |

> [!WARNING]
> **Strict LaTeX Rule Reminder:** When writing expressions like **\vert T_1 - T_2 \vert** in exam scripts, KTU examiners prefer the use of parentheses (T₁ − T₂) since belt tensions are always positive scalar quantities. The absolute value notation is rarely needed in this module.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ratio of belt tensions is critical in the design of:
- **Industrial machinery drives** (conveyor systems, mills, lathes)
- **Automotive accessory drives** (alternator belts, timing belts)
- **Agricultural equipment** (combine harvesters, tractor PTOs)
- **HVAC systems** (fan and blower drives)
- **Textile machinery** (spinning and weaving machines)

Engineers use this ratio to determine the minimum wrap angle required to prevent slippage, select appropriate belt materials with suitable friction coefficients, and design tensioning mechanisms for reliable power transmission.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Ratio of Belt Tensions (Euler's Equation)

### Setup and Free Body Diagram Description

Consider a pulley of radius **R** rotating in the anticlockwise direction. A belt is wrapped around the pulley over an angle of contact **θ**. Let:
- **T** = tension in the belt at any arbitrary section
- **T + dT** = tension after the belt has moved through an infinitesimal angle **dθ**
- **dN** = normal reaction on the elemental belt length
- **F_f** = friction force = μ · dN

The belt element subtends an angle **dθ** at the center of the pulley. The arc length of the element is **R · dθ**.

### Step 1: Force Resolution

The forces acting on the elemental belt section are:
1. Tension **T** on one side (acting tangentially toward the element)
2. Tension **T + dT** on the other side (acting tangentially away from the element)
3. Normal reaction **dN** acting radially outward
4. Friction force **μ · dN** acting tangentially, opposing the relative motion of the belt

### Step 2: Resolving Forces Along the Tangential Direction

Taking equilibrium of the elemental belt section along the tangential direction (the direction in which the belt is about to slip):

$$
(T + dT) \cos\left(\frac{d\theta}{2}\right) - T \cos\left(\frac{d\theta}{2}\right) - \mu \cdot dN = 0
$$

The two cosine terms can be simplified because for very small angles, **cos(dθ/2) ≈ 1**. The angle dθ is infinitesimally small, so:

$$
\cos\left(\frac{d\theta}{2}\right) \approx 1
$$

This reduces the tangential equilibrium equation to:

$$
(T + dT) - T - \mu \cdot dN = 0
$$

$$
dT = \mu \cdot dN
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This equation shows that the change in tension across the elemental arc is purely due to the friction force developed at the contact interface.

### Step 3: Resolving Forces Along the Radial Direction

For the radial equilibrium (assuming the belt is in contact with the pulley and pressing against it):

$$
dN = T \sin\left(\frac{d\theta}{2}\right) + (T + dT) \sin\left(\frac{d\theta}{2}\right)
$$

Using the small angle approximation **sin(dθ/2) ≈ dθ/2**:

$$
dN = T \cdot \frac{d\theta}{2} + (T + dT) \cdot \frac{d\theta}{2}
$$

$$
dN = (2T + dT) \cdot \frac{d\theta}{2}
$$

Since **dT** is infinitesimally small compared to **2T**, we can neglect the dT term:

$$
dN = T \cdot d\theta
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The normal reaction on the elemental belt arc is directly proportional to the local tension and the subtended angle.

### Step 4: Forming the Differential Equation

Substituting the value of **dN** from Step 3 into the equation from Step 2:

$$
dT = \mu \cdot T \cdot d\theta
$$

Rearranging to separate variables:

$$
\frac{dT}{T} = \mu \cdot d\theta
$$

### Step 5: Integration with Boundary Conditions

Integrating both sides from the slack side (T₂ at θ = 0) to the tight side (T₁ at θ = θ):

$$
\int_{T_2}^{T_1} \frac{dT}{T} = \int_{0}^{\theta} \mu \cdot d\theta
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right) = \mu \theta
$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$
\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This is the famous Euler's Belt Friction Equation. It establishes that the ratio of tensions grows exponentially with both the friction coefficient and the angle of wrap.

### Step 6: Verification with Limiting Cases

- If **μ = 0** (frictionless surface): T₁ / T₂ = e⁰ = 1, meaning no tension difference can be sustained.
- If **θ = 0** (no contact): T₁ / T₂ = e⁰ = 1, confirming that no power can be transmitted without contact.
- If both **μ** and **θ** are large: the ratio becomes very large, which is the desired condition for efficient power transmission.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Centrifugal Tension

### Setup

Consider a belt of mass **m** per unit length moving with linear velocity **v** around a pulley of radius **R**. The belt follows a circular path, so each elemental mass experiences a centripetal acceleration directed toward the center of the pulley.

### Step 1: Centripetal Force on an Elemental Belt Length

For an elemental length **dl** of the belt, the mass is **m · dl**. The centripetal force required to keep this element in circular motion is:

$$
dF_c = (m \cdot dl) \cdot \frac{v^2}{R}
$$

### Step 2: Relating Elemental Length to Angle

The arc length **dl** is related to the angle subtended **dθ** by:

$$
dl = R \cdot d\theta
$$

Substituting this into the force equation:

$$
dF_c = m \cdot R \cdot d\theta \cdot \frac{v^2}{R} = m \cdot v^2 \cdot d\theta
$$

### Step 3: Relating Force to Tension Difference

This centripetal force is provided by the difference in tensions on the two sides of the element, acting along the chord (which approximates the radial direction for small angles). For small **dθ**:

$$
dF_c = dT \cdot \frac{d\theta}{1}
$$

Wait, the correct relation from the earlier derivation is:

$$
dT = m \cdot v^2 \cdot d\theta
$$

(after careful analysis of the radial equilibrium with the centrifugal contribution).

### Step 4: Total Centrifugal Tension

The total centrifugal tension is the constant value that must be added uniformly to the entire belt:

$$
T_c = m \cdot v^2
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This shows that the centrifugal tension is independent of the pulley radius and depends only on the belt mass per unit length and the square of the linear velocity. This is why high-speed belt drives suffer significant power loss due to centrifugal effects.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Conditions Affecting the Ratio

| Condition | Effect on T₁/T₂ | Engineering Implication |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------------------|
| Increase in μ (better friction) | Ratio increases exponentially | Use rough belt material, crowned pulleys |
| Increase in θ (more wrap) | Ratio increases exponentially | Use idler pulleys, cross-belt drives |
| Increase in belt speed (v) | Indirect: T_c increases, effective ratio decreases | Limit maximum operating speed |
| Increase in belt mass (m) | T_c increases, effective tension decreases | Use lightweight materials at high speeds |
| Reduction in centrifugal tension (T_c) | Both effective tensions approach raw tensions | Design for optimal v where T_c is minimum fraction |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State Euler's belt friction equation and explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**

Euler's belt friction equation relates the tensions on the tight side (T₁) and slack side (T₂) of a belt in contact with a pulley. It is given by:

$$
\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}
$$

Where **μ** is the coefficient of friction between the belt and the pulley, and **θ** is the angle of wrap in radians.

**Physical Significance:**
- The equation establishes that the ratio of tensions grows exponentially with the contact angle and the friction coefficient.
- It represents the maximum tension ratio that can be sustained without slippage.
- It forms the basis for selecting the minimum wrap angle in belt drive design.
- The equation assumes a flexible, non-extensible belt with uniform friction distribution.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define centrifugal tension in a belt drive and write its expression.**

**Model Answer:**

**Centrifugal Tension (T_c):** It is the additional tension induced in a moving belt due to the centrifugal effect of its own mass as it travels around the pulleys at high speed.

The mathematical expression is:

$$
T_c = m \cdot v^2
$$

Where:
- **m** = mass of the belt per unit length (kg/m)
- **v** = linear velocity of the belt (m/s)

Centrifugal tension is independent of the radius of the pulley and reduces the effective driving tension available for power transmission.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the relation T₁/T₂ = eᵘᶿ for a flat belt drive, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flat belt drive has the following specifications: pulley diameters 300 mm and 150 mm, center distance 600 mm, coefficient of friction 0.3, and belt speed 12 m/s. Calculate the angle of wrap on the larger pulley and the ratio of belt tensions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Assumptions:**
1. The belt is perfectly flexible and inextensible.
2. The coefficient of friction μ is constant over the entire contact area.
3. The belt mass is neglected in the basic derivation (no centrifugal effect).
4. The belt is about to slip (limiting friction condition).
5. The pulley surface is smooth (no grooves or irregularities).

**Derivation Steps:**

Consider an elemental belt arc of length **ds = R·dθ** wrapped around a pulley of radius **R**.

**Forces on the element:**
- Tension T on the entering side
- Tension T + dT on the leaving side
- Normal reaction dN acting radially outward
- Friction force μ·dN acting tangentially opposing slip

**Tangential Equilibrium:**

$$
(T + dT) - T - \mu \cdot dN = 0
$$

$$
dT = \mu \cdot dN \quad \text{...(1)}
$$

**Radial Equilibrium (using small angle approximation):**

$$
dN = 2T \cdot \sin\left(\frac{d\theta}{2}\right) \approx T \cdot d\theta \quad \text{...(2)}
$$

**Substituting (2) into (1):**

$$
dT = \mu \cdot T \cdot d\theta
$$

$$
\frac{dT}{T} = \mu \cdot d\theta
$$

**Integrating from T₂ to T₁ (when θ varies from 0 to θ):**

$$
\int_{T_2}^{T_1} \frac{dT}{T} = \int_{0}^{\theta} \mu \cdot d\theta
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right) = \mu \theta
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}}
$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given Data:**
- Larger pulley diameter: D₁ = 300 mm = 0.3 m
- Smaller pulley diameter: D₂ = 150 mm = 0.15 m
- Center distance: C = 600 mm = 0.6 m
- Coefficient of friction: μ = 0.3
- Belt speed: v = 12 m/s

**Step 1: Calculate the angle of wrap on the larger pulley.**

For an open belt drive, the angle of wrap on the larger pulley is:

$$
\theta_1 = \pi + 2\alpha
$$

Where **α** is given by:

$$
\sin\alpha = \frac{r_1 - r_2}{C} = \frac{D_1 - D_2}{2C}
$$

$$
\sin\alpha = \frac{0.3 - 0.15}{2 \times 0.6} = \frac{0.15}{1.2} = 0.125
$$

$$
\alpha = \sin^{-1}(0.125) = 7.18° = 0.1253 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate θ₁:**

$$
\theta_1 = \pi + 2 \times 0.1253 = 3.1416 + 0.2507 = 3.3923 \text{ rad}
$$

Converting to degrees: θ₁ = 194.37°

**Step 3: Calculate the ratio of tensions:**

$$
\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta_1} = e^{0.3 \times 3.3923}
$$

$$
\mu \theta_1 = 0.3 \times 3.3923 = 1.0177
$$

$$
\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{1.0177} = 2.767
$$

**Final Answer:** The angle of wrap on the larger pulley is **3.39 rad (194.37°)**, and the ratio of belt tensions is **T₁/T₂ ≈ 2.77**.

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Explain the concept of centrifugal tension in belt drives. Derive an expression for the maximum power transmitted by a belt, considering the effect of centrifugal tension. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A leather belt transmits 15 kW of power at a belt speed of 18 m/s. The angle of wrap is 170° and the coefficient of friction is 0.28. The mass of the belt per meter length is 0.5 kg/m. Calculate the tensions in the tight and slack sides of the belt. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Concept of Centrifugal Tension:**

When a belt runs at high speed, its mass creates a centrifugal force that pulls the belt away from the pulley surface. This produces an additional uniform tension throughout the belt called centrifugal tension (T_c), which reduces the effective tension available for power transmission.

**Expression for Centrifugal Tension:**

$$
T_c = m \cdot v^2
$$

**Power Transmitted by the Belt:**

The power transmitted is the product of the net effective driving force and the belt velocity:

$$
P = (T_1 - T_c) - (T_2 - T_c)) \cdot v = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v
$$

When centrifugal tension is considered, the modified Euler equation becomes:

$$
\frac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu \theta}
$$

Let the effective tensions be:

$$
(T_1 - T_c) = \sigma_1 \cdot A \quad \text{and} \quad (T_2 - T_c) = \sigma_2 \cdot A
$$

Where σ₁ and σ₂ are the maximum and minimum safe stresses, and A is the cross-sectional area of the belt.

From the modified Euler equation:

$$
\frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_2} = e^{\mu \theta}
$$

The power transmitted per unit cross-sectional area is:

$$
\frac{P}{A \cdot v} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \sigma_1 \left(1 - \frac{1}{e^{\mu \theta}}\right)
$$

For maximum power transmission, the condition is derived by differentiating the power expression with respect to velocity and setting it to zero. The result gives the optimal belt speed.

**Maximum Power Equation:**

$$
P_{max} = (T_1 - T_c) \left(1 - \frac{1}{e^{\mu \theta}}\right) \cdot v
$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given Data:**
- Power: P = 15 kW = 15000 W
- Belt speed: v = 18 m/s
- Angle of wrap: θ = 170° = (170 × π/180) = 2.967 rad
- Coefficient of friction: μ = 0.28
- Mass per unit length: m = 0.5 kg/m

**Step 1: Calculate the centrifugal tension.**

$$
T_c = m \cdot v^2 = 0.5 \times (18)^2 = 0.5 \times 324 = 162 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the effective tension difference.**

From the power equation:

$$
T_1 - T_2 = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{15000}{18} = 833.33 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the ratio of effective tensions.**

$$
\frac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu \theta} = e^{0.28 \times 2.967}
$$

$$
\mu \theta = 0.28 \times 2.967 = 0.8308
$$

$$
e^{0.8308} = 2.295
$$

**Step 4: Solve the simultaneous equations.**

Let X = T₁ − T_c and Y = T₂ − T_c.

From Step 3: X / Y = 2.295, so X = 2.295Y

From Step 2: T₁ − T₂ = X − Y = 833.33 N

$$
2.295Y - Y = 833.33
$$

$$
1.295Y = 833.33
$$

$$
Y = 643.5 \text{ N}
$$

$$
X = 2.295 \times 643.5 = 1477.0 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate the actual tensions.**

$$
T_2 = Y + T_c = 643.5 + 162 = 805.5 \text{ N}
$$

$$
T_1 = X + T_c = 1477.0 + 162 = 1639.0 \text{ N}
$$

**Final Answer:** Tight side tension **T₁ ≈ 1639 N** and slack side tension **T₂ ≈ 805.5 N**.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Ratio of Belt Tensions Problems:**
>
> 1. **Angle Conversion Error:** Students frequently forget to convert the angle of wrap from degrees to radians before substituting into the Euler equation. KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** for using degrees directly in the exponential term.
>
> 2. **Ignoring Centrifugal Tension:** When belt speed exceeds 10 m/s, centrifugal tension MUST be considered. Forgetting to add T_c to both T₁ and T₂ results in **2-3 marks deduction**.
>
> 3. **Wrong Angle of Wrap Formula:** For cross-belt drives, the formula is different from open belt drives. Using the wrong formula leads to **2 marks deduction**.
>
> 4. **Sign Convention in Differential Equation:** The direction of the friction force is critical in the derivation. Reversing its sign leads to an inverted ratio, which is a fundamental error costing **3-4 marks**.
>
> 5. **Skipping Assumptions in Derivations:** KTU 2024 scheme requires explicit listing of assumptions before derivations. Omitting them costs **1-2 marks**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Euler's Belt Friction Equation:** The ratio T₁/T₂ = eᵘᶿ is the cornerstone formula for belt drive analysis. Always convert θ to radians.

- **Centrifugal Tension Formula:** T_c = m·v² must be applied whenever the belt velocity is significant. This tension reduces the effective driving tension.

- **Modified Ratio:** When centrifugal effects are considered, use (T₁ − T_c)/(T₂ − T_c) = eᵘᶿ.

- **Angle of Wrap Formulas:**
  - Open belt drive: θ_large = π + 2α, where sin α = (r₁ − r₂)/C
  - Cross belt drive: θ = π − 2α + 2π = π + 2α (revised for cross configuration)
  - Smaller pulley wrap: θ_small = π − 2α

- **Power Equation:** P = (T₁ − T₂) × v, where T₁ − T₂ is the effective driving force.

- **Effective Tension:** The maximum power transmission occurs when the centrifugal tension is properly accounted for in the design.

- **Standard μ Values:** Remember typical coefficient of friction values for leather (0.25-0.35) and rubber (0.30-0.40) belts on cast iron pulleys for quick calculations.

- **Belt Velocity Range:** Optimal belt velocities are typically between 10-25 m/s for industrial applications.

- **Limiting Cases:** Verify that the formula reduces to T₁ = T₂ when μ = 0 or θ = 0.

- **Safety Factor:** KTU problems often incorporate a safety factor of 1.2-1.5 in belt design calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT527 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Dynamics of Machinery (PERAT527)**, **Power Transmitted** refers to the rate at which useful mechanical work is transferred from the **driver (driving pulley)** to the **driven (driven pulley)** through a flexible friction-based transmission element — most commonly a **belt drive**, **rope drive**, or **chain drive**. 

Mathematically, for a belt running at a linear velocity `v` m/s with `T₁` as the tension in the tight side and `T₂` as the tension in the slack side:

$$P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v \quad \text{(Watts)}$$

The study of power transmission involves determining the **maximum power** that a belt can transmit without slipping, considering the effects of **centrifugal tension**, **friction angle**, and **angle of contact (wrap angle)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is critical for Module 3 (Belt, Rope and Chain Drives). Expect direct derivations of power equations, condition for maximum power, and numerical problems involving centrifugal tension in End Semester Examinations (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are pulling a **sled** across snow using a long rope. The harder you pull (more force), and the faster you run (more velocity), the more "power" you deliver to the sled. Now imagine the rope passes over a **pulley** (a spinning wheel) that is connected to the sled's axle.

- The **tight side** of the rope (between you and the pulley) is under **high tension** `T₁` — that's where the energy is "loaded."
- The **slack side** (between the pulley and the sled) is under **low tension** `T₂` — that's where the energy is "spent."
- The **difference** `(T₁ − T₂)` is the **net useful force** that actually does the work of moving the sled.
- When you start running very fast, the rope itself has a tendency to **fly outward** (centrifugal effect), tightening on both sides equally. This **robs effective tension** that could have been used to pull the sled.

> **Real-World Engineering Examples:**
> - **Car alternator drive belt** (V-belt transferring ~2–5 kW).
> - **Industrial conveyor systems** transferring tens of kW.
> - **Cottage industry flour mills** using flat leather belts.
> - **Crane hoists** using wire ropes.

## 1.3 Key Physical Parameters (Highlighted Constants)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used in KTU Problems:**
> - Belt linear velocity, `v` — typically **between 10 m/s and 30 m/s** for flat belts (above 30 m/s, belt flaps excessively).
> - Coefficient of friction, `μ` — for leather on cast iron ≈ **0.28 to 0.35**; for rubber on cast iron ≈ **0.25 to 0.30**.
> - Angle of contact, `θ` — must be in **radians** when used inside the Euler's belt friction equation `e^(μθ)`.
> - Belt density (for centrifugal tension): leather ≈ **1000 kg/m³**, rubber ≈ **1100–1200 kg/m³**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Step-by-Step Logic

The complete analysis of power transmission in a belt drive is built on **four foundational pillars**:

### Pillar 1: Basic Power Equation (Without Centrifugal Effects)
The **net effective driving force** at the rim of the pulley is the difference between the two side tensions.

$$F_{\text{net}} = T_1 - T_2$$

When this force acts on the belt moving at linear velocity `v`:

$$P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v \quad \text{[Watts]}$$

### Pillar 2: Ratio of Tensions (Euler's Belt Friction Equation)
For a belt to operate **without slipping**, the tension ratio is governed by the **capstan equation** (Euler–Eytelwein equation):

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$$

where:
- `μ` = coefficient of friction between belt and pulley
- `θ` = angle of contact (wrap angle) in radians
- For an open belt drive: `θ = π + (2α)` where `2α` is the angle subtended by the groove at the larger pulley.
- For a crossed belt drive: `θ = π + 2α` for both pulleys.

### Pillar 3: Centrifugal Tension (Critical Real-World Effect)
When the belt moves at high velocity, each particle of the belt is subjected to **centrifugal force acting outward**. This creates an **additional tension `T_c`** that is **uniform on both tight and slack sides**, thereby reducing the net effective tension.

$$T_c = m \cdot v^2 = \rho \cdot A \cdot v^2$$

where:
- `m` = mass per unit length of the belt (kg/m)
- `ρ` = density of belt material (kg/m³)
- `A` = cross-sectional area of belt (m²)
- `v` = belt linear velocity (m/s)

The **effective tensions** for power transmission become:
- Effective tight side tension: `T₁ − T_c`
- Effective slack side tension: `T₂ − T_c`

**Revised Power Equation (with centrifugal tension):**

$$P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v = \{(T_1 - T_c) - (T_2 - T_c)\} \cdot v$$

> [!NOTE]
> Interestingly, the centrifugal tension **cancels out** in the simple power equation `P = (T₁ − T₂)v`. However, it directly affects the **maximum power condition** and the **initial tension requirements**.

### Pillar 4: Initial Tension (The Pre-Tension Required)
Before the belt is loaded, both sides have the **same pre-tension** `T₀`. Once loaded, the tension redistributes as `T₁` (tight) and `T₂` (slack). Neglecting centrifugal tension:

$$T_0 = \frac{T_1 + T_2}{2}$$

Including centrifugal tension, since `T_c` is always present:

$$T_0 = \frac{T_1 + T_2}{2} + T_c$$

### Pillar 5: Condition for Maximum Power Transmission
This is the **most important derivation** in KTU examinations. To find the condition where the belt transmits the **maximum possible power**, we differentiate power with respect to `T_c` (or equivalently, with respect to `v`) and set it to zero.

**Setup:** Let the maximum allowable tension in the tight side be `T` (a fixed design parameter limited by belt strength). Then:

$$T_1 = T \quad \text{(fixed, governed by belt material strength)}$$

Since `T_c = mv²`, and `T₁/T₂ = e^(μθ)`:

$$T_2 = \frac{T_1}{e^{\mu \theta}} = \frac{T}{e^{\mu \theta}}$$

**Power transmitted:**

$$P = (T_1 - T_2) v = \left( T - \frac{T}{e^{\mu \theta}} \right) v = T \left( 1 - \frac{1}{e^{\mu \theta}} \right) v$$

But `v = \sqrt{T_c / m}`, so we can write `T_1 = T = 3 T_c` at the condition of maximum power (proven in derivation section).

**Maximum Power Condition Result:**

$$T_1 = 3 \cdot T_c \quad \text{and} \quad T_2 = T_c$$

This means at maximum power:
- The tight side carries **3 times** the centrifugal tension.
- The slack side carries **1 time** the centrifugal tension.

**Maximum Power Equation:**

$$P_{\max} = \frac{T^2}{4 m e^{\mu \theta} / (e^{\mu \theta} - 1)} \quad \text{(general form, derived below)}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving any KTU belt drive numerical. **Memorize all entries** before the exam.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Application | Standard Units |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v$ | Basic power transmitted | Watts (W) |
| 2 | $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$ | Euler's belt friction equation (no-slip) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $T_c = m v^2 = \rho A v^2$ | Centrifugal tension in belt | Newtons (N) |
| 4 | $T_0 = \dfrac{T_1 + T_2}{2} + T_c$ | Initial (pre-load) tension on belt | N |
| 5 | $T_1 = 3 T_c$ | Tight-side tension at max power | N |
| 6 | $T_2 = T_c$ | Slack-side tension at max power | N |
| 7 | $v_{\max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{3m}}$ | Belt velocity at maximum power | m/s |
| 8 | $P_{\max} = \dfrac{T^2}}{3m e^{\mu\theta}/(e^{\mu\theta}-1)}$ | Maximum power transmittable | W |
| 9 | $v = \dfrac{\pi d n}{60}$ | Belt linear velocity from pulley speed | m/s |
| 10 | $\theta = \pi + 2\alpha$ | Open belt — larger pulley contact angle | rad |
| 11 | $\theta = \pi - 2\alpha$ | Open belt — smaller pulley contact angle | rad |
| 12 | $\theta = \pi + 2\alpha$ | Crossed belt — both pulleys (same) | rad |
| 13 | $\sin\alpha = \dfrac{r_1 - r_2}{C}$ | Angle of V-groove for open belt drive | rad |
| 14 | $v_1 = v_2 (1 - \text{slip})$ | Belt velocity considering slip | m/s |

> **Note on Units:** Belt power in **kilowatts (kW)** = `(T₁ − T₂) × v / 1000`. Belt velocity `v` in **m/s**. Tensions in **Newtons (N)**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The analysis of power transmission in belt drives is **foundational** to nearly every rotating machine:

- **Automotive engineering:** Engine accessory drives (alternator, water pump, AC compressor) — engineers select V-belts based on max power curves.
- **Industrial machinery:** CNC machines, lathes, milling machines — pulley ratios and belt selections depend on these equations.
- **Agricultural engineering:** Tractor PTO drives, threshers, combine harvesters.
- **Textile mills:** Spinning frames running at high belt speeds where centrifugal tension dominates.
- **Elevators and cranes:** Wire rope hoists where safety factor depends on initial tension calculations.

The **maximum power condition** is the design cornerstone: it tells the engineer the **optimal belt speed** to transmit the most power with the least belt wear.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Condition for Maximum Power Transmission

This is the **flagship derivation** in KTU Module 3. The full step-by-step algebraic transition is shown below.

### Given
- Maximum allowable belt tension (set by belt material strength) = `T` (constant).
- Belt mass per unit length = `m` (kg/m).
- Belt linear velocity = `v` (m/s).
- Coefficient of friction = `μ`.
- Angle of contact = `θ` (radians).

### Step 1: Express Tensions in Terms of Centrifugal Tension

By Euler's equation:
$$T_2 = \frac{T_1}{e^{\mu \theta}}$$

Since the maximum allowable tension is `T`, we set the tight side equal to this limit:
$$T_1 = T$$

Therefore:
$$T_2 = \frac{T}{e^{\mu \theta}}$$

### Step 2: Write the Power Equation

Power transmitted by the belt is the net tension times belt velocity:
$$P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v$$

Substituting `T₁` and `T₂`:
$$P = \left( T - \frac{T}{e^{\mu \theta}} \right) \cdot v = T \left( \frac{e^{\mu \theta} - 1}{e^{\mu \theta}} \right) \cdot v$$

### Step 3: Substitute Velocity in Terms of Centrifugal Tension

Centrifugal tension is:
$$T_c = m v^2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad v = \sqrt{\frac{T_c}{m}}$$

So power becomes:
$$P = T \left( \frac{e^{\mu \theta} - 1}{e^{\mu \theta}} \right) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{T_c}{m}}$$

### Step 4: Express Maximum Tension T in Terms of T_c

The total tension in the tight side `T₁` consists of the **effective pulling tension** plus the **centrifugal tension**:
$$T_1 = (T_1 - T_c) + T_c$$

The effective pulling tension in the tight side is `(T₁ − T_c)`. The ratio of effective tensions must still satisfy the belt friction equation:
$$\frac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu \theta}$$

We are told `T₁ = T` (fixed design limit). Solving for `T_2`:
$$T_2 = T_c + \frac{T - T_c}{e^{\mu \theta}}$$

Substituting into the power equation `P = (T₁ − T₂)v = (T − T₂)v`:

$$P = \left[ T - T_c - \frac{T - T_c}{e^{\mu \theta}} \right] v = (T - T_c) \left( \frac{e^{\mu \theta} - 1}{e^{\mu \theta}} \right) v$$

### Step 5: Differentiate Power with Respect to T_c (Treating T as Constant)

To find the **maximum power**, we treat `T` as constant (belt material limit) and `T_c` as the variable (since `T_c` depends on velocity, and velocity is what we choose).

$$\frac{dP}{dT_c} = \left( \frac{e^{\mu \theta} - 1}{e^{\mu \theta}} \right) \cdot \frac{d}{dT_c} \left[ (T - T_c) \cdot v \right]$$

But `v = \sqrt{T_c / m}`, so:
$$P = \left( \frac{e^{\mu \theta} - 1}{e^{\mu \theta}} \right) \cdot (T - T_c) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{T_c}{m}}$$

Differentiating with respect to `T_c` (treating `m`, `T`, `θ`, `μ` as constants):

$$\frac{dP}{dT_c} = \left( \frac{e^{\mu \theta} - 1}{m \, e^{\mu \theta}} \right) \cdot \frac{d}{dT_c} \left[ (T - T_c) \cdot T_c^{1/2} \right]$$

Apply the **product rule** `d/dx [u·v] = u'v + uv'` with `u = (T − T_c)`, `v = T_c^(1/2)`:

$$\frac{d}{dT_c} \left[ (T - T_c) \cdot T_c^{1/2} \right] = (-1) \cdot T_c^{1/2} + (T - T_c) \cdot \frac{1}{2} T_c^{-1/2}$$

$$= -T_c^{1/2} + \frac{T - T_c}{2 T_c^{1/2}} = \frac{-2 T_c + T - T_c}{2 T_c^{1/2}} = \frac{T - 3 T_c}{2 \sqrt{T_c}}$$

### Step 6: Set the Derivative to Zero (Maxima Condition)

$$\frac{dP}{dT_c} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T - 3 T_c = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{T = 3 T_c}$$

This is the **famous condition**:
$$T_1 = T = 3 T_c$$

### Step 7: Find the Slack-Side Tension at Max Power

From Step 4 expression for `T_2`:
$$T_2 = T_c + \frac{T - T_c}{e^{\mu \theta}} = T_c + \frac{3T_c - T_c}{e^{\mu \theta}} = T_c + \frac{2 T_c}{e^{\mu \theta}}$$

If the wrap angle is large and `e^(μθ)` is much greater than 1, the second term becomes negligible, and:
$$T_2 \approx T_c$$

**General result:**
$$\boxed{T_2 = T_c \left( 1 + \frac{2}{e^{\mu \theta}} \right)}$$

For practical KTU problems where `e^(μθ) >> 1`:
$$\boxed{T_2 \approx T_c}$$

### Step 8: Maximum Power Expression

Substituting `T₁ = 3T_c` and `T₂ ≈ T_c` into `P = (T₁ − T₂)v`:
$$P_{\max} = (3 T_c - T_c) \cdot v = 2 T_c \cdot v$$

Since `T_c = mv²`:
$$P_{\max} = 2 m v^2 \cdot v = 2 m v^3$$

Also, from `T_c = T/3 = mv²`:
$$v = \sqrt{\frac{T}{3m}}$$

So the **maximum power** becomes:
$$P_{\max} = 2 m \left( \sqrt{\frac{T}{3m}} \right)^3 = 2 m \cdot \frac{T^{3/2}}{(3m)^{3/2}} = \frac{2 T^{3/2}}{3 \sqrt{3} \cdot m^{1/2}}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\max} = \frac{2}{3\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{T^{3/2}}{\sqrt{m}} \approx 0.385 \, \frac{T^{3/2}}{\sqrt{m}}}$$

### Step 9: More General Expression Including μ and θ

Using the **exact** `T_2 = T_c (1 + 2/e^(μθ))`:
$$P_{\max} = (T_1 - T_2)v = \left( 3T_c - T_c - \frac{2T_c}{e^{\mu\theta}} \right) \sqrt{\frac{T_c}{m}}$$

$$= 2T_c \left( 1 - \frac{1}{e^{\mu\theta}} \right) \sqrt{\frac{T_c}{m}} = 2T_c \left( \frac{e^{\mu\theta} - 1}{e^{\mu\theta}} \right) \sqrt{\frac{T_c}{m}}$$

With `T_c = T/3`:
$$\boxed{P_{\max} = \frac{2T}{3} \left( \frac{e^{\mu\theta} - 1}{e^{\mu\theta}} \right) \sqrt{\frac{T}{3m}} = \frac{2}{3\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{T^{3/2}}{\sqrt{m}} \cdot \frac{e^{\mu\theta} - 1}{e^{\mu\theta}}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Initial Tension Formula

Consider a belt drive at rest. The belt is stretched with a pre-tension `T₀`. When the drive operates:
- Tight side tension becomes `T₁`.
- Slack side tension becomes `T₂`.
- The **stretch in the tight side** must equal the **reduction in stretch in the slack side** (belt is inextensible, so the total length is approximately constant).

Mathematically, the elongation of the tight side is proportional to `(T₁ − T₀)`, and the contraction of the slack side is proportional to `(T₀ − T₂)`. Equating these:

$$\frac{T_1 - T_0}{T_0 - T_2} = \frac{L_{\text{tight}}}{L_{\text{slack}}}$$

For a simple analysis (assuming equal lengths on both sides, or that centrifugal effect is ignored), the **average** gives:

$$T_0 = \frac{T_1 + T_2}{2}$$

**Including centrifugal tension** (since both sides gain `T_c` due to rotation):

$$T_0 = \frac{(T_1 - T_c) + (T_2 - T_c)}{2} + T_c = \frac{T_1 + T_2}{2}$$

Wait — this simplification shows that **centrifugal tension does NOT change the initial tension** in this idealized form. The more careful form is:

$$T_0 = \frac{T_1 + T_2 + 2 T_c}{2} = \frac{T_1 + T_2}{2} + T_c$$

This is the **standard KTU formula** for initial tension.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** A flat belt transmits **15 kW** at a belt speed of **20 m/s**. The angle of contact on the smaller pulley is **160°**, and the coefficient of friction is **0.3**. Find:
(a) The tensions in the tight and slack sides.
(b) The initial tension required.
(c) The belt mass per unit length if the centrifugal tension is to be 10% of the tight side tension.

### Step (a): Tight and Slack Side Tensions

Power equation:
$$P = (T_1 - T_2) v$$

$$15{,}000 = (T_1 - T_2) \times 20 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T_1 - T_2 = 750 \text{ N}$$

Angle of contact: `θ = 160° = 160 × π/180 = 2.7925` rad.

Euler's equation:
$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta} = e^{0.3 \times 2.7925} = e^{0.8378} = 2.3115$$

From `T_1 = 2.3115 T_2`:
$$2.3115 T_2 - T_2 = 750 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 1.3115 T_2 = 750$$
$$T_2 = 571.9 \text{ N}, \quad T_1 = 1321.9 \text{ N}$$

### Step (b): Initial Tension

$$T_0 = \frac{T_1 + T_2}{2} = \frac{1321.9 + 571.9}{2} = 946.9 \text{ N}$$

(Neglecting centrifugal tension for this part.)

### Step (c): Belt Mass per Unit Length

Centrifugal tension: `T_c = 0.10 × T_1 = 0.10 × 1321.9 = 132.19` N.

$$T_c = m v^2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 132.19 = m \times (20)^2 = 400 m$$
$$m = 0.3305 \text{ kg/m}$$

**Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Tight-side tension $T_1$ | 1321.9 N |
| Slack-side tension $T_2$ | 571.9 N |
| Initial tension $T_0$ | 946.9 N |
| Belt mass per unit length $m$ | 0.3305 kg/m |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define centrifugal tension in a belt drive. Why does it reduce the power transmission capacity of the belt?**

**Model Answer:**

**Centrifugal Tension** is the additional tension induced in a belt due to the centrifugal force acting on the belt mass when it moves at high velocity around the pulleys. It is given by:

$$T_c = m \cdot v^2$$

where `m` is the mass per unit length of the belt (kg/m) and `v` is the linear velocity of the belt (m/s).

**Why it reduces power transmission capacity:**
As `T_c` increases, it adds equally to both the tight-side and slack-side tensions, thereby **reducing the effective pulling tension** `(T₁ − T_c) − (T₂ − T_c)` available to do work. The net effective tension available at the rim of the pulley decreases, lowering the power that can be transmitted before the belt slips or the tight side exceeds the maximum allowable stress. Hence, designers select an **optimal belt velocity** corresponding to the maximum power condition to balance this trade-off.

> **Examiner's Tip:** Use the keyword *"equally on both sides"* to earn full marks. Many students forget that `T_c` is symmetric.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the condition for maximum power transmission in a belt drive.**

**Model Answer:**

The condition for maximum power transmission in a belt drive is obtained by differentiating the power equation with respect to centrifugal tension and setting the derivative to zero. The result is:

$$\boxed{T_1 = 3 \cdot T_c \quad \text{and} \quad T_2 = T_c}$$

This means that at maximum power, the tension in the **tight side is three times** the centrifugal tension, and the tension in the **slack side equals** the centrifugal tension. Under this condition, the belt operates at an optimal velocity `v = \sqrt{T/(3m)}` that maximizes useful power without slip.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions typically have an **internal choice**. Below are TWO completely independent alternatives modeled on actual KTU papers.

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) Derive the condition for maximum power transmission in a flat belt drive, clearly stating the assumptions made.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) A flat belt transmits 20 kW at a belt speed of 18 m/s. The angle of contact on the smaller pulley is 170° and the coefficient of friction is 0.28.
