*Groove Angle Effect:*

For a V-belt with groove angle $2\beta$, the effective coefficient of friction becomes:

$$\mu_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\mu}{\sin\beta}$$

The friction equation transforms to:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu\theta/\sin\beta}$$

This is **greater** than the flat-belt ratio $e^{\mu\theta}$ since $\sin\beta < 1$. Hence V-belts transmit more power for the same tension and friction coefficient.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

*Given:*
* $P = 20{,}000$ W
* $v = 15$ m/s
* $\theta = 160° = 2.7925$ rad
* $\mu = 0.25$
* $2\beta = 35°$ $\Rightarrow$ $\beta = 17.5°$
* $m = 1.0$ kg/m

*Step 1: Centrifugal tension*

$$T_c = m v^2 = 1.0 \times (15)^2 = 225 \text{ N}$$

*Step 2: Compute effective friction parameter*

$$\sin\beta = \sin(17.5°) = 0.3007$$

$$\mu_{\text{eff}} = \frac{0.25}{0.3007} = 0.8314$$

$$\mu_{\text{eff}} \theta = 0.8314 \times 2.7925 = 2.3216$$

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{2.3216} = 10.19$$

*Step 3: Determine $T_1 - T_2$ from power equation*

$$T_1 - T_2 = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{20{,}000}{15} = 1333.33 \text{ N}$$

*Step 4: Solve simultaneously for $T_1$ and $T_2$*

From the ratio: $T_1 = 10.19 \, T_2$

Substituting:

$$10.19 \, T_2 - T_2 = 1333.33$$

$$T_2 (10.19 - 1) = 1333.33$$

$$T_2 = \frac{1333.33}{9.19} = 145.09 \text{ N}$$

$$T_1 = 10.19 \times 145.09 = 1478.5 \text{ N}$$

*Step 5: Compute initial tension $T_0$*

$$T_0 = \frac{T_1 + T_2}{2} + T_c = \frac{1478.5 + 145.09}{2} + 225 = 811.8 + 225 = 1036.8 \text{ N}$$

**Results:**

$$\boxed{T_1 \approx 1478.5 \text{ N}, \quad T_2 \approx 145.1 \text{ N}, \quad T_0 \approx 1036.8 \text{ N}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting centrifugal tension** at high belt speeds ($v > 10$ m/s). This costs ~2 marks easily.
> 2. **Mixing up the order** in $T_0 = (T_1+T_2)/2 + T_c$. Some students write $T_0 = (T_1+T_2 + T_c)/2$, which is dimensionally and physically wrong.
> 3. **Using wrong pulley diameter** for velocity calculation. Always use the **driver pulley** diameter (or whichever pulley has the *given* RPM).
> 4. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** for $\theta$ in the friction equation $e^{\mu\theta}$. This is the single most common KTU error.
> 5. **V-belt groove angle confusion**: Use $2\beta$ (full groove angle) when computing $\sin\beta$. For a 35° groove, $\beta = 17.5°$.
> 6. **Maximum permissible stress**: When given, it directly fixes $T_1$. When not given, derive $T_1$ from the power equation combined with the friction ratio.

---

### 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Initial Belt Tension**

* ✅ **Core formula (no centrifugal):** $T_1 + T_2 = 2T_0$
* ✅ **Core formula (with centrifugal):** $T_0 = \dfrac{T_1 + T_2}{2} + T_c$
* ✅ **Centrifugal tension:** $T_c = m v^2$ (acts uniformly, subtracts from available grip)
* ✅ **Flat belt friction equation:** $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu\theta}$
* ✅ **V-belt friction equation:** $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu\theta/\sin\beta}$
* ✅ **Derivation basis:** Conservation of belt length via elastic strain energy
* ✅ **Assumption:** Belt is perfectly elastic with constant $AE$ along its length
* ✅ **Practical guideline:** Initial tension is usually **1.5× to 2× the slack-side tension** in well-designed drives
* ✅ **KTU exam tip:** Always declare whether centrifugal tension is included; examiners allocate separate marks for the $T_c$ term
* ✅ **Sanity check:** $T_1 > T_0 > T_2$ must always hold; if not, your arithmetic is wrong
* ✅ **Units check:** All tensions in N, $v$ in m/s, $m$ in kg/m, $T_c$ in N
* ✅ **Engineering significance:** Insufficient $T_0$ causes slip; excessive $T_0$ reduces bearing life and wastes energy

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Initial Tension on Power Transmission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT527 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effect of Initial Tension on Power Transmission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — EFFECT OF INITIAL TENSION ON POWER TRANSMISSION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Initial Tension (T₀):** It is the static tension given to a belt at the time of installation, before the belt starts transmitting any power. It is the tension required to keep the belt taut on the pulleys and prevent slippage during operation.

When a belt is mounted on pulleys but is not transmitting any power (stationary condition), it is stretched slightly to provide a pre-tension called **Initial Tension (T₀)**. Once the belt starts transmitting motion, this single tension **T₀** splits into two different tensions:

- **T₁** = Tension on the **tight side** (driving side)
- **T₂** = Tension on the **slack side** (driven side)

The fundamental governing relation (neglecting centrifugal tension) is:

$$
T_1 + T_2 = 2T_0
$$

When centrifugal effects are considered, the modified relation becomes:

$$
T_1 + T_2 + 2T_c = 2T_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_1 - T_2 = 2(T_0 - T_c)
$$

where $T_c$ is the centrifugal tension produced due to the rotating belt mass.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Tug-of-War Rope**
>
> Imagine two teams pulling on a single rope over a frictionless pulley. Before the match starts, a referee tightens the rope so neither team has slack — that **pretightening force** is the **Initial Tension (T₀)**.
>
> When Team A starts pulling harder (driver pulley), the rope tension on Team A's side becomes higher ($T_1$) and Team B's side becomes lower ($T_2$). The **total** tension in the rope, however, stays roughly constant — equal to **2T₀** — because the rope's length and stretchiness haven't changed.
>
> This is exactly what happens in a belt drive: the belt is a flexible loop, its elastic length is fixed, so the *sum* of tight and slack side tensions must equal twice the initial tension.

### 1.3 Physical Significance

| Quantity | Symbol | Unit | Physical Meaning |
|----------|--------|------|------------------|
| Tight side tension | T₁ | N (Newtons) | Force on driving side of belt |
| Slack side tension | T₂ | N (Newtons) | Force on driven side of belt |
| Initial tension | T₀ | N (Newtons) | Static pre-tension given before operation |
| Centrifugal tension | T_c | N (Newtons) | Tension due to belt mass moving in a circular path |
| Belt velocity | v | m/s | Linear velocity of belt |
| Belt mass per unit length | m | kg/m | Mass per metre of belt |
| Angle of lap | θ | radians (rad) | Angle of contact between belt and smaller pulley |
| Coefficient of friction | μ | dimensionless | Belt-pulley friction |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

### Step 1: Why does Initial Tension exist?

A belt cannot transmit power if it is completely slack. Before any load is applied, the belt must be stretched enough to **establish frictional grip** on the pulley. This pre-stretch produces $T_0$.

> **Why?** Without $T_0$, the belt would simply slip around the pulley without transferring torque.

### Step 2: How does $T_0$ split into $T_1$ and $T_2$?

When the driver pulley rotates and applies a tangential force to engage the driven pulley, the tension on the **approach side** of the driver increases from $T_0$ to $T_1$, while the tension on the **departure side** decreases from $T_0$ to $T_2$.

Because the **elastic length of the belt remains constant** (Hookean assumption), the average tension over one full loop must remain at $T_0$:

$$
\frac{T_1 + T_2}{2} = T_0
$$

### Step 3: Why include Centrifugal Tension ($T_c$)?

At higher belt speeds, every particle of the belt traces a circular path. Even when the belt is **idling** (not transmitting power), each particle requires a centripetal force directed toward the pulley center. This is supplied by an additional tension $T_c = m v^2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** At high speeds, centrifugal tension can absorb **30–60%** of the initial tension, leaving very little for actual power transmission. This is why there exists an **optimum belt velocity** that maximizes power.

### Step 4: Governing Equation for Power

Power transmitted by a belt is the product of **net driving force** and **belt velocity**:

$$
P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v
$$

Using the relation $T_1 - T_2 = 2(T_0 - T_c)$:

$$
P = 2(T_0 - T_c) \cdot v = 2(T_0 - m v^2) \cdot v
$$

This shows that **as $v$ increases**, $P$ initially increases, reaches a maximum, and then decreases due to the growing $m v^2$ term.

### Step 5: Maximum Power Condition

Differentiating $P$ with respect to $v$ and setting to zero:

$$
\frac{dP}{dv} = 2(T_0 - 3 m v^2) = 0
$$

This yields the **optimum belt velocity**:

$$
v_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{T_0}{3m}}
$$

At this velocity, the **maximum power** becomes:

$$
P_{max} = \frac{4}{3} T_0 \sqrt{\frac{T_0}{3m}} = \frac{4 T_0^{3/2}}{3\sqrt{3m}}
$$

And the **optimum initial tension** for a given velocity is:

$$
T_0 = 3 m v^2
$$

### Step 6: Maximum Power Without Centrifugal Tension

If we ignore centrifugal effects (low-speed approximation):

$$
T_1 - T_2 = 2T_0 \quad \text{(maximum, when } T_2 = 0\text{)}
$$

$$
P_{max} = 2 T_0 \cdot v
$$

The condition $T_2 = 0$ means the slack side is just taut — no slack whatsoever.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the **high-yield formula bank** for this topic. Every KTU board question on this chapter will use one or more of these equations.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Condition |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $T_1 + T_2 = 2 T_0$ | Tension equilibrium (no centrifugal) | N (Newtons) |
| 2 | $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$ | Eytelwein's belt friction equation | dimensionless |
| 3 | $T_1 - T_2 = 2(T_0 - T_c)$ | Effective driving tension | N |
| 4 | $T_c = m v^2$ | Centrifugal tension | N |
| 5 | $P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v$ | Power transmitted | W (Watts) |
| 6 | $P = 2(T_0 - m v^2) \cdot v$ | Power in terms of $T_0$ and $v$ | W |
| 7 | $v_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{T_0}{3m}}$ | Optimum belt velocity | m/s |
| 8 | $T_{0,opt} = 3 m v^2$ | Optimum initial tension for given $v$ | N |
| 9 | $P_{max} = \dfrac{4 T_0^{3/2}}{3\sqrt{3m}}$ | Maximum power with centrifugal effects | W |
| 10 | $P_{max} = 2 T_0 \cdot v$ | Maximum power (centrifugal ignored) | W |

> **Where:** $m$ = mass per unit length of belt (kg/m), $\mu$ = coefficient of friction, $\theta$ = angle of lap (radians), $v$ = belt velocity (m/s).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Significance of This Theory:**
>
> - **Automotive Drive Belts:** The serpentine belt in car engines is pre-tensioned using a **tensioner pulley**. Manufacturers must choose $T_0$ so the operating speed (~20 m/s) is close to $v_{opt}$.
> - **Conveyor Belts:** Mining and bulk-material conveyors operate at low speeds (1–3 m/s), where centrifugal effects are negligible, so they use the simpler $P = 2 T_0 \cdot v$ formula.
> - **Industrial V-Belts and Timing Belts:** Designed with optimal mass-to-strength ratios so $v_{opt}$ matches the application (typically 15–30 m/s).
> - **Elevator and Hoist Cables:** Engineers use a high safety factor on $T_0$ to ensure $T_2 > 0$ at all times, preventing belt flutter and slip.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relation $T_1 + T_2 = 2T_0$ (Without Centrifugal Tension)

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Assumption:** The belt length is constant (Hooke's law with negligible change in elastic stretch during operation).

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Treatment

Let the belt be mounted on two pulleys. Before motion, every element of the belt carries tension $T_0$.

When power transmission begins, the section approaching the driver pulley gets **stretched further** (from $T_0$ to $T_1$), while the section leaving the driver pulley gets **relaxed** (from $T_0$ to $T_2$).

By conservation of elastic length:

$$
\text{Increase in length on tight side} = \text{Decrease in length on slack side}
$$

Using Hooke's Law ($\Delta L = \frac{T \cdot L}{A E}$):

$$
\frac{(T_1 - T_0) L_1}{A E} = \frac{(T_0 - T_2) L_2}{A E}
$$

For a belt of uniform cross-section and material, and considering the belt on either pulley occupies approximately equal arc lengths ($L_1 \approx L_2$), this reduces to:

$$
T_1 - T_0 = T_0 - T_2
$$

$$
\boxed{T_1 + T_2 = 2 T_0}
$$

**Verification (Dimensional Check):** Both $T_1$ and $T_2$ are in Newtons; the sum equals $2 T_0$ in Newtons. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Modified Relation With Centrifugal Tension

When the belt moves at velocity $v$, consider a small element of belt of mass $dm$ on the pulley of radius $R$. The centripetal force required is $\dfrac{(dm) v^2}{R}$.

This is provided by an additional tension $T_c$ acting tangentially on both sides of the element:

$$
2 T_c \sin\!\left(\frac{d\alpha}{2}\right) = \frac{dm \cdot v^2}{R}
$$

For small $d\alpha$, $\sin(d\alpha/2) \approx d\alpha/2$ and $dm = m R \, d\alpha$:

$$
2 T_c \cdot \frac{d\alpha}{2} = m R \, d\alpha \cdot \frac{v^2}{R}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_c = m v^2}
$$

Now apply tension equilibrium to the moving belt:

$$
T_1 + T_2 + 2 T_c = 2 T_0
$$

Because the belt is now stretched uniformly by $T_c$ around the entire loop, the **effective** tensions available for power transmission are $T_1 - T_c$ and $T_2 - T_c$. The new relation becomes:

$$
(T_1 - T_c) + (T_2 - T_c) = 2 T_0 - 2 T_c
$$

Subtracting the two modified tensions:

$$
\boxed{T_1 - T_2 = 2(T_0 - T_c)}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Expression for Power Transmitted

Using the belt friction equation:

$$
T_1 = T_2 \, e^{\mu \theta}
$$

Add $T_2$ to both sides:

$$
T_1 + T_2 = T_2 (e^{\mu \theta} + 1)
$$

Substituting $T_1 + T_2 = 2 T_0$:

$$
2 T_0 = T_2 (e^{\mu \theta} + 1)
$$

$$
T_2 = \frac{2 T_0}{e^{\mu \theta} + 1}
$$

$$
T_1 = \frac{2 T_0 \, e^{\mu \theta}}{e^{\mu \theta} + 1}
$$

The net tangential driving force is:

$$
T_1 - T_2 = \frac{2 T_0 (e^{\mu \theta} - 1)}{e^{\mu \theta} + 1}
$$

Power transmitted at belt velocity $v$:

$$
\boxed{P = \frac{2 T_0 (e^{\mu \theta} - 1)}{e^{\mu \theta} + 1} \cdot v}
$$

When centrifugal effects are included, replace $T_0$ with $(T_0 - m v^2)$:

$$
\boxed{P = \frac{2 (T_0 - m v^2)(e^{\mu \theta} - 1)}{e^{\mu \theta} + 1} \cdot v}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Condition for Maximum Power

Treating $\mu$ and $\theta$ as constants (fixed design parameters), power $P$ is a function of $T_0$ and $v$:

$$
P = 2(T_0 - m v^2) \cdot v
$$

**Differentiation** with respect to $v$ (taking $T_0$ as constant):

$$
\frac{dP}{dv} = 2 \left[ (T_0 - m v^2)(1) + v \cdot (-2 m v) \right]
$$

$$
\frac{dP}{dv} = 2 (T_0 - m v^2 - 2 m v^2) = 2(T_0 - 3 m v^2)
$$

Set $\dfrac{dP}{dv} = 0$:

$$
T_0 - 3 m v^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{T_0}{3m}}
$$

**Second Derivative Check:**

$$
\frac{d^2 P}{dv^2} = -12 m v < 0 \quad \text{(at } v = v_{opt}\text{)}
$$

This confirms that $P$ is at a **maximum** (not a minimum).

**Maximum Power Value:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{max} &= 2 \left(T_0 - m \cdot \frac{T_0}{3m}\right) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{T_0}{3m}} \\
&= 2 \left(\frac{2 T_0}{3}\right) \sqrt{\frac{T_0}{3m}} \\
&= \frac{4 T_0}{3} \sqrt{\frac{T_0}{3m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{max} = \frac{4 T_0^{3/2}}{3 \sqrt{3m}}}
$$

**Optimum Initial Tension for a Given Velocity:**

If belt velocity $v$ is fixed, then $P$ depends only on $T_0$:

$$
\frac{dP}{dT_0} = 2 v > 0 \quad \text{(always positive)}
$$

This means **higher $T_0$ always increases power** for a given $v$. However, the belt material has a maximum allowable tension (limited by strength). Hence the practical optimum is:

$$
\boxed{T_{0,\text{practical}} = T_{\text{max allowable}}/2 \quad \text{(or from } T_2 > 0 \text{ safety condition)}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example (Worked Out)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A flat belt drive has the following specifications:
> - Initial tension: $T_0 = 3000$ N
> - Mass of belt per metre: $m = 0.5$ kg/m
> - Belt velocity: $v = 15$ m/s
> - Angle of lap on smaller pulley: $\theta = 180° = \pi$ rad
> - Coefficient of friction: $\mu = 0.25$

**Step 1: Calculate Centrifugal Tension**

$$
T_c = m v^2 = 0.5 \times (15)^2 = 0.5 \times 225 = 112.5 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Effective Tension Difference**

$$
T_1 - T_2 = 2(T_0 - T_c) = 2(3000 - 112.5) = 2 \times 2887.5 = 5775 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Eytelwein's Equation**

$$
\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta} = e^{0.25 \times \pi} = e^{0.7854} \approx 2.1933
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $T_1$ and $T_2$**

$$
T_1 = 2.1933 \, T_2
$$

$$
T_1 - T_2 = (2.1933 - 1) T_2 = 1.1933 \, T_2 = 5775 \text{ N}
$$

$$
T_2 = \frac{5775}{1.1933} \approx 4839.5 \text{ N}
$$

$$
T_1 = 2.1933 \times 4839.5 \approx 10614.0 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate Power Transmitted**

$$
P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v = 5775 \times 15 = 86625 \text{ W} \approx 86.625 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6: Check for Optimum Velocity**

$$
v_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{T_0}{3m}} = \sqrt{\frac{3000}{3 \times 0.5}} = \sqrt{2000} \approx 44.72 \text{ m/s}
$$

Since actual velocity (15 m/s) is below optimum, the system is operating on the **safe, rising-power region** of the P-v curve.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow Diagram — Power Transmission in a Belt Drive

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Pulley Rotating] --> B[Belt Engagement Begins]
    B --> C[Initial Tension T0 Splits]
    C --> D[Tight Side Tension T1]
    C --> E[Slack Side Tension T2]
    D --> F[Frictional Grip on Pulley]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Is Velocity High?}
    G -- No --> H[Neglect Centrifugal Tension]
    G -- Yes --> I[Include Centrifugal Tension Tc equals m v squared]
    H --> J[Power P equals T1 minus T2 times v]
    I --> K[Effective Tension equals T1 minus T2 equals 2 of T0 minus Tc]
    K --> J
    J --> L{V equals Vopt?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Pmax equals 4 T0 to the power 3 over 2 divided by 3 root 3m]
    L -- No --> N[Suboptimal Power Transmission]
    M --> O[Maximum Efficiency Zone]
    N --> P[Operating Point]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Effect of Initial Tension on Power

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Initial Tension T0] --> B[Static Belt Pre-load]
    B --> C[Power Transmission P]
    B --> D[T1 tight side Tension]
    B --> E[T2 slack side Tension]
    C --> F{Velocity Check}
    F --> G[Low Speed: P equals 2 T0 times v]
    F --> H[High Speed: P equals 2 of T0 minus mv squared times v]
    H --> I[dP by dv equals 0]
    I --> J[Vopt equals root T0 over 3m]
    J --> K[Pmax Point]
    G --> L[Linear Increase Region]
    H --> M[Parabolic Peak Region]
    K --> N[Decline due to Centrifugal Loss]
```

## 4.3 Tension Distribution Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Belt at Rest] --> Init[Initial Tension T0 throughout]
    Init --> Motion[Belt in Motion]
    Motion --> Driver[Approaching Driver Pulley]
    Motion --> Driven[Leaving Driver Pulley]
    Driver --> T1B[Tension Rises to T1]
    Driven --> T2B[Tension Falls to T2]
    T1B --> SumCheck[T1 plus T2 equals 2 T0]
    T2B --> SumCheck
    SumCheck --> SpeedCheck{Belt Speed}
    SpeedCheck --> Low[Low Speed: Tc negligible]
    SpeedCheck --> High[High Speed: Tc equals m v squared]
    High --> Modified[T1 minus T2 equals 2 of T0 minus Tc]
    Low --> Simple[T1 minus T2 equals 2 T0]
    Modified --> Output[Reduced Power Capacity]
    Simple --> Output2[Maximum Theoretical Power]
```

## 4.4 Power vs. Velocity Curve (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Velocity equals 0] --> B[P equals 0]
    B --> C[Linear Rise Region]
    C --> D{Optimum Velocity Vopt}
    D --> E[Peak Power Pmax]
    E --> F[Decline due to Centrifugal Effects]
    F --> G[P approaches 0 as v increases]
```

> **Reading the Curve:** The bell-shaped $P$–$v$ graph reveals the engineering trade-off — designers must operate belts **near $v_{opt}$** to balance power capacity against centrifugal losses.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`
**Q: Define Initial Tension in a belt drive. State the fundamental relation connecting tight side, slack side, and initial tensions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Initial Tension ($T_0$) is the static pre-tension applied to a belt at the time of installation, before the belt begins to transmit any power. It ensures the belt is taut around the pulleys and is essential for frictional grip.

> **Fundamental Relation (2 Marks):** During power transmission, the belt has two tensions — $T_1$ (tight side) and $T_2$ (slack side). Because the elastic length of the belt remains constant:
>
> $$T_1 + T_2 = 2 T_0$$

This relation holds for low-speed belts where centrifugal effects are negligible.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`
**Q: What is the optimum velocity of a belt for maximum power transmission? How is it derived?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Optimum Velocity (2 Marks):** The belt velocity at which maximum power is transmitted (considering centrifugal tension) is:
>
> $$v_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{T_0}{3m}}$$
>
> where $T_0$ is the initial tension and $m$ is the mass per unit length of the belt.

> **Derivation Outline (1 Mark):** Starting from $P = 2(T_0 - m v^2) v$, differentiating and equating to zero:
>
> $$\frac{dP}{dv} = 2(T_0 - 3 m v^2) = 0 \Rightarrow v_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{T_0}{3m}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Type]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the power transmitted by a belt drive in terms of initial tension $T_0$, coefficient of friction $\mu$, angle of lap $\theta$, and belt velocity $v$. Include centrifugal tension in your derivation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A flat belt drive has the following specifications:
- Initial tension $T_0 = 2500$ N
- Mass per metre of belt $m = 0.4$ kg/m
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.3$
- Smaller pulley diameter $d = 600$ mm
- Belt velocity $v = 20$ m/s
- Speed ratio = 2

Calculate: (i) Centrifugal tension, (ii) Tensions on tight and slack sides, (iii) Power transmitted.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Derivation [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Tension Relation with Centrifugal Effect (2 Marks)**

When the belt moves at velocity $v$, each element of belt mass $dm$ requires centripetal force. This produces a centrifugal tension $T_c = m v^2$ uniformly around the loop.

$$
T_1 + T_2 + 2 T_c = 2 T_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_1 - T_2 = 2(T_0 - m v^2)
$$

**Step 2: Apply Eytelwein's Friction Equation (2 Marks)**

$$
T_1 = T_2 \, e^{\mu \theta}
$$

Solving simultaneously:

$$
T_2 = \frac{2(T_0 - m v^2)}{e^{\mu \theta} + 1}, \qquad T_1 = \frac{2(T_0 - m v^2) e^{\mu \theta}}{e^{\mu \theta} + 1}
$$

**Step 3: Power Equation (2 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v \\
&= \frac{2(T_0 - m v^2)(e^{\mu \theta} - 1)}{e^{\mu \theta} + 1} \cdot v
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Final Boxed Expression (1 Mark)**

$$
\boxed{P = \frac{2(T_0 - m v^2)(e^{\mu \theta} - 1)}{e^{\mu \theta} + 1} \cdot v}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

**Given Data:**
- $T_0 = 2500$ N, $m = 0.4$ kg/m, $\mu = 0.3$
- Smaller pulley diameter $d = 600$ mm $= 0.6$ m
- $v = 20$ m/s, speed ratio = 2

**Step 1: Calculate Angle of Lap on Smaller Pulley (2 Marks)**

For an open belt drive with speed ratio 2:
- Larger pulley diameter $D = 2 \times d = 1200$ mm $= 1.2$ m
- Angle of lap on smaller pulley $\alpha = \pi - \dfrac{(D-d)}{C} \times 1$ rad, where $C$ is centre distance.

For standard design, assume $\alpha \approx 180° = \pi$ rad (typical for speed ratio 2):
$$
\theta = \pi \text{ rad} = 3.1416 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2: Centrifugal Tension (1 Mark)**

$$
T_c = m v^2 = 0.4 \times (20)^2 = 0.4 \times 400 = 160 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3: Effective Tension Difference (1 Mark)**

$$
T_1 - T_2 = 2(T_0 - T_c) = 2(2500 - 160) = 2 \times 2340 = 4680 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4: Compute $e^{\mu \theta}$ (1 Mark)**

$$
\mu \theta = 0.3 \times \pi = 0.9425
$$
$$
e^{0.9425} = 2.5667
$$

**Step 5: Calculate $T_1$ and $T_2$ (1 Mark)**

$$
T_2 = \frac{4680}{2.5667 + 1} = \frac{4680}{3.5667} \approx 1312.0 \text{ N}
$$

$$
T_1 = 2.5667 \times 1312.0 \approx 3367.5 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 6: Power Transmitted (1 Mark)**

$$
P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v = 4680 \times 20 = 93600 \text{ W} = 93.6 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_c = 160 \text{ N}, \quad T_1 \approx 3367.5 \text{ N}, \quad T_2 \approx 1312 \text{ N}, \quad P = 93.6 \text{ kW}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Type]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the effect of initial tension on power transmission in a belt drive. Discuss the variation of power with belt velocity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Two pulleys of diameters 500 mm and 250 mm are connected by a flat belt. The mass of the belt is 0.6 kg/m, the initial tension is 1800 N, and the coefficient of friction is 0.28. The belt speed is 18 m/s. Find: (i) Optimum velocity, (ii) Maximum power that can be transmitted at this optimum velocity, (iii) Actual power transmitted at the given speed.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Conceptual Explanation [7 Marks]**

**Sketch Description (2 Marks):**

A schematic belt drive showing driver pulley, driven pulley, with markings for:
- $T_1$ on tight side (arrowed away from driver)
- $T_2$ on slack side (arrowed toward driver)
- $T_0$ as the pre-tension before motion
- Belt velocity $v$ indicated by tangential arrow

**Conceptual Points (5 Marks):**

1. **Initial tension ($T_0$) acts as the reservoir of frictional capacity.** It splits into $T_1$ and $T_2$ when motion begins: $T_1 + T_2 = 2T_0$.

2. **At low speeds** (centrifugal effects negligible), $T_1 - T_2 = 2T_0$ (max). Power $P = 2T_0 \cdot v$ grows linearly with $v$.

3. **At higher speeds**, centrifugal tension $T_c = m v^2$ rises as $v^2$, **robbing** tension from useful transmission: $T_1 - T_2 = 2(T_0 - m v^2)$.

4. **Power Curve is parabolic:** $P = 2(T_0 - m v^2) \cdot v$. It rises, peaks at $v_{opt} = \sqrt{T_0/(3m)}$, then falls.

5. **Engineering Implication:** Designers must balance — too low $T_0$ gives low max power; too high $T_0$ accelerates belt wear. The optimum $T_0 = 3 m v^2$ for a fixed application speed.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 500$ mm, $D_2 = 250$ mm
- $m = 0.6$ kg/m, $T_0 = 1800$ N
- $\mu = 0.28$, $v = 18$ m/s

**Step 1: Angle of Lap on Smaller Pulley (1 Mark)**

For an open belt drive, angle of lap on smaller pulley:
$$
\theta = \pi + \frac{(D_1 - D_2)}{C} \quad \text{(assuming equal arc distribution)}
$$

For typical centre distance assumption, take $\theta \approx \pi + \frac{0.25}{0.75} \approx 3.475$ rad ≈ 199°

Or simpler assumption: $\theta \approx \pi$ rad for this problem.

**Step 2: Optimum Velocity (1 Mark)**

$$
v_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{T_0}{3m}} = \sqrt{\frac{1800}{3 \times 0.6}} = \sqrt{1000} \approx 31.62 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3: Maximum Power at Optimum Velocity (2 Marks)**

$$
P_{max} = \frac{4 T_0^{3/2}}{3 \sqrt{3m}} = \frac{4 \times (1800)^{1.5}}{3 \times \sqrt{3 \times 0.6}}
$$

$$
(1800)^{1.5} = 1800 \times \sqrt{1800} = 1800 \times 42.426 = 76367.4
$$

$$
\sqrt{3 \times 0.6} = \sqrt{1.8} = 1.3416
$$

$$
P_{max} = \frac{4 \times 76367.4}{3 \times 1.3416} = \frac{305469.6}{4.0248} \approx 75900 \text{ W} = 75.9 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4: Actual Power at $v = 18$ m/s (3 Marks)**

Centrifugal tension:
$$
T_c = m v^2 = 0.6 \times 18^2 = 0.6 \times 324 = 194.4 \text{ N}
$$

Effective tension difference:
$$
T_1 - T_2 = 2(T_0 - T_c) = 2(1800 - 194.4) = 2 \times 1605.6 = 3211.2 \text{ N}
$$

Using $\mu \theta = 0.28 \times \pi = 0.8796$, $e^{0.8796} = 2.410$:

$$
T_2 = \frac{3211.2}{2.410 + 1} = \frac{3211.2}{3.410} \approx 941.7 \text{ N}
$$

$$
T_1 = 2.410 \times 941.7 \approx 2269.5 \text{ N}
$$

Actual power:
$$
P_{actual} = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v = 3211.2 \times 18 = 57801.6 \text{ W} \approx 57.8 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answers:**

$$
\boxed{v_{opt} \approx 31.62 \text{ m/s}, \quad P_{max} \approx 75.9 \text{ kW}, \quad P_{actual} \approx 57.8 \text{ kW}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $\theta$ to radians:** Students often use $180°$ directly in $e^{\mu \theta}$, yielding a wrong exponent. Always convert: $180° = \pi$ rad.
>
> 2. **Mixing up $T_1$ and $T_2$ in the friction equation:** Always write $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta}$, where $T_1$ is on the **tight side** (the side approaching the driver). Reversing this flips the formula.
>
> 3. **Omitting centrifugal tension in numericals:** At $v > 10$ m/s, examiners expect $T_c = m v^2$ to be included. Skipping it costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Incorrectly assuming $T_2 = 0$ for "maximum" power:** When centrifugal effects are included, $T_2 = 0$ is NOT the condition; instead, $v_{opt} = \sqrt{T_0/(3m)}$ is the optimum condition.
>
> 5. **Unit errors:** Always express final power in **Watts (W) or kilowatts (kW)** — never in N·m/s without conversion. Belt mass must be in **kg/m**, not g/m.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to write the final boxed answer:** KTU examiners explicitly state "boxed answers get bonus clarity marks." Always box the final numerical answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Print This Section Before the Exam**

### ✅ Core Concepts (Must-Know)

- **Initial tension** $T_0$ is the **static pre-tension** applied before motion.
- During transmission, $T_0$ splits into $T_1$ (tight) and $T_2$ (slack).
- Fundamental relation: **$T_1 + T_2 = 2T_0$** (without centrifugal).
- With centrifugal: **$T_1 - T_2 = 2(T_0 - m v^2)$**.

### ✅ Critical Equations (Memorize)

- $T_c = m v^2$ (centrifugal tension)
- $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$ (belt friction equation — Eytelwein)
- $P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v$ (power transmitted)
- $v_{opt} = \sqrt{T_0/(3m)}$ (optimum velocity for max power)
- $P_{max} = \dfrac{4 T_0^{3/2}}{3\sqrt{3m}}$ (maximum power with centrifugal effects)

### ✅ Engineering Insights

- **Power vs. velocity is a bell-shaped curve**, not linear.
- **Operating below $v_{opt}$ is safe but underutilized.**
- **Operating above $v_{opt}$ wastes energy** to centrifugal losses.
- **Higher $T_0$ always increases power** but reduces belt life.

### ✅ Pitfalls to Avoid

- ❌ Using degrees in $e^{\mu \theta}$.
- ❌ Forgetting $T_c$ at high speeds.
- ❌ Confusing $T_1$ and $T_2$.
- ❌ Mixing units (kg vs. grams, m/s vs. km/hr).

### ✅ Common Numerical Pattern in KTU

Given $T_0$, $m$, $\mu$, $\theta$, $v$ → Find $T_1$, $T_2$, $P$.
Step 1: $T_c = m v^2$
Step 2: $T_1 - T_2 = 2(T_0 - T_c)$
Step 3: $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta}$
Step 4: Solve two equations simultaneously.
Step 5: $P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v$

### ✅ Maximum Power Condition Summary

| Scenario | Condition for Max Power | $P_{max}$ Expression |
|----------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Neglecting $T_c$ | $T_2 = 0$ | $P_{max} = 2 T_0 v$ |
| Including $T_c$ | $v = v_{opt} = \sqrt{T_0/(3m)}$ | $P_{max} = \dfrac{4 T_0^{3/2}}{3\sqrt{3m}}$ |

---

**END OF NOTES — MODULE 3: EFFECT OF INITIAL TENSION ON POWER TRANSMISSION**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Belt Creep
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT527 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Belt Creep

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Belt Creep** is the phenomenon of *relative sliding* between the belt and the pulley caused solely by the **elastic stretching and contraction** of the belt material as it moves from the slack side to the tight side and back. It is *not* the same as mechanical slip, which arises from insufficient frictional grip.

In formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, belt creep refers to the small but measurable difference in the linear velocities of the belt on the tight side and the slack side, produced by the *tangential elastic deformation* of the belt as it traverses the driving and driven pulleys. The tight side (under tension $T_1$) is stretched more than the slack side (under tension $T_2$), so the same surface arc of the pulley carries slightly more belt material on one side than the other.

**Key Operational Metric:** The magnitude of creep is proportional to $(T_1 - T_2)$ and inversely proportional to the **axial stiffness** ($AE$, where $A$ is the cross-sectional area and $E$ is the Young's Modulus of the belt material).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a long, soft rubber band stretched between two posts. Now pull the rubber band in such a way that the tension on the left half is **T₁** (high) and on the right half is **T₂** (low). The left half is visibly *thinner and longer*, while the right half is *thicker and shorter*. If a finger now runs along the rubber band from left to right at a constant linear speed, the finger "feels" a slight lag as the band transitions from the stretched state to the relaxed state. The band is *consuming* some of the finger's motion just to relax. This "lost motion" is precisely **Belt Creep**.

In a real belt drive:
- The **driving pulley** feeds belt material onto the tight side. The belt stretches → it must take *more time* to lay out the same linear length.
- The **driven pulley** receives this stretched belt. As it leaves, the belt relaxes → its linear length (in unstretched terms) increases.

The net effect: the driven pulley rotates **slower** than the ideal kinematic prediction $\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{d_1}{d_2}$.

---

### 1.3 Slip vs. Creep — The Crucial Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **Slip** is a *frictional failure* (insufficient grip). It is typically denoted in **%** and depends on the coefficient of friction $\mu$ and the angle of contact $\theta$.
>
> **Creep** is an *elastic deformation effect* (inherent to flexible belts). It is typically small (often **< 1%** for rubber/polyurethane belts) and depends on the belt's modulus $E$ and tension ratio $T_1/T_2$.

| Phenomenon | Root Cause | Magnitude | Direction |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Slip** | Insufficient frictional grip | $\sim$ 1–5 % (or more) | Belt lags the pulley |
| **Creep** | Elastic stretching / contraction | $\sim$ 0.1–1 % | Belt stretches on tight side |

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Units

- **Young's Modulus ($E$):** For leather belts $E \approx 25$–$50 \text{ MPa}$; for rubber belts $E \approx 5$–$25 \text{ MPa}$; for synthetic polyurethanes $E \approx 30$–$100 \text{ MPa}$.
- **Tensions $T_1$, $T_2$:** Expressed in **Newtons (N)**.
- **Linear Velocity $v = \frac{\pi d N}{60}$:** Expressed in **m/s** (S.I. units).
- **Belt Creep:** Has units of **m/s** (a linear velocity difference) — the *same* units as the belt's linear velocity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physical Mechanism (Structured Logic)

The creeping phenomenon is governed by a sequence of *deterministic* steps. Let us break down the operational logic point-by-point:

1. **Tension Step:** The belt enters the driving pulley at the slack-side tension $T_2$ and exits at the tight-side tension $T_1$. Since $T_1 > T_2$, the belt *must* stretch as it wraps around the driving pulley.
2. **Geometric Constraint (No Mechanical Slip):** At the contact arc, the belt surface moves with the *exact* linear velocity of the pulley surface: $v = \frac{\pi d N}{60}$. There is no relative motion at the *contact patch* in the absence of slip.
3. **Elastic Stretching:** A length element $L$ (unstretched) on the tight side has stretched to $L + dL_1$, where $dL_1 = \frac{T_1 L}{A E}$.
4. **Velocity Mismatch:** Because the same *surface* length of the pulley ($v \cdot dt$) corresponds to a *different unstretched length* on the two sides, the **rate of belt material** (in unstretched terms) passing per unit time differs between the two sides.
5. **Mass Flow Invariance:** The belt is a *closed loop* — the same belt material is everywhere. The mass flow rate (in original-length terms) is identical on both sides, *forcing* the linear velocity of the belt material to differ between the tight and slack sides.
6. **Numerical Output:** The difference between these two linear velocities is mathematically called the **belt creep** — a kinematic consequence of elasticity, not friction.

---

### 2.2 Why Does Creep Matter in Engineering?

- **Velocity Ratio Error:** In precision drives (e.g., timing belts for textile machinery, automotive camshaft drives, robotics), even a $0.5\%$ creep can cause *phase errors* and *timing mismatches*.
- **Power Loss (Minor):** A creeping belt does *not* dissipate significant power — it merely *delays* the transmission of motion. The energy is stored as elastic strain and returned.
- **Design Implication:** To minimise creep, designers use belts with **high Young's modulus** (steel-cord, kevlar-reinforced, or polyurethane timing belts) and operate them at **low tension ratios** ($T_1/T_2$ as close to 1 as possible).

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below summarises **all** essential equations required for solving KTU 2024 scheme questions on this topic. All symbols use S.I. units.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Linear Belt Velocity (no creep) | $v = \dfrac{\pi d N}{60}$ | $d$ in m, $N$ in rpm, $v$ in m/s |
| 2 | Elongation on Tight Side | $dL_1 = \dfrac{T_1 \, L}{A \, E}$ | $T_1$ in N, $L$ in m, $A$ in m², $E$ in N/m², $dL_1$ in m |
| 3 | Elongation on Slack Side | $dL_2 = \dfrac{T_2 \, L}{A \, E}$ | Same as above with $T_2$ |
| 4 | **Belt Creep (magnitude)** | $\Delta v = \dfrac{v \, (T_1 - T_2)}{A \, E} = \dfrac{\pi d N (T_1 - T_2)}{60 \, A \, E}$ | $\Delta v$ in m/s |
| 5 | **Velocity Ratio (with creep, no slip)** | $\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{d_1}{d_2} \cdot \dfrac{(L + dL_2)}{(L + dL_1)} = \dfrac{d_1}{d_2} \cdot \dfrac{1 + (T_2/AE)}{1 + (T_1/AE)}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Velocity Ratio (linearised, $T/AE \ll 1$) | $\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \approx \dfrac{d_1}{d_2} \left[ 1 - \dfrac{(T_1 - T_2)}{A E} \right]$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | % Reduction in Velocity Ratio | $\% \text{ creep} \approx \dfrac{(T_1 - T_2)}{A E} \times 100$ | % |
| 8 | Centrifugal Tension (contextual) | $T_C = m v^2$ | $m$ in kg/m, $v$ in m/s, $T_C$ in N |

> [!NOTE]
> The **most-tested formula** in KTU exams is **#4** (Belt Creep) and **#5** (Velocity Ratio with Creep). Memorise these two.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Industry / Domain | Relevance of Belt Creep |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Automotive Camshaft Drives** | Even sub-percent creep alters valve timing; mitigated by *toothed (synchronous) belts* which have *negligible creep* |
| **Industrial Conveyor Systems** | Creep determines the steady-state speed error between motor and conveyor belt |
| **Tape Recorders / Data Storage** | Historically, magnetic tape drives suffered from *flutter* caused by creep-induced velocity fluctuations |
| **Textile Machinery** | Strict synchronisation of spindles is needed; creep is a major design constraint |
| **Robotics & CNC** | Timing belts in linear actuators must have minimal creep for positional accuracy |
| **Bicycle Drive Systems** | Modern multi-speed bikes use chain drives (not belts) precisely *because* rubber belts creep |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Belt Creep Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important derivation** in the KTU 2024 Module 3 syllabus for Belt Creep. Every step is written out explicitly — no logical jumps.

**Given Data and Setup:**

Consider a flat belt drive transmitting power from a driving pulley of diameter $d$ rotating at $N$ rpm to a driven pulley. Let:

- $T_1$ = Tension in the belt on the **tight side** (N)
- $T_2$ = Tension in the belt on the **slack side** (N), with $T_1 > T_2$
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of the belt (m²)
- $E$ = Young's modulus of elasticity of the belt material (N/m²)
- $L$ = Length of belt between the centres of the two pulleys, on either the tight or slack side (m)

**Step 1: Compute the Elongation on the Tight Side**

By the definition of Young's modulus, the tensile stress is $\sigma_1 = T_1 / A$, and the strain is $\epsilon_1 = dL_1 / L$. By Hooke's Law:

$$
\sigma_1 = E \, \epsilon_1
$$

$$
\frac{T_1}{A} = E \, \frac{dL_1}{L}
$$

Solving for $dL_1$:

$$
dL_1 = \frac{T_1 \, L}{A \, E}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Elongation on the Slack Side**

By identical reasoning, with tension $T_2$:

$$
dL_2 = \frac{T_2 \, L}{A \, E}
$$

**Step 3: Establish the Geometric Constraint (No Mechanical Slip)**

At the contact patch between belt and pulley, the belt surface moves at the *exact* linear velocity of the pulley surface (no slip). Therefore:

$$
v = \frac{\pi d N}{60}
$$

**Step 4: Map Surface Length to Unstretched Belt Material**

A small surface length $d\ell$ of belt on the pulley corresponds to an *unstretched* length of belt material equal to:

- On the **tight side** (stretched by $dL_1$): $d\ell_1^{\text{orig}} = d\ell \cdot \dfrac{L}{L + dL_1}$
- On the **slack side** (stretched by $dL_2$): $d\ell_2^{\text{orig}} = d\ell \cdot \dfrac{L}{L + dL_2}$

**Logical Reasoning:** Because the belt is stretched on the tight side, a unit of *surface length* on the pulley contains *less* unstretched belt material. The factor $L/(L + dL_1)$ correctly accounts for this geometric stretching.

**Step 5: Equate Mass Flow Rates (Conservation Principle)**

Since the belt is a closed loop, the rate at which *unstretched* belt material passes any cross-section must be the same on both sides. Over a small time $dt$, the belt surface moves $v \cdot dt$ at the pulley.

The unstretched material passing per unit time:

- Tight side rate $= v \cdot \dfrac{L}{L + dL_1}$
- Slack side rate $= v \cdot \dfrac{L}{L + dL_2}$

The *difference* between these two rates is the **belt creep** (a relative sliding velocity):

$$
\text{Creep} = v \cdot \frac{L}{L + dL_1} - v \cdot \frac{L}{L + dL_2}
$$

Wait — let us re-examine the sign carefully. Since $dL_1 > dL_2$, the tight-side rate $v \cdot \frac{L}{L + dL_1}$ is *less* than the slack-side rate. The creep magnitude is therefore:

$$
\Delta v = v \cdot L \left( \frac{1}{L + dL_2} - \frac{1}{L + dL_1} \right)
$$

$$
\Delta v = v \cdot L \cdot \frac{(L + dL_1) - (L + dL_2)}{(L + dL_1)(L + dL_2)}
$$

$$
\Delta v = v \cdot L \cdot \frac{dL_1 - dL_2}{(L + dL_1)(L + dL_2)}
$$

**Step 6: Apply the Engineering Approximation**

For typical belt materials and operating tensions, the elongations are *very small* compared to $L$ (i.e., $dL/L \ll 1$). Therefore:

$$
(L + dL_1)(L + dL_2) \approx L^2
$$

Substituting this approximation:

$$
\Delta v \approx v \cdot L \cdot \frac{dL_1 - dL_2}{L^2} = v \cdot \frac{dL_1 - dL_2}{L}
$$

**Step 7: Substitute the Elongation Expressions**

From Steps 1 and 2, $dL_1 - dL_2 = \frac{(T_1 - T_2) L}{A E}$. Substituting:

$$
\Delta v = v \cdot \frac{1}{L} \cdot \frac{(T_1 - T_2) \, L}{A \, E}
$$

$$
\Delta v = \frac{v \, (T_1 - T_2)}{A \, E}
$$

**Step 8: Final Form of the Belt Creep Equation**

Substituting $v = \frac{\pi d N}{60}$:

$$
\boxed{\Delta v = \frac{\pi \, d \, N \, (T_1 - T_2)}{60 \, A \, E}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Check:** $[dL_1 - dL_2]/L$ is dimensionless (strain difference); $v$ has units m/s; therefore $\Delta v$ has units m/s. ✓

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Velocity Ratio with Creep

**Step 1: Start from the no-slip kinematic constraint**

The unstretched material flow rate must be identical on both sides:

$$
v_{\text{tight}} \cdot \frac{L}{L + dL_1} = v_{\text{slack}} \cdot \frac{L}{L + dL_2}
$$

The belt surface velocity on the driving pulley equals the surface velocity of the driving pulley:

$$
v_{\text{tight}} = \frac{\pi d_1 N_1}{60}
$$

The belt surface velocity on the driven pulley equals the surface velocity of the driven pulley:

$$
v_{\text{slack}} = \frac{\pi d_2 N_2}{60}
$$

**Step 2: Equate and Solve for the Velocity Ratio**

$$
\frac{\pi d_1 N_1}{60} \cdot \frac{L}{L + dL_1} = \frac{\pi d_2 N_2}{60} \cdot \frac{L}{L + dL_2}
$$

Cancelling $\pi L / 60$ from both sides:

$$
\frac{d_1 N_1}{L + dL_1} = \frac{d_2 N_2}{L + dL_2}
$$

**Step 3: Isolate $N_2 / N_1$**

$$
\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{d_1}{d_2} \cdot \frac{L + dL_2}{L + dL_1}
$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $L$:

$$
\boxed{\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{d_1}{d_2} \cdot \frac{1 + (dL_2 / L)}{1 + (dL_1 / L)}}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute Hooke's Law Expressions**

Since $dL_1 / L = T_1 / (AE)$ and $dL_2 / L = T_2 / (AE)$:

$$
\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{d_1}{d_2} \cdot \frac{1 + (T_2 / AE)}{1 + (T_1 / AE)}
$$

**Step 5: Linearise for Small Strains**

For $T_1 / AE \ll 1$ and $T_2 / AE \ll 1$, we use the identity $\frac{1+x}{1+y} \approx 1 + x - y$:

$$
\frac{N_2}{N_1} \approx \frac{d_1}{d_2} \cdot \left[ 1 + \frac{T_2}{AE} - \frac{T_1}{AE} \right] = \frac{d_1}{d_2} \cdot \left[ 1 - \frac{T_1 - T_2}{AE} \right]
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** Since $T_1 > T_2$, the bracketed term is *less than unity*, meaning the driven pulley rotates *slower* than the ideal kinematic prediction. **Creep causes a loss in velocity ratio.**

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem [Mock]:** A flat belt drive has a driving pulley of diameter $d_1 = 300 \text{ mm}$ rotating at $N_1 = 1440 \text{ rpm}$. The belt has cross-section $A = 600 \text{ mm}^2$ and Young's modulus $E = 25 \text{ MPa}$. The tight-side tension is $T_1 = 1800 \text{ N}$ and the slack-side tension is $T_2 = 600 \text{ N}$. Assuming no mechanical slip, determine:
**(a)** The linear velocity of the belt surface.
**(b)** The magnitude of belt creep.
**(c)** The reduction in velocity ratio (in %) due to creep.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Linear velocity:

$$
v = \frac{\pi d_1 N_1}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.300 \times 1440}{60}
$$

$$
v = \frac{\pi \times 432}{60} = 22.62 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(b)** Belt creep:

$$
\Delta v = \frac{v (T_1 - T_2)}{A E} = \frac{22.62 \times (1800 - 600)}{(600 \times 10^{-6}) \times (25 \times 10^{6})}
$$

Compute denominator: $(600 \times 10^{-6}) \times (25 \times 10^{6}) = 600 \times 25 = 15000 \text{ N}$

Compute numerator: $22.62 \times 1200 = 27144$

$$
\Delta v = \frac{27144}{15000} = 1.81 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(c)** % reduction in velocity ratio:

$$
\% \text{ reduction} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{AE} \times 100 = \frac{1200}{15000} \times 100 = 8.00 \%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A *8%* reduction in velocity ratio is **substantial** and indicates that the belt is operating at a *very high tension ratio* (here $T_1/T_2 = 3$). In practice, this belt would be replaced or re-tensioned. Rubber belts typically have $T_1/T_2 \le 2$ to keep creep below 1%.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Mechanism of Belt Creep

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *functional flow* of the belt-creep phenomenon, including the cause-effect chain and the velocity relationships.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1[Pulley Rotates at N RPM] --> p2[Surface Velocity v equals pi d N over 60]
    p2 --> t1[Belt on Tight Side Experiences Tension T1]
    p2 --> t2[Belt on Slack Side Experiences Tension T2]
    t1 --> s1[Strain on Tight Side equals T1 over AE]
    t2 --> s2[Strain on Slack Side equals T2 over AE]
    s1 --> c1[Elongation dL1 equals T1 L over AE]
    s2 --> c2[Elongation dL2 equals T2 L over AE]
    c1 --> vp1[Linear Velocity of Belt Material v tight is REDUCED]
    c2 --> vp2[Linear Velocity of Belt Material v slack is INCREASED]
    vp1 --> ce[Belt Creep delta v equals v times T1 minus T2 over AE]
    vp2 --> ce
    ce --> vr1[Velocity Ratio N2 over N1 is REDUCED]
    vr1 --> vr2[Driven Pulley Rotates SLOWER than Ideal]
    vr2 --> eng1[Engineering Mitigation: Use High E Belts and Low Tension Ratio]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram traces the *causal chain*: pulley rotation → surface velocity → two distinct tensions → two distinct strains → two distinct elongations → velocity mismatch → creep → reduced velocity ratio → driven pulley lag. The mitigation node (eng1) provides the design takeaway.

---

### 4.2 Conceptual Schematic — Side View of Belt with Creep Annotation

The following ASCII schematic complements the Mermaid diagram by showing the *spatial distribution* of elongation along the belt loop.

```
        DRIVING PULLEY                              DRIVEN PULLEY
        (Diameter d1, N1)                           (Diameter d2, N2)
        ┌──────────────┐                            ┌──────────────┐
        │     ●●●●     │                            │     ●●●●     │
        │   ●●●●●●●    │ ─── TIGHT SIDE (T1) ───►  │   ●●●●●●●    │
        │     ●●●●     │   Belt is STRETCHED        │     ●●●●     │
        └──────────────┘   by dL1 = T1L/(AE)        └──────────────┘
                │                                            │
                │   Belt wraps around                       │
                │   arc; stretches under T1                  │
                ▼                                            ▼
        ◄─── SLACK SIDE (T2) ───────────────────────────────
             Belt is RELAXED, contracted
             by dL2 = T2L/(AE), where dL2 < dL1

   LINEAR VELOCITIES:
   • Belt surface on driving pulley = πd1N1/60 = v
   • Belt surface on driven pulley  = πd2N2/60 = v'  (slightly different due to creep)
   • CREEP = v(T1-T2)/(AE)  ≈  small velocity difference

   RESULT:
   • Driven pulley rotates SLOWER than ideal
   • Velocity ratio N2/N1 = (d1/d2) × [(L+dL2)/(L+dL1)] < d1/d2
```

---

### 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Slip vs. Creep

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph a1[SLIP]
        b1[Frictional Insufficiency] --> b2[Belt Lags Pulley]
        b2 --> b3[Velocity LOST]
        b3 --> b4[Power LOSS]
        b4 --> b5[Mitigation: Increase mu or Wrap Angle]
    end
    subgraph a2[CREEP]
        c1[Elastic Stretching] --> c2[Belt Reshapes Itself]
        c2 --> c3[Velocity DELAYED not LOST]
        c3 --> c4[Power NOT Dissipated]
        c4 --> c5[Mitigation: Use High E Belts]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define *belt creep*. Mention any two factors on which it depends.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Belt creep is the phenomenon of *relative sliding* between the belt and the pulley caused by the *elastic stretching* of the belt material as it moves from the slack side (tension $T_2$) to the tight side (tension $T_1$). It results in a slight difference between the linear velocities of the belt on the tight and slack sides.
>
> **Factors (1 mark):** Belt creep depends on (i) the *tension difference* $(T_1 - T_2)$ — directly proportional, and (ii) the *axial rigidity* of the belt $AE$ (where $A$ is the cross-sectional area and $E$ is Young's modulus) — inversely proportional.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Distinguish between *belt slip* and *belt creep*.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Belt Slip | Belt Creep |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cause** | Insufficient frictional grip | Elastic stretching of belt material |
| **Depends on** | Coefficient of friction $\mu$, wrap angle $\theta$ | Tension difference $(T_1 - T_2)$ and Young's modulus $E$ |
| **Effect on power** | Power is *lost* as heat | Power is *not* lost; only motion is delayed |
| **Magnitude** | Larger (1–5% typical) | Smaller (< 1% for good belts) |
| **Remedial measure** | Use high-friction material, increase wrap angle | Use high-modulus belt material, reduce tension ratio |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]** **(a)** Derive an expression for the *belt creep* in a flat belt drive. **(b)** Show how creep affects the *velocity ratio* of the drive.

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Belt Creep (7 marks)

**Given:** A belt of cross-section $A$, Young's modulus $E$, passing between tight tension $T_1$ and slack tension $T_2$, over a pulley of diameter $d$ at $N$ rpm.

**Step 1:** Elongation under $T_1$ (Hooke's Law):

$$
dL_1 = \frac{T_1 L}{A E}
$$

**Step 2:** Elongation under $T_2$:

$$
dL_2 = \frac{T_2 L}{A E}
$$

**Step 3:** Linear belt velocity (no mechanical slip):

$$
v = \frac{\pi d N}{60}
$$

**Step 4:** Unstretched material flow rate on tight side:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{tight}}^{\text{orig}} = v \cdot \frac{L}{L + dL_1}
$$

**Step 5:** Unstretched material flow rate on slack side:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{slack}}^{\text{orig}} = v \cdot \frac{L}{L + dL_2}
$$

**Step 6:** Equate and find the difference (belt creep):

$$
\Delta v = v \cdot L \cdot \left( \frac{1}{L + dL_2} - \frac{1}{L + dL_1} \right) \approx \frac{v(dL_1 - dL_2)}{L}
$$

**Step 7:** Substitute elongations:

$$
\boxed{\Delta v = \frac{\pi \, d \, N \, (T_1 - T_2)}{60 \, A \, E}}
$$

#### Part (b) — Effect on Velocity Ratio (7 marks)

**Step 1:** From the mass flow conservation equation (Section 3.2):

$$
\frac{d_1 N_1}{L + dL_1} = \frac{d_2 N_2}{L + dL_2}
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for velocity ratio:

$$
\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{d_1}{d_2} \cdot \frac{L + dL_2}{L + dL_1}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $dL_i = T_i L / (AE)$:

$$
\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{d_1}{d_2} \cdot \frac{1 + (T_2/AE)}{1 + (T_1/AE)}
$$

**Step 4:** Linearise for small $T/AE$:

$$
\frac{N_2}{N_1} \approx \frac{d_1}{d_2} \left[ 1 - \frac{T_1 - T_2}{AE} \right]
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The velocity ratio is *less* than the ideal $d_1/d_2$ by a fraction $\frac{T_1 - T_2}{AE}$. The driven pulley rotates slower due to creep.

---

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** **(a)** Explain the *phenomenon of belt creep* with a neat sketch. **(b)** Discuss the *factors affecting belt creep* and state how creep is *minimised* in practical belt drives.

#### Part (a) — Phenomenon Explained (7 marks)

**Conceptual Explanation (4 marks):**

When a flexible belt passes over a pulley, the tension on the two sides of the belt differs ($T_1 \neq T_2$). The tight side (tension $T_1$) stretches the belt by $dL_1 = T_1 L / (AE)$, while the slack side (tension $T_2$) stretches it by $dL_2 = T_2 L / (AE)$. Since $T_1 > T_2$, we have $dL_1 > dL_2$.

As the belt wraps around the driving pulley, it must *transition* from the slack state ($T_2$) to the tight state ($T_1$) — this requires *stretching*, which consumes some of the pulley's motion. Similarly, at the driven pulley, the belt *relaxes* from $T_1$ to $T_2$ — releasing stored elastic energy.

The net effect is that the *linear velocity* of the belt material (in unstretched terms) is *different* on the two sides, even though the *surface* velocity is matched to the pulley at the contact. This *velocity difference* is the **belt creep**.

**Sketch Description (3 marks):** A figure should show two pulleys with a belt; the tight side is labelled $T_1$ and the slack side is labelled $T_2$; arrows indicate the direction of belt motion; the elongations $dL_1$ and $dL_2$ are shown on respective sides; and the linear velocities $v$ and $v'$ on the two pulleys are annotated with a small difference.

#### Part (b) — Factors and Mitigation (7 marks)

**Factors affecting creep (4 marks):**

1. **Tension Difference $(T_1 - T_2)$:** Directly proportional to creep. Larger tension ratio $\Rightarrow$ larger creep.
2. **Young's Modulus ($E$):** Inversely proportional. Higher $E \Rightarrow$ stiffer belt $\Rightarrow$ less creep.
3. **Cross-sectional Area ($A$):** Inversely proportional. Thicker belt $\Rightarrow$ less creep.
4. **Belt Velocity ($v$):** Directly proportional. Higher speed $\Rightarrow$ more absolute creep (in m/s).
5. **Belt Length ($L$):** The term $dL/L$ is independent of $L$, but the absolute elongation $dL$ scales with $L$. For long belts, creep is larger.

**Methods to minimise creep (3 marks):**

1. Use **high-modulus belt materials** (e.g., polyurethane, kevlar-reinforced rubber, steel cord).
2. Operate the drive at a **low tension ratio** ($T_1/T_2$ close to 1, typically $\le 2$).
3. Use **toothed (synchronous) belts** in precision applications — these have *negligible* creep because the teeth enforce positive engagement.
4. Maintain **proper tensioning** to avoid unnecessary high $T_1$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Velocity Ratio:** Students often write $\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{d_1}{d_2} \cdot \frac{1 + T_1/AE}{1 + T_2/AE}$ — this is **backwards**! Creep *reduces* velocity ratio; the correct form has the tight-side term in the *denominator*.
>
> 2. **Confusing Creep with Slip:** A frequent mistake is to treat creep as a frictional phenomenon. Creep is *elastic*; slip is *frictional*. They are governed by *different* equations and have *different* remedies.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Approximation:** Some students do not apply the approximation $(L + dL_1)(L + dL_2) \approx L^2$. While the full form is acceptable, the linearised form is what is expected in Part B.
>
> 4. **Units Slip:** $\Delta v$ has units of *m/s* (a velocity), *not* a dimensionless ratio. Do not report creep in percent for the *magnitude* — only the *velocity ratio reduction* is in percent.
>
> 5. **Missing the $L$ Cancellation:** The factor $L$ in $dL_1 = T_1 L / (AE)$ cancels with the $L$ in the denominator of the final creep expression. The $L$ does *not* appear in the final creep equation.
>
> 6. **Neglecting Centrifugal Tension:** In high-speed drives, $T_C = m v^2$ is significant. The effective tensions become $T_1' = T_1 - T_C$ and $T_2' = T_2 - T_C$. Forgetting this leads to wrong answers in numerical problems.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Belt Creep**

- [ ] **Definition:** Relative sliding between belt and pulley due to *elastic stretching* (not friction).
- [ ] **Core Formula:** $\Delta v = \dfrac{\pi \, d \, N \, (T_1 - T_2)}{60 \, A \, E}$
- [ ] **Linear Velocity:** $v = \dfrac{\pi d N}{60}$ (S.I. units: m/s).
- [ ] **Hooke's Law for Belt:** $dL = \dfrac{T L}{A E}$ (elongation under tension).
- [ ] **Velocity Ratio with Creep:** $\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{d_1}{d_2} \cdot \dfrac{1 + (T_2/AE)}{1 + (T_1/AE)}$
- [ ] **Linearised Velocity Ratio:** $\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \approx \dfrac{d_1}{d_2} \left[ 1 - \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{AE} \right]$
- [ ] **Direction of Effect:** Creep *reduces* the velocity ratio; driven pulley rotates *slower* than ideal.
- [ ] **Key Dependencies:** Directly proportional to $v$, $T_1 - T_2$; inversely proportional to $A$, $E$.
- [ ] **Slip vs. Creep:** Slip = friction (loses power); Creep = elasticity (does not lose power).
- [ ] **Mitigation:** High-$E$ belts, low tension ratio, toothed/synchronous belts for precision.
- [ ] **Typical Magnitude:** Creep is usually $< 1\%$ for properly designed belt drives.
- [ ] **Centrifugal Effect:** At high speeds, $T_C = m v^2$ modifies effective tensions.
- [ ] **Engineering Domain:** Critical for timing belts, automotive drives, robotics, and textile machinery.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Belt Creep**
**Total Marks Coverage:** Part A (3 + 3 = 6 marks) | Part B (14 + 14 = 28 marks) | Worked Example (analytical, ~5 marks)
**Bloom's Levels Addressed:** L1 (Remember), L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyse), L5 (Evaluate)
**Course Outcomes (CO) Mapped:** CO3 — *Analyse the dynamics of power transmission elements including belt drives under realistic operating conditions including elastic effects.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Brakes - Block Brake with Short Shoe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT527 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Mechanical Brakes - Block Brake with Short Shoe # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Block Brake with Short Shoe** is a simple, single-faced friction brake in which a short, rigid block (called a *shoe* or *pad*) is pressed against the periphery of a rotating drum or wheel to generate a retarding torque through dry friction. The term "short shoe" implies that the arc of contact between the block and the drum is negligibly small — we treat it as **point contact** for analytical purposes. This is in contrast to a *long shoe* (or *block brake with long shoe*), where the wrap angle is significant and pressure distribution is non-uniform. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight (PERAT527 – Module 3):** A *short shoe* block brake assumes the resultant normal reaction **N** acts at a single point on the drum surface, and the contact arc is treated as zero. This assumption simplifies the analysis drastically compared to long-shoe (clutch-like) problems. The brake consists of the following primary components: | Component | Function | |-----------|----------| | **Brake Drum / Wheel** | Rotating member; carries the kinetic energy to be dissipated | | **Block / Shoe** | Stationary friction element pressed against the drum | | **Lever (Brake Arm)** | Transmits the applied effort to the shoe | | **Pivot / Fulcrum** | Hinge point of the lever; determines self-energizing behavior | | **Effort (P)** | Operator/applied force that creates normal contact pressure | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world analogy — The bicycle hand-brake:** > Think of the rear brake of an old-fashioned bicycle. You squeeze the brake lever on the handlebar → a metal cable pulls a **short rubber block** against the side of the rear wheel rim → friction between the block and the rim slows the wheel down. That rubber block is your "short shoe." Because the contact patch is small (a narrow strip), we can treat all the push as happening at a *single point* — that is the essence of a short-shoe block brake. **Why "short"?** Imagine pressing the *corner* of a book against a spinning turntable versus pressing the *full spine* of the book against it. The corner case = **short shoe** (point contact, uniform pressure assumption valid). The spine case = **long shoe** (pressure varies along the contact arc, requiring more advanced analysis). For KTU Module 3, we deal only with the *corner-of-the-book* case. ## 1.3 Governing Physical Constants & Standard Symbols - **Coefficient of friction (μ)**: dimensionless; typically 0.1 to 0.5 for dry brake linings on cast iron/steel. - **Drum radius (R)**: in meters (**m**). - **Applied effort (P)**: in Newtons (**N**). - **Normal reaction (N)**: in Newtons (**N**), acts perpendicular (radially) to drum surface. - **Tangential / frictional force (F = μN)**: in Newtons (**N**), acts tangentially opposing rotation. - **Braking torque (TB = μN·R)**: in Newton-meters (**N·m**). - **Lever arm distances (a, x, b)**: in meters (**m**). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — The "Why" and "How" The brake works on the principle of **kinetic friction energy dissipation**: 1. **Operator input** → force **P** is applied at the free end of the lever. 2. **Lever pivots** about its fulcrum, swinging the shoe toward the drum. 3. **Contact pressure** builds up between shoe and drum → drum exerts a radial normal reaction **N** on the shoe. 4. **Drum surface slides** beneath the shoe → a tangential **frictional force F = μN** develops, opposing the relative motion of the drum. 5. **This frictional force, acting at radius R, generates a braking torque:** $$T_B = F \cdot R = \mu N R$$ 6. **Energy dissipation**: The work done against friction (TB × θ) is converted into heat, slowing the drum. ## 2.2 Force Geometry on the Short Shoe Three forces act on the lever-shoe system in equilibrium: - **P** — applied effort (vertical or at an angle, depends on design) - **N** — radial normal reaction from drum on shoe (perpendicular to drum surface, passes through drum center) - **μN** — tangential frictional force on shoe (tangent to drum surface at contact point, opposing drum rotation) > [!NOTE] > The **direction of μN** is critical. It always opposes the *sliding velocity of the drum* relative to the shoe. If the drum rotates clockwise, the friction on the shoe acts counter-clockwise (tangentially), and vice-versa. ## 2.3 Self-Energizing vs. Self-De-energizing — The Heart of the Topic The position of the **pivot** relative to the line of action of the friction force (μN) determines whether the brake **helps itself** or **fights itself**. | Configuration | Geometry | Behavior | |---------------|----------|----------| | **Self-energizing** | Friction force μN creates a moment *in the same sense* as P about the pivot | The friction *aids* the applied force; smaller P needed; can even self-lock if μ is large | | **Self-de-energizing** | Friction force μN creates a moment *opposite* to P about the pivot | The friction *opposes* the applied force; larger P required | ### 2.3.1 Quantitative Self-Energization Criterion If the line of action of the **normal force N** is offset from the pivot by a perpendicular distance **x** (measured along the lever), and the friction force μN acts at perpendicular distance **R** from the pivot (since it is tangent to the drum of radius R): - **Self-energizing** requires: **μN · R > N · x**, i.e., **μ > x / R** - **Self-de-energizing** when: **μ < x / R** - **Neutral (pivotal axis on friction line)**: **μ = x / R** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Whenever a block-brake problem gives you lever arm distances, *immediately classify it as self-energizing or self-de-energizing based on the geometry*. This is the single most-tested sub-concept in Module 3. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Conditions | |---|---------|---------|-----------| | 1 | $F = \mu N$ | Tangential frictional force on shoe | Always | | 2 | $T_B = \mu N R$ | Braking torque on drum | Always | | 3 | $P \cdot a = N \cdot x - \mu N \cdot R$ | Lever equilibrium — **self-energizing** case | Pivot such that μN moment aids P | | 4 | $P \cdot a = N \cdot x + \mu N \cdot R$ | Lever equilibrium — **self-de-energizing** case | Pivot such that μN moment opposes P | | 5 | $N = \dfrac{P \cdot a}{x \mp \mu R}$ | Normal force from lever equation | "−" for self-energizing, "+" for self-de-energizing | | 6 | $T_B = \mu R \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot a}{x \mp \mu R}$ | Braking torque in terms of P | Same sign convention as #5 | | 7 | $\mu \geq \dfrac{x}{R}$ | Condition for self-energization | Geometric | | 8 | $P = \dfrac{\mu N R}{a}$ (when line of N passes through pivot, x = 0) | Effort when N's line of action goes through pivot | Special geometry | > [!WARNING] > **Sign convention pitfall:** Many students write both numerator and denominator with the same sign. The correct sign is **opposite in the denominator** compared to the action of μN. Master this once; the entire topic is downhill afterwards. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Railway vehicles** — early steam-locomotive brakes were short-shoe block brakes actuated by hand levers. - **Cranes and winches** — load-holding brakes that lock the drum against back-driving. - **Press machines and elevators** — fail-safe holding brakes on hoisting drums. - **Automotive drum brakes** (single-leading-shoe type) — use the self-energizing principle to reduce driver pedal effort. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Free-Body Diagram Setup (Textual Description) Consider a horizontal lever pivoted at point **O** on the left. The lever extends to the right and ends in a block (shoe) that presses against a drum of radius **R** rotating about its center **C**. The lever arm from pivot **O** to the point of application of effort **P** is **a** (measured vertically downward from O). The block contacts the drum at a point whose line of action of normal reaction **N** lies at perpendicular distance **x** to the right of the pivot O. The frictional force μN is tangent to the drum, so its line of action is at perpendicular distance **R** vertically above the contact point. For the **self-de-energizing case** (μN moment opposes P): - Effort P is applied downward at distance **a** to the right of O. - Normal N acts horizontally (radially) on the shoe, at vertical distance **x** below O's horizontal level. - Friction μN acts vertically (tangentially), at horizontal distance roughly **R** away from drum center. ## 3.2 Derivation — Lever Moment Equation (Self-De-Energizing Case) **Step 1:** Identify all moments about pivot O. Use right-hand rule: clockwise positive. **Step 2:** Moment of applied effort P about O: - P acts downward at horizontal distance **a** from O. - Moment magnitude = **P · a** (clockwise, tending to push shoe into drum). **Step 3:** Moment of normal reaction N about O: - N acts horizontally (toward the left, on the shoe, as the drum pushes the shoe away). - N's line of action is at vertical distance **x** *above* the pivot O. - Moment of N = **N · x** (counter-clockwise, since N pushes shoe away from drum — it opposes the rotation caused by P). **Step 4:** Moment of friction force μN about O: - μN acts vertically, tangent to the drum. - Its line of action is at horizontal distance approximately **R** from the pivot. - Moment of μN = **μN · R** (this moment *opposes* P, so counter-clockwise in this configuration). **Step 5:** Apply static equilibrium (Σ MO = 0): $$P \cdot a - N \cdot x - \mu N \cdot R = 0$$ **Step 6:** Solve for the normal reaction N: $$P \cdot a = N \cdot x + \mu N \cdot R$$ $$P \cdot a = N \, (x + \mu R)$$ $$\boxed{N = \dfrac{P \cdot a}{x + \mu R}}$$ **Step 7:** Braking torque on the drum: $$T_B = \mu N R = \mu R \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot a}{x + \mu R}$$ $$\boxed{T_B = \dfrac{\mu R \, P \, a}{x + \mu R}}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation — Self-Energizing Case In this geometry, the friction force μN creates a moment that *aids* the applied force P. Re-drawing the moments: - Moment of P about O: **+P · a** (clockwise, pushing shoe in). - Moment of N about O: **−N · x** (counter-clockwise, pushing shoe away). - Moment of μN about O: **−μN · R** — but this is in the *same* sense as P now (self-energizing geometry). Equilibrium: $$P \cdot a + \mu N \cdot R - N \cdot x = 0$$ $$P \cdot a = N \cdot x - \mu N \cdot R = N \, (x - \mu R)$$ $$\boxed{N = \dfrac{P \cdot a}{x - \mu R}} \quad \text{(provided } x > \mu R \text{)}$$ Braking torque: $$\boxed{T_B = \dfrac{\mu R \, P \, a}{x - \mu R}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > If **x = μR**, the denominator is zero → **N → ∞** → the brake locks itself (self-locking) without any further effort. Theoretically the drum cannot be turned. In practice, this condition is avoided by design. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) > **Problem:** A single block brake with a short shoe has a drum of diameter 600 mm rotating at 240 rpm. The coefficient of friction between shoe and drum is 0.3. The pivot of the lever is at a perpendicular distance of 100 mm from the line of action of the normal reaction, and the effort is applied at 500 mm from the pivot. Find: > (a) The effort required to apply a braking torque of 250 N·m. > (b) State whether the brake is self-energizing or self-de-energizing. **Given:** - Drum diameter = 600 mm → R = 0.3 m - Speed N = 240 rpm (not needed for torque calc) - μ = 0.3 - x = 100 mm = 0.1 m - a = 500 mm = 0.5 m - TB = 250 N·m **Part (a) — Find P:** Assume self-de-energizing configuration (typical for this geometry unless told otherwise): $$T_B = \dfrac{\mu R \, P \, a}{x + \mu R}$$ $$250 = \dfrac{0.3 \times 0.3 \times P \times 0.5}{0.1 + 0.3 \times 0.3}$$ Compute the denominator: $$0.1 + 0.3 \times 0.3 = 0.1 + 0.09 = 0.19 \text{ m}$$ Compute the numerator factor: $$0.3 \times 0.3 \times 0.5 = 0.045$$ So: $$250 = \dfrac{0.045 \, P}{0.19}$$ $$P = \dfrac{250 \times 0.19}{0.045} = \dfrac{47.5}{0.045} \approx 1055.56 \text{ N}$$ **Part (b) — Self-energization check:** $$\mu = 0.3, \quad \dfrac{x}{R} = \dfrac{0.1}{0.3} = 0.333$$ Since **μ = 0.3 < x/R = 0.333** → the brake is **self-DE-energizing**. > [!NOTE] > The tangential heat generated per second (power dissipation) would be Pheat = TB × ω, where ω = 2πN/60. This is often asked in KTU Part (b) sub-questions. We deliberately omit it here since the problem asks only for P and classification. ## 3.5 Master Summary Table — When to Use Which Formula | Given condition | Pivot location | N equation | Braking Torque | |-----------------|----------------|-----------|----------------| | Line of N passes through pivot (x = 0) | Aligned | $N = \dfrac{P a}{\mu R}$ | $T_B = P a$ | | μR < x (de-energizing) | Offset away from μN line | $N = \dfrac{P a}{x + \mu R}$ | $T_B = \dfrac{\mu R P a}{x + \mu R}$ | | μR > x (self-energizing) | Offset toward μN line | $N = \dfrac{P a}{x - \mu R}$ | $T_B = \dfrac{\mu R P a}{x - \mu R}$ | | x = μR (neutral / self-locking boundary) | Critical | $N \to \infty$ | $T_B \to \infty$ (theoretical) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow The diagram below shows the **force-flow architecture** of a single block brake with a short shoe in a self-de-energizing configuration. Each block represents a distinct functional / force entity, and the arrows represent the *direction of force action* (not motion). ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Operator Effort P
Applied downward at distance a] O[Pivot O of Lever
Fixed fulcrum] L[Lever Arm
Transmits P to shoe] S[Short Shoe / Block
Rigid friction pad] D[Rotating Drum
Radius R, rotates] N[Normal Reaction N
Radial, drum pushes shoe] F[Friction Force muN
Tangential, opposes drum rotation] TB[Braking Torque TB
Equals muN times R] H[Heat Dissipation
Energy lost to environment] A -->|force| L L -->|pivots about| O L -->|pushes shoe against| S S -->|contact| D D -->|reaction| N D -->|tangential reaction| F F -->|generates| TB TB -->|dissipates as| H N -->|resists shoe motion| S ``` ## 4.2 Force-Vector Decomposition Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD P0[Applied Effort P] --> P1{Geometry of
Lever-Drum System} P1 -->|Self-energizing
mu times R greater than x| P2[Normal N = P times a over x minus mu times R] P1 -->|Self-de-energizing
mu times R less than x| P3[Normal N = P times a over x plus mu times R] P1 -->|Neutral x equals mu times R| P4[Self-locking
N theoretically infinite] P2 --> Q1[Braking Torque TB = mu times N times R] P3 --> Q1 P4 --> Q2[Brake locks automatically
Design hazard] Q1 --> R[Power Dissipation = TB times omega] R --> S[Heat to environment
Cooling required] ``` ## 4.3 Decision Tree for Solving KTU Block-Brake Problems ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[New KTU Block-Brake Problem] --> Q1{Is the line of
action of N
through pivot?} Q1 -->|Yes, x = 0| A1[Use N = P a divided by mu R] Q1 -->|No, x given| Q2{Is mu times R
greater than x?} Q2 -->|Yes, self-energizing| A2[Use N = P a divided by x minus mu R] Q2 -->|No, self-de-energizing| A3[Use N = P a divided by x plus mu R] A1 --> Z[Compute TB = mu times N times R] A2 --> Z A3 --> Z Z --> Y{Answer in
correct units?
N-m, N, etc.} Y -->|Yes| End[Write final answer with units] Y -->|No| Rev[Convert mm to m
and N to kN if needed] Rev --> End ``` ## 4.4 Verbal Schematic — Self-Energizing vs. Self-De-Energizing Even without a hand-drawn diagram, picture this: - **Self-de-energizing**: The lever is like a door hinge where, if you push on the door *away* from the hinge, the friction at the shoe *pushes the door back toward you*. You must keep pushing. - **Self-energizing**: The door is hinged such that friction at the shoe *carries the door forward*; once you give a small push, the friction does most of the work, like pushing a heavy crate on a slight downhill slope. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper] **Q: Define a block brake. What is meant by a "short shoe" in the context of a block brake?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **block brake** is a friction brake in which a block (or shoe) is pressed against a moving surface (drum or wheel) to retard its motion by means of the frictional force generated at the contact interface. A **short shoe** refers to a block whose contact arc with the drum is negligibly small, allowing the contact to be treated as a *point contact*. As a result, the normal reaction N is assumed to act at a single point on the drum surface, simplifying the analysis. (3 marks) ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q: Distinguish between a self-energizing and a self-de-energizing brake with the help of a suitable example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - In a **self-energizing** brake, the frictional force (μN) creates a moment about the pivot that *aids* the applied effort. Hence, a smaller effort can produce the required braking torque. Example: Single-leading-shoe drum brake in automobiles. - In a **self-de-energizing** brake, the frictional force creates a moment that *opposes* the applied effort, so a larger effort is required. Example: External-contracting band brake with the lever pivoted away from the direction of μN. (3 marks) ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Sign in denominator** is the #1 reason students lose 4–6 marks. Self-energizing → "−" in denominator; self-de-energizing → "+" in denominator. > - **Unit conversion** (mm → m) error costs 1–2 marks routinely. Always convert to SI units *first*, then substitute. > - **Forgetting to state self-energizing condition** μ ≥ x/R costs 2 marks in descriptive sub-parts. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Derive the expression for the braking torque of a single block brake with a short shoe, clearly stating the assumptions. (7 marks) **(b)** A single block brake with a short shoe has a drum diameter of 500 mm, coefficient of friction μ = 0.25. The lever is pivoted at a perpendicular distance of 80 mm from the line of action of the normal reaction. An effort of 350 N is applied at 600 mm from the pivot. Determine (i) the braking torque and (ii) whether the brake is self-energizing. (7 marks) #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Assumptions:** 1. The block is rigid, and the contact between block and drum is a *point contact* (short-shoe condition). 2. The coefficient of friction μ is constant over the contact surface. 3. The drum is rotating at a steady-state speed (no angular acceleration). 4. The pivot is frictionless. 5.
