The pressure distribution at the contact is uniform; the resultant N acts at the geometric center of the contact. **Free-body diagram:** Three forces act on the lever-shoe system — applied effort **P**, normal reaction **N** (radial), and friction force **μN** (tangential). **Derivation (self-de-energizing case):** Taking moments about the pivot O and equating to zero: $$P \cdot a = N \cdot x + \mu N \cdot R$$ where: - a = perpendicular distance of P from pivot - x = perpendicular distance of N's line of action from pivot - R = drum radius (perpendicular distance of μN's line of action from pivot) Solving for N: $$N = \dfrac{P \cdot a}{x + \mu R}$$ The braking torque on the drum is: $$T_B = \mu N R = \mu R \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot a}{x + \mu R}$$ $$\boxed{T_B = \dfrac{\mu R \, P \, a}{x + \mu R}}$$ This is the required expression. (7 marks) #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** - Drum diameter = 500 mm → R = 0.25 m - μ = 0.25 - x = 80 mm = 0.08 m - a = 600 mm = 0.6 m - P = 350 N **Step 1 — Braking torque:** $$T_B = \dfrac{\mu R \, P \, a}{x + \mu R}$$ Compute numerator: $$\mu R \, P \, a = 0.25 \times 0.25 \times 350 \times 0.6 = 13.125 \text{ N·m}$$ Compute denominator: $$x + \mu R = 0.08 + 0.25 \times 0.25 = 0.08 + 0.0625 = 0.1425 \text{ m}$$ Therefore: $$T_B = \dfrac{13.125}{0.1425} = 92.105 \approx 92.1 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 2 — Self-energization check:** $$\mu = 0.25, \quad \dfrac{x}{R} = \dfrac{0.08}{0.25} = 0.32$$ Since **μ = 0.25 < x/R = 0.32**, the brake is **self-de-energizing**. (7 marks) --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a single block brake with a short shoe. State the conditions under which the brake is self-energizing. (7 marks) **(b)** In a single block brake with a short shoe, the line of action of the normal reaction is at a perpendicular distance of 50 mm from the pivot. The drum has a diameter of 400 mm and rotates at 200 rpm. If the coefficient of friction is 0.3 and the effort applied is 250 N at 400 mm from the pivot, calculate (i) the braking torque, and (ii) the power absorbed by the brake. (7 marks) #### Model Solution — Part (a) A single block brake with a short shoe consists of a lever pivoted at one end. The other end of the lever carries a short friction block (shoe) that presses against a rotating drum. An effort **P** is applied to the lever, which causes the shoe to be forced against the drum. The drum exerts a normal reaction **N** on the shoe, and the relative sliding between drum and shoe produces a frictional force **μN** acting tangentially. This frictional force, acting at the drum's radius R, generates a braking torque $T_B = \mu N R$ that opposes the drum's rotation. **Free-body diagram:** A typical sketch shows: - Pivot at the left end of a horizontal lever. - Drum rotating clockwise (or counter-clockwise) at the right. - Force P applied downward at the right end of the lever. - Normal N acting horizontally to the right (drum pushing shoe away). - Friction μN acting tangentially (upward or downward depending on rotation direction). **Self-energization conditions:** - The friction force μN must produce a moment about the pivot that **aids** the applied effort P. - Mathematically: **μN · R > N · x**, i.e., **μ > x / R**. - Equivalently, the pivot must lie on the *opposite* side of the line of action of μN from the line of action of N. (7 marks) #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** - x = 50 mm = 0.05 m - Drum diameter = 400 mm → R = 0.2 m - N (speed) = 200 rpm - μ = 0.3 - P = 250 N - a = 400 mm = 0.4 m **Step 1 — Braking torque (assume self-de-energizing):** $$T_B = \dfrac{\mu R \, P \, a}{x + \mu R}$$ Numerator: $$\mu R \, P \, a = 0.3 \times 0.2 \times 250 \times 0.4 = 6.0 \text{ N·m}$$ Denominator: $$x + \mu R = 0.05 + 0.3 \times 0.2 = 0.05 + 0.06 = 0.11 \text{ m}$$ $$T_B = \dfrac{6.0}{0.11} = 54.545 \approx 54.55 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 2 — Power absorbed:** Angular velocity: $$\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 200}{60} = 20.944 \text{ rad/s}$$ Power absorbed by the brake: $$P_{absorbed} = T_B \times \omega = 54.55 \times 20.944 = 1142.5 \text{ W} \approx 1.143 \text{ kW}$$ (7 marks) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Block brake with short shoe** = point contact; treat N as concentrated at a single point. - **Three forces on the lever**: applied effort P, normal reaction N, friction μN. - **Braking torque**: $T_B = \mu N R$ — universal. - **Lever moment equation (self-de-energizing)**: $P \cdot a = N \cdot x + \mu N \cdot R$. - **Lever moment equation (self-energizing)**: $P \cdot a = N \cdot x - \mu N \cdot R$. - **Normal force formula (self-de-energizing)**: $N = \dfrac{P a}{x + \mu R}$. - **Normal force formula (self-energizing)**: $N = \dfrac{P a}{x - \mu R}$. - **Self-energizing criterion**: $\mu \geq \dfrac{x}{R}$. Below this, the brake is self-de-energizing. - **Self-locking condition**: $x = \mu R$ leads to $N \to \infty$ — design hazard; always avoid in practice. - **Power dissipated by brake**: $P_{diss} = T_B \cdot \omega = T_B \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ (in watts). - **Sign convention mastery is non-negotiable** — wrong sign in the denominator is the single biggest mark-loser. - **Always convert mm to m** before substituting numerical values into formulas. - **For a "line of action of N passing through pivot" (x = 0)**: the formula simplifies to $T_B = P \cdot a$. - **For a 14-mark question**, expect either a pure derivation OR a derivation + numerical + classification of self-energization. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Takeaway:** Master the *sign convention* between self-energizing (μR > x) and self-de-energizing (μR < x). All KTU Module 3 block-brake numericals hinge on this single binary classification.

topics.title: Analysis of Forces Acting on Drum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT527 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Analysis of Forces Acting on Drum # Module 3: Analysis of Forces Acting on Drum ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Rigid)** — A *drum brake* is a friction-based braking or transmitting device in which a rotating cylindrical surface (the **drum**) is pressed against by a stationary block (the *shoe* or *block*) to develop a tangential friction force, thereby absorbing or transmitting energy. The *analysis of forces acting on the drum* involves determining the normal reaction $(N)$, the tangential friction force $(\mu N)$, the actuating force on the lever, and the braking/transmitting torque, under static equilibrium of the lever mechanism. ### 1.1 Formal Classification of Drum Brakes | Type | Description | Typical Use | |------|-------------|-------------| | **Single Block (Single Shoe)** | One block presses on drum from one side | Light loads, hand brakes | | **Pivoted Block (Long / Short Shoe)** | Block is pivoted at one corner | Crane, elevator brakes | | **Double Block (Two-Shoe)** | Two blocks press symmetrically | Industrial, heavy machinery | | **Internal Expanding Shoe** | Two pivoted shoes press *outward* on inner drum | Automobiles (drum brake) | | **External Contracting Shoe** | Two shoes press *inward* on outer drum | Hoists, winches | ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy — The Bicycle Hand Brake** > Imagine pressing the lever of a bicycle hand brake with your hand. The lever pushes a rubber pad (the *block*) against the spinning metal rim (the *drum*). The harder you press, the more the rim is squeezed, the greater the friction, and the faster the bicycle slows down. > > In engineering language: > - Your hand force $\rightarrow$ **Applied force $F$** > - The push on the rubber pad $\rightarrow$ **Normal reaction $N$** > - The rubbing grip $\rightarrow$ **Tangential friction force $\mu N$** > - The spinning rim $\rightarrow$ **Rotating drum** > > Now the *tricky* part: when the block pivots (as in real brake levers), the friction force itself can either *help* or *hurt* your effort. This is the entire essence of "**self-energization**" — a concept KTU examiners *love* to test. ### 1.3 Fundamental Assumptions in KTU Analysis For all derivations that follow, KTU 2024 standard practice uses these **stated assumptions** (memorize them — they appear in every model answer): 1. The drum rotates at constant angular velocity (no angular acceleration of drum). 2. The drum is a **rigid body** (does not deflect under load). 3. The block/shoe is a **rigid body**. 4. The coefficient of friction $\mu$ is **uniform** across the contact surface. 5. The wear is **uniform** (used in pivoted/wrapped brakes). 6. The pivot friction is **negligible**, unless explicitly stated otherwise. 7. The line of action of the normal reaction is **tangential to the drum at the contact point**. ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > Standard values to remember (in **bold** for KTU questions): > - Coefficient of friction for cast iron on cast iron: $\mu \approx \mathbf{0.25}$ to $\mathbf{0.40}$ > - Coefficient of friction for leather on cast iron: $\mu \approx \mathbf{0.30}$ to $\mathbf{0.50}$ > - Maximum allowable pressure on the block: $p_{max} \approx \mathbf{1 \text{ to } 2 \text{ N/mm}^2}$ for leather, $\mathbf{0.5 \text{ to } 1.0 \text{ N/mm}^2}$ for wood > - The line of action of $N$ is at the **center of contact** (for uniform pressure assumption). > - For **uniform wear** (long-time operation), the line of action of $N$ is at the **edge of the block** (the entry side, for a pivoted block). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Force Components Acting on the Drum When a block presses against a rotating drum, three force interactions occur at the contact: 1. **Normal Reaction $N$** — acts radially (perpendicular to the drum surface at the contact point). 2. **Tangential Friction Force $\mu N$** — acts tangentially to the drum at the contact point, opposing relative motion. 3. **Braking/Transmitting Torque $T$** — produced by $\mu N$ acting at the drum radius $R$. $$T = \mu N R$$ > [!NOTE] > The radius $R$ here is the **effective radius** — for uniform pressure, $R$ is the geometric drum radius; for uniform wear, $R$ is replaced by $\frac{R_1 + R_2}{2}$ in band/pivot analyses. For *single-block drum analysis*, $R$ is the drum radius at the point of contact. ## 2.2 The Generic Lever Equilibrium Setup Consider a single block brake lever as shown conceptually below. A **block of height $h$** is pressed against a **drum of radius $R$**. The block is pivoted at one end of the lever. Let: - $F$ = applied force at lever end - $a$ = distance of $F$ from pivot - $b$ = distance of pivot from center of drum (horizontal) - $c$ = vertical distance of the line of action of $N$ from the pivot - $N$ = normal reaction on the block (from the drum, equal to push on lever) - $\mu N$ = friction force on the block (opposes drum rotation) The **moment equilibrium about the pivot** gives: $$F \cdot a = N \cdot b \pm \mu N \cdot c$$ where the sign ($\pm$) is decided by the direction of $\mu N$ relative to the moment arm of $N$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the $\pm$ sign?** KTU examiners mark this as a critical concept. The friction force $\mu N$ may create a moment that *assists* or *opposes* the applied force $F$. The cases are: > - **$(+)$ sign** $\Rightarrow$ Friction *opposes* $F$ $\Rightarrow$ **Self-de-energizing brake** (need more force) > - $(-)$ sign $\Rightarrow$ Friction *assists* $F$ $\Rightarrow$ **Self-energizing brake** (need less force, can lock) ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU EXAM CHEAT SHEET — Drum Force Analysis** | Sl. | Brake Type / Case | Key Equation | Definition of Terms | Self-Energizing? | |---:|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Simple block brake** | $F \cdot a = N \cdot b$ | No pivot friction, $\mu N$ passes through pivot | No | | 2 | **Pivoted block — uniform pressure** | $F = \dfrac{\mu N b \pm \mu^2 N c}{a}$ | $c$ = half-block height | Depends on direction | | 3 | **Pivoted block — uniform wear** | Line of $N$ at edge: $c = h$ | $h$ = block height | May self-lock | | 4 | **Block pivoted at top** (drum CW) | $F = \dfrac{\mu N b + \mu^2 N c}{a}$ | Friction *adds* to $N$ moment | **No** (de-energizing) | | 5 | **Block pivoted at top** (drum CCW) | $F = \dfrac{\mu N b - \mu^2 N c}{a}$ | Friction *subtracts* from $N$ moment | **Yes** (self-energizing) | | 6 | **Internal expanding shoe** (one shoe) | $\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}} = \dfrac{b - \mu c}{b + \mu c}$ | Ratio of shoe torques | Depends on $\mu c / b$ | | 7 | **Condition for self-lock** | $\mu b \geq c$ | Block will lock without $F$ | Lock | | 8 | **Energy absorbed** | $E = T \cdot \theta$ | $\theta$ = total angular displacement (rad) | — | | 9 | **Heat dissipation rate** | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | $\omega$ = drum angular velocity (rad/s) | — | | 10 | **Braking torque on drum** | $T_{brake} = \mu N R$ | $R$ = drum radius | — | > **Note on units:** $F$ in $\mathbf{N}$, $N$ in $\mathbf{N}$, $b, c, a$ in $\mathbf{m}$ or $\mathbf{mm}$ (be consistent), $\mu$ is **dimensionless**, $T$ in $\mathbf{N \cdot m}$. ## 2.4 Why This Matters in Real Engineering > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility** — Drum brake analysis is the cornerstone of: > - **Automotive brake design** (drum and disc systems) > - **Crane and hoist safety** (over-speed protection) > - **Elevator emergency brakes** (rope-grab drum) > - **Heavy press machinery** (flywheel braking) > - **Locomotive dynamic braking** > > A miscalculation in the self-energization condition can result in brake **lockup** (catastrophic) or **brake failure** (catastrophic in the other direction). KTU frequently asks students to **identify** which case applies from a given figure. ## 2.5 Condition for Self-Energization and Self-Lock The **mathematical condition** for a single pivoted block to be self-energizing is: $$\mu c \geq b$$ If this holds, the friction force alone (with $F = 0$) can sustain the load, meaning the brake **self-locks** and cannot be released easily. This is **dangerous** in most applications. Conversely, the condition for **de-energization** (need to apply more force as speed increases) is: $$\mu c < b$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequently Asked:** "What is the significance of the term $\mu c$ in the pivot moment?" The answer: $\mu c$ is the moment arm contribution of the friction force $\mu N$ about the pivot. If it can replace the role of the normal-reaction moment $\mu N \cdot b$ entirely, the brake is self-locking. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Single Block Brake — Pivoted at One End (Uniform Pressure) ### Setup and Free Body Diagram Description Consider a horizontal lever $AO$. The drum of radius $R$ contacts a rectangular block of height $h$ (block top at $A$ and block bottom at the contact edge). The lever is pivoted at $A$. Force $F$ is applied vertically downward at end $O$. The block pushes the lever with reaction $N$ (radially inward on lever, i.e., horizontal toward pivot at distance $b$ from pivot). The friction force $\mu N$ acts vertically — the direction depends on drum rotation. **Take drum rotation: Clockwise (CW)** ### Step 1 — Identify all forces and their points of action - Applied force $F$ acts at distance $a$ from pivot $A$, downward. - Normal reaction $N$ acts at the contact point, horizontal, at height $c$ from pivot, distance $b$ horizontally. - Friction force $\mu N$ acts at the contact point, vertical (upward, since drum CW surface at contact moves right, so friction on lever from drum is upward). ### Step 2 — Take moments about pivot $A$ Taking **counter-clockwise positive**: - Moment of $F$ (downward at distance $a$): $+F \cdot a$ (CCW about $A$). - Moment of $N$ (acting at distance $b$ from pivot, pushing lever away from drum, so tending to rotate the lever CW about $A$): $-N \cdot b$. - Moment of $\mu N$ (upward at distance $b$ from $A$): Since the contact is below and to the right of pivot, an upward $\mu N$ creates a CCW moment: $+\mu N \cdot c$ — wait, we need to re-examine geometry. **Re-examining the standard KTU geometry** (block pivoted at top, drum below lever, contact at bottom of block): Let the pivot be at the **top** of the block. The block hangs down to contact the drum. $N$ acts horizontally (pushing lever away from drum center). $\mu N$ acts tangentially (along the drum rotation direction, but on the lever it is opposite — so $\mu N$ is *downward* on the lever if drum rotates CCW as seen). For **drum rotating CW**, the contact surface of the drum moves to the right. Friction on the drum from the block is to the left. By Newton's third law, friction on the block from the drum is to the right (the drum drags the block right). At the contact point, this means the friction force on the lever is directed along the tangent — and creates a moment. To avoid geometric confusion, the **standard KTU convention** is: > When the line of action of $\mu N$ tends to **rotate the lever in the same sense** as the applied force $F$, the friction **assists** the applied force (self-energizing). The equation becomes $F a = N b - \mu N c$ (friction moment opposes $N$ moment). > > When the line of action of $\mu N$ tends to **rotate the lever opposite** to $F$, the friction **opposes** the applied force (self-de-energizing). The equation becomes $F a = N b + \mu N c$. ### Step 3 — Write the equilibrium equation (Self-de-energizing case, drum CW) $$F \cdot a = N \cdot b + \mu N \cdot c$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for $F$ in terms of $N$ $$F \cdot a = N (b + \mu c)$$ $$F = \frac{N (b + \mu c)}{a}$$ ### Step 5 — Solve for $N$ in terms of $F$ $$N = \frac{F \cdot a}{(b + \mu c)}$$ ### Step 6 — Express the braking torque on the drum The friction force at the drum surface is $\mu N$, acting at radius $R$. Thus the braking torque is: $$T_{brake} = \mu N R = \frac{\mu R F a}{(b + \mu c)}$$ This is the **KTU standard final answer** for a single pivoted block brake in the de-energizing case. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Self-Energizing Case (Drum Rotating CCW) When the drum rotation reverses, the direction of $\mu N$ on the lever reverses. The moment of $\mu N$ now **opposes** the moment of $N$ (rather than adding to it). $$F \cdot a = N \cdot b - \mu N \cdot c$$ $$F = \frac{N (b - \mu c)}{a}$$ $$T_{brake} = \frac{\mu R F a}{(b - \mu c)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Interpretation:** Notice that as $\mu c \to b$, $T_{brake} \to \infty$. This means a *small* applied force produces a *very large* braking torque — the brake becomes **self-energizing**. If $\mu c = b$ exactly, the brake self-locks and $F = 0$ is enough. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Internal Expanding Brake — Two Shoes ### Setup Two pivoted shoes are inside a drum, pressing outward. Let shoe 1 (primary) and shoe 2 (secondary) be pivoted at points $P_1$ and $P_2$ respectively. A common actuating force is applied through a mechanism. Let $N_1$ and $N_2$ be normal reactions on shoes 1 and 2. Friction forces are $\mu N_1$ and $\mu N_2$. Let $a$ be the lever arm of the actuating force on each shoe (assume same), $b$ = horizontal distance of pivot from shoe contact, $c$ = vertical distance of line of action of $N$ from pivot. ### Step 1 — Moment equilibrium of shoe 1 (CW drum, self-energizing for shoe 1) For the **self-energizing** shoe (let's say shoe 1), friction assists: $$F \cdot a = N_1 \cdot b - \mu N_1 \cdot c$$ $$F = \frac{N_1 (b - \mu c)}{a}$$ ### Step 2 — Moment equilibrium of shoe 2 (now shoe 2 is de-energizing for CW drum) For the **de-energizing** shoe: $$F \cdot a = N_2 \cdot b + \mu N_2 \cdot c$$ $$F = \frac{N_2 (b + \mu c)}{a}$$ ### Step 3 — Take the ratio $$\frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{b + \mu c}{b - \mu c}$$ Since $T_1 = \mu N_1 R$ and $T_2 = \mu N_2 R$: $$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{b + \mu c}{b - \mu c}$$ This is the **famous KTU result** for internal expanding brake shoe analysis. It shows that the **self-energizing shoe carries more torque** than the de-energizing shoe — a critical fact for design. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** If $\mu c \geq b$, then $T_2$ becomes infinite or negative, indicating the brake is self-locking on the de-energizing side. This sets a design constraint: $b > \mu c$ always. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Energy and Heat Dissipation in Drum Braking When a drum brake brings a rotating system to rest, the **kinetic energy of the rotating system** is converted to heat. $$E_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$$ where $I$ is the moment of inertia (in $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2$) and $\omega$ is the angular velocity (in $\text{rad/s}$). This energy must be absorbed by the brake: $$E_{brake} = T_{brake} \cdot \theta = \mu N R \cdot \theta$$ where $\theta$ is the **total angle** (in radians) through which the drum rotates during braking. **Equating the two:** $$\frac{1}{2} I \omega^2 = \mu N R \cdot \theta$$ ### Step 1 — Solve for braking force $$\mu N = \frac{I \omega^2}{2 R \theta}$$ ### Step 2 — Express required normal reaction $$N = \frac{I \omega^2}{2 \mu R \theta}$$ ### Step 3 — Express the rate of heat dissipation (power) $$P = T_{brake} \cdot \omega = \mu N R \omega$$ This power (in **Watts**) must be less than the **rated heat dissipation capacity** of the drum material to prevent thermal failure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Numerical Pattern:** A typical 14-mark problem gives $I$, $\omega$, $R$, $\theta$, and $\mu$ and asks for the required braking force and the heat to be dissipated. The total energy dissipated is the kinetic energy, but the **rate** depends on the braking time. ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** A single-block pivoted drum brake has the following data: - Drum radius $R = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.3 \text{ m}$ - Lever arm $a = 500 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \text{ m}$ - Distance of pivot from drum center horizontally $b = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$ - Distance of line of action of $N$ below pivot $c = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$ - Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.3$ - Applied force $F = 500 \text{ N}$ **Find:** 1. Normal reaction $N$ 2. Braking torque $T_{brake}$ 3. Whether the brake is self-energizing for the given drum rotation ### Solution **Step 1 — Identify the case** The lever pivots at the top, block below, drum below the block. We need to determine which formula applies based on drum rotation direction. We assume **drum CW** (self-de-energizing case for this geometry): $$F \cdot a = N \cdot b + \mu N \cdot c$$ **Step 2 — Substitute values** $$500 \times 0.5 = N \times 0.25 + (0.3) \times N \times 0.1$$ $$250 = N (0.25 + 0.03) = 0.28 N$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $N$** $$N = \frac{250}{0.28} = 892.86 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4 — Compute braking torque** $$T_{brake} = \mu N R = 0.3 \times 892.86 \times 0.3 = 80.36 \text{ N} \cdot \text{m}$$ **Step 5 — Check self-energization condition** $$\mu c = 0.3 \times 0.1 = 0.03$$ $$b = 0.25$$ $$\mu c < b \implies \text{Not self-locking}$$ For CW rotation, this is the **self-de-energizing** case (friction opposes $F$). If drum rotates CCW (reversing the friction direction), the equation becomes: $$F \cdot a = N \cdot b - \mu N \cdot c$$ $$N = \frac{250}{0.22} = 1136.36 \text{ N}$$ $$T_{brake} = 0.3 \times 1136.36 \times 0.3 = 102.27 \text{ N} \cdot \text{m}$$ **Comparison:** For the *same* applied force, the CCW rotation produces more braking torque (102.27 vs 80.36 N·m) — this is the **self-energizing direction**. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Drum Brake Force Analysis Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Identify Drum Brake Type] --> B{Single or Multi-Block?} B -->|Single| C{Pivoted or Not?} B -->|Multi| D{Symmetric Internal or External?} C -->|Not Pivoted| E[Simple Block
F a = N b] C -->|Pivoted| F{Drum Rotation
Direction?} F -->|CW| G[De-energizing Case
F a = N b + mu N c] F -->|CCW| H[Self-energizing Case
F a = N b - mu N c] D -->|Internal Expanding| I[Shoe 1 Self-energizing
Shoe 2 De-energizing] D -->|External Contracting| J[Shoe 1 De-energizing
Shoe 2 Self-energizing] I --> K[Compute N1 and N2
Use ratio T1/T2 formula] J --> L[Compute N1 and N2
Use ratio T1/T2 formula] G --> M{Check Self-Lock:
mu c >= b?} H --> M M -->|Yes| N[WARNING: Self-Locking Brake
Dangerous in Most Applications] M -->|No| O[Safe Operating Range] K --> P[Compute Braking Torque
T brake = mu N R] L --> P E --> P O --> P N --> P P --> Q[Compute Energy Dissipation
E = T brake times theta] Q --> R[Verify Heat Capacity
P = T brake times omega] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Free-Body Concept Map (Pivoted Block) ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Pivot Point A] -->|Distance a| F[Applied Force F
Downward] P -->|Distance b horizontal| N[Normal Reaction N
Horizontal] P -->|Distance c vertical| FR[Friction Force mu N
Vertical] N -->|Creates CW Moment| M1[N times b] FR -->|Direction depends on rotation| M2[mu N times c] F -->|Creates CCW Moment| M3[F times a] M1 --> SUM[Sum of Moments = 0] M2 --> SUM M3 --> SUM SUM --> OUT{Sign of mu N term?} OUT -->|Positive| DE[De-energizing
F = N times b + mu N c / a] OUT -->|Negative| SE[Self-energizing
F = N times b - mu N c / a] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Force Interaction Flow — Drum Braking System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input: Rotating Drum
with Angular Velocity omega] --> B[Block Presses Against Drum
Normal Reaction N] B --> C[Friction Force mu N develops
at Contact Point] C --> D[Braking Torque T = mu N R
opposes Rotation] D --> E[Drum Decelerates
alpha = T / I] E --> F[Kinetic Energy Converted
E k = half I omega squared] F --> G[Heat Generated in Drum
Q = m c delta T] G --> H{Check Temperature
Rise Limit} H -->|Within Limit| I[Safe Operation] H -->|Exceeds Limit| J[Brake Failure Risk] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note for KTU 2024:** These Mermaid diagrams illustrate the **logical flow of analysis**. In the actual KTU university exam, students are expected to draw **clear, labeled free-body diagrams** of the lever with all force vectors and moment arms. Practice drawing the FBD in your answer scripts — it carries 2-3 marks even before calculations begin. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Q:** Define the term *self-energizing brake* and state the condition under which a single pivoted block brake becomes self-locking. **Model Answer:** > A *self-energizing brake* is one in which the friction force developed at the drum-block interface assists the applied effort in producing the braking action, thereby requiring a smaller applied force for the same braking torque. > > A *self-locking* brake is a special (and dangerous) case where the brake locks automatically even when the applied effort $F = 0$. > > **Condition for self-locking of a single pivoted block brake:** > $$\mu c \geq b$$ > > where $\mu$ = coefficient of friction, $c$ = vertical distance of the line of action of the normal reaction $N$ from the pivot, and $b$ = horizontal distance of the line of action of $N$ from the pivot. > > **Derivation hint:** The required force $F = \dfrac{N(b - \mu c)}{a}$ in the self-energizing case. When $\mu c = b$, the numerator becomes zero, meaning no applied force is needed — the brake is self-locked. If $\mu c > b$, the term $F$ becomes negative (impossible physically), confirming the lockup condition. ### Question A2 **`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`** **Q:** Differentiate between **uniform pressure** and **uniform wear** theories used in the analysis of pivoted block brakes. Which is more realistic in long-term operation? **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Uniform Pressure Theory | Uniform Wear Theory | |--------|------------------------|---------------------| | **Basic assumption** | Pressure $p$ is constant across the contact face | Wear rate $p \cdot v$ is constant (where $v$ is sliding velocity) | | **Mathematical basis** | $p = \text{const}$ | $p \cdot r = \text{const}$ | | **Line of action of $N$** | At the **geometric center** of the contact face | At the **edge** of the contact face (entry side for a pivoted block) | | **Use case** | New brake, short duration | Long-term operational brake | | **Realism in practice** | Less realistic for worn brakes | **More realistic** in long-term service | > The **uniform wear theory** is more realistic in long-term operation because as the block wears, the contact pressure redistributes such that the product $p \cdot v$ (wear rate) tends to remain constant. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) — Pivoted Single Block Brake **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 3 Internal Choice]`** A single-block pivoted brake operates on a drum of diameter **600 mm**. The block is pivoted at one end of the lever. The following data are given: - Distance of the applied force from pivot: $a = 750 \text{ mm}$ - Distance of the pivot from the line of action of normal reaction: $b = 400 \text{ mm}$ - Distance of the line of action of friction force from pivot: $c = 150 \text{ mm}$ - Coefficient of friction between block and drum: $\mu = 0.35$ **Solve the following:** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks If the brake is required to absorb a torque of **2 kN·m**, determine the force $F$ that must be applied at the end of the lever (i) when the drum rotates in such a direction that the brake is *self-energizing*, and (ii) when the rotation is reversed (de-energizing case). **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify the formula** For **self-energizing** case: $$F \cdot a = N \cdot b - \mu N \cdot c \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = \frac{N(b - \mu c)}{a}$$ For **de-energizing** case: $$F \cdot a = N \cdot b + \mu N \cdot c \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = \frac{N(b + \mu c)}{a}$$ **Step 2 — Express $N$ in terms of braking torque** Braking torque on the drum: $$T_{brake} = \mu N R$$ Given $T_{brake} = 2 \text{ kN} \cdot \text{m} = 2000 \text{ N} \cdot \text{m}$, $R = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.3 \text{ m}$: $$N = \frac{T_{brake}}{\mu R} = \frac{2000}{0.35 \times 0.3} = \frac{2000}{0.105} = 19047.62 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $F$ for self-energizing case** $$F_{SE} = \frac{N(b - \mu c)}{a}$$ Compute $\mu c = 0.35 \times 0.15 = 0.0525 \text{ m}$ $$b - \mu c = 0.4 - 0.0525 = 0.3475 \text{ m}$$ $$F_{SE} = \frac{19047.62 \times 0.3475}{0.75} = \frac{6619.05}{0.75} = 8825.4 \text{ N} \approx 8.83 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 4 — Compute $F$ for de-energizing case** $$b + \mu c = 0.4 + 0.0525 = 0.4525 \text{ m}$$ $$F_{DE} = \frac{19047.62 \times 0.4525}{0.75} = \frac{8619.05}{0.75} = 11492.07 \text{ N} \approx 11.49 \text{ kN}$$ **Conclusion:** Less force (8.83 kN) is needed when the brake is self-energizing. The required force roughly **doubles** the de-energizing case value if $\mu c$ approaches $b$. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks If the applied force is limited to **9 kN**, determine the maximum braking torque that can be transmitted in each case. Also, check the self-locking condition. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Find $N$ from $F$ (self-energizing case)** $$N_{SE} = \frac{F \cdot a}{b - \mu c} = \frac{9000 \times 0.75}{0.3475} = \frac{6750}{0.3475} = 19424.46 \text{ N}$$ **Step 2 — Find braking torque (self-energizing)** $$T_{SE} = \mu N R = 0.35 \times 19424.46 \times 0.3 = 2039.57 \text{ N} \cdot \text{m} \approx 2.04 \text{ kN} \cdot \text{m}$$ **Step 3 — Find $N$ from $F$ (de-energizing case)** $$N_{DE} = \frac{F \cdot a}{b + \mu c} = \frac{9000 \times 0.75}{0.4525} = \frac{6750}{0.4525} = 14917.13 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4 — Find braking torque (de-energizing)** $$T_{DE} = \mu N R = 0.35 \times 14917.13 \times 0.3 = 1566.30 \text{ N} \cdot \text{m} \approx 1.57 \text{ kN} \cdot \text{m}$$ **Step 5 — Self-locking check** $$\mu c = 0.0525 \text{ m}, \quad b = 0.4 \text{ m}$$ $$\mu c \ll b \implies \text{Not self-locking}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b) Pitfalls:** > - Students often confuse the two cases and use the same formula for both. **Read the direction of rotation** carefully from the figure. > - The self-locking check is **frequently asked** as a sub-part. A complete answer must explicitly compute $\mu c$ and compare with $b$. > - Marks are deducted for not stating the units of $N$, $F$, and $T$ in the final answer. > - **Common mistake:** Forgetting to convert mm to m before substituting. Always convert all dimensions to a **consistent unit system** (preferably SI). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Expanding Brake Analysis **`[KTU University Exam - June 2023 - Module 3 Internal Choice]`** An internal expanding brake has two pivoted shoes operating on a drum of diameter **400 mm**. The actuating force is applied through a lever. The following data are given for **each shoe**: - Distance of the actuating force from pivot: $a = 300 \text{ mm}$ - Distance of the pivot from the contact point horizontally: $b = 200 \text{ mm}$ - Distance of the line of action of normal reaction from pivot: $c = 50 \text{ mm}$ - Coefficient of friction: $\mu = 0.3$ - Actuating force on each shoe: $F = 600 \text{ N}$ **Solve the following:** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks Determine the normal reactions $N_1$ and $N_2$ on each shoe, and the ratio of braking torques. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify which shoe is self-energizing** In an internal expanding brake with a CW-rotating drum, **shoe 1** (right side) is *self-energizing* and **shoe 2** (left side) is *de-energizing*. **Step 2 — Equation for shoe 1 (self-energizing)** $$F \cdot a = N_1 \cdot b - \mu N_1 \cdot c$$ $$N_1 = \frac{F \cdot a}{b - \mu c} = \frac{600 \times 0.3}{0.2 - (0.3)(0.05)}$$ Compute $\mu c = 0.3 \times 0.05 = 0.015 \text{ m}$ $$N_1 = \frac{180}{0.2 - 0.015} = \frac{180}{0.185} = 972.97 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3 — Equation for shoe 2 (de-energizing)** $$F \cdot a = N_2 \cdot b + \mu N_2 \cdot c$$ $$N_2 = \frac{F \cdot a}{b + \mu c} = \frac{180}{0.2 + 0.015} = \frac{180}{0.215} = 837.21 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4 — Ratio of braking torques** Since $T_1 = \mu N_1 R$ and $T_2 = \mu N_2 R$: $$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{972.97}{837.21} = 1.162$$ **Conclusion:** The self-energizing shoe (shoe 1) carries **16.2% more braking torque** than the de-energizing shoe (shoe 2) for the same applied force. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks Determine the total braking torque and the coefficient of friction required to make the brake self-locking on the de-energizing shoe. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute total braking torque** Total torque: $$T_{total} = T_1 + T_2 = \mu R (N_1 + N_2) = 0.3 \times 0.2 \times (972.97 + 837.21)$$ $$T_{total} = 0.3 \times 0.2 \times 1810.18 = 108.61 \text{ N} \cdot \text{m}$$ **Step 2 — Self-locking condition on de-energizing shoe** For self-locking on the **de-energizing** shoe, the friction term must equal the normal term: $$\mu_{lock} \cdot c = b$$ $$\mu_{lock} = \frac{b}{c} = \frac{0.2}{0.05} = 4.0$$ **Step 3 — Physical interpretation** A coefficient of friction of $\mu = 4.0$ is **physically unrealistic** (typical values are 0.1 to 0.5). Therefore, this brake **cannot self-lock** under normal conditions. The design is **safe** from lockup. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b) Pitfalls:** > - Students often forget to **sum the torques** for total braking torque; many calculate only one shoe's torque. The total is $T_1 + T_2$. > - The self-locking condition $\mu c = b$ must be **derived or stated clearly** — do not just write the answer. > - Always comment on the **practicality** of the self-locking value. If $\mu_{lock} > 1$, the brake is intrinsically safe from lockup. > - Units must be consistent: **convert all mm to m** at the start of the problem. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Drum Force Analysis** - **Definition**: A drum brake uses friction between a rotating drum and a stationary block to absorb or transmit energy. - **Three force types** at contact: $N$ (normal), $\mu N$ (friction), $T_{brake} = \mu N R$ (torque). - **Single block, non-pivoted**: $F \cdot a = N \cdot b$ (no moment from friction). - **Pivoted block, de-energizing**: $F = \dfrac{N(b + \mu c)}{a}$. - **Pivoted block, self-energizing**: $F = \dfrac{N(b - \mu c)}{a}$. - **Self-locking condition**: $\mu c \geq b$ (avoid in design). - **Internal expanding brake**: $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = \dfrac{b + \mu c}{b - \mu c}$. - **External contracting brake**: Similar ratio, but shoes 1 and 2 are reversed (shoe 1 is de-energizing for CW drum). - **Energy absorbed**: $E = T \cdot \theta$ (in Joules). - **Power dissipated as heat**: $P = T \cdot \omega$ (in Watts). - **Kinetic energy to dissipate**: $E_k = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2$. - **Uniform pressure** vs **uniform wear**: line of action of $N$ at center vs edge of block respectively. - **Critical for KTU**: always identify the **direction of drum rotation** before writing the moment equation. - **Critical for KTU**: always check the **self-locking condition** in design problems. - **Common pitfall**: sign of $\mu N$ moment — draw the FBD carefully and label moment arms ($a$, $b$, $c$). - **Real-world application**: automobile drum brakes, crane brakes, elevator safety brakes, winch brakes, flywheel brakes in industrial presses. - **Design rule of thumb**: keep $b > 2 \mu c$ for a comfortable safety margin against self-locking. - **KTU typical 14-mark format**: 7 marks for deriving/computing $N$ and $F$, 7 marks for computing braking torque and self-locking check. - **Numerical tolerance**: KTU accepts answers within $\pm 1\%$ of the exact value due to rounding differences. ---

topics.title: Pivoted Block Brake with Long Shoe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT527 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pivoted Block Brake with Long Shoe

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Pivoted Block Brake** is a friction brake in which a rigid block (called the *shoe*) is mounted on a lever that rotates about a fixed fulcrum (pivot), pressing the shoe against the rim of a rotating drum or wheel to generate a tangential braking force through friction.

A **Long Shoe** is a specific design configuration of a pivoted block brake in which the pivot of the lever is positioned such that **the line of action of the resultant tangential friction force passes directly through the pivot point**. Because of this geometric coincidence, the friction force produces **zero moment** about the pivot, simplifying the moment equilibrium equation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Definition (KTU 2024):**
> *In a pivoted block brake with a long shoe, the tangential braking force (friction force) acts in such a way that its line of action passes through the fulcrum (pivot) of the lever. Consequently, only the normal reaction between the shoe and the drum contributes to the moment about the pivot.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Bicycle Hand-Brake Analogy:**
> Imagine the hand-brake lever of a classic bicycle. You squeeze the lever at the grip (your effort **F**). The lever pivots at the clamp on the handlebar (the **pivot**). At the far end of the lever (or via a cable), the brake shoe squeezes the wheel rim, generating friction that slows the wheel.
>
> - **Effort arm** (L₁) = distance from grip to pivot (long, for mechanical advantage).
> - **Load arm** (L₂) = distance from pivot to shoe (short, so a small hand force creates a large normal force on the rim).
>
> In a **long shoe** design, the lever is laid out so that the friction force at the shoe slides *along* a line that would, if extended, intersect the pivot. Hence the friction force does not "twist" the lever — it only "pushes." In a **short shoe** (e.g., many car drum brakes), the friction force lies below the pivot and *does* twist the lever, either helping (self-energizing) or hindering (self-de-energizing) your effort.

### 1.3 Components & Standard Notation

The following symbols are used throughout the KTU analysis of this topic:

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
|:---:|---|:---:|
| **F** | Applied effort at the lever end | **N (Newton)** |
| **N** | Normal reaction between shoe and drum | **N** |
| **P** | Tangential (friction) braking force = **μN** | **N** |
| **μ** | Coefficient of friction between shoe & drum | dimensionless |
| **r** | Radius of the brake drum | **m** |
| **L₁** | Effort arm — distance from pivot to point of F | **m** |
| **L₂** | Load arm — distance from pivot to line of action of N | **m** |
| **T_b** | Braking torque transmitted to drum | **N·m** |
| **W** | Work done / energy absorbed during braking | **J** |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Coefficient-of-Friction Values (dry contact, rough cast iron):**
> - μ ≈ **0.15 to 0.20** — Cast iron on cast iron, dry
> - μ ≈ **0.25 to 0.40** — Leather / fibre on cast iron
> - μ ≈ **0.30 to 0.50** — Wood on metal
> - μ ≈ **0.40 to 0.60** — Brake lining material on cast iron (typical automotive value)

These values are essential for KTU numerical problems and must be committed to memory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Long Shoe Condition

A pivoted block brake works on the principle of **frictional energy dissipation**. When the brake is applied:

1. The operator applies effort **F** at the free end of the lever.
2. The lever rotates about its pivot, pressing the shoe against the rotating drum.
3. The shoe-drum contact generates a **normal reaction N** perpendicular to the friction surface.
4. Because the drum surface is sliding relative to the shoe, a **friction force P = μN** is mobilized tangentially, opposing the relative motion.
5. This tangential force multiplied by the drum radius gives the **braking torque T_b = P × r = μN × r**.

The defining geometric peculiarity of the **long shoe** is:

> [!NOTE]
> **Long Shoe Geometric Condition:**
> The pivot of the lever is positioned so that the resultant tangential friction force's **line of action** passes through the pivot. This means the friction force has **zero moment arm** about the pivot — it cannot "twist" the lever in either direction.

Mathematically:

$$
M_P^{\,\text{about pivot}} = P \times \underbrace{0}_{\text{zero moment arm}} = 0
$$

This makes the long shoe a **direction-insensitive** brake: the braking effort is the same whether the drum rotates clockwise or counter-clockwise (because friction has no moment to either help or hinder the applied force).

### 2.2 Free-Body Force Analysis

Three force systems act on the lever:

**(a) Applied effort F** — at the free end of the lever, distance L₁ from the pivot, perpendicular to the lever.

**(b) Reaction from the shoe at the drum contact** — decomposed into:
- **Normal component N** — along the radius at the contact point, distance L₂ from the pivot.
- **Tangential component P = μN** — along the tangent at the contact point.
- *Long-shoe condition:* the line of action of P passes through the pivot → its moment = 0.

**(c) Pivot reaction R** — at the pivot, with no moment contribution (zero arm).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Pivoted Block Brake with Long Shoe**

| # | Formula | Description | Notes / Assumptions |
|:--:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $\sum M_{\text{pivot}} = 0 \Rightarrow F \cdot L_1 = N \cdot L_2$ | Moment equilibrium about pivot | Friction moment = 0 (long-shoe) |
| 2 | $N = F \cdot \dfrac{L_1}{L_2}$ | Normal reaction in terms of effort F | Mechanical advantage = L₁/L₂ |
| 3 | $P = \mu N = \mu F \dfrac{L_1}{L_2}$ | Tangential friction (braking) force | Direction opposes rotation |
| 4 | $T_b = P \cdot r = \mu F \dfrac{L_1 \, r}{L_2}$ | Braking torque absorbed by drum | Main KTU answer for "braking torque" |
| 5 | $T_b = \mu N r$ | Braking torque in terms of N | Useful when N is given |
| 6 | $F = \dfrac{T_b \, L_2}{\mu \, L_1 \, r}$ | Required effort for a desired T_b | Inverse design formula |
| 7 | $\dfrac{L_1}{L_2} = \dfrac{N}{F}$ | Mechanical advantage of the lever | Always > 1 for force amplification |
| 8 | Direction of rotation | **Does NOT affect** the moment equation | Unique feature of long shoe |

### 2.4 Why the Long Shoe Matters in Engineering

| Engineering Field | Application of Pivoted Long-Shoe Brake |
|---|---|
| **Cranes & Hoists** | Load-holding brakes on hoisting drums — direction-independent operation is critical as the load can drive the drum in either sense. |
| **Elevators** | Emergency and holding brakes on traction sheaves. |
| **Conveyor Systems** | Stopping belt conveyors carrying bi-directional loads. |
| **Heavy Machinery** | Where a simple, robust, unidirectional-design brake is required. |
| **Machine Tools** | Spindle stop brakes on lathes and milling machines. |

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Long Shoe" is preferred in practice:**
> 1. The effort F required is **independent of the direction of drum rotation** — safer for bi-directional drives.
> 2. **No self-de-energizing failure mode** — the brake cannot "release itself" due to drum spin direction (unlike some short-shoe designs).
> 3. Mathematical analysis is simpler — friction force drops out of the moment equation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Moment Equilibrium Equation

We derive the relation between applied effort **F** and normal reaction **N** for the long-shoe configuration, starting from first principles.

**Step 1 — Identify the system and the pivot.**

Consider a rigid lever pivoted at point O (the fulcrum). The lever is horizontal.
- The applied effort **F** acts at end A, a perpendicular distance **L₁** from O.
- The shoe is attached at end C, a perpendicular distance **L₂** from O.
- The brake drum of radius **r** rotates about its axis and contacts the shoe at C.

$$
\text{Pivot} = O, \quad OA = L_1, \quad OC = L_2
$$

**Step 2 — Identify all forces on the lever.**

The forces acting on the lever are:

$$
\{\,F,\; N,\; P = \mu N,\; R_O\,\}
$$

where:
- $F$ — applied effort at A (perpendicular to the lever),
- $N$ — normal reaction at C, perpendicular to the friction surface,
- $P = \mu N$ — friction force at C, tangent to the drum surface,
- $R_O$ — reaction at the pivot O (no moment about O).

**Step 3 — Apply the long-shoe geometric condition.**

By definition of a long shoe, the **line of action of P passes through the pivot O**.

$$
\therefore \quad \text{Moment arm of } P \text{ about } O = 0
$$

Hence:

$$
M_P^{\,\text{about } O} = P \cdot 0 = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Sum moments about O for static equilibrium.**

The applied effort F tends to rotate the lever in one direction (say, clockwise), and the normal reaction N tends to rotate it in the opposite direction (counter-clockwise). For equilibrium:

$$
\sum M_O = 0
$$

$$
F \cdot L_1 \;-\; N \cdot L_2 \;-\; P \cdot 0 = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify using the long-shoe condition.**

The $P \cdot 0$ term vanishes:

$$
F \cdot L_1 - N \cdot L_2 = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\,F \cdot L_1 = N \cdot L_2\,}
$$

This is the **fundamental moment equation** for a long-shoe pivoted block brake.

**Step 6 — Solve for N in terms of F.**

$$
N = F \cdot \dfrac{L_1}{L_2}
$$

The factor $\dfrac{L_1}{L_2}$ is the **mechanical advantage** of the lever. Since in practice $L_1 > L_2$, a modest effort F can generate a much larger normal reaction N — this is why hand-operated brakes work.

**Step 7 — Express the tangential friction force P.**

By the Coulomb law of dry friction:

$$
P = \mu N = \mu F \cdot \dfrac{L_1}{L_2}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the braking torque.**

The tangential force P acts at the drum surface, a distance r from the drum axis. The braking torque is therefore:

$$
T_b = P \cdot r = \mu N \cdot r = \mu F \cdot \dfrac{L_1 \, r}{L_2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_b = \dfrac{\mu \, F \, L_1 \, r}{L_2}\,}
$$

**Step 9 — Cross-check dimensions.**

$$
[T_b] = \frac{(\text{dimensionless})(\text{N})(\text{m})(\text{m})}{(\text{m})} = \text{N·m} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

### 3.2 Inverse Design — Required Effort for a Given Braking Torque

Sometimes we know the braking torque we need (e.g., to stop a known load) and wish to find the operating effort F.

From Step 8:

$$
T_b = \dfrac{\mu \, F \, L_1 \, r}{L_2}
$$

Solving for F:

$$
F = \dfrac{T_b \, L_2}{\mu \, L_1 \, r}
$$

This is the design equation for sizing the operating handle or pedal force of the brake.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem statement:**
A pivoted block brake with a long shoe has:
- Effort arm $L_1 = 400$ mm
- Load arm $L_2 = 100$ mm
- Drum radius $r = 200$ mm
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.3$
- Applied effort $F = 50$ N

**Find:** (i) Normal reaction N, (ii) Tangential braking force P, (iii) Braking torque $T_b$.

**Solution:**

**(i) Normal reaction:**

$$
N = F \cdot \dfrac{L_1}{L_2} = 50 \cdot \dfrac{400}{100} = 50 \cdot 4 = 200 \; \text{N}
$$

**(ii) Tangential braking force:**

$$
P = \mu N = 0.3 \times 200 = 60 \; \text{N}
$$

**(iii) Braking torque:**

$$
T_b = P \cdot r = 60 \times 0.2 \; \text{m} = 12 \; \text{N·m}
$$

**Cross-verification using the direct formula:**

$$
T_b = \dfrac{\mu F L_1 r}{L_2} = \dfrac{0.3 \times 50 \times 0.4 \times 0.2}{0.1} = \dfrac{1.2}{0.1} = 12 \; \text{N·m} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

### 3.4 Comparative Matrix — Long Shoe vs Short Shoe

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Comparison for KTU Board Exam:**

| Feature | Long Shoe | Short Shoe |
|---|---|---|
| Line of action of friction force | **Passes through pivot** | Does **not** pass through pivot |
| Friction moment about pivot | **Zero** | Non-zero (has moment arm) |
| Moment equation | $F L_1 = N L_2$ | $F L_1 = N L_2 \pm \mu N a$ |
| Sensitivity to rotation direction | **None** — effort F is the same either way | **Sensitive** — effort differs for CW vs CCW |
| Self-energizing possible? | **No** (cannot happen geometrically) | **Yes** — if pivot is on the friction side |
| Equation complexity | Simple | More complex |
| Preferred when | Bidirectional drives; safety-critical applications | Unidirectional drives where mechanical advantage can be exploited |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

The diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of a Pivoted Block Brake with Long Shoe, showing how the input effort is transformed through the lever-block-drum mechanism into a braking torque.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Effort F]:::inputNode --> B[Lever L1 - Effort Arm]:::leverNode
    B --> C{Pivot O - Fulcrum}:::pivotNode
    C --> D[Lever L2 - Load Arm]:::leverNode
    D --> E[Brake Shoe Block]:::shoeNode
    E --> F[Normal Reaction N - Radial]:::forceNode
    E --> G[Tangential Friction P equals mu N]:::forceNode
    F --> H[Drum Surface Contact]:::drumNode
    G --> H
    H --> I[Braking Torque T_b equals P times r]:::outputNode
    H --> J[Heat Dissipation - Energy Loss]:::heatNode
    I --> K[Decelerates Drum Rotation]:::outputNode

    classDef inputNode fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1
    classDef leverNode fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#BF360C
    classDef pivotNode fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457,stroke-width:3px,color:#880E4F
    classDef shoeNode fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#4A148C
    classDef forceNode fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20
    classDef drumNode fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#37474F,stroke-width:2px,color:#263238
    classDef outputNode fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#B71C1C
    classDef heatNode fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#E65100
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow Description

The diagram above traces the **causal chain** from operator input to braking output:

1. **Input Stage** — The operator applies an effort **F** at the free end of the lever.
2. **Lever Mechanism** — The effort travels along the lever of length **L₁** (effort arm) toward the pivot.
3. **Pivot (Fulcrum)** — The pivot **O** acts as the rotation center; effort moment is amplified through the lever ratio.
4. **Load Arm** — The lever continues from the pivot along the shorter arm **L₂**, now carrying the amplified normal force.
5. **Brake Shoe Contact** — At the end of the load arm, the shoe contacts the rotating drum, generating two reaction components:
   - **Normal Reaction N** — perpendicular to the friction surface, pushing the shoe away from the drum.
   - **Friction Force P = μN** — tangent to the drum, opposing drum rotation.
6. **Drum Interface** — At the contact zone, kinetic energy is converted to heat.
7. **Output** — A braking torque **T_b = P × r** is delivered to the drum, decelerating its rotation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Aid for the Diagram:**
> - **Blue node** → Operator's input (effort).
> - **Orange nodes** → Mechanical lever components.
> - **Pink node (pivot)** → Mechanical fulcrum, the rotation center of the lever.
> - **Green nodes** → Forces generated at the contact.
> - **Grey node** → Drum (the rotating element being braked).
> - **Red nodes** → Output (braking torque and rotational deceleration).
> - **Yellow node** → Energy side-effect (heat — kinetic energy is dissipated as thermal energy).

### 4.3 Equivalent Free-Body Representation (Textual Schematic)

For clarity during exam-time sketching, the canonical free-body of the lever is:

$$
\underbrace{F \downarrow}_{\text{at A, distance } L_1} \;\;\; \underbrace{\;\longleftarrow\; O \;\longrightarrow\;}_{\text{Pivot}} \;\;\; \underbrace{\;\uparrow N \;\; \leftarrow P}_{\text{at C, distance } L_2}
$$

where:
- **F** is downward at end A (effort).
- **O** is the pivot (fulcrum).
- **N** is upward at C (normal reaction from drum on shoe).
- **P = μN** is horizontal at C (friction, direction set by drum rotation).
- The lever itself is a rigid horizontal member.

> [!NOTE]
> **For the long shoe, the line of P (horizontal) passes through O**, hence the friction force has no moment about O. If you ever see this condition in an exam figure, immediately recognize it as a **long-shoe problem** and use the simplified moment equation $F L_1 = N L_2$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
Define a **long shoe** in the context of a pivoted block brake. State the moment equilibrium equation that governs it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **long shoe** in a pivoted block brake is one in which the brake block (shoe) is mounted on a lever such that the **line of action of the tangential friction force passes through the pivot** of the lever.

Because of this geometric arrangement, the friction force creates **no moment** about the pivot. Only the normal reaction at the shoe-drum contact contributes to the moment balance.

The governing moment equilibrium equation about the pivot is:

$$
F \cdot L_1 = N \cdot L_2
$$

where $F$ is the applied effort, $L_1$ the effort arm, $N$ the normal reaction, and $L_2$ the load arm.

*(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — explanation of friction moment = 0; 1 mark — moment equation)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
Why is the braking effort **F** required in a long-shoe pivoted block brake **independent of the direction of rotation** of the drum? Justify with one sentence of physics reasoning.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a long-shoe brake, the **friction force passes through the pivot** and therefore exerts **zero moment** about the pivot irrespective of its direction (clockwise or anti-clockwise tangential action).

Since the friction force contributes nothing to the moment equilibrium equation, reversing the direction of drum rotation only reverses the direction of the friction force — but not its moment (which is zero in both cases). Consequently, the required operating effort **F** is the **same magnitude** for both rotational directions of the drum.

*(2 marks — physical reasoning; 1 mark — conclusion)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3 — Choice A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** [7 marks] A pivoted block brake with a long shoe has the following specifications:
- Effort applied at the free end of the lever, $F = 80$ N (perpendicular to lever).
- Effort arm, $L_1 = 500$ mm.
- Load arm, $L_2 = 125$ mm.
- Coefficient of friction between shoe and drum, $\mu = 0.35$.
- Drum radius, $r = 250$ mm.

Calculate:
1. The normal reaction $N$ at the shoe-drum interface.
2. The tangential braking (friction) force $P$.
3. The braking torque $T_b$ transmitted to the drum.

**(b)** [7 marks] If the coefficient of friction drops to $\mu = 0.25$ due to wear of the shoe lining (all other parameters unchanged), recalculate the braking torque and comment on the **percentage loss** in braking effectiveness.

---

**Model Solution — Choice A:**

### Part (a) — Braking Torque Calculation

**Step 1 — Normal reaction $N$:**

$$
N = F \cdot \dfrac{L_1}{L_2} = 80 \times \dfrac{500}{125} = 80 \times 4 = 320 \; \text{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Tangential braking force $P$:**

$$
P = \mu N = 0.35 \times 320 = 112 \; \text{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Braking torque $T_b$:**

$$
T_b = P \cdot r = 112 \times 0.250 \; \text{m} = 28 \; \text{N·m}
$$

**Cross-check using direct formula:**

$$
T_b = \dfrac{\mu F L_1 r}{L_2} = \dfrac{0.35 \times 80 \times 0.500 \times 0.250}{0.125} = \dfrac{3.5}{0.125} = 28 \; \text{N·m} \quad \checkmark
$$

### Part (b) — Effect of Worn Shoe Lining ($\mu = 0.25$)

**Step 4 — New normal reaction** (depends only on F, L₁, L₂ — unchanged):

$$
N = 320 \; \text{N} \quad (\text{same as before})
$$

**Step 5 — New tangential force:**

$$
P_{\text{new}} = \mu_{\text{new}} N = 0.25 \times 320 = 80 \; \text{N}
$$

**Step 6 — New braking torque:**

$$
T_{b,\text{new}} = P_{\text{new}} \cdot r = 80 \times 0.250 = 20 \; \text{N·m}
$$

**Step 7 — Percentage loss in braking torque:**

$$
\% \text{ Loss} = \dfrac{T_b - T_{b,\text{new}}}{T_b} \times 100 = \dfrac{28 - 20}{28} \times 100 = \dfrac{8}{28} \times 100 \approx 28.57 \%
$$

**Comment:** A drop in $\mu$ from $0.35$ to $0.25$ (about $28.6\%$ drop) produces an equal percentage drop in braking torque. This shows that **braking torque is directly proportional to the coefficient of friction** in a long-shoe pivoted block brake, and **regular inspection of shoe lining condition is critical** for braking reliability.

---

**Q4 — Choice B. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3 — Alternative]**

**(a)** [7 marks] **Derive** the expression for the braking torque of a pivoted block brake with a long shoe, starting from the moment equilibrium of the lever about its pivot. Clearly state the long-shoe geometric condition you are using.

**(b)** [7 marks] A pivoted block brake with a long shoe is required to deliver a braking torque of $T_b = 180$ N·m on a drum of radius $r = 300$ mm. The coefficient of friction is $\mu = 0.4$. The lever has an effort arm of $L_1 = 600$ mm and a load arm of $L_2 = 150$ mm.

Calculate the required operating effort $F$. Also calculate the **mechanical advantage** of the lever.

---

**Model Solution — Choice B:**

### Part (a) — Derivation

**Assumptions:**
- The brake shoe is rigid and pivoted at one end of the lever.
- The contact pressure is uniform across the contact area (uniform-pressure assumption).
- The line of action of the tangential friction force passes through the pivot (long-shoe condition).
- The drum rotates at a constant angular speed during the analysis instant.

**Free-body diagram of the lever:**
- Effort $F$ at A, distance $L_1$ from pivot.
- Normal reaction $N$ and friction $P = \mu N$ at C, distance $L_2$ from pivot.
- Pivot reaction at O.

**Moment equilibrium about O:**

$$
\sum M_O = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; F \cdot L_1 - N \cdot L_2 - P \cdot 0 = 0
$$

(Long-shoe condition: friction's moment arm = 0.)

$$
\Rightarrow \quad F \cdot L_1 = N \cdot L_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = F \cdot \dfrac{L_1}{L_2}
$$

**Friction force:**

$$
P = \mu N = \mu F \cdot \dfrac{L_1}{L_2}
$$

**Braking torque on drum:**

$$
\boxed{T_b = P \cdot r = \dfrac{\mu \, F \, L_1 \, r}{L_2}}
$$

### Part (b) — Required Operating Effort

**Given:** $T_b = 180$ N·m, $r = 0.300$ m, $\mu = 0.4$, $L_1 = 0.600$ m, $L_2 = 0.150$ m.

**Step 1 — Required normal reaction $N$:**

$$
N = \dfrac{T_b}{\mu \, r} = \dfrac{180}{0.4 \times 0.300} = \dfrac{180}{0.12} = 1500 \; \text{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Required operating effort $F$:**

$$
F = \dfrac{N \, L_2}{L_1} = \dfrac{1500 \times 0.150}{0.600} = \dfrac{225}{0.600} = 375 \; \text{N}
$$

**Cross-check using inverse formula:**

$$
F = \dfrac{T_b \, L_2}{\mu \, L_1 \, r} = \dfrac{180 \times 0.150}{0.4 \times 0.600 \times 0.300} = \dfrac{27}{0.072} = 375 \; \text{N} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3 — Mechanical advantage (MA):**

$$
\text{MA} = \dfrac{L_1}{L_2} = \dfrac{0.600}{0.150} = 4
$$

This means the lever multiplies the operator's hand effort by a factor of 4 to produce the normal reaction on the drum.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS — READ CAREFULLY**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to m.** If $r = 250$ mm and you compute $T_b = P \times r$ in mm, your answer will be 1000× too large (or too small, depending on convention). **Always write $r = 0.250$ m in the formula.**
>
> 2. **Mixing up $L_1$ and $L_2$.** $L_1$ is the arm where $F$ is applied (effort arm); $L_2$ is the arm where $N$ acts (load arm). Reversing them gives the reciprocal of the correct answer.
>
> 3. **Including a friction moment in a long-shoe problem.** The defining feature of the long shoe is that the friction force's moment about the pivot is **zero**. Adding a $\pm \mu N \cdot c$ term is the hallmark of a **short-shoe** problem and will be marked wrong here.
>
> 4. **Using the wrong direction for $P$.** Although $P$ does not appear in the moment equation for a long shoe, it still appears in the braking-torque formula $T_b = P \cdot r$ as a **scalar magnitude**. The direction is implicit in "braking" — it always opposes drum rotation.
>
> 5. **Skipping units in the final answer.** KTU examiners deduct marks if the final answer is just a number without the unit (N, N·m, m, etc.). Always write the unit explicitly.
>
> 6. **Confusing "uniform pressure" with "uniform wear" assumptions.** Long-shoe analysis uses the **uniform-pressure** assumption. If a problem statement says "uniform wear," the resultant normal force does NOT pass through the geometric centre of the contact area, and a different formula applies.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — Pivoted Block Brake with Long Shoe**

- [ ] **Definition:** A *long shoe* has its pivot placed so that the friction force's line of action passes through the pivot ⇒ friction moment = 0.
- [ ] **Core Equation:** $\;F L_1 = N L_2\;$ — this is the **only** moment equation you need for long-shoe problems.
- [ ] **Normal Reaction:** $\;N = F \cdot (L_1/L_2)\;$ — lever multiplies the effort.
- [ ] **Tangential Force:** $\;P = \mu N\;$ — Coulomb's law of dry friction.
- [ ] **Braking Torque:** $\;T_b = P \cdot r = \mu F L_1 r / L_2\;$ — main answer for "braking torque" questions.
- [ ] **Mechanical Advantage:** $\;\text{MA} = L_1/L_2 > 1\;$ for force amplification.
- [ ] **Inverse Design:** $\;F = T_b L_2 / (\mu L_1 r)\;$ — given $T_b$, find $F$.
- [ ] **Direction Insensitivity:** Effort $F$ is the **same** for both directions of drum rotation (a key advantage over short shoe).
- [ ] **No Self-Energizing:** Long-shoe brakes cannot be self-energizing because friction has no moment.
- [ ] **Units Discipline:** Always convert mm → m before substitution; always quote final answer with unit (N, N·m).
- [ ] **Standard μ values:** Cast iron on cast iron ≈ 0.15–0.20; brake lining ≈ 0.4–0.5; leather ≈ 0.25.
- [ ] **Common exam pattern:** Find $N$ → find $P$ → find $T_b$. These three quantities form the standard 3-step solution.
- [ ] **Comparison with Short Shoe:** If the problem says "line of action of friction does **not** pass through pivot" or mentions "self-energizing," switch to the short-shoe analysis with $F L_1 = N L_2 \pm \mu N c$.
- [ ] **Heat dissipation:** The kinetic energy of the drum is converted to heat at the shoe-drum interface — always mention this in design-oriented questions.
- [ ] **Application Areas:** Cranes, hoists, elevators, conveyors — any bi-directional drive where direction-independent braking is essential.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Band Brakes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT527 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Band Brakes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Band Brake (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **band brake** is a friction brake in which a flexible band (made of leather, fabric, or a metal strip lined with friction material) is wrapped partially around a rotating drum or pulley. The band is tightened by means of a lever mechanism, and the resulting frictional contact between the band and the drum generates a **braking torque** that resists or arrests the rotation of the shaft.

The braking action is governed by the classical **belt friction equation**:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$$

where:
- **T₁** = Tension on the **tight side** (N)
- **T₂** = Tension on the **slack side** (N)
- **μ** = Coefficient of friction between the band and the drum
- **θ** = Angle of wrap of the band around the drum (radians)

The **braking torque** developed is:

$$M_t = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot r$$

where **r** is the effective radius of the brake drum (m).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bicycle Rim Brake:**
> Imagine pressing the brake lever of an old-style bicycle. A metal "band" (the brake shoe and cable assembly) clamps onto the rim of the wheel. The harder you pull the lever, the tighter the band grips the rim, and the more friction is generated. The rotating wheel experiences a retarding torque and slows down. A band brake in machinery works on **exactly the same principle** — except the band is a continuous loop wrapped around the drum, and the drum is much larger, transmitting significant torque.

## 1.3 Classification of Band Brakes

Band brakes are broadly classified based on **band geometry** and **direction of actuation**:

| Sl. No. | Type | Description | Common Application |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Simple Band Brake** | Single lever applies tension to one end of the band; the other end is fixed to a pin. | Cranes, winches, hoists |
| 2 | **Differential Band Brake** | A single lever is pivoted between two fixed band ends. The fulcrum position differentiates the lever arms. | Heavy-duty industrial machinery |
| 3 | **Expanding Band Brake** | The band is mounted *inside* the drum and forced outward by levers to engage the drum surface. | Automotive drum brakes (internal type) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 3):**
> The syllabus explicitly focuses on **simple and differential band brakes**, with emphasis on **self-energizing** and **self-de-energizing** conditions, derivation of braking torque, and determination of maximum braking capacity.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Coefficient of friction (μ) for leather-metal contact ≈ **0.3 – 0.5**
- Coefficient of friction for asbestos-metal ≈ **0.25 – 0.4**
- Angle of wrap (θ) is typically between **π (180°)** and **2.5π (450°)** depending on drum geometry
- Maximum recommended peripheral velocity ≈ **30 m/s** for leather-lined bands
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The operating cycle of a band brake can be decomposed into the following structured steps:

1. **Lever Actuation:** The operator applies a force **P** at the free end of the lever pivoted at a fulcrum point.
2. **Band Tightening:** The lever pulls one end of the band, creating tension in it.
3. **Friction Engagement:** The band presses against the rotating drum due to this tension.
4. **Friction Force Generation:** Frictional forces develop at every elemental contact point along the wrap angle, resisting drum rotation.
5. **Tension Imbalance:** Because friction resists the band's slip relative to the drum, the tension is higher on the side where the band *enters* the contact (tight side, **T₁**) and lower on the side where it *leaves* the contact (slack side, **T₂**).
6. **Net Braking Torque:** The difference **(T₁ − T₂)** acts at radius **r**, producing the braking torque **Mₜ = (T₁ − T₂)r**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core "Why" Behind the Equation:**
> The exponential relationship **T₁/T₂ = e^(μθ)** arises from integrating the differential friction force over an infinitesimal arc length **ds = r·dθ**. The exponential *grows* with wrap angle θ and friction μ — meaning **more wrap = exponentially more braking capacity**.

## 2.2 Force Analysis — Simple Band Brake

For a **simple band brake** with a single lever of length **l**, lever pivot at distance **a** from the fixed end, and effort force **P** at the free end:

**Lever Equilibrium (taking moments about the pivot):**

$$P \cdot l = T_2 \cdot a$$

Solving for T₂:

$$T_2 = \frac{P \cdot l}{a}$$

The tight-side tension from the belt friction equation:

$$T_1 = T_2 \cdot e^{\mu \theta} = \frac{P \cdot l}{a} \cdot e^{\mu \theta}$$

**Braking Torque:**

$$M_t = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot r = \frac{P \cdot l}{a}(e^{\mu \theta} - 1) \cdot r$$

## 2.3 Force Analysis — Differential Band Brake

In a differential band brake, the lever is pivoted at a point **O** between two fixed band ends at distances **a** and **b** from the fulcrum:

**Lever Moment Equilibrium:**

$$P \cdot l + T_2 \cdot b = T_1 \cdot a$$

Rearranging for P:

$$P = \frac{T_1 \cdot a - T_2 \cdot b}{l}$$

Using **T₁ = T₂·e^(μθ)**:

$$P = \frac{T_2 \cdot a \cdot e^{\mu \theta} - T_2 \cdot b}{l} = \frac{T_2}{l}(a \cdot e^{\mu \theta} - b)$$

**Braking Torque:**

$$M_t = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot r = T_2(e^{\mu \theta} - 1) \cdot r$$

## 2.4 Self-Energizing and Self-De-Energizing Conditions

> [!CAUTION]
> **This is a KTU examiner favourite — almost every board exam includes this concept.**

In a **differential band brake**, the braking action itself can either **assist** or **resist** the applied effort:

### Self-Energizing Condition
The braking torque creates a moment about the fulcrum that **reduces** the effort required.

$$T_1 \cdot a > T_2 \cdot b$$

Since **T₁ = T₂·e^(μθ)**:

$$T_2 \cdot e^{\mu \theta} \cdot a > T_2 \cdot b$$

$$e^{\mu \theta} > \frac{b}{a} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{T_1}{T_2} > \frac{b}{a}$$

> If this condition is satisfied, **P becomes small or even negative** — the brake holds *itself* engaged.

### Self-De-Energizing Condition
The braking torque creates a moment that **opposes** engagement, requiring greater effort.

$$T_1 \cdot a < T_2 \cdot b \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{T_1}{T_2} < \frac{b}{a}$$

### Neutral / Indifferent Condition

$$T_1 \cdot a = T_2 \cdot b \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{b}{a}$$

## 2.5 Maximum Braking Capacity

For a **simple band brake**, when the tight-side tension **T₁** equals the maximum permissible value **T_max** (limited by band material strength):

$$T_{max} = T_2 \cdot e^{\mu \theta} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_2 = \frac{T_{max}}{e^{\mu \theta}}$$

Maximum braking torque:

$$(M_t)_{max} = T_{max} \cdot r \left(1 - \frac{1}{e^{\mu \theta}}\right) = T_{max} \cdot r \left(1 - e^{-\mu \theta}\right)$$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Sl. No. | Formula | Description | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $T_1 / T_2 = e^{\mu \theta}$ | Belt friction (Euler's) equation | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $M_t = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot r$ | Braking torque | N·m |
| 3 | $T_2 = P \cdot l / a$ | Slack-side tension (simple brake) | N |
| 4 | $T_1 = T_2 \cdot e^{\mu \theta}$ | Tight-side tension | N |
| 5 | $M_t = (P \cdot l / a)(e^{\mu \theta} - 1) \cdot r$ | Simple brake torque | N·m |
| 6 | $P = (T_2 / l)(a \cdot e^{\mu \theta} - b)$ | Effort for differential brake | N |
| 7 | $M_t = T_2(e^{\mu \theta} - 1) \cdot r$ | Differential brake torque | N |
| 8 | Self-energizing: $a \cdot e^{\mu \theta} > b$ | Condition for self-energizing | Inequality |
| 9 | Self-de-energizing: $a \cdot e^{\mu \theta} < b$ | Condition for self-de-energizing | Inequality |
| 10 | $(M_t)_{max} = T_{max} \cdot r(1 - e^{-\mu \theta})$ | Max braking torque | N·m |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cranes & Hoists:** Band brakes provide fail-safe braking under heavy suspended loads.
- **Elevators:** Differential band brakes act as a safety mechanism during power failure.
- **Agricultural Machinery:** Tractors and harvesters use band brakes for high-torque stopping.
- **Mining Conveyors:** Heavy-duty band brakes handle enormous kinetic energies.
- **Locomotive Hand-brakes:** Manual band brakes serve as parking brakes in railway systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Belt Friction Equation (T₁/T₂ = e^(μθ))

Consider an elemental arc of the band wrapped around the drum, subtending angle **dθ** at the centre.

**Step 1:** Let the tension on the two sides of the elemental arc be **T** and **T + dT** (the tension increases from the slack to the tight side due to friction).

**Step 2:** The normal reaction on the elemental arc is:

$$dN = T \cdot d\theta$$

> **Reasoning:** The two tensions T and (T + dT) act tangentially. Their resultant in the radial direction is balanced by the normal reaction dN. Resolving tangentially and using small-angle approximation: **dN ≈ T·dθ**.

**Step 3:** The frictional force on the elemental arc is:

$$dF = \mu \cdot dN = \mu \cdot T \cdot d\theta$$

**Step 4:** For equilibrium of the elemental arc in the tangential direction:

$$(T + dT) - T = dF = \mu \cdot T \cdot d\theta$$

$$dT = \mu \cdot T \cdot d\theta$$

**Step 5:** Separate variables:

$$\frac{dT}{T} = \mu \cdot d\theta$$

**Step 6:** Integrate between limits: when θ varies from 0 to θ, T varies from T₂ to T₁:

$$\int_{T_2}^{T_1} \frac{dT}{T} = \mu \int_{0}^{\theta} d\theta$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right) = \mu \theta$$

**Step 7:** Exponentiating both sides:

$$\boxed{\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Braking Torque for Simple Band Brake

**Given:** Effort **P**, lever length **l**, distance from pivot to fixed band end = **a**, drum radius = **r**, friction coefficient = **μ**, wrap angle = **θ**, drum rotates in the direction that tightens the band.

**Step 1:** Apply lever moment equilibrium about the pivot. The fixed end carries slack tension T₂ (since the band pulls the lever *down* on that side when drum rotates to tighten the other side):

$$P \cdot l = T_2 \cdot a$$

> **Reasoning:** The applied effort P at distance l creates a clockwise moment; the band tension T₂ at distance a creates a counter-clockwise moment. For static equilibrium, these moments balance.

**Step 2:** Solve for T₂:

$$T_2 = \frac{P \cdot l}{a}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the belt friction equation to get T₁:

$$T_1 = T_2 \cdot e^{\mu \theta} = \frac{P \cdot l}{a} \cdot e^{\mu \theta}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the net tangential force on the drum = (T₁ − T₂):

$$T_1 - T_2 = \frac{P \cdot l}{a}(e^{\mu \theta} - 1)$$

**Step 5:** Compute the braking torque:

$$\boxed{M_t = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot r = \frac{P \cdot l}{a}(e^{\mu \theta} - 1) \cdot r}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Self-Energizing Condition for Differential Band Brake

**Step 1:** In a differential brake, the lever moment equation is:

$$P \cdot l + T_2 \cdot b = T_1 \cdot a$$

> **Reasoning:** The friction torque at the drum tends to rotate the band. Depending on drum rotation direction, the band pulls the lever either to *assist* or *oppose* the applied effort P.

**Step 2:** Substitute T₁ = T₂·e^(μθ):

$$P \cdot l + T_2 \cdot b = T_2 \cdot e^{\mu \theta} \cdot a$$

$$P \cdot l = T_2(a \cdot e^{\mu \theta} - b)$$

**Step 3:** For the brake to be **self-energizing**, the term in parentheses must be positive AND T₂ must be greater than zero:

$$a \cdot e^{\mu \theta} - b > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a \cdot e^{\mu \theta} > b$$

**Step 4:** This gives the **self-energizing ratio condition**:

$$\boxed{\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta} > \frac{b}{a}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning:** If the lever arm **a** (associated with T₁) is long enough, OR if the wrap angle is large enough (e^(μθ) is large), then the friction-generated moment on the T₁ side *exceeds* the counter-moment on the T₂ side. The brake "locks itself" — even with P = 0, friction keeps the brake engaged.

**Step 5:** Alternatively, from the lever equation:

$$P = \frac{T_2(a \cdot e^{\mu \theta} - b)}{l}$$

If **a·e^(μθ) > b**, then P can be **zero or negative** (negative P means the brake is *self-locking* — P must act *upward* to release the brake).

## 3.4 Numerical Solved Example (Worked Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** A simple band brake has a lever of length 500 mm, with the band attached 100 mm from the pivot. An effort of 50 N is applied at the lever end. The brake drum has a diameter of 300 mm. The coefficient of friction is 0.3 and the angle of wrap is 270°. Find the braking torque.

**Given Data:**
- Lever length: l = 500 mm = 0.5 m
- Distance from pivot: a = 100 mm = 0.1 m
- Effort: P = 50 N
- Drum diameter: d = 300 mm → r = 0.15 m
- Coefficient of friction: μ = 0.3
- Angle of wrap: θ = 270° = (270 × π/180) = 4.712 rad

**Step 1: Compute slack-side tension T₂:**

$$T_2 = \frac{P \cdot l}{a} = \frac{50 \times 0.5}{0.1} = 250 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Compute e^(μθ):**

$$\mu \theta = 0.3 \times 4.712 = 1.4136$$

$$e^{1.4136} = 4.111$$

**Step 3: Compute tight-side tension T₁:**

$$T_1 = T_2 \cdot e^{\mu \theta} = 250 \times 4.111 = 1027.7 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Compute net tangential force:**

$$T_1 - T_2 = 1027.7 - 250 = 777.7 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Compute braking torque:**

$$M_t = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot r = 777.7 \times 0.15 = 116.66 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{M_t \approx 116.66 \text{ N·m}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Simple Band Brake

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeInput[Operator Effort P] --> nodeLever[Brake Lever l]
    nodeLever --> nodePivot[Fulcrum Pivot]
    nodePivot --> nodeBandTight[Tight Side End T1]
    nodePivot --> nodeBandSlack[Slack Side End T2]
    nodeBandTight --> nodeDrumFric[Friction Zone mu theta]
    nodeBandSlack --> nodeDrumFric
    nodeDrumFric --> nodeDrum[Rotating Drum Radius r]
    nodeDrum --> nodeTorque[Braking Torque Mt]
    nodeBandTight --> nodeCalcT1[T1 equals T2 times e to power mu theta]
    nodeBandSlack --> nodeCalcT2[T2 equals P times l divided by a]
    nodeCalcT1 --> nodeTorque
    nodeCalcT2 --> nodeTorque
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Force Flow in a Differential Band Brake

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph LEVER[MECHANISM]
        nodeA[Distance a to T1 side]
        nodeB[Distance b to T2 side]
        nodeL[Length l of effort arm]
        nodeP[Applied Effort P]
    end
    subgraph BRAKE[BAND AND DRUM]
```
