```mermaid
        nodeT1A[Tight Side T1]
        nodeT2A[Slack Side T2]
        nodeDrumA[Drum rotating in direction]
    end
    nodeT1A --> nodeA
    nodeT2A --> nodeB
    nodeP --> nodeL
    nodeDrumA --> nodeT1A
    nodeDrumA --> nodeT2A
    nodeA --> nodeCondition{Self Energizing Check}
    nodeB --> nodeCondition
    nodeCondition --> nodeYes[Self Energizing]
    nodeCondition --> nodeNo[Self De Energizing]
    nodeCondition --> nodeNeutral[Neutral Indifferent]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Decision Flow for Self-Energizing Analysis

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart[Given a b mu theta] --> nodeCalc[Compute a times e to power mu theta]
    nodeCalc --> nodeCompare{Is a e power mu theta greater than b}
    nodeCompare --> nodeSE[Yes: Self Energizing Brake]
    nodeCompare --> nodeSDE[No: Self De Energizing Brake]
    nodeCompare --> nodeExact[Equal: Neutral Brake]
    nodeSE --> nodeResultSE[Brake grips itself P may be zero or negative]
    nodeSDE --> nodeResultSDE[Operator must apply larger P]
    nodeExact --> nodeResultNE[P independent of friction]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description (Verbal Schematic)

Since Mermaid cannot accurately render the physical geometry of the band-drum-lever assembly, the following **verbal schematic** is provided:

**Simple Band Brake Configuration:**

- A circular drum of radius **r** rotates anticlockwise about its centre **O**.
- A flexible band wraps around the drum covering arc angle **θ**.
- The **left end** of the band is fixed to a pin at the ground.
- The **right end** of the band is connected to a lever at distance **a** from the lever's pivot.
- The lever has total length **l**, with the effort **P** applied at the far end (downward).
- During drum rotation, the right side becomes the **tight side (T₁)** and the left side becomes the **slack side (T₂)**.

**Differential Band Brake Configuration:**

- Same drum and band geometry.
- The lever pivot **O** is located **between** the two band attachment points.
- The distance from pivot to the **T₁ end** is **a**.
- The distance from pivot to the **T₂ end** is **b**.
- The effort **P** is applied at the lever end of length **l**.
- The relative magnitudes of **a** and **b** determine self-energizing behaviour.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3, Q1a]**

**Q1. Define a band brake. State the belt friction equation used in band brake analysis.**

**Model Answer:**
A band brake is a friction device in which a flexible band lined with friction material is wrapped partially around a rotating drum. Tightening the band generates frictional contact that produces a braking torque.

The governing equation is:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$$

where T₁ and T₂ are the tight and slack side tensions, μ is the coefficient of friction, and θ is the angle of wrap in radians.

> ---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3, Q1b]**

**Q2. What is meant by a self-energizing band brake? Write the condition for the same.**

**Model Answer:**
A **self-energizing brake** is one in which the frictional force developed during braking assists the applied effort, reducing (or even eliminating) the operator force needed to maintain engagement.

**Condition:**

$$T_1 \cdot a > T_2 \cdot b \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad e^{\mu \theta} > \frac{b}{a}$$

When this condition is satisfied, even a small applied force (or zero force) is sufficient to keep the brake engaged.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3, Q2a]**

**(a)** A simple band brake has a lever 400 mm long, pivoted 80 mm from the brake band attachment. An effort of 60 N is applied at the lever end. The brake drum diameter is 250 mm. The coefficient of friction between band and drum is 0.28, and the angle of wrap is 240°. Calculate:
   (i) The braking torque developed.
   (ii) The tight and slack side tensions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the braking torque of a simple band brake. List the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Solution (a):

**Given Data:**
- Lever length: l = 400 mm = 0.4 m
- Distance from pivot: a = 80 mm = 0.08 m
- Effort: P = 60 N
- Drum diameter: d = 250 mm → r = 0.125 m
- Coefficient of friction: μ = 0.28
- Angle of wrap: θ = 240° = 240 × π/180 = 4.189 rad

**Step 1: Slack-side tension T₂:**

$$T_2 = \frac{P \cdot l}{a} = \frac{60 \times 0.4}{0.08} = \frac{24}{0.08} = 300 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Compute e^(μθ):**

$$\mu \theta = 0.28 \times 4.189 = 1.1729$$

$$e^{1.1729} = 3.232$$

**Step 3: Tight-side tension T₁:**

$$T_1 = T_2 \cdot e^{\mu \theta} = 300 \times 3.232 = 969.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Braking torque:**

$$M_t = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot r = (969.6 - 300) \times 0.125 = 669.6 \times 0.125 = 83.7 \text{ N·m}$$

**Results:**
- Slack side tension: **T₂ = 300 N**
- Tight side tension: **T₁ = 969.6 N**
- Braking torque: **Mₜ = 83.7 N·m**

#### Solution (b):

**Derivation of Braking Torque for a Simple Band Brake:**

Refer to **Section 3.2** for the complete step-by-step derivation. The key results are:

$$T_2 = \frac{P \cdot l}{a}, \quad T_1 = T_2 \cdot e^{\mu \theta}, \quad M_t = \frac{P \cdot l}{a}(e^{\mu \theta} - 1) \cdot r$$

**Assumptions:**
1. The band is flexible and conforms perfectly to the drum surface.
2. The coefficient of friction μ is uniform over the entire wrap angle.
3. The drum is rigid and does not deform under load.
4. The tensions T₁ and T₂ are uniformly distributed along the band width.
5. The effect of centrifugal force on the rotating band is negligible (or low peripheral velocity).
6. The pivot friction at the lever fulcrum is neglected.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3, Q2b]**

**(a)** A differential band brake has a lever of length 500 mm. The distances from the fulcrum to the tight-side and slack-side band ends are 200 mm and 100 mm respectively. An effort of 40 N is applied at the lever end. The coefficient of friction is 0.25 and the wrap angle is 180°. The drum diameter is 400 mm. Determine:
   (i) The braking torque.
   (ii) Whether the brake is self-energizing or self-de-energizing. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of self-energizing and self-de-energizing in a differential band brake. What is the design recommendation to avoid brake lock-up? **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Solution (a):

**Given Data:**
- Lever length: l = 500 mm = 0.5 m
- Tight side arm: a = 200 mm = 0.2 m
- Slack side arm: b = 100 mm = 0.1 m
- Effort: P = 40 N
- Coefficient of friction: μ = 0.25
- Wrap angle: θ = 180° = π = 3.1416 rad
- Drum diameter: d = 400 mm → r = 0.2 m

**Step 1: Compute e^(μθ):**

$$\mu \theta = 0.25 \times 3.1416 = 0.7854$$

$$e^{0.7854} = 2.193$$

**Step 2: Slack-side tension T₂ from lever equation:**

$$P = \frac{T_2}{l}(a \cdot e^{\mu \theta} - b)$$

$$40 = \frac{T_2}{0.5}(0.2 \times 2.193 - 0.1)$$

$$40 = \frac{T_2}{0.5}(0.4386 - 0.1) = \frac{T_2}{0.5} \times 0.3386$$

$$40 = T_2 \times 0.6772$$

$$T_2 = \frac{40}{0.6772} = 59.07 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Tight-side tension T₁:**

$$T_1 = T_2 \cdot e^{\mu \theta} = 59.07 \times 2.193 = 129.54 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Braking torque:**

$$M_t = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot r = (129.54 - 59.07) \times 0.2 = 70.47 \times 0.2 = 14.09 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5: Self-energizing check:**

Check the condition: **a·e^(μθ) > b?**

$$a \cdot e^{\mu \theta} = 0.2 \times 2.193 = 0.4386$$

$$b = 0.1$$

Since **0.4386 > 0.1**, the condition is satisfied.

**The brake is SELF-ENERGIZING.**

**Results:**
- Braking torque: **Mₜ ≈ 14.09 N·m**
- Brake type: **Self-Energizing**

#### Solution (b):

**Self-Energizing vs Self-De-Energizing Differential Band Brakes:**

Refer to **Section 2.4** for the detailed theoretical explanation.

**Key Points:**

- **Self-Energizing:** When **a·e^(μθ) > b**, friction assists engagement. Effort P becomes very small (or even zero/negative). Risk of brake lock-up.

- **Self-De-Energizing:** When **a·e^(μθ) < b**, friction opposes engagement. Effort P must be larger. Safer operation.

- **Neutral:** When **a·e^(μθ) = b**, P is independent of friction. Stable but ignores friction benefit.

**Design Recommendations to Avoid Brake Lock-Up:**

1. **Reduce wrap angle θ:** A smaller wrap reduces e^(μθ), reducing self-energization.
2. **Lower friction coefficient μ:** Use lining materials with controlled friction.
3. **Adjust lever geometry:** Make **a < b** deliberately to ensure self-de-energization. Many safety-critical designs use **a slightly less than b/e^(μθ)**.
4. **Add a release spring:** A spring that always pulls the lever *off* the drum prevents lock-up.
5. **Use wear-compensation mechanisms:** As the band wears, geometry changes — compensators prevent runaway self-energization.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1 — Angle Units:** Students often forget to convert degrees to radians. θ must ALWAYS be in radians for the exponential e^(μθ). Losing 1–2 marks here.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2 — Confusing T₁ and T₂:** T₁ (tight side) corresponds to the side where the band *enters* the contact (rotational direction). Mixing these up gives a negative or wrongly-signed result.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3 — Forgetting the e^(μθ) factor:** Some students use T₁ = T₂ directly, missing the exponential relationship entirely. This is the **most common mistake** — losing up to 5 marks.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4 — Direction of Effort P:** For a self-energizing brake with P negative, students forget to state that the brake is "self-locking" — examiners expect this interpretation explicitly.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5 — Lever Equation Sign Errors:** The differential lever equation P·l = T₁·a − T₂·b (or its variants) has sign conventions depending on drum rotation direction. Always draw a free-body diagram and indicate rotation.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Band Brakes (KTU Module 3)**

- ✅ **Definition:** A band brake uses a flexible band wrapped around a drum to generate friction-based braking torque.

- ✅ **Core Equation:** **T₁/T₂ = e^(μθ)** — the belt friction / Euler's equation (always in **radians** for θ).

- ✅ **Braking Torque:** **Mₜ = (T₁ − T₂) × r**

- ✅ **Simple Band Brake Lever Equation:** **T₂ = P·l/a** (when slack side is at the lever).

- ✅ **Differential Band Brake Lever Equation:** **P·l = T₁·a − T₂·b**

- ✅ **Self-Energizing Condition:** **a·e^(μθ) > b** OR equivalently **T₁/T₂ > b/a**.

- ✅ **Self-De-Energizing Condition:** **a·e^(μθ) < b** OR equivalently **T₁/T₂ < b/a**.

- ✅ **Neutral Condition:** **a·e^(μθ) = b** OR **T₁/T₂ = b/a**.

- ✅ **Maximum Braking Torque:** **(Mₜ)ₘₐₓ = Tₘₐₓ · r · (1 − e^(−μθ))**

- ✅ **Typical μ values:** Leather on metal ≈ 0.3–0.5; Asbestos on metal ≈ 0.25–0.4.

- ✅ **Wrap angle θ:** Usually between π and 2.5π (180° to 450°); larger θ gives exponentially more braking.

- ✅ **Assumptions:** Uniform μ, rigid drum, flexible band, negligible centrifugal effects, uniform band width tension.

- ✅ **Design for Safety:** For safety-critical applications, use **self-de-energizing** design (avoid lock-up).

- ✅ **Units reminder:** All lengths in metres, forces in Newtons, torque in N·m.

- ✅ **Exam Tip:** Always draw a free-body diagram showing rotation direction, label tight/slack sides clearly, and convert θ to radians before computing e^(μθ).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Band Brake
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT527 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Differential Band Brake # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Differential Band Brake** is a friction brake in which a flexible band (lined with friction material) is wrapped partly around a rotating drum, and the two ends of the band are attached to a single **lever pivoted at an intermediate point** (the fulcrum lies *between* the two band ends). Because the lever pivots between the two tensions, the difference of the moment arms of the tight-side and slack-side tensions produces a net moment that either **aids** or **opposes** the applied effort, depending on the direction of rotation. This inherent property—where the brake torque is a *differential* function of the two tensions—is the defining feature of this brake. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Tag (Module 3 – Brakes & Dynamometers):** A *differential band brake* is a band brake whose lever is hinged at a point **between** the two band ends (i.e., the fulcrum does not coincide with either end of the lever). This distinguishes it from a **simple band brake**, where the fulcrum is located at one extreme end of the lever. ## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are pulling a heavy suitcase with a strap that loops around the wheel axis. If you attach the pulling handle to the strap very close to where the strap leaves the wheel, you need a lot of force to slow the wheel down. But if you attach it on the *other side* of the loop—almost directly opposite—you can produce the same braking effect with a much smaller pulling force. In fact, the strap itself "helps" tighten around the wheel as it spins, almost as if it is trying to brake itself. This self-helping behaviour is exactly what a **differential band brake** exploits. By placing the pivot (fulcrum) of the lever **between** the two ends of the band, the tension on the *tight side* of the band and the tension on the *slack side* act on different moment arms. Their difference produces an additional torque that either helps the operator (self-energising) or fights the operator (self-de-energising), depending purely on the **direction of drum rotation**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Industrial Context:** Differential band brakes are commonly used in **cranes, winches, hoists, conveyor drives, and rail vehicles**, where high braking torque is required from a small applied effort—especially in applications where the drum always rotates in *one* direction. ## 1.3 Physical Parameters & Standard Metrics The following physical quantities and constants are central to analysing a differential band brake: - **Friction coefficient (μ)** — dimensionless, typically between **0.20 and 0.45** for leather/steel lined bands. - **Band tension ratio** — $T_1 / T_2 = e^{\mu \theta}$ (simple friction), where $\theta$ is the angle of wrap in **radians**. - **Braking torque** — $M_t = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot R$, in **N·m**, where $R$ is the drum radius in **m**. - **Length of lever arms** — measured from the pivot to the band end and from the pivot to the point of effort application, in **m**. - **Applied effort (P)** — operator force at the lever end, in **N**. > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner Heuristic:** Whenever a *band brake* problem gives you "limiting friction" or "just slipping" condition, it almost always means you should use the **belt-friction relation** $T_1 = T_2 \, e^{\mu \theta}$ and not a simple $\mu N$ formula. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Construction & Operating Principle The constructional features of a differential band brake are as follows: 1. A **flexible friction-lined band** (steel or fabric) is wrapped partially around a rotating drum. 2. The drum is keyed to the **shaft** whose rotation is to be controlled. 3. One end of the band is attached to point **A** of a lever; the *other* end is attached to point **C** of the same lever. 4. The lever is **pivoted at point B**, which lies *between* A and C (this is what makes it "differential"). 5. An effort **P** is applied at the free end of the lever, and a **stop** limits the lever's travel in the off-direction. 6. A **spring** (in some designs) maintains the brake in the applied (braking) condition. **Direction Convention (Universal in KTU Problems):** - **CW rotation** of the drum → the **top** of the band moves in the direction of rotation; the band end attached to the lever side that is pulled *toward* the drum tightens first. By convention, the end connected to the **longer arm** from the pivot is **$T_1$ (tight side)**, and the other is **$T_2$ (slack side)**. - **CCW rotation** → the tension roles reverse (the band end on the *shorter* arm becomes the tight side). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Self-Energising vs Self-De-Energising:** > A differential band brake is **self-energising** when the moment of the tight-side tension about the pivot *assists* the applied effort P (i.e., the brake grips harder as the drum rotates). It is **self-de-energising** when the tight-side moment *opposes* the applied effort. Whether the brake is energising or de-energising depends on (a) the direction of rotation and (b) the relative lengths of the lever arms on either side of the pivot. ## 2.2 Logical Analysis – Step by Step The analysis proceeds by taking moments about the pivot B for both possible directions of rotation. **Step 1:** Identify the two lever arms. - $a$ = perpendicular distance from pivot B to the line of action of the band tension on one end of the lever. - $b$ = perpendicular distance from pivot B to the line of action of the band tension on the other end. - $l$ = distance from pivot B to the point of effort application. **Step 2:** Decide which tension ($T_1$ or $T_2$) acts on which side of the pivot, based on the assumed direction of rotation. **Step 3:** Write the moment equilibrium equation of the lever about B: $$\sum M_B = 0$$ **Step 4:** Substitute the belt-friction relation $T_1 = T_2 \, e^{\mu \theta}$ to obtain a single equation in $T_2$ (or $T_1$). **Step 5:** Compute the braking torque $M_t = (T_1 - T_2) R$ on the drum. ## 2.3 The Two Canonical Cases ### Case I – Clockwise (CW) Rotation of the Drum When the drum rotates clockwise, the *upper* portion of the band moves with the drum. The end of the band that the drum "tries to pull" becomes the tight side. The standard KTU convention is: - $T_1$ acts on the lever arm at distance $a$ from the pivot. - $T_2$ acts on the lever arm at distance $b$ from the pivot. - Effort $P$ acts at distance $l$ from the pivot. Taking moments about B (with anticlockwise positive, for a typical lever arrangement): $$P \cdot l = T_1 \cdot a - T_2 \cdot b$$ If $T_1 \cdot a > T_2 \cdot b$ → the brake is **self-energising** in the CW direction (P could even be zero and the brake would still hold). ### Case II – Counter-Clockwise (CCW) Rotation of the Drum When the drum rotates CCW, the tension roles swap: - $T_1$ now acts on the arm of length $b$. - $T_2$ now acts on the arm of length $a$. $$P \cdot l = T_1 \cdot b - T_2 \cdot a$$ The brake is now self-energising in the CCW direction *only if* $T_1 \cdot b > T_2 \cdot a$. > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write the same moment equation for *both* directions. **The lever arm assignments for $T_1$ and $T_2$ must be swapped when the rotation direction reverses.** Losing this switch costs the entire problem's marks. ## 2.4 Condition for Self-Energising and Self-De-Energising A brake is **self-energising** in a given direction if, with effort $P = 0$, the moment of the band tensions alone about the pivot is sufficient to keep the band tight (i.e., tends to wrap the band further around the drum). For **CW rotation** (using the Case I equation): $$T_1 \cdot a > T_2 \cdot b \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{Self-energising}$$ Using $T_1 = T_2 e^{\mu \theta}$: $$T_2 e^{\mu \theta} a > T_2 b \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; e^{\mu \theta} > \frac{b}{a} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; a > b \, e^{-\mu \theta}$$ For **CCW rotation** (using the Case II equation): $$T_1 \cdot b > T_2 \cdot a \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; b > a \, e^{-\mu \theta}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Maximum and Minimum Braking Torque:** When the drum rotates in the *self-energising* direction, the effort required is *minimum* (P_min). When it rotates in the *self-de-energising* direction, the effort is *maximum* (P_max). KTU problems frequently ask for the ratio $P_{max} / P_{min}$. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Belt-friction (capstan) equation | $T_1 = T_2 \, e^{\mu \theta}$ | $T_1$ = tight side, $T_2$ = slack side, $\theta$ in **rad** | | 2 | Braking torque on drum | $M_t = (T_1 - T_2) R$ | N·m, R = drum radius | | 3 | Effort for **CW** rotation | $P \, l = T_1 \, a - T_2 \, b$ | Lever in equilibrium about pivot B | | 4 | Effort for **CCW** rotation | $P \, l = T_1 \, b - T_2 \, a$ | Note the *swap* of $a$ and $b$ | | 5 | CW self-energising condition | $T_1 a > T_2 b$ | Brake grips without external effort | | 6 | CCW self-energising condition | $T_1 b > T_2 a$ | Same as above but reversed | | 7 | Effort at the *drum* (CW) | $P = \dfrac{T_1 a - T_2 b}{l}$ | N | | 8 | Effort at the *drum* (CCW) | $P = \dfrac{T_1 b - T_2 a}{l}$ | N | | 9 | Ratio of efforts (max/min) | $\dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{min}} = \dfrac{T_1 b - T_2 a}{T_1 a - T_2 b}$ | Dimensionless; KTU-favourite question | | 10 | Angle of wrap (typical full wrap) | $\theta = 2\pi - 2\alpha$ | $\alpha$ = angle subtended by the gap at the band ends | > **Note on Pivoting:** The perpendicular distances $a$ and $b$ are **NOT the lengths of the lever segments** unless the band tensions are tangential to the drum. In general, $a$ and $b$ must be measured perpendicular to the line of action of the respective band tension. ## 2.6 Engineering Utility Differential band brakes are preferred in applications where: - **One-directional rotation** is the norm (e.g., winch drums that only wind up the load), allowing the designer to choose the self-energising direction to be the working direction. - **Compact, high-torque braking** is needed in a small space (the band wraps the drum, providing large contact area). - **Fail-safe operation** is desired: a spring-applied, hydraulically or pneumatically released differential band brake is the industry standard for crane hoist drums, mine hoists, and rail vehicle parking brakes. - The **drum of the prime mover (motor/engine)** is conveniently available to act as the brake drum. > [!TIP] > **Industry Tip (KTU 2024, NEP-2020 context):** Modern electric vehicles (EVs) and regenerative braking systems use *electromechanical* analogues of the differential band brake principle—the controller continuously adjusts the "tight-side" and "slack-side" braking torques electronically, mimicking the self-energising behaviour with software. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Braking-Torque Equation (CW Rotation) Consider a differential band brake with a lever pivoted at B. Let: - $T_1$ = tight-side band tension (acts at perpendicular distance $a$ from B). - $T_2$ = slack-side band tension (acts at perpendicular distance $b$ from B). - $P$ = effort applied at distance $l$ from B. - $R$ = radius of the drum. - $\mu$ = coefficient of friction between band and drum. - $\theta$ = angle of wrap of the band on the drum, in radians. **Equilibrium of the lever (CW drum rotation, standard KTU convention):** Taking moments about the pivot B and equating the *driving* moment to the sum of *resisting* moments: $$ \begin{aligned} P \cdot l &= T_1 \cdot a - T_2 \cdot b \end{aligned} $$ Substituting the capstan (belt-friction) relation $T_1 = T_2 \, e^{\mu \theta}$: $$ \begin{aligned} P \cdot l &= T_2 \, e^{\mu \theta} \cdot a - T_2 \cdot b \\ P \cdot l &= T_2 \, \left( a \, e^{\mu \theta} - b \right) \end{aligned} $$ Solving for the slack-side tension: $$ \begin{aligned} T_2 &= \frac{P \cdot l}{a \, e^{\mu \theta} - b} \end{aligned} $$ And the tight-side tension is then: $$ \begin{aligned} T_1 &= T_2 \, e^{\mu \theta} = \frac{P \cdot l \, e^{\mu \theta}}{a \, e^{\mu \theta} - b} \end{aligned} $$ The **net braking torque** exerted on the drum is the difference of the two tensions times the radius: $$ \begin{aligned} M_t &= (T_1 - T_2) \, R \\ &= \left( \frac{P \, l \, e^{\mu \theta}}{a \, e^{\mu \theta} - b} - \frac{P \, l}{a \, e^{\mu \theta} - b} \right) R \\ &= \frac{P \, l \, R \, (e^{\mu \theta} - 1)}{a \, e^{\mu \theta} - b} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Quick-Check:** If the denominator $a \, e^{\mu \theta} - b$ becomes **zero or negative**, the computed $T_2$ blows up (or becomes negative). Physically, this means the brake is **self-locking** — it holds the drum even with $P = 0$ or with $P$ applied in the *opposite* direction. This is the boundary condition for self-energising behaviour. ## 3.2 Derivation of the CCW Effort Equation When the drum rotates in the **CCW** direction, the friction direction on the band reverses. The tight side now shifts to the *other* lever end. The KTU convention is that the band end attached to the lever arm of length $b$ now carries the tight-side tension $T_1$, while the arm of length $a$ carries the slack-side tension $T_2$. Taking moments about the pivot B: $$ \begin{aligned} P \cdot l &= T_1 \cdot b - T_2 \cdot a \end{aligned} $$ Substituting $T_1 = T_2 \, e^{\mu \theta}$: $$ \begin{aligned} P \cdot l &= T_2 \, e^{\mu \theta} \cdot b - T_2 \cdot a \\ P \cdot l &= T_2 \, (b \, e^{\mu \theta} - a) \end{aligned} $$ Solving for the slack-side tension in the CCW case: $$ \begin{aligned} T_2 &= \frac{P \cdot l}{b \, e^{\mu \theta} - a} \end{aligned} $$ The braking torque in the CCW direction is: $$ \begin{aligned} M_t &= (T_1 - T_2) \, R \\ &= T_2 \, (e^{\mu \theta} - 1) \, R \\ &= \frac{P \, l \, R \, (e^{\mu \theta} - 1)}{b \, e^{\mu \theta} - a} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Ratio of Maximum to Minimum Braking Effort (or Torque) A classic KTU question is: *"Find the ratio of maximum effort to minimum effort for the brake to operate in both directions of rotation."* Since the brake is *self-energising* in one direction and *self-de-energising* in the other, the effort required is different for the two directions. - **Minimum effort** $P_{min}$ corresponds to the **self-energising** direction (the direction in which the tight-side tension moment assists the operator). - **Maximum effort** $P_{max}$ corresponds to the **self-de-energising** direction. Let us assume the *CW* direction is self-energising (i.e., $T_1 a > T_2 b$) and the *CCW* direction is self-de-energising. Then: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{min} \cdot l &= T_1 \cdot a - T_2 \cdot b \\ P_{max} \cdot l &= T_1 \cdot b - T_2 \cdot a \end{aligned} $$ The ratio is: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{P_{max}}{P_{min}} &= \frac{T_1 \cdot b - T_2 \cdot a}{T_1 \cdot a - T_2 \cdot b} \end{aligned} $$ Substituting $T_1 = T_2 \, e^{\mu \theta}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{P_{max}}{P_{min}} &= \frac{T_2 \, e^{\mu \theta} \, b - T_2 \, a}{T_2 \, e^{\mu \theta} \, a - T_2 \, b} \\ &= \frac{b \, e^{\mu \theta} - a}{a \, e^{\mu \theta} - b} \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **Sanity Check:** If $a = b$ (pivot exactly at the lever's mid-point), then $P_{max} = P_{min}$ and the brake behaves *identically* in both directions — it is a *non-differential* symmetric band brake. This is the limiting case the formula must reproduce. Plugging $a = b$ into the ratio gives $1$, which is correct. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A differential band brake has a drum of diameter **$300\,\text{mm}$** rotating at **$200\,\text{rpm}$**. The band is lined with a material having $\mu = 0.30$, and the angle of wrap is **$\theta = 270° = 3\pi/2$ rad**. The lever has the following dimensions: - $a = 100\,\text{mm}$ (pivot to band end on tight side for CW), - $b = 60\,\text{mm}$ (pivot to band end on slack side for CW), - $l = 400\,\text{mm}$ (pivot to point of effort application). Find: 1. The effort $P$ required when the drum rotates clockwise. 2. The effort $P$ required when the drum rotates counter-clockwise. 3. The braking torque in each case. 4. The ratio $P_{max} / P_{min}$. **Given data (in SI units):** - $R = 0.150\,\text{m}$, $\mu = 0.30$, $\theta = 4.7124\,\text{rad}$, - $a = 0.100\,\text{m}$, $b = 0.060\,\text{m}$, $l = 0.400\,\text{m}$. **Pre-computation — Belt-friction ratio:** $$ \begin{aligned} e^{\mu \theta} &= e^{0.30 \times 4.7124} \\ &= e^{1.4137} \\ &\approx 4.111 \end{aligned} $$ ### Part (1) — CW Rotation Effort Moment equation (CW case): $$ \begin{aligned} P_{CW} \cdot l &= T_1 \cdot a - T_2 \cdot b \\ P_{CW} \cdot l &= T_2 (a \, e^{\mu\theta} - b) \\ P_{CW} &= \frac{T_2 (a \, e^{\mu\theta} - b)}{l} \end{aligned} $$ But we need a *second* equation to fix $T_2$. The additional constraint comes from the **braking torque** required. Suppose the problem specifies the braking torque, say $M_t = 80\,\text{N·m}$ (typical for a small hoist). Then: $$ \begin{aligned} M_t &= (T_1 - T_2) R = T_2 (e^{\mu\theta} - 1) R \\ 80 &= T_2 \times (4.111 - 1) \times 0.150 \\ 80 &= T_2 \times 3.111 \times 0.150 \\ 80 &= T_2 \times 0.4667 \\ T_2 &= \frac{80}{0.4667} \approx 171.4\,\text{N} \end{aligned} $$ Then: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{CW} \cdot 0.400 &= T_2 (a \, e^{\mu\theta} - b) \\ P_{CW} \times 0.400 &= 171.4 \times (0.100 \times 4.111 - 0.060) \\ &= 171.4 \times (0.4111 - 0.060) \\ &= 171.4 \times 0.3511 \\ &\approx 60.18\,\text{N·m} \\ P_{CW} &= \frac{60.18}{0.400} \approx 150.5\,\text{N} \end{aligned} $$ ### Part (2) — CCW Rotation Effort For the CCW direction, the arm assignments swap: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{CCW} \cdot l &= T_1 \cdot b - T_2 \cdot a \\ P_{CCW} &= \frac{T_2 (b \, e^{\mu\theta} - a)}{l} \\ &= \frac{171.4 \times (0.060 \times 4.111 - 0.100)}{0.400} \\ &= \frac{171.4 \times (0.2467 - 0.100)}{0.400} \\ &= \frac{171.4 \times 0.1467}{0.400} \\ &\approx 62.86\,\text{N} \end{aligned} $$ ### Part (3) — Braking Torques - CW: $M_t = 80\,\text{N·m}$ (as specified). - CCW: Same braking torque requirement ⇒ $M_t = 80\,\text{N·m}$. ### Part (4) — Ratio $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{P_{max}}{P_{min}} &= \frac{b \, e^{\mu\theta} - a}{a \, e^{\mu\theta} - b} \\ &= \frac{0.060 \times 4.111 - 0.100}{0.100 \times 4.111 - 0.060} \\ &= \frac{0.2467 - 0.100}{0.4111 - 0.060} \\ &= \frac{0.1467}{0.3511} \\ &\approx 0.418 \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Observation:** Here $P_{CCW} < P_{CW}$, which means the *CCW* direction is the self-energising one (because $b \, e^{\mu\theta} > a$, i.e., $0.2467 > 0.100$). So the actual minimum effort is $P_{CCW}$ and the maximum is $P_{CW}$. The ratio is *less than 1* in this case, indicating a stronger self-energising effect in the CCW direction. KTU papers accept either "ratio" interpretation as long as the calculation is correct. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid) Because the physical geometry of a differential band brake is difficult to render cleanly with a Mermaid diagram (it requires tangent lines, lever arcs, and moment-arm perpendiculars), we use a **block-level functional architecture** showing how the lever, drum, band, and effort interact under the two rotation directions. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[DCMotor or Prime Mover] -->|Rotational input| B[Brake Drum
Radius R] B -->|Friction contact
Angle theta| C[Friction Lined Band] C -->|T1 tight side| D[Lever End Point A
Arm length a] C -->|T2 slack side| E[Lever End Point C
Arm length b] D -->|T1 vector| F[Lever
Pivoted at B] E -->|T2 vector| F F -->|Effort P at arm l| G[Operator / Hydraulic Actuator] F -->|Moment balance| H{Selection Logic} H -->|CW rotation| I[P_CW equals T1a minus T2b over l] H -->|CCW rotation| J[P_CCW equals T1b minus T2a over l] I --> K[Braking Torque Mt equals T1 minus T2 times R] J --> K K -->|Decelerates| A style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600 style C fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000 style F fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600 style H fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#6600cc style K fill:#ffe1ff,stroke:#990099 ``` ## 4.2 Schematic Description of the Differential Band Brake The physical layout (for student sketch-book purposes) is: ``` T1 ●━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━● ╱ BAND ╲ ╱ ╲ ╱ ┌──────────┐ ╲ ●━━━━━━┤ DRUM ├━━━━━━● │ (R, ω) │ A │ │ C ●━━━━━━┘ └━━━━━━● | | | b a | (band ends) |←─────→│ | (pivot B) | | l | ● ──→ P (effort) ``` - **A** = one end of the band, attached to the lever at perpendicular distance $a$ from the pivot. - **C** = the other end of the band, attached to the lever at perpendicular distance $b$ from the pivot. - **B** = the pivot (fulcrum) of the lever, located *between* A and C. - **P** = effort applied at the free end of the lever at distance $l$ from B. - $T_1$ and $T_2$ are the band tensions (tight and slack side respectively, depending on rotation direction). > [!NOTE] > **How to Read the Diagram:** When the drum rotates clockwise, the *upper* portion of the band is dragged along by friction. The band end at A (longer arm, distance $a$ from B) is pulled tight, becoming $T_1$. The end at C (shorter arm, distance $b$) slackens to $T_2$. Reversing the rotation reverses these roles. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Define a differential band brake. How does it differ from a simple band brake?** **Model Answer:** A differential band brake is a friction brake in which a flexible band is wrapped around a drum, and the two ends of the band are attached to a lever whose **pivot lies between the two band ends**. The difference of the moments of the two band tensions about the pivot produces either a *self-energising* or *self-de-energising* effect, depending on the direction of drum rotation. In contrast, a **simple band brake** has its pivot located at one of the *extreme ends* of the lever (typically at the slack-side band end). This eliminates the differential moment, and the effort equation is simply $P \cdot l = T_1 \cdot a$ (if pivot is at the slack end) or $P \cdot l = T_2 \cdot b$ (depending on arrangement). The key distinction is the **location of the fulcrum**. ### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: State the condition for a differential band brake to be self-energising in a given direction of rotation.** **Model Answer:** A differential band brake is self-energising in a direction of rotation when the moment of the **tight-side tension** about the pivot *exceeds* the moment of the **slack-side tension**, i.e., it tends to wrap the band further around the drum even without an external effort. In equation form, for CW rotation: $$T_1 \cdot a > T_2 \cdot b$$ Using the capstan equation $T_1 = T_2 \, e^{\mu \theta}$, this simplifies to: $$e^{\mu \theta} > \frac{b}{a} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad a > b \, e^{-\mu \theta}$$ If this condition is satisfied, the brake grips harder as the drum rotates, and the operator may even need to *push* the lever to release the brake. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question B (Set 1) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]` #### **Question A (14 Marks)** A differential band brake has a drum of diameter **$400\,\text{mm}$** and the angle of contact of the band is **$240°$**. The coefficient of friction between the band and the drum is **$0.30$**. The brake lever is pivoted at a point that divides the lever into two segments of lengths **$100\,\text{mm}$** (band-end side) and **$60\,\text{mm}$** (effort side). The effort is applied at **$500\,\text{mm}$** from the pivot. **(a) [7 Marks]** Determine the braking torque and the effort required at the lever when the drum rotates clockwise. Take the speed of the drum as $150\,\text{rpm}$. Assume the brake is required to develop a tangential braking force of $1.5\,\text{kN}$ on the drum. **(b) [7 Marks]** If the direction of rotation is reversed, find the new effort required and comment on whether the brake is self-energising in this direction. **Model Solution:** **Pre-computation:** - $R = 0.200\,\text{m}$, $\theta = 240° = 4\pi/3 \approx 4.189\,\text{rad}$, $\mu = 0.30$ - $a = 0.100\,\text{m}$, $b = 0.060\,\text{m}$, $l = 0.500\,\text{m}$ - $F_t = T_1 - T_2 = 1500\,\text{N}$ (tangential braking force) **Belt-friction ratio:** $$ \begin{aligned} e^{\mu\theta} &= e^{0.30 \times 4.189} = e^{1.2566} \approx 3.514 \end{aligned} $$ **Tension difference (from given tangential force):** $$ \begin{aligned} T_1 - T_2 &= 1500\,\text{N} \\ T_2 (e^{\mu\theta} - 1) &= 1500 \\ T_2 \times 2.514 &= 1500 \\ T_2 &= \frac{1500}{2.514} \approx 596.7\,\text{N} \\ T_1 &= T_2 \times 3.514 \approx 2097\,\text{N} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (a) — CW rotation:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{CW} \cdot l &= T_1 \cdot a - T_2 \cdot b \\ P_{CW} \times 0.500 &= (2097 \times 0.100) - (596.7 \times 0.060) \\ &= 209.7 - 35.80 \\ &= 173.9\,\text{N·m} \\ P_{CW} &= \frac{173.9}{0.500} \approx 347.8\,\text{N} \end{aligned} $$ **Braking torque:** $$ \begin{aligned} M_t &= (T_1 - T_2) R = 1500 \times 0.200 = 300\,\text{N·m} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b) — CCW rotation (arm assignment reversed):** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{CCW} \cdot l &= T_1 \cdot b - T_2 \cdot a \\ P_{CCW} \times 0.500 &= (2097 \times 0.060) - (596.7 \times 0.100) \\ &= 125.8 - 59.67 \\ &= 66.15\,\text{N·m} \\ P_{CCW} &= \frac{66.15}{0.500} \approx 132.3\,\text{N} \end{aligned} $$ **Self-energising check (CCW):** Since $P_{CCW} < P_{CW}$, the CCW direction is the *self-energising* direction. The condition is: $$ \begin{aligned} T_1 \cdot b &> T_2 \cdot a \\ 2097 \times 0.060 &> 596.7 \times 0.100 \\ 125.8 &> 59.67 \;\;\checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** The brake is self-energising in the **CCW** direction. #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice A differential band brake operates on a hoist drum of diameter **$500\,\text{mm}$**. The coefficient of friction is **$0.25$** and the band wraps the drum through an angle of **$300°$**. The lever is pivoted such that the two moment arms are in the ratio **$a : b = 3 : 2$** and the effort arm is **$l = 1.2 a$**. The hoist is required to lower a load of **$5\,\text{kN}$** at a steady speed of **$0.8\,\text{m/s}$**. **(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the tensions in the band and the effort required at the lever when the drum rotates in the lowering direction (assume the brake operates in the self-de-energising direction during lowering for safety). **(b) [7 Marks]** What would be the effort if the drum accidentally reversed direction (e.g., during power failure)? Comment on the safety implications. **Model Solution (Skeleton):** **Pre-computation:** - $R = 0.250\,\text{m}$, $\theta = 300° = 5\pi/3 \approx 5.236\,\text{rad}$, $\mu = 0.25$ - $a = 3k$, $b = 2k$ for some $k$; $l = 3.6 k$ - Braking force: $F_t = W = 5000\,\text{N}$ (steady lowering, drum torque balances weight torque) **Belt-friction ratio:** $$ \begin{aligned} e^{\mu\theta} &= e^{0.25 \times 5.236} = e^{1.309} \approx 3.702 \end{aligned} $$ **Tensions:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_1 - T_2 &= 5000 \\ T_2 (3.702 - 1) &= 5000 \\ T_2 \times 2.702 &= 5000 \\ T_2 &\approx 1850.5\,\text{N} \\ T_1 &\approx 6851\,\text{N} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (a) — Lowering (self-de-energising direction):** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{low} \cdot l &= T_1 \cdot a - T_2 \cdot b \\ &= (6851 \times 3k) - (1850.5 \times 2k) \\ &= 20553k - 3701k = 16852k \\ P_{low} \cdot 3.6k &= 16852k \\ P_{low} &= \frac{16852}{3.6} \approx 4681\,\text{N} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b) — Reversed direction:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{up} \cdot l &= T_1 \cdot b - T_2 \cdot a \\ &= (6851 \times 2k) - (1850.5 \times 3k) \\ &= 13702k - 5551.5k = 8150.5k \\ P_{up} \cdot 3.6k &= 8150.5k \\ P_{up} &= \frac{8150.5}{3.6} \approx 2264\,\text{N} \end{aligned} $$ **Safety Implication:** The effort during *accidental reversal* ($2264\,\text{N}$) is *less* than the lowering effort ($4681\,\text{N}$). This means the brake becomes self-energising in the reversal direction and could over-brake (lock the drum, causing a shock load on the cable). For a hoist, this is a **safety hazard** — a *load-fuse* or *over-speed governor* must be installed. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Differential Band Brake:** > 1. **Forgetting to swap $a$ and $b$** when the rotation direction reverses. This single error makes the entire solution wrong and costs 7-8 marks. > 2. **Using $T_1 - T_2$ for the net tangential force** without confirming which end is tight side. Always identify $T_1$ first based on rotation. > 3. **Forgetting radians:** $e^{\mu\theta}$ requires $\theta$ in **radians**, not degrees. A common slip-up costs the answer's order of magnitude. > 4. **Mis-identifying the pivot:** If the pivot is *at* one end of the lever, it is a *simple* band brake — the differential equations do *not* apply. > 5. **Sign convention confusion:** When taking moments, always be consistent with the sign of effort P. The KTU convention is to take the *applied* moment of P as positive. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Summary — Differential Band Brake** - **Core Definition:** A band brake whose lever fulcrum lies *between* the two band ends. - **Key Relation:** $T_1 = T_2 \, e^{\mu\theta}$ (capstan / belt-friction equation). - **Braking Torque:** $M_t = (T_1 - T_2) \, R$. - **CW Effort Equation:** $P \cdot l = T_1 \cdot a - T_2 \cdot b$ (tight side on the $a$-arm). - **CCW Effort Equation:** $P \cdot l = T_1 \cdot b - T_2 \cdot a$ (tight side on the $b$-arm). - **Self-Energising (CW):** $T_1 a > T_2 b$, equivalently $a > b \, e^{-\mu\theta}$. - **Self-Energising (CCW):** $T_1 b > T_2 a$, equivalently $b > a \, e^{-\mu\theta}$. - **Effort Ratio:** $\dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{min}} = \dfrac{b \, e^{\mu\theta} - a}{a \, e^{\mu\theta} - b}$ (always positive). - **Limiting case:** $a = b$ ⇒ $P_{max} = P_{min}$ (symmetric, non-differential behaviour). - **Dimensional check:** All lever arms in **metres**, tensions in **N**, torque in **N·m**. - **Angle of wrap:** $\theta$ must always be in **radians** inside the exponent. - **Pivot location rule:** If pivot is at one extreme end → simple band brake; pivot *between* ends → differential band brake. - **Industrial use:** Cranes, winches, mine hoists, rail vehicles, conveyor drives — anywhere one-directional braking with high torque from small effort is required. - **Safety note:** Self-energising direction must be *opposite* to the load-driven (free-fall) direction in hoists, to prevent uncontrolled descent. ---

topics.title: Free Vibration of Single Degree Undamped Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT527 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Free Vibration of Single Degree Undamped Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF)** vibrating system is a mechanical system whose instantaneous spatial configuration can be completely described by a single independent generalized coordinate. The term *undamped* specifies the absence of any energy-dissipating element (dashpot/friction), while *free vibration* indicates that the system oscillates solely under the action of internal restoring forces after being subjected to a single initial disturbance — no external periodic force acts on it after release.

Mathematically, the SDOF undamped free vibration model represents the simplest non-trivial oscillatory system in classical mechanics and forms the foundational block upon which all multi-degree-of-freedom and continuous-system analyses are constructed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Free vibration refers to motion that occurs *naturally* when a system is displaced from its equilibrium position and released. For an **undamped** system, total mechanical energy remains strictly conserved during oscillation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine pulling a child on a swing slightly to one side and letting go. The swing oscillates back and forth across its resting position, slowing down only because of air drag and friction at the pivot. Now, hypothetically, **remove all friction and air drag** — the swing would oscillate *forever* with the same maximum displacement on every side. That is an **undamped free vibration**.

Another everyday example is a mass hanging from a stretched coil spring: pull the mass down by some distance and release it. The spring alternately stretches and compresses, converting kinetic energy into potential energy and back again, with no loss in between. The number of oscillations per second is its **natural frequency** — a property determined entirely by the mass and the stiffness of the spring, *not* by how hard you pulled it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box**
> - **Degree of Freedom (DOF):** The minimum number of independent coordinates required to completely specify the configuration of a system at any instant in time.
> - **Free Vibration:** Oscillation of a system about its mean position, after an initial displacement or velocity, with no subsequent external excitation.
> - **Undamped Vibration:** A vibration in which the total mechanical energy of the system is conserved — no energy is dissipated as heat, sound, or any other form.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Mass (m):** Measured in **kg** (SI) or **N·s²/m**.
- **Stiffness (k):** Measured in **N/m** (SI) or **kg/s²**.
- **Natural Angular Frequency (ωₙ):** Measured in **rad/s**.
- **Natural Frequency (fₙ):** Measured in **Hz (s⁻¹)**.
- **Time Period (T):** Measured in **seconds (s)**.
- **Static Deflection (δ):** Measured in **m or mm**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** SDOF undamped analysis is the launching pad for all vibration engineering — it is used in designing vehicle suspensions, building seismic dampers, turbine blade modeling, machine tool chatter avoidance, and earthquake-resistant structures.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Canonical SDOF System

A canonical SDOF undamped vibrating system consists of exactly three ideal elements:

1. **Mass element (m):** Stores kinetic energy. Assumed rigid, lumped at a point.
2. **Stiffness element (k):** Stores potential energy. Provides a linear restoring force $F = -kx$.
3. **Equilibrium reference:** The static equilibrium position where net force on the mass is zero.

A system is classified as **SDOF** if one independent coordinate **x(t)** fully describes the motion of every particle in the system.

## 2.2 Assumptions of the Idealized Model

- The mass is rigid and the spring is massless.
- The spring force is **linearly proportional** to displacement (Hooke's Law region).
- No energy dissipation — no friction, no air drag, no internal damping.
- Motion is constrained to a single direction.
- The system has a unique static equilibrium position.

## 2.3 Free-Body Diagram Logic

Step-by-step logic used to write the equation of motion:

1. **Choose a coordinate** $x$ measured from the static equilibrium position.
2. **Apply Newton's Second Law** to the mass: $\sum F = ma = m\ddot{x}$.
3. **Identify the restoring force** exerted by the spring: $F_s = -kx$ (negative because it always acts toward the equilibrium).
4. **Equate and rearrange** to obtain the standard second-order linear homogeneous ODE.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Equation of motion | $m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$ | — | Standard linear homogeneous ODE |
| 2 | Natural angular frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | rad/s | Independent of initial conditions |
| 3 | Natural frequency | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi}$ | Hz | Cycles per second |
| 4 | Time period | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n}$ | s | Time for one complete oscillation |
| 5 | Static deflection | $\delta_{st} = \dfrac{mg}{k}$ | m | Spring stretch under gravity |
| 6 | Natural frequency via $\delta_{st}$ | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$ | rad/s | Useful when stiffness is unknown |
| 7 | Springs in **parallel** | $k_{eq} = k_1 + k_2 + k_3 + \dots$ | N/m | Same displacement, add forces |
| 8 | Springs in **series** | $\dfrac{1}{k_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{k_1} + \dfrac{1}{k_2} + \dfrac{1}{k_3} + \dots$ | N/m | Same force, add displacements |
| 9 | General solution | $x(t) = C_1\cos(\omega_n t) + C_2\sin(\omega_n t)$ | m | $C_1, C_2$ determined by initial conditions |
| 10 | Compact form | $x(t) = X\sin(\omega_n t + \phi)$ | m | $X$ = amplitude, $\phi$ = phase angle |
| 11 | Amplitude | $X = \sqrt{C_1^{\,2} + C_2^{\,2}}$ | m | Peak displacement from equilibrium |
| 12 | Phase angle | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{C_1}{C_2}\right)$ | rad | Encodes initial condition mix |
| 13 | Constants from initial conditions | $C_1 = x(0),\ \ C_2 = \dfrac{\dot{x}(0)}{\omega_n}$ | — | At $t = 0$ |
| 14 | Maximum velocity | $\dot{x}_{max} = X\omega_n$ | m/s | Occurs at mean position |
| 15 | Maximum acceleration | $\ddot{x}_{max} = X\omega_n^{\,2}$ | m/s² | Occurs at extremes |
| 16 | Total energy | $E = \dfrac{1}{2}kX^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}mX^2\omega_n^{\,2}$ | J | Strictly constant (undamped) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** A heavier mass lowers the natural frequency (slower oscillation); a stiffer spring raises it. Doubling the mass reduces $\omega_n$ by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$; doubling the stiffness raises $\omega_n$ by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The SDOF undamped model is the workhorse of preliminary design in:

- **Civil Engineering:** Approximate seismic response of low-rise buildings.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Tuning machine tool supports to avoid chatter.
- **Aerospace:** Estimating wing-flutter frequencies.
- **Automotive:** Initial sizing of suspension springs.
- **Instrumentation:** Designing galvanometers and seismographs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Equation of Motion via Newton's Second Law

**Setup:** A rigid mass $m$ is attached to a linear spring of stiffness $k$. The other end of the spring is fixed to an immovable wall. The mass slides horizontally on a frictionless surface. Let $x$ be the displacement of the mass from its static equilibrium position, measured positive to the right.

**Step 1 — Identify the forces acting on the mass.**

The only force in the horizontal direction is the spring force. By Hooke's Law:

$$F_s = -k\,x$$

The negative sign is critical: it indicates that the spring force always acts **opposite** to the displacement, i.e., it always *restores* the mass back toward equilibrium.

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law.**

$$F_{net} = m\,a = m\,\dfrac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Equate the net force to the spring force.**

$$m\,\dfrac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} = -k\,x$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into the standard homogeneous form.**

Bring all terms to the left-hand side:

$$m\,\dfrac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} + k\,x = 0$$

This is the **governing differential equation** for an SDOF undamped free vibration system.

**Step 5 — Simplify by dividing through by m.**

$$\dfrac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} + \dfrac{k}{m}\,x = 0$$

Define the **natural angular frequency**:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

The equation collapses to the canonical simple harmonic motion form:

$$\ddot{x} + \omega_n^{\,2}\,x = 0$$

## 3.2 Derivation II — General Solution of the Equation of Motion

The ODE $\ddot{x} + \omega_n^{\,2}\,x = 0$ is a second-order linear homogeneous ODE with constant coefficients.

**Step 1 — Assume an exponential trial solution.**

$$x(t) = e^{st}$$

**Step 2 — Compute derivatives and substitute.**

$$\dot{x} = s\,e^{st}, \quad \ddot{x} = s^{2}\,e^{st}$$

Substituting into the ODE:

$$s^{2}\,e^{st} + \omega_n^{\,2}\,e^{st} = 0$$

$$(s^{2} + \omega_n^{\,2})\,e^{st} = 0$$

Since $e^{st} \neq 0$, we obtain the **auxiliary (characteristic) equation**:

$$s^{2} + \omega_n^{\,2} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve the characteristic equation.**

$$s = \pm\sqrt{-\omega_n^{\,2}} = \pm\,i\,\omega_n$$

The two roots are purely imaginary conjugates: $s_1 = +i\omega_n$ and $s_2 = -i\omega_n$.

**Step 4 — Write the general solution as a linear combination.**

$$x(t) = C_1\,e^{\,i\omega_n t} + C_2\,e^{-i\omega_n t}$$

Using Euler's identity $e^{\pm i\theta} = \cos\theta \pm i\sin\theta$, and recognizing that physical displacement must be real, we collapse the expression to:

$$x(t) = A\cos(\omega_n t) + B\sin(\omega_n t)$$

where $A$ and $B$ are real constants determined by the initial conditions.

**Step 5 — Convert to the single-amplitude form.**

The sum of a cosine and a sine of the same argument can be re-expressed as a single sinusoid:

$$x(t) = X\sin(\omega_n t + \phi)$$

where the amplitude $X$ and phase $\phi$ are:

$$X = \sqrt{A^{2} + B^{2}}, \qquad \phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{A}{B}\right)$$

## 3.3 Derivation III — Application of Initial Conditions

Let the system be released from rest at an initial displacement $x_0$:

$$x(0) = x_0, \qquad \dot{x}(0) = 0$$

**Step 1 — Apply $x(0) = x_0$.**

$$x(0) = A\cos(0) + B\sin(0) = A = x_0$$

So $A = x_0$.

**Step 2 — Apply $\dot{x}(0) = 0$.**

Differentiate the general solution:

$$\dot{x}(t) = -A\omega_n\sin(\omega_n t) + B\omega_n\cos(\omega_n t)$$

At $t = 0$:

$$\dot{x}(0) = -A\omega_n\sin(0) + B\omega_n\cos(0) = B\omega_n = 0$$

Therefore $B = 0$.

**Step 3 — Final displacement expression.**

$$x(t) = x_0\cos(\omega_n t)$$

This is the *purely cosine* motion — the mass starts at $x_0$, oscillates symmetrically about the mean, and returns to $x_0$ after every period $T = 2\pi/\omega_n$.

**General case** with both initial displacement $x_0$ and initial velocity $v_0$:

$$x(t) = x_0\cos(\omega_n t) + \dfrac{v_0}{\omega_n}\sin(\omega_n t)$$

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Equation of Motion via Energy Method

The energy method provides a more general route that bypasses vector force summation and is therefore powerful for complex systems.

**Step 1 — Write the kinetic energy of the mass.**

$$T = \dfrac{1}{2}m\,\dot{x}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Write the potential energy stored in the spring.**

$$U = \dfrac{1}{2}k\,x^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply conservation of total mechanical energy** (valid because the system is undamped):

$$T + U = E = \text{constant}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{2}m\,\dot{x}^{2} + \dfrac{1}{2}k\,x^{2} = E$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate both sides with respect to time.**

$$m\,\dot{x}\,\ddot{x} + k\,x\,\dot{x} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Factor out the non-zero velocity $\dot{x}$.**

$$\dot{x}\,\big(m\,\ddot{x} + k\,x\big) = 0$$

Since $\dot{x}$ is not identically zero (the system is oscillating), the term in parentheses must vanish:

$$m\,\ddot{x} + k\,x = 0$$

This reproduces the same governing equation as the Newton's method — confirming the energy approach.

## 3.5 Derivation V — Equivalent Stiffness of Spring Combinations

### (a) Springs in Parallel

In a parallel arrangement, both springs undergo the **same displacement** $x$ but their individual forces add:

$$F_1 = k_1 x, \qquad F_2 = k_2 x$$

The total restoring force is:

$$F = F_1 + F_2 = (k_1 + k_2)\,x = k_{eq}\,x$$

$$\boxed{k_{eq} = k_1 + k_2 + k_3 + \dots}$$

### (b) Springs in Series

In a series arrangement, both springs experience the **same force** $F$ but their individual extensions add up:

$$x_1 = \dfrac{F}{k_1}, \qquad x_2 = \dfrac{F}{k_2}$$

The total displacement is:

$$x = x_1 + x_2 = \dfrac{F}{k_1} + \dfrac{F}{k_2} = F\left(\dfrac{1}{k_1} + \dfrac{1}{k_2}\right) = \dfrac{F}{k_{eq}}$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{k_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{k_1} + \dfrac{1}{k_2} + \dfrac{1}{k_3} + \dots}$$

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A mass of $m = 10$ kg is suspended from a spring of stiffness $k = 2500$ N/m. The mass is pulled down by $40$ mm from equilibrium and released from rest. Find:
(a) Natural frequency and time period.
(b) Maximum velocity.
(c) Displacement as a function of time.

**Solution:**

(a) Natural angular frequency:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2500}{10}} = \sqrt{250} = 15.811\ \text{rad/s}$$

Time period:

$$T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = \dfrac{2\pi}{15.811} = 0.3974\ \text{s}$$

(b) Maximum velocity (occurs at mean position):

$$\dot{x}_{max} = X\,\omega_n = (0.040)(15.811) = 0.6325\ \text{m/s}$$

(c) Displacement function (initial velocity = 0, so $B = 0$):

$$x(t) = 0.040\,\cos(15.811\,t)\ \text{m}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — SDOF Undamped System Architecture

The following block diagram captures the **physical layout** of the canonical SDOF undamped mass-spring system along with the **flow of analytical information** from each component into the equation of motion.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Fixed Wall - Immovable Reference] --> B[Linear Spring - Stiffness k N/m]
    B --> C[Rigid Mass m kg]
    C --> D[Frictionless Guide - Constrains Motion to 1D]
    D --> E[Static Equilibrium Position x equals 0]
    C -->|Displacement x t| F[Restoring Force F equals minus k times x]
    F --> G[Apply Newton Second Law m x double dot equals F]
    G --> H[Governing ODE m x double dot plus k x equals 0]
    H --> I[Define Natural Frequency omega n equals sqrt k over m]
    I --> J[Solve ODE using trial x equals exp s t]
    J --> K[Characteristic Equation s squared plus omega n squared equals 0]
    K --> L[Roots s equals plus or minus i omega n]
    L --> M[General Solution x of t equals A cos omega n t plus B sin omega n t]
    M --> N[Apply Initial Conditions x of 0 equals x0 and x dot of 0 equals v0]
    N --> O[Final Solution x of t equals X sin omega n t plus phi]
    O --> P[Output Amplitude X and Phase phi]
    P --> Q[Time Period T equals 2 pi over omega n]
    P --> R[Maximum Velocity X omega n]
    P --> S[Maximum Acceleration X omega n squared]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Equivalent Stiffness Decision Tree

This flowchart guides the student through the proper procedure for combining multiple springs before substituting into the natural-frequency formula.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Multiple Springs Present in System] --> B{Are all springs attached between the same two nodes?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Parallel Formula k eq equals k1 plus k2 plus k3 ...]
    B -- No --> D{Are springs connected end to end in a single chain?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use Series Formula 1 over k eq equals 1 over k1 plus 1 over k2 plus 1 over k3 ...]
    D -- No --> F[Reduce Network Step by Step - Identify sub clusters that are purely series or purely parallel]
    F --> G[Compute k eq for each sub cluster]
    G --> H[Repeat until a single equivalent k eq remains]
    C --> I[Substitute k eq into omega n equals sqrt k eq over m]
    E --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Compute f n T X and other required parameters]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence — Energy Method Workflow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Step 1 Identify all moving masses] --> B[Step 2 Express Kinetic Energy T as half m v squared]
    B --> C[Step 3 Identify all elastic elements]
    C --> D[Step 4 Express Potential Energy U as half k x squared]
    D --> E[Step 5 Apply Conservation T plus U equals Constant]
    E --> F[Step 6 Differentiate with respect to time]
    F --> G[Step 7 Factor out common x dot term]
    G --> H[Step 8 Set remaining bracket to zero to obtain ODE]
    H --> I[Step 9 Solve ODE using standard harmonic methods]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define the following terms with respect to vibrating systems:
> (i) Degree of freedom
> (ii) Natural frequency
> (iii) Free vibration

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Degree of Freedom (DOF):** The minimum number of independent coordinates required to completely define the configuration of a vibrating system at any instant of time. For a single mass sliding on a frictionless surface constrained to move along a single straight line, the DOF = 1.

(ii) **Natural Frequency (ωₙ or fₙ):** The frequency at which a system oscillates naturally when displaced from its equilibrium position and released, with no external excitation. For an SDOF system, the natural angular frequency is given by $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ rad/s, and the natural frequency in Hz is $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ Hz.

(iii) **Free Vibration:** The vibration of a system that occurs when the system is disturbed from its equilibrium position and then allowed to oscillate without the influence of any external force. The motion is sustained entirely by the system's own internal restoring force and stored energy.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** State the differential equation governing the free vibration of a single degree of freedom undamped system. Derive the expression for its natural frequency.

**Model Answer:**

**Governing equation:**
$$m\,\dfrac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} + k\,x = 0$$

**Derivation of natural frequency:**

Applying Newton's second law to the mass attached to a linear spring (with displacement $x$ measured from static equilibrium):

$$m\ddot{x} = -kx \quad\Longrightarrow\quad m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$

Dividing through by $m$:

$$\ddot{x} + \dfrac{k}{m}\,x = 0$$

This is a second-order linear homogeneous ODE of the form $\ddot{x} + \omega_n^{\,2}x = 0$, where the coefficient of $x$ must be $\omega_n^{\,2}$. Therefore:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}\ \text{rad/s}$$

This is the **natural angular frequency** of the system.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the equation of motion of a single degree of freedom undamped free vibration system using Newton's second law and hence obtain the general solution. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A machine of mass **80 kg** is supported on a spring of stiffness **20 kN/m**. The machine is observed to vibrate with a peak-to-peak amplitude of **6 mm**. Calculate:
  (i) the natural frequency and time period,
  (ii) the maximum velocity and maximum acceleration of the mass,
  (iii) the total energy of the system. **(7 Marks)**

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Using the principle of conservation of energy, derive the equation of motion for a single degree of freedom undamped free vibration system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two springs of stiffness **k₁ = 3 N/mm** and **k₂ = 6 N/mm** are connected (i) in parallel, and (ii) in series, to a mass of **m = 5 kg**. Determine the natural frequency and time period for each configuration. **(7 Marks)**

---

### 5.2.1 Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — System description:** Consider a rigid mass $m$ attached to one end of a linear spring of stiffness $k$. The other end of the spring is fixed. The mass is constrained to move horizontally on a frictionless surface.

**Step 2 — Free-body diagram:** Let $x$ be the displacement of the mass measured from the static equilibrium position. The only force acting on the mass in the horizontal direction is the spring force.

**Step 3 — Hooke's Law:**

$$F_s = -k\,x$$

**Step 4 — Newton's Second Law:**

$$m\,\ddot{x} = -k\,x$$

**Step 5 — Standard form:**

$$m\,\ddot{x} + k\,x = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \ddot{x} + \omega_n^{\,2}x = 0, \quad \text{where } \omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$$

**Step 6 — Solution by trial:** Let $x = e^{st}$. Substituting gives the characteristic equation $s^{2} + \omega_n^{\,2} = 0$ with roots $s = \pm i\omega_n$.

**Step 7 — General solution:**

$$x(t) = C_1\cos(\omega_n t) + C_2\sin(\omega_n t)$$

Equivalently, in compact form:

$$x(t) = X\sin(\omega_n t + \phi), \quad X = \sqrt{C_1^{2} + C_2^{2}}, \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{C_1}{C_2}\right)$$

This is **simple harmonic motion (SHM)**. — *(7 marks distributed across the steps: 1 mark each for steps 1–4, 1 mark for step 5, 1 mark for step 6, 1 mark for step 7.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $m = 80$ kg, $k = 20{,}000$ N/m, total peak-to-peak amplitude $= 6$ mm $\Rightarrow$ amplitude $X = 3$ mm $= 0.003$ m.

**(i) Natural frequency and time period:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{20{,}000}{80}} = \sqrt{250} = 15.811\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \dfrac{15.811}{2\pi} = 2.517\ \text{Hz}$$

$$T = \dfrac{1}{f_n} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = 0.397\ \text{s}$$

**(ii) Maximum velocity and acceleration:**

$$\dot{x}_{max} = X\,\omega_n = (0.003)(15.811) = 0.0474\ \text{m/s} = 47.4\ \text{mm/s}$$

$$\ddot{x}_{max} = X\,\omega_n^{\,2} = (0.003)(250) = 0.750\ \text{m/s}^{2}$$

**(iii) Total energy:**

$$E = \dfrac{1}{2}kX^{2} = \dfrac{1}{2}(20{,}000)(0.003)^{2} = 0.090\ \text{J} = 90\ \text{mJ}$$

---

### 5.2.2 Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Derivation via Energy Method (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Kinetic Energy:** For a mass $m$ moving with velocity $\dot{x}$:

$$T = \dfrac{1}{2}m\dot{x}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Potential Energy:** For a linear spring of stiffness $k$ stretched by $x$:

$$U = \dfrac{1}{2}k x^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Conservation of Energy:** Since the system is undamped, the total mechanical energy is constant:

$$T + U = E \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \dfrac{1}{2}m\dot{x}^{2} + \dfrac{1}{2}k x^{2} = E$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate with respect to time:**

$$m\dot{x}\ddot{x} + k x\dot{x} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Factor out $\dot{x}$:**

$$\dot{x}\,(m\ddot{x} + kx) = 0$$

**Step 6 — Argue non-triviality:** For the system to be in motion, $\dot{x} \not\equiv 0$. Therefore:

$$m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$

This is the same governing ODE as obtained from Newton's method. — *(7 marks distributed as 1 mark each for the 6 derivation steps + 1 mark for the concluding statement.)*

---

**Part (b) — Springs in Parallel and Series (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $k_1 = 3$ N/mm $= 3000$ N/m, $k_2 = 6$ N/mm $= 6000$ N/m, $m = 5$ kg.

### Case (i) — Springs in Parallel

$$k_{eq} = k_1 + k_2 = 3000 + 6000 = 9000\ \text{N/m}$$

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_{eq}}{m}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9000}{5}} = \sqrt{1800} = 42.426\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = 6.752\ \text{Hz}$$

$$T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = 0.1481\ \text{s}$$

### Case (ii) — Springs in Series

$$\dfrac{1}{k_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{k_1} + \dfrac{1}{k_2} = \dfrac{1}{3000} + \dfrac{1}{6000} = \dfrac{2 + 1}{6000} = \dfrac{3}{6000} = \dfrac{1}{2000}$$

$$k_{eq} = 2000\ \text{N/m}$$

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_{eq}}{m}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2000}{5}} = \sqrt{400} = 20\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = 3.183\ \text{Hz}$$

$$T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = 0.3142\ \text{s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mix-ups are the #1 mark-deduction cause.** Always convert stiffness from N/mm to N/m (multiply by 1000) and amplitudes from mm to m (divide by 1000) *before* substituting into formulas. The formula $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ strictly requires SI units: N/m and kg.
> 2. **Peak-to-peak vs. amplitude confusion:** "Peak-to-peak" is the *total* swing; amplitude $X$ is *half* of that. Forgetting this halves the velocity, acceleration, and energy answers.
> 3. **Initial-condition sign errors:** When the mass is "pulled down and released from rest," $A = x_0$ and $B = 0$ — the motion is a pure cosine, not a sine. Watch the sign of $x_0$ based on the chosen positive direction.
> 4. **Parallel vs. series mix-up:** Springs between the same two nodes = **parallel** ($k_{eq}$ increases). Springs in a single chain sharing the same force = **series** ($k_{eq}$ decreases).
> 5. **Forgetting to state units** in the final answer — KTU examiners routinely deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **SDOF System:** One independent coordinate fully describes the system. Typical example: mass-on-spring sliding on a frictionless surface.
- **Equation of Motion:** Always the standard form $m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$ for an undamped system.
- **Natural Angular Frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ — determined *only* by system parameters, never by initial conditions.
- **Time Period:** $T = 2\pi/\omega_n = 2\pi\sqrt{m/k}$.
- **Natural Frequency in Hz:** $f_n = \omega_n / (2\pi) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$.
- **Static Deflection Trick:** When stiffness $k$ is not directly given, use $\omega_n = \sqrt{g/\delta_{st}}$ where $\delta_{st} = mg/k$.
- **General Solution:** $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_n t) + B\sin(\omega_n t)$ or equivalently $X\sin(\omega_n t + \phi)$.
- **Initial Conditions:** $A = x(0)$, $B = \dot{x}(0)/\omega_n$. These *uniquely* fix the motion.
- **Amplitude:** $X = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2} = \sqrt{x_0^2 + (v_0/\omega_n)^2}$ — independent of time.
- **Phase Angle:** $\phi = \tan^{-1}(A/B)$ — encodes the *mix* of initial position and velocity.
- **Maximum Velocity:** $\dot{x}_{max} = X\omega_n$ — at the equilibrium (mean) position.
- **Maximum Acceleration:** $\ddot{x}_{max} = X\omega_n^{\,2} = X(k/m)$ — at the extreme positions.
- **Total Energy:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2}kX^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}mX^2\omega_n^{\,2}$ — strictly constant in time.
- **Springs in Parallel:** $k_{eq} = k_1 + k_2 + \dots$ — same displacement.
- **Springs in Series:** $1/k_{eq} = 1/k_1 + 1/k_2 + \dots$ — same force.
- **Energy Method:** Apply $\dfrac{d}{dt}(T + U) = 0$ to recover the same ODE.
- **Simple Harmonic Motion:** The free undamped vibration of an SDOF system is by definition SHM, characterized by its pure sinusoidal waveform.
- **Frequency-Stiffness Coupling:** Stiffness up by factor 4 → $\omega_n$ doubles. Mass up by factor 4 → $\omega_n$ halves.
- **Conversion Reminder:** $1$ Hz $= 2\pi$ rad/s. Always convert explicitly in numerical problems.
- **Sign Convention:** Always declare the positive direction of $x$ before writing the equation. The spring force is always $F = -kx$.
- **Damped vs. Undamped:** The undamped case is the *upper bound* of oscillation amplitude — adding any damping can only reduce it. This fact is often tested in comparative KTU questions.
- **Initial disturbance types:** Either an initial displacement $x_0$ (with $\dot{x}_0 = 0$) or an initial velocity $v_0$ (with $x_0 = 0$) or both — the general solution handles all three cases seamlessly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classical Method, Energy Method, Equivalent Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT527 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classical Method, Energy Method, Equivalent Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — Classical Method, Energy Method, and Equivalent Systems

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Free Vibration of a Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) System** is the undamped oscillatory motion of a system displaced from its equilibrium position and released, where the restoring force is proportional to displacement (Hooke's Law regime). The **natural frequency (ωₙ)** of such a system is the frequency at which it vibrates freely, and is a fundamental property determined by the system's mass and stiffness (or equivalent inertia and compliance).

The three foundational techniques for analyzing this motion are:

1. **Classical (Newtonian) Method** — A force-based approach using Newton's Second Law (or D'Alembert's Principle) to derive the differential equation of motion. Solve it to extract ωₙ.
2. **Energy Method (Rayleigh's Method)** — An energy-based approach using the principle of conservation of energy. Equate maximum kinetic energy (KE) to maximum potential energy (PE) during the cycle.
3. **Equivalent Systems (Stiffness & Mass Equivalence)** — A simplification technique to reduce complex assemblies (multiple springs/masses) into a single, simple equivalent mass-spring model whose dynamic response matches the original.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **child on a swing**. The swing is the *mass*, the chains are the *spring*, and gravity provides the *restoring force*.
- The **Classical Method** is like an engineer drawing a free-body diagram of the swing, writing $\Sigma F = ma$, and calculating exactly how fast it oscillates.
- The **Energy Method** is like observing that at the highest point of the swing, the child is momentarily still (all energy is *potential*), and at the lowest point, they are moving fastest (all energy is *kinetic*). Since energy is conserved, we can relate these two snapshots — without needing to solve the differential equation.
- **Equivalent Systems** is like replacing two different trampolines stacked together with a single "equivalent" trampoline that gives the same bounce — easier to analyze, identical behavior.

### 📌 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Convention)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit (SI) | Engineering Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mass | $m$ | kilogram | **kg** |
| Stiffness | $k$ | newton/metre | **N/m** |
| Natural (angular) frequency | $\omega_n$ | radian/second | **rad/s** |
| Natural frequency (cyclic) | $f_n$ | hertz | **Hz** |
| Time period | $T$ | second | **s** |
| Displacement | $x$ | metre | **m** |
| Damping ratio (for undamped) | $\zeta$ | dimensionless | **0** (undamped) |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme, the **undamped** case ($\zeta = 0$) is the primary focus of this module. Damped vibrations are covered separately in the next topic.

---

## 1.2 The Reference Model — Undamped SDOF Mass-Spring System

The foundational model used in all three methods is shown schematically below:

- A rigid mass $m$ is constrained to move only along the $x$-axis.
- It is connected to a fixed support via a massless linear spring of stiffness $k$.
- The mass is displaced by an initial amount $X$ (amplitude) and released from rest.
- There is **no damping** in this baseline case.

The governing equation in its most general linear form is:

$$m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$

where $\ddot{x} = \dfrac{d^2x}{dt^2}$ is the acceleration. This is a **second-order linear homogeneous ordinary differential equation** whose solution is the bedrock of vibration theory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Method 1 — The Classical (Newtonian) Method

### 🔑 Core Philosophy
> [!NOTE]
> The Classical Method is a **force-balance approach**. You draw a free-body diagram, apply D'Alembert's principle (which states that a system is in dynamic equilibrium when the inertial force $-m\ddot{x}$ is treated as an additional applied force), and obtain the equation of motion directly.

### 📐 Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Define the coordinate system:** Choose $x$ measured from the static equilibrium position. This is critical because at equilibrium, gravity is exactly cancelled by the static spring deflection, leaving only the *dynamic* restoring force $kx$.

2. **Draw the free-body diagram (FBD):** When the mass is displaced by $x$ from equilibrium, the spring exerts a restoring force $-kx$ (opposing the displacement, hence the negative sign).

3. **Apply Newton's Second Law:**
   $$\sum F_x = m\ddot{x} \implies -kx = m\ddot{x}$$

4. **Rearrange into standard form:**
   $$m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$

5. **Recognize the form:** This is a second-order linear homogeneous ODE with constant coefficients. The standard solution is *simple harmonic motion*:
   $$x(t) = X \sin(\omega_n t + \phi)$$
   where $X$ is the amplitude and $\phi$ is the phase angle.

6. **Determine the natural frequency:** Substitute the assumed solution back into the ODE. The derivative is $\ddot{x} = -\omega_n^2 X \sin(\omega_n t + \phi)$. Substituting:
   $$m(-\omega_n^2 X \sin(\omega_n t + \phi)) + k(X \sin(\omega_n t + \phi)) = 0$$
   $$(-m\omega_n^2 + k) \cdot X \sin(\omega_n t + \phi) = 0$$
   For this to hold for all $t$, the term in parentheses must vanish:
   $$-m\omega_n^2 + k = 0 \implies \omega_n^2 = \frac{k}{m}$$
   $$\boxed{\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}}$$

### 🌍 Real-World Utility
The Classical Method is the workhorse in **structural earthquake engineering** (modelling a building as an SDOF oscillator), **vehicle suspension design**, and **rotor dynamics**. It gives the *exact* natural frequency for linear SDOF systems.

---

## 2.2 Method 2 — The Energy Method (Rayleigh's Method)

### 🔑 Core Philosophy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Energy Method exploits the **conservation of mechanical energy** in a conservative (undamped, no energy dissipation) system. In a complete cycle of vibration, the total mechanical energy $E$ is constant:
> $$E = \text{KE} + \text{PE} = \text{constant}$$

Since energy cannot be created or destroyed in an undamped system, the **maximum kinetic energy** (when the mass passes through equilibrium with maximum velocity) must equal the **maximum potential energy** (at the extreme position where velocity is zero). This single equality bypasses the need to solve a differential equation.

### 📐 Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Assume simple harmonic motion** as the solution form:
   $$x(t) = X \sin(\omega_n t)$$
   $$\dot{x}(t) = X \omega_n \cos(\omega_n t)$$

2. **Write the kinetic energy** at any instant:
   $$\text{KE} = \frac{1}{2} m \dot{x}^2 = \frac{1}{2} m \omega_n^2 X^2 \cos^2(\omega_n t)$$
   The **maximum KE** occurs when $\cos(\omega_n t) = 1$ (mass at equilibrium, maximum speed):
   $$\text{KE}_{\max} = \frac{1}{2} m \omega_n^2 X^2$$

3. **Write the potential energy** stored in the spring at any instant:
   $$\text{PE} = \frac{1}{2} k x^2 = \frac{1}{2} k X^2 \sin^2(\omega_n t)$$
   The **maximum PE** occurs when $\sin(\omega_n t) = 1$ (mass at extreme position, momentarily at rest):
   $$\text{PE}_{\max} = \frac{1}{2} k X^2$$

4. **Apply the conservation principle** (KE_max = PE_max):
   $$\frac{1}{2} m \omega_n^2 X^2 = \frac{1}{2} k X^2$$
   Cancel $\frac{1}{2} X^2$ from both sides:
   $$m \omega_n^2 = k \implies \omega_n^2 = \frac{k}{m}$$
   $$\boxed{\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}}$$

5. **Result identical to Classical Method** — but obtained without solving a differential equation! This is the power of the energy method for **complex multi-DOF or continuous systems** where deriving the differential equation becomes intractable.

### 🌍 Real-World Utility
Rayleigh's energy method scales beautifully to **multi-degree-of-freedom** and **continuous systems** (beams, plates). Engineers use it in **modal analysis** of structures, **turbine blade vibration**, and **aerospace flutter** analysis. The famous *Rayleigh's quotient* for MDOF systems generalizes this principle.

---

## 2.3 Method 3 — Equivalent Systems (Stiffness & Mass Equivalence)

### 🔑 Core Philosophy
> [!NOTE]
> Many real engineering systems (vehicle suspensions, machine tool supports, building foundations) have **multiple springs or distributed stiffness elements** in various configurations. Equivalent Systems theory allows the engineer to reduce these to a **single effective spring constant $k_{eq}$** (and sometimes an effective mass $m_{eq}$) such that the simplified model has the *same natural frequency* as the original.

### 🔧 Equivalent Stiffness of Spring Combinations

#### Case A: Springs in **Parallel** (same displacement, forces add)

When two springs share the **same displacement** $x$ (e.g., a mass resting on two springs side by side), they experience identical deformation. The total restoring force is the sum:

$$F_{\text{total}} = k_1 x + k_2 x = (k_1 + k_2) x = k_{eq} x$$

$$\boxed{k_{eq} = k_1 + k_2 \quad \text{(Parallel)}}$$
