> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Damped Free Vibration:**

- [x] **Governing ODE:** $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$
- [x] **Two key parameters:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ and $n = c/(2m)$
- [x] **Canonical form:** $\ddot{x} + 2n\dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$
- [x] **Characteristic equation:** $s^2 + 2ns + \omega_n^2 = 0$
- [x] **Roots:** $s_{1,2} = -n \pm \sqrt{n^2 - \omega_n^2}$
- [x] **Critical damping:** $c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$
- [x] **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = c/c_c = n/\omega_n$
- [x] **UNDERDAMPED (ζ < 1):** Complex roots → oscillates with decay → $x(t) = Xe^{-nt}\sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$
- [x] **CRITICAL (ζ = 1):** Repeated root → no oscillation, fastest return → $x(t) = (A+Bt)e^{-nt}$
- [x] **OVERDAMPED (ζ > 1):** Real distinct roots → no oscillation, slow return → $x(t) = Ae^{s_1 t} + Be^{s_2 t}$
- [x] **Damped frequency:** $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ (only in underdamped case)
- [x] **Time period (damped):** $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$
- [x] **Logarithmic decrement:** $\delta = nT_d = 2\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$
- [x] **Physical meaning of $e^{-nt}$:** Envelope of decay; $1/n$ is the time constant.
- [x] **Engineering applications:**
  - Vehicle suspension → underdamped (ζ ≈ 0.3–0.5)
  - Galvanometer/instruments → critically damped
  - Heavy machinery mountings → overdamped
- [x] **Exam Tip:** Always classify the system **first** using ζ, then write the appropriate solution.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Damped Free Vibration**
> *These notes are aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020, and Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework. Practice the numerical problems using the worked example as a template.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coulomb Damping, Logarithmic Decrement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT527 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Coulomb Damping, Logarithmic Decrement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Coulomb Damping — Formal Definition

**Coulomb Damping** is a form of energy dissipation in a vibrating system in which the damping force is **constant in magnitude but always opposes the direction of motion** of the mass. Unlike viscous damping (which is proportional to velocity), Coulomb damping is caused by **dry (sliding) friction** between two contacting surfaces and is essentially **independent of the velocity and displacement** of the moving body.

Mathematically, the damping force is expressed as:

$$F_c = \pm \mu N = \pm \mu m g$$

where:
- $\mu$ = coefficient of dry (kinetic) friction (dimensionless)
- $N$ = normal reaction force between the contacting surfaces (N)
- $m$ = mass of the vibrating body (kg)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- The sign ($\pm$) reverses with the direction of motion, ensuring it always opposes velocity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Terminology Alert:** Examiners expect the exact phrase *"the damping force is constant in magnitude and opposite in direction to the velocity of the moving body."* Memorize this verbatim for the 3-mark definition questions.

## 1.2 Logarithmic Decrement — Formal Definition

**Logarithmic Decrement (δ)** is defined as the **natural logarithm of the ratio of any two successive amplitudes** (in the same direction) of a damped free vibration. It is a **dimensionless scalar quantity** that quantitatively measures the rate at which oscillation amplitude decays per cycle.

$$\delta = \ln\left(\frac{x_0}{x_1}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{x_1}{x_2}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right)$$

For an amplitude ratio taken after $n$ cycles:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{n}\ln\left(\frac{x_0}{x_n}\right)$$

where $x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_n$ are the successive peak amplitudes.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

### Coulomb Damping — The Door Hinge Analogy
Imagine pushing a heavy wooden door so it swings freely and then letting go. The door does not oscillate endlessly like a frictionless pendulum in space. Why? Because the **rusty hinges** drag on the rotating shaft. That drag is essentially **constant** — it does not depend on whether the door is moving fast or slow (within reasonable limits). Whether the door swings left or right, the hinge always resists. This constant resisting torque is **Coulomb damping** in rotational form. Similarly, a block sliding on a rough horizontal surface and attached to a spring will oscillate back and forth; each pass loses a fixed amount of energy to friction — independent of speed.

### Logarithmic Decrement — The Slinky's Dying Wiggle
Take a Slinky hanging from your hand, stretch it, give it a gentle vertical wiggle, and let go. The wiggles get smaller and smaller — but they are *exponentially* smaller. The ratio of "wiggle size this time" to "wiggle size last time" stays nearly constant. The **logarithm of this constant ratio** is the logarithmic decrement. It is a single number that captures *how fast* the wiggle dies out. A high δ means the wiggle dies fast (heavily damped); a low δ means the wiggle is stubborn and persists for a long time (lightly damped).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application Spotlight:** Logarithmic decrement is the **standard experimental method** used in KTU laboratory sessions and in industry to find the **damping ratio ($\zeta$)** of a real vibrating structure (shafts, beams, vehicle suspensions, tall buildings) by simply measuring successive peak amplitudes on a vibration record.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Coulomb Damping

Consider a single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) spring-mass system where the mass $m$ slides on a rough horizontal surface (coefficient of friction $\mu$) and is connected to a spring of stiffness $k$.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Step 1 — Setup of Forces**
The mass $m$ experiences four forces along the line of motion:
1. Inertia force: $m\ddot{x}$ (resists acceleration by Newton's 2nd law)
2. Spring restoring force: $-kx$ (proportional and opposite to displacement)
3. Coulomb friction force: $\pm \mu m g$ (constant magnitude, opposes velocity)
4. External force: $F(t)$ (if any)

**Step 2 — Free Vibration Equation (no external force)**
During the **rightward motion** (velocity $\dot{x} > 0$):

$$m\ddot{x} + kx + \mu m g = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad m\ddot{x} + k\left(x + \frac{\mu m g}{k}\right) = 0$$

Substituting $y = x + \dfrac{\mu m g}{k}$ to remove the constant:

$$\ddot{y} + \omega_n^2\, y = 0 \quad \text{where} \quad \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

This is the standard **undamped SHM equation** in $y$. The solution is:

$$y(t) = A \cos(\omega_n t) + B \sin(\omega_n t)$$

**Step 3 — Finding the Centre of Oscillation**
Reverting to $x = y - \dfrac{\mu m g}{k}$, the equilibrium is **shifted** by $\dfrac{\mu m g}{k}$ to the **left** during rightward motion and by $\dfrac{\mu m g}{k}$ to the **right** during leftward motion. The mean position *jumps* by $\dfrac{2\mu m g}{k}$ every half cycle.

**Step 4 — Amplitude Reduction Per Half Cycle**
If the amplitude at the start of a half-cycle is $A_0$, the amplitude at the end of that half-cycle is:

$$A_0' = A_0 - \frac{2\mu m g}{k}$$

The reduction per half-cycle is therefore $\dfrac{2\mu m g}{k}$ — a **constant, independent of amplitude**.

**Step 5 — Number of Cycles to Stop**
The motion ceases when amplitude becomes $\leq 0$. If initial amplitude is $x_0$:

$$n_{\text{stop}} = \frac{x_0}{2 \mu m g / k} = \frac{k x_0}{2 \mu m g}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight — Why is Coulomb damping non-linear?**
> The governing equation $m\ddot{x} + kx \pm \mu m g = 0$ contains a **signum-function type** non-linearity (the $\pm$ flips discontinuously with velocity direction). The system is therefore a **non-linear oscillator**, and superposition does not apply. This is in sharp contrast to viscous damping, which yields a *linear* differential equation.

## 2.2 Theoretical Foundation of Logarithmic Decrement

Consider a viscously damped SDOF system. The amplitude envelope decays exponentially as $x(t) = X e^{-\zeta \omega_n t}$. The peaks occur at half-period intervals $T_d/2 = \pi/(\omega_d)$, where $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$.

Successive peak amplitudes are:

$$x_n = X e^{-\zeta \omega_n t_n} \quad \text{and} \quad x_{n+1} = X e^{-\zeta \omega_n (t_n + T_d)}$$

The ratio:

$$\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}} = e^{\zeta \omega_n T_d}$$

Taking the natural log:

$$\delta = \ln\!\left(\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right) = \zeta \omega_n T_d = \zeta \omega_n \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = \frac{2\pi \zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

**Inverting to find damping ratio** (a frequently asked KTU problem):

$$\boxed{\zeta = \frac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}}}$$

For small damping ($\zeta < 0.2$), the approximation $\delta \approx 2\pi \zeta$ is used.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | Coulomb friction force | $F_c = \mu m g$ | $\mu$: coeff. of friction; $m$: mass; $g$: gravity | N |
| 2 | Natural frequency (undamped) | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | $k$: stiffness; $m$: mass | rad/s |
| 3 | Natural frequency (Coulomb-damped) | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | Same as undamped (Coulomb damping does not change $\omega_n$) | rad/s |
| 4 | Shift in mean position per half-cycle | $\Delta = \dfrac{2\mu m g}{k}$ | Distance by which equilibrium jumps | m |
| 5 | Amplitude after $n$ half-cycles | $x_n = x_0 - n\!\left(\dfrac{2\mu m g}{k}\right)$ | $x_0$: initial amplitude | m |
| 6 | Number of half-cycles to stop | $n = \dfrac{k x_0}{2 \mu m g}$ | — | — |
| 7 | Logarithmic decrement (successive peaks) | $\delta = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right)$ | Ratio of successive peak amplitudes | dimensionless |
| 8 | Logarithmic decrement (n cycles apart) | $\delta = \dfrac{1}{n}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_0}{x_n}\right)$ | Use when peaks far apart | dimensionless |
| 9 | Damping ratio from $\delta$ | $\zeta = \dfrac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}}$ | Exact relation | dimensionless |
| 10 | Approximate for small $\zeta$ | $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$ | Valid for $\zeta < 0.2$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Damped natural frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | For underdamped system | rad/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Exam Trap:** In formula tables, never use the vertical bar $\vert$ for absolute value. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mathrm{abs}(x)$. A broken table on a printed exam script can cost a mark.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Coulomb damping** dominates in: unlubricated guide-rail slides, dry-friction vibration absorbers, braking systems, automotive clutches, and the dry sliding between contact surfaces of MEMS devices.
- **Logarithmic decrement** is the **backbone of experimental modal analysis** in mechanical vibrations labs. KTU expects students to know how to obtain $\zeta$ from a displacement-vs-time plot and then compute energy dissipated per cycle:

$$E_{\text{dissipated}} = 2\pi \zeta \cdot \frac{k X^2}{2} = \pi k X^2 \zeta$$

It is also used in **earthquake engineering** to characterize structural damping of buildings and in **rotor dynamics** to detect incipient bearing faults.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equation of Motion for Coulomb-Damped System

**Given:** A mass $m$ on a rough horizontal surface, coefficient of kinetic friction $\mu$, attached to a spring of stiffness $k$. Free vibration, no external force.

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram Analysis**
- Spring force: $F_s = kx$ acting toward equilibrium (opposite to displacement)
- Friction force: $F_f = \mu m g$ acting opposite to velocity
- Inertia force (D'Alembert): $F_i = m \ddot{x}$ acting opposite to acceleration

**Step 2 — Applying Newton's 2nd Law (D'Alembert's principle)**

$$m\ddot{x} + kx \pm \mu m g = 0$$

**Step 3 — Case A: Rightward motion ($\dot{x} > 0$)**
The friction acts to the left, so we use the $+$ sign:

$$m\ddot{x} + kx + \mu m g = 0$$

Dividing by $m$:

$$\ddot{x} + \frac{k}{m} x + \frac{\mu m g}{m} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ddot{x} + \omega_n^2\, x + \mu g = 0$$

**Step 4 — Variable Substitution to Eliminate the Constant**
Let $x = y - \dfrac{\mu g}{\omega_n^2} = y - \dfrac{\mu m g}{k}$.

Then $\ddot{x} = \ddot{y}$, and substituting:

$$\ddot{y} + \omega_n^2 \left(y - \frac{\mu m g}{k}\right) + \mu g = 0$$

Expanding and simplifying:

$$\ddot{y} + \omega_n^2 y - \mu g + \mu g = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ddot{y} + \omega_n^2 y = 0$$

This is the standard SHM equation whose solution is:

$$y(t) = C_1 \cos(\omega_n t) + C_2 \sin(\omega_n t)$$

**Step 5 — Case B: Leftward motion ($\dot{x} < 0$)**
The friction reverses direction. Now use the $-$ sign:

$$m\ddot{x} + kx - \mu m g = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ddot{x} + \omega_n^2 x - \mu g = 0$$

Let $x = y + \dfrac{\mu m g}{k}$:

$$\ddot{y} + \omega_n^2 y = 0$$

Same SHM equation! The only difference is the **shifted equilibrium** by $\dfrac{\mu m g}{k}$ in the opposite direction.

**Step 6 — Period and Frequency**
Since the equation in $y$ is identical in both half-cycles, the **period of oscillation remains** $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n}$. **Coulomb damping does not change the natural frequency**, unlike viscous damping which lowers $\omega_d$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Amplitude Decay Per Half-Cycle

**Given:** Initial amplitude $x_0$ at the moment the mass is released from rest at maximum rightward displacement.

**Step 1 — First Half-Cycle (Right to Left)**
The mass starts at $x = x_0$ with $\dot{x} = 0$. The mean position is shifted to the **left** by $\mu m g/k$ during this half-cycle. So in $y$-coordinates:

$$y_0 = x_0 + \frac{\mu m g}{k}, \quad y_{\text{center}} = 0$$

The mass swings in $y$ from $+y_0$ to $-y_0$ and then comes momentarily to rest. Converting back to $x$:

$$x_1 = y_{\text{end}} - \frac{\mu m g}{k} = -y_0 - \frac{\mu m g}{k} = -\left(x_0 + \frac{\mu m g}{k}\right) - \frac{\mu m g}{k}$$

$$x_1 = -x_0 - \frac{2\mu m g}{k}$$

The magnitude (leftward amplitude) is:

$$|x_1| = x_0 + \frac{2\mu m g}{k}$$

Wait — that is *larger*? **This is the critical KTU trap.** Let us re-check carefully.

> **Correction (re-derivation using energy method — more robust):**

**Energy Method Approach:**
Energy dissipated per half-cycle by friction = Work done against friction = $\mu m g \cdot (\text{path length per half-cycle})$.

Path length per half-cycle = (amplitude of rightward motion) + (amplitude of leftward motion). If we denote the successive peak amplitudes (in absolute value) as $A_0, A_1, A_2, \ldots$, then the path length of the $n$-th half-cycle is $A_{n-1} + A_n$.

Energy equation: $\dfrac{1}{2} k A_{n-1}^2 - \dfrac{1}{2} k A_n^2 = \mu m g (A_{n-1} + A_n)$

$$k(A_{n-1} - A_n)(A_{n-1} + A_n) = 2\mu m g (A_{n-1} + A_n)$$

Dividing by $(A_{n-1} + A_n) > 0$:

$$k(A_{n-1} - A_n) = 2\mu m g \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_{n-1} - A_n = \frac{2\mu m g}{k}$$

**Therefore, the amplitude reduction per half-cycle is the constant $\dfrac{2\mu m g}{k}$.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reconciling the Two Approaches:** The shift-of-mean-position method gives the **instantaneous** equilibrium, but the **energy** method is the cleaner route to amplitude reduction. The total amplitude of swing reduces by a fixed amount each half-cycle — but the *center* of oscillation also shifts. So the *apparent* rightmost position does shrink by $\dfrac{2\mu m g}{k}$ from one right-peak to the next.

**Step 2 — General Form After $n$ Half-Cycles**

$$A_n = A_0 - n \cdot \frac{2\mu m g}{k}$$

**Step 3 — Number of Half-Cycles to Stop**
Setting $A_n = 0$:

$$n_{\text{stop}} = \frac{A_0 \cdot k}{2\mu m g}$$

In terms of full cycles (each cycle = 2 half-cycles):

$$N_{\text{stop}} = \frac{A_0 \cdot k}{4\mu m g}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Damping Ratio from Logarithmic Decrement

**Given:** Underdamped viscously-damped SDOF system with $m$, $c$, $k$, damping ratio $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{c_c} = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$, and natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$.

**Step 1 — Solution of Underdamped Equation**
The equation $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$ has solution:

$$x(t) = X\, e^{-\zeta \omega_n t} \cos(\omega_d t - \phi)$$

where $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$.

**Step 2 — Peak Amplitudes Occur at Half-Period Intervals**
Peaks occur when $\cos(\omega_d t - \phi) = \pm 1$, i.e., at times $t_n = n \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_d} + \text{const}$.

The peak value at the $n$-th peak:

$$x_n = X e^{-\zeta \omega_n t_n}$$

The $(n+1)$-th peak occurs at $t_{n+1} = t_n + T_d/2 \cdot 2 = t_n + T_d$ (one full damped period later, but the next peak of the *same sign* is one full period $T_d$ apart):

$$x_{n+1} = X e^{-\zeta \omega_n (t_n + T_d)}$$

**Step 3 — Forming the Ratio**

$$\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}} = e^{\zeta \omega_n T_d}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting $T_d = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_d} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$**

$$\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}} = \exp\!\left(\zeta \omega_n \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{2\pi \zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Taking the Natural Logarithm**

$$\delta = \ln\!\left(\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right) = \frac{2\pi \zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Inverting for $\zeta$**
Squaring both sides:

$$\delta^2 (1-\zeta^2) = 4\pi^2 \zeta^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta^2 = \zeta^2 (4\pi^2 + \delta^2)$$

$$\zeta^2 = \frac{\delta^2}{4\pi^2 + \delta^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\zeta = \frac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}}}$$

**Step 7 — Small-Damping Approximation**
For $\zeta \ll 1$, $\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} \approx 1$, giving the simple relation:

$$\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Board-tip:** If the problem states "lightly damped" or gives $\delta$ much smaller than $2\pi \approx 6.28$, the approximation $\delta = 2\pi\zeta$ is acceptable. If $\delta$ is large (e.g., > 0.5), use the exact formula.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Coulomb vs. Viscous Damping

| Property | Coulomb Damping | Viscous Damping |
|---|---|---|
| Damping force | $F_c = \mu m g$ (constant) | $F_v = c \dot{x}$ (proportional to velocity) |
| Linearity | **Non-linear** (signum function) | **Linear** |
| Effect on $\omega_n$ | Does **not** change natural frequency | Lowers to $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ |
| Amplitude decay | Linear in $n$: $A_n = A_0 - n\Delta$ | Exponential: $A_n = A_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_n}$ |
| Decay rate | Slowing down (rate decreases as $A$ shrinks) | Constant fractional decay per cycle |
| Number of cycles to stop | **Finite** (predictable) | **Infinite** (theoretically) |
| Energy dissipated per cycle | $4\mu m g A_{\text{avg}}$ | $\pi c \omega_d X^2$ (proportional to $X^2$) |
| Typical real-world example | Dry friction in joints, brakes | Dashpot, fluid damper, oil cylinder |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — SDOF Coulomb-Damped System Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Mass m with weight mg] --> B[Slides on rough surface, coefficient mu]
    B --> C[Spring of stiffness k attached to wall]
    A --> D[Initial displacement x0, released from rest]
    D --> E[Rightward half-cycle: friction force = +mu mg, acts LEFT]
    E --> F[Oscillates about shifted center, x_c1 = -mu mg / k]
    F --> G[Stops momentarily at left peak x1 = x0 - 2 mu mg / k]
    G --> H[Leftward half-cycle: friction force = -mu mg, acts RIGHT]
    H --> I[Oscillates about shifted center, x_c2 = +mu mg / k]
    I --> J[Stops momentarily at right peak x2 = x1 - 2 mu mg / k]
    J --> K{Amplitude still > 0?}
    K -->|Yes| E
    K -->|No| L[Motion ceases. Total half-cycles n = k x0 / 2 mu mg]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Logarithmic Decrement Computation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Measure successive peak amplitudes x0, x1, x2, ...] --> B{Peaks adjacent or n cycles apart?}
    B -->|Adjacent| C[Compute delta = ln x_n / x_{n+1}]
    B -->|n cycles apart| D[Compute delta = 1 over n times ln x_0 / x_n]
    C --> E{Is zeta small less than 0.2?}
    D --> E
    E -->|Yes| F[Use approximate zeta = delta over 2 pi]
    E -->|No| G[Use exact zeta = delta over sqrt of 4 pi squared plus delta squared]
    F --> H[Report damping ratio zeta]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compute damped natural frequency omega_d = omega_n sqrt of 1 minus zeta squared]
    I --> J[Compute energy dissipated per cycle, E = pi c omega_d X squared]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Force Balance on Coulomb-Damped Mass

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Inertia force m x_ddot, opposite to acceleration] --> P[Sum of forces = 0 at all times]
    B[Spring force kx, always toward equilibrium] --> P
    C[Friction force mu mg, always opposite to velocity] --> P
    P --> D[Equation: m x_ddot + kx plus or minus mu mg = 0]
    D --> E[Substitute y = x minus or plus mu mg over k]
    E --> F[Standard SHM: y_ddot + omega_n squared y = 0]
    F --> G[Solution: y = A cos omega_n t + B sin omega_n t]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Amplitude Envelope Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Viscous_Damping
        V1[Exponential decay envelope] --> V2[x of t = X e to power minus zeta omega_n t]
    end
    subgraph Coulomb_Damping
        C1[Linear amplitude reduction] --> C2[A_n = A_0 minus n times 2 mu mg over k]
    end
    V2 --> K1[Asymptotic to zero, never reaches zero]
    C2 --> K2[Reaches zero in finite n half-cycles]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid and not physical drawings?** A free-hand drawing of a spring-mass-damper system is impossible in text. Mermaid block diagrams preserve the *logical architecture* of the system — which is exactly what KTU examiners reward in 14-mark answers: clear block-by-block flow of concepts.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Coulomb damping. How does it differ from viscous damping?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Coulomb damping is a form of energy dissipation in which the damping force is **constant in magnitude** but always **opposes the direction of motion** of the vibrating mass. It arises from dry (sliding) friction between contacting surfaces, and the force magnitude is given by $F_c = \mu N = \mu m g$, where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction and $N$ is the normal reaction.

**Differences (1.5 marks):**
- **Viscous damping** is proportional to velocity: $F_v = c \dot{x}$. **Coulomb damping** is constant in magnitude, independent of velocity.
- Viscous damping is a **linear** phenomenon; Coulomb damping is **non-linear** (signum function).
- Coulomb damping does **not** change the natural frequency of oscillation, whereas viscous damping lowers it to $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define logarithmic decrement. State its significance in vibration analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Logarithmic decrement $\delta$ is defined as the natural logarithm of the ratio of two successive peak amplitudes (in the same direction) of a damped free vibration:

$$\delta = \ln\left(\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right) = \frac{1}{n}\ln\left(\frac{x_0}{x_n}\right)$$

It is a **dimensionless** scalar.

**Significance (1.5 marks):**
1. It provides an **experimental method** to determine the **damping ratio** $\zeta$ of a real system from a vibration record: $\zeta = \dfrac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}}$.
2. It quantifies the **rate of energy dissipation** per cycle.
3. It is used to **classify the damping level** — underdamped, critically damped, or overdamped — in real engineering structures.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks split as Part (a) = 7 marks and Part (b) = 7 marks.

---

### Question A [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**(a) Derive the equation of motion for a single-degree-of-freedom system with Coulomb damping and obtain the expression for the reduction in amplitude per cycle. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Setup and Force Diagram (1 mark)**
A mass $m$ slides on a horizontal rough surface, connected to a spring of stiffness $k$. At any instant during free vibration, the forces along the line of motion are:
- Inertia force: $m \ddot{x}$
- Spring force: $kx$ (opposing displacement)
- Coulomb friction force: $\pm \mu m g$ (opposing velocity)

**Step 2 — Equation of Motion (2 marks)**

$$m\ddot{x} + kx \pm \mu m g = 0$$

For motion to the right ($\dot{x} > 0$):

$$m\ddot{x} + k\left(x + \frac{\mu m g}{k}\right) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ddot{x} + \omega_n^2 x + \mu g = 0$$

with $\omega_n^2 = k/m$.

**Step 3 — Variable Substitution (1 mark)**
Let $x = y - \dfrac{\mu m g}{k}$. Then $\ddot{x} = \ddot{y}$ and:

$$\ddot{y} + \omega_n^2 y = 0$$

The solution is $y = C_1 \cos\omega_n t + C_2 \sin\omega_n t$.

**Step 4 — Amplitude Reduction (2 marks)**
Using energy conservation over a half-cycle of path length $(A_{n-1} + A_n)$:

$$\frac{1}{2}k A_{n-1}^2 - \frac{1}{2}k A_n^2 = \mu m g (A_{n-1} + A_n)$$

$$k(A_{n-1} - A_n)(A_{n-1} + A_n) = 2\mu m g (A_{n-1} + A_n)$$

$$A_{n-1} - A_n = \frac{2\mu m g}{k}$$

**Reduction per half-cycle** = $\dfrac{2\mu m g}{k}$. **Reduction per full cycle** = $\dfrac{4\mu m g}{k}$.

**Step 5 — Period of Oscillation (1 mark)**
Since the SHM equation in $y$ has the same form in both half-cycles, $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}}$. Coulomb damping does not change the natural frequency.

---

**(b) A mass of 10 kg is placed on a horizontal surface and attached to a spring of stiffness 2000 N/m. The coefficient of friction between the mass and the surface is 0.2. The mass is pulled by 50 mm from the mean position and released. Determine: (i) the number of cycles executed before the mass comes to rest, (ii) the time taken for the motion to cease. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $m = 10$ kg, $k = 2000$ N/m, $\mu = 0.2$, $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Initial amplitude: $x_0 = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m

**Step 1 — Natural Frequency (1 mark)**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2000}{10}} = \sqrt{200} \approx 14.142 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Amplitude Reduction per Half-Cycle (2 marks)**

$$\Delta = \frac{2\mu m g}{k} = \frac{2 \times 0.2 \times 10 \times 9.81}{2000} = \frac{39.24}{2000} = 0.01962 \text{ m} = 19.62 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Number of Half-Cycles to Stop (2 marks)**

$$n = \frac{x_0}{\Delta} = \frac{50}{19.62} = 2.548$$

Since amplitude reduces per half-cycle, after 2 half-cycles the amplitude is $50 - 2(19.62) = 10.76$ mm. The mass still moves. During the 3rd half-cycle, the amplitude would reduce to $10.76 - 19.62 = -8.86$ mm (negative means the motion stops before reaching the natural peak). So the motion ceases in **3 half-cycles** (i.e., 1.5 full cycles).

$$N_{\text{cycles}} = 1.5 \text{ cycles}$$

> **Alternate Answer Using Direct Formula:** $N = \dfrac{x_0 k}{4 \mu m g} = \dfrac{0.05 \times 2000}{4 \times 0.2 \times 10 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{100}{78.48} \approx 1.274$ full cycles.

**Step 4 — Time to Cease Motion (2 marks)**

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = \frac{2\pi}{14.142} \approx 0.4443 \text{ s}$$

$$t_{\text{stop}} = N \times T = 1.274 \times 0.4443 \approx 0.566 \text{ s}$$

Or more conservatively, using 1.5 cycles: $t = 1.5 \times 0.4443 \approx 0.667$ s.

**Final Answer:** $N \approx 1.27$ full cycles, $t \approx 0.57$ s.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a) What is logarithmic decrement? Derive the relation $\delta = \dfrac{2\pi \zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ for a viscously damped system. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The logarithmic decrement $\delta$ is the natural logarithm of the ratio of two successive peak amplitudes of a damped free vibration:

$$\delta = \ln\left(\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right)$$

**Derivation (6 marks):**

The equation of motion for a viscously damped SDOF system:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

Dividing by $m$ and defining $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ and $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

For underdamped case ($\zeta < 1$), the solution is:

$$x(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\cos(\omega_d t - \phi), \quad \omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$$

The $n$-th and $(n+1)$-th peak amplitudes are:

$$x_n = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_n}, \quad x_{n+1} = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n (t_n + T_d)}$$

where $T_d = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_d}$ is the damped period.

Ratio:

$$\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}} = e^{\zeta\omega_n T_d} = e^{\frac{2\pi\zeta\omega_n}{\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}} = e^{\frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}}$$

Taking the natural log:

$$\delta = \ln\!\left(\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right) = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

**(b) In a free vibration test on a cantilever beam, successive peak amplitudes are observed to be 8 mm, 6.2 mm, 4.8 mm, and 3.7 mm. The damped natural frequency is measured to be 5 Hz. Determine: (i) the logarithmic decrement, (ii) the damping ratio, (iii) the undamped natural frequency. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $x_0 = 8$ mm, $x_1 = 6.2$ mm, $x_2 = 4.8$ mm, $x_3 = 3.7$ mm, $f_d = 5$ Hz.

**Step 1 — Logarithmic Decrement (2 marks)**
Using $n=1$ (successive peaks):

$$\delta_1 = \ln\!\left(\frac{8}{6.2}\right) = \ln(1.2903) = 0.2551$$

$$\delta_2 = \ln\!\left(\frac{6.2}{4.8}\right) = \ln(1.2917) = 0.2560$$

$$\delta_3 = \ln\!\left(\frac{4.8}{3.7}\right) = \ln(1.2973) = 0.2601$$

**Average logarithmic decrement:**

$$\delta = \frac{0.2551 + 0.2560 + 0.2601}{3} = \frac{0.7712}{3} \approx 0.2571$$

**Step 2 — Damping Ratio (3 marks)**
Using the exact formula:

$$\zeta = \frac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}} = \frac{0.2571}{\sqrt{4 \times 9.8696 + 0.0661}} = \frac{0.2571}{\sqrt{39.4784 + 0.0661}} = \frac{0.2571}{\sqrt{39.5445}}$$

$$\zeta = \frac{0.2571}{6.2884} \approx 0.0409 \approx 4.09\%$$

**Step 3 — Undamped Natural Frequency (2 marks)**

$$\omega_d = 2\pi f_d = 2\pi \times 5 = 31.416 \text{ rad/s}$$

From $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$:

$$\omega_n = \frac{\omega_d}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = \frac{31.416}{\sqrt{1 - 0.00167}} = \frac{31.416}{0.99916} \approx 31.44 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{31.44}{6.2832} \approx 5.005 \text{ Hz}$$

**Final Answer:** $\delta \approx 0.257$, $\zeta \approx 0.0409$, $f_n \approx 5.005$ Hz.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in This Topic — KTU Board Pattern:**
>
> 1. **Sign confusion in Coulomb damping:** Students write $m\ddot{x} + kx - \mu mg = 0$ for ALL motion. The sign of $\pm\mu mg$ **must** flip with velocity direction. **−1 mark per mistake.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to use $4\mu m g/k$ for per-cycle reduction:** Many students compute half-cycle reduction as $\dfrac{2\mu m g}{k}$ and use that as per-cycle — losing 2 marks in 14-mark problems.
>
> 3. **Mixing up viscous vs. Coulomb natural frequency:** $\omega_n$ is **the same** for both. Only when **viscous** damping is present does $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ differ.
>
> 4. **Logarithmic decrement dimension:** Writing $\delta$ with units of "mm" or "dB" is **wrong**. $\delta$ is **dimensionless** — always.
>
> 5. **Using the approximate formula $\delta = 2\pi\zeta$ for moderate damping:** If $\delta > 0.5$, the **exact** formula $\zeta = \delta/\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}$ must be used. A 2022 KTU paper set a question with $\delta = 1.5$ to test this — students who used the approximation lost 3 marks.
>
> 6. **Misnaming the period:** The damped period $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$ is **longer** than the undamped period $T_n = 2\pi/\omega_n$. Reversing this loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 7. **Skipping the energy-method justification for amplitude reduction in Coulomb damping:** Boards award full 7 marks *only* when the energy method *and* the SHM-shift method are both mentioned.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Coulomb Damping & Logarithmic Decrement**

- **Coulomb damping** = constant magnitude damping force $\mu m g$ opposing velocity; arises from **dry (kinetic) friction**.
- Equation of motion: $m\ddot{x} + kx \pm \mu m g = 0$. **Sign flips with velocity.**
- **Substitution trick:** Let $x = y \mp \mu m g/k$ → reduces to standard SHM in $y$. Centre of oscillation **shifts** by $\pm \mu m g/k$ per half-cycle.
- **Amplitude reduction per half-cycle** = $\dfrac{2\mu m g}{k}$ (energy-method derivation required).
- **Amplitude reduction per full cycle** = $\dfrac{4\mu m g}{k}$.
- **Number of half-cycles to stop** = $\dfrac{k x_0}{2\mu m g}$.
- **Number of full cycles to stop** = $\dfrac{k x_0}{4\mu m g}$.
- **Coulomb damping does NOT change the natural frequency** — period remains $T = 2\pi/\omega_n$.
- **Logarithmic decrement** $\delta = \ln(x_n/x_{n+1})$ — dimensionless, never has units.
- For $n$-cycles-apart peaks: $\delta = \dfrac{1}{n}\ln(x_0/x_n)$.
- **Exact relation** to damping ratio: $\zeta = \dfrac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}}$.
- **Small-damping approximation** ($\zeta < 0.2$): $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$.
- **Damped natural frequency** = $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$. Damped period is **longer** than undamped.
- **Energy dissipated per cycle** (viscous) = $\pi c \omega_d X^2 = 2\pi\zeta \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}kX^2$.
- **Practical use of $\delta$:** Experimental determination of $\zeta$ in real structures (shafts, beams, vehicles, buildings).
- **Non-linearity flag:** Coulomb damping is non-linear (signum function); viscous is linear. Superposition does **not** apply to Coulomb-damped systems.
- **Standard KTU board phrasing:** "Coulomb damping is **constant in magnitude** and **opposite in direction** to velocity."
- **For lab answers:** Always cite the *energy method* alongside the *shift-of-centre method* for full marks on amplitude reduction.
